topics.title: Natural Sources of Vegetable Oils: Coconut, Palm, Peanut, Rice Bran, Sesame, Mustard, Sunflower Seeds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT748 - FAT AND OIL PROCESSING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Natural Sources of Vegetable Oils: Coconut, Palm, Peanut, Rice Bran, Sesame, Mustard, Sunflower Seeds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

Vegetable oils (also termed **vegetable fats** when solid at ambient temperature) are **lipid materials** extracted from plant-based natural sources — predominantly from seeds, fruits, nuts, and germs — through mechanical expression, solvent extraction, or a combination of both. They are composed primarily of **triacylglycerols (TAGs)**, with minor proportions of **phospholipids, glycolipids, tocopherols, sterols, waxes, free fatty acids (FFA), and pigments**.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus (PEFTT748 – Fat and Oil Processing Technology), the term **"Natural Sources of Vegetable Oils"** refers to the botanical origin, agricultural maturity state, post-harvest handling, and the **inherent physico-chemical composition** of seven major oil-bearing crops studied in Module 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme – PEFTT748, Module 1):**
> Students must be able to identify the botanical source, oil-bearing tissue, average oil yield (% on dry basis), dominant fatty acid family, characteristic minor constituents, and the typical industrial extraction route for **coconut, palm, peanut, rice bran, sesame, mustard, and sunflower seeds**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Plant's Energy Piggy Bank"

Imagine each plant as a **careful home-owner saving money for a rainy day**. Just as humans deposit cash into a piggy bank, plants convert photosynthetically-fixed solar energy into **dense triglyceride molecules** and store them inside specific organs:

- **Fruits & kernels** (coconut, palm) → the plant stores oil for the next generation's seedling.
- **Seeds** (sunflower, sesame, mustard, peanut) → oil is the packed lunch for the embryo to germinate.
- **Bran layers** (rice bran) → oil is a leftover from the polishing step of rice milling; for the seed itself, it is protective.

When humans process these crops, they are essentially **"cracking open the piggy bank"** of nature and refining the energy-rich triglycerides for food, fuel (biodiesel), cosmetics, and oleochemicals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metric:** The commercial price and utility of a vegetable oil is governed by its **Iodine Value (IV)** — a chemical measure of unsaturation, expressed as **g I₂ absorbed per 100 g of oil**. Higher IV = more unsaturated = more reactive = healthier for the body but less stable for frying.

## 1.3 Classification of the Seven Oils by Dominant Fatty Acid

| Fatty Acid Family | Chain Type | Member Oils (Module 1) |
|---|---|---|
| **Lauric oils** | C12 dominant | Coconut |
| **Palmitic-oleic oils** | C16 + C18:1 dominant | Palm |
| **Oleic-linoleic oils** | C18:1 + C18:2 dominant | Peanut, Sunflower, Sesame, Rice Bran |
| **Erucic oils** | C22:1 dominant | Mustard |

---

## 1.4 Quick-Reference Botanical Snapshot

| Oil | Botanical Name | Oil-Bearing Tissue | State at 25 °C |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coconut | *Cocos nucifera* | Endosperm (copra) | Solid / Semi-solid |
| Palm | *Elaeis guineensis* | Mesocarp (Flesh) | Semi-solid |
| Peanut | *Arachis hypogaea* | Cotyledons (kernel) | Liquid |
| Rice Bran | *Oryza sativa* | Bran layer | Liquid (wax-laden) |
| Sesame | *Sesamum indicum* | Seed | Liquid |
| Mustard | *Brassica juncea* | Seed | Liquid |
| Sunflower | *Helianthus annuus* | Seed (achene) | Liquid |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Individual Oil Profiles

### 2.1.1 Coconut Oil (*Cocos nucifera*)

- **Source tissue:** Dried kernel of the coconut fruit, commercially known as **copra** (moisture < 6 %).
- **Extraction:** Dry process — expeller pressing of copra, followed by solvent extraction of the residual cake.
- **Typical yield:** **62–72 %** oil on dry basis.
- **Dominant fatty acid:** **Lauric acid (C12:0) ≈ 45–52 %**.
- **Characteristic minor constituent:** **Caprylic (C8:0) and Capric (C10:0) acids** — conferring quick digestion and antimicrobial (anti-viral/anti-bacterial) properties.
- **High smoke point (≈ 232 °C)** when refined → excellent for high-temperature frying and confectionery fats.

### 2.1.2 Palm Oil (*Elaeis guineensis*)

- **Source tissue:** **Mesocarp** of the fresh fruit bunch (FFB). (The kernel gives *palm kernel oil*, a lauric oil, but the Module 1 focus is the **mesocarp-derived palm oil**.)
- **Extraction route:** Wet process — fruit is sterilized (steamed at **3 bar, 140 °C, 60–90 min**), stripped, digested, and pressed. Crude oil is then clarified by centrifugation.
- **Typical yield:** **20–25 %** of FFB.
- **Dominant fatty acids:** **Palmitic acid (C16:0) ≈ 44 %** and **Oleic acid (C18:1) ≈ 39 %**.
- **Signature minor constituent:** **β-Carotene (500–700 ppm)** — gives crude oil a deep orange-red hue; destroyed during standard refining, retained in "red palm oil".
- **Versatility:** Naturally semi-solid → can be fractionated into **palm olein** (liquid, frying) and **palm stearin** (solid, margarine/shortening).

### 2.1.3 Peanut Oil (*Arachis hypogaea*)

- **Source tissue:** Cotyledons (after removal of red skin/testa).
- **Extraction:** Expeller pressing + solvent (hexane) extraction.
- **Typical yield:** **45–55 %** oil on dry basis.
- **Dominant fatty acids:** **Oleic (C18:1) ≈ 50 %**, **Linoleic (C18:2) ≈ 30 %**.
- **Characteristic minor constituent:** **Behenic (C22:0) and Lignoceric (C24:0)** acids, plus **phytosterols** (β-sitosterol).
- **Distinct aroma compound:** **2-Methylbutanal & 2-Propanal** — produced during roasting of peanuts; crucial for *groundnut chutney* aroma.

### 2.1.4 Rice Bran Oil (*Oryza sativa*)

- **Source tissue:** The **bran layer** (pericarp + aleurone + germ fragments) separated as a by-product of rice milling.
- **Typical yield:** **15–23 %** oil from bran.
- **Dominant fatty acids:** **Oleic (C18:1) ≈ 38 %**, **Linoleic (C18:2) ≈ 34 %**, **Palmitic (C16:0) ≈ 21 %**.
- **Signature minor constituents:**
  - **γ-Oryzanol** (a ferulic-acid ester of triterpene alcohols) — clinically claimed to lower LDL cholesterol.
  - **Waxes (long-chain esters)** — these precipitate during dewaxing step of refining.
  - **Lipase enzyme** — *Lipase activity in fresh bran hydrolyzes oil → FFA rises at >5 %/day*, demanding immediate stabilization after milling.

### 2.1.5 Sesame Oil (*Sesamum indicum*)

- **Source tissue:** Tiny flat seeds; **hulled** before oil extraction.
- **Typical yield:** **45–55 %** oil.
- **Dominant fatty acids:** **Oleic (C18:1) ≈ 40–45 %**, **Linoleic (C18:2) ≈ 38–43 %**.
- **Signature minor constituent:** **Sesamin, Sesamolin, and Sesamol** — unique **lignans** that act as natural antioxidants and provide the characteristic **Baudouin test** colour reaction (red with furfural-HCl).
- **Ancient oil:** One of the earliest documented edible oils (Indian Ayurvedic texts).

### 2.1.6 Mustard Oil (*Brassica juncea / B. campestris / B. nigra*)

- **Source tissue:** Small round seeds (~ 1 mm diameter), often cold-pressed.
- **Typical yield:** **30–35 %** oil.
- **Dominant fatty acids:**
  - **Erucic acid (C22:1) ≈ 40–50 %** — historically a concern for cardiac lipidosis; modern cultivars ("*canola*") bred for < 2 % erucic acid.
  - **Oleic, Linoleic, Linolenic** in lower amounts.
- **Signature minor constituent:** **Allyl isothiocyanate** — released from **sinigrin** by the enzyme myrosinase upon crushing; gives the pungent, tear-inducing aroma of mustard.
- **State:** Liquid at room temperature; turns cloudy below 10 °C due to erucic acid crystallization.

### 2.1.7 Sunflower Seed Oil (*Helianthus annuus*)

- **Source tissue:** Kernel of the achene (the "seed" inside the hull).
- **Typical yield:** **40–50 %** oil on dehulled seed.
- **Dominant fatty acids:**
  - **Traditional cultivar:** Linoleic (C18:2) ≈ 60–70 % — a "linoleic-type" oil.
  - **Modern hybrid:** Oleic (C18:1) ≈ 80–85 % — a "high-oleic" variety (NuSun / Pervenets / high-oleic hybrids) used for industrial frying.
- **Signature minor constituent:** **α-Tocopherol (Vitamin E)** ≈ 60 mg/100 g — natural antioxidant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Connection:** Sunflower oil is the most important feedstock for **interesterification** and **partial hydrogenation** processes covered in later modules because its high linoleic/oleic content responds well to these structural modifications.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory equations a KTU student must memorize for Module 1 calculations:**

### 2.2.1 Acid Value (AV)

$$
AV = \frac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W}
$$

Where: *V* = mL of KOH used, *N* = Normality of KOH (≈ **0.1 N**), *W* = weight of oil (g), 56.1 = equivalent weight of KOH.

Units: **mg KOH/g oil**.

### 2.2.2 Free Fatty Acid (FFA) as Oleic Acid

$$
FFA(\%) = \frac{V \times N \times 28.2}{W}
$$

(Since equivalent weight of oleic acid = 282/10 = **28.2**)

### 2.2.3 Iodine Value (Wijs Method)

$$
IV = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 12.69}{W}
$$

Where: *V_b* = blank titre, *V_s* = sample titre, *N* = normality of Na₂S₂O₃ (≈ **0.1 N**), 12.69 = equivalent weight of I₂ ÷ 10.

### 2.2.4 Saponification Value (SV)

$$
SV = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 56.1}{W}
$$

### 2.2.5 Saponification Equivalent (Molar Mass Estimate)

$$
M_w = \frac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{SV}
$$

> A *lower SV* → *higher mean molecular weight* of the constituent fatty acids (e.g., coconut, with C12 dominance, has the **highest SV ≈ 250–260** because shorter chains need more KOH molecules per gram of oil).

### 2.2.6 Calculated Typical Values (Important!)

| Oil | IV (g I₂/100 g) | SV (mg KOH/g) | Cloud Point (°C) | Slip Melting Point (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Coconut | 7 – 12 | 248 – 265 | — | 23 – 26 |
| Palm | 50 – 55 | 195 – 205 | — | 33 – 39 |
| Peanut | 85 – 100 | 188 – 196 | 0 – 3 | –2 (liquid) |
| Sunflower | 110 – 143 | 188 – 194 | –6 | –18 (liquid) |
| Sesame | 103 – 116 | 188 – 195 | –4 | –6 (liquid) |
| Mustard | 96 – 112 | 170 – 180 | –8 | –10 (liquid) |
| Rice bran | 90 – 108 | 180 – 195 | –5 | –12 (liquid) |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These seven oils together account for roughly **> 70 % of the global vegetable-oil trade**. They are the raw material for:

1. **Food industry** — cooking, salad dressings, vanaspati, margarines.
2. **Oleochemical industry** — fatty acids, fatty alcohols, soaps, surfactants.
3. **Biofuel sector** — FAMEs (Fatty Acid Methyl Esters) for biodiesel.
4. **Cosmetics & pharma** — refined, deodorized, bleached grades for emollients.
5. **Industrial lubricants & paints** — after epoxidation, sulphurization, or polymerisation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1: Calculating the Mean Molecular Weight of Coconut Oil

**Given:** A coconut oil sample has a saponification value of **SV = 256 mg KOH/g**.

**Step 1 — Recall the definition of Saponification Value.**

One gram of oil reacts with a fixed amount of KOH. The number of moles of KOH consumed per gram equals the number of moles of triglycerides, because each triglyceride has **exactly three ester linkages**, each consuming **one mole of KOH**.

So,
$$
\text{moles of KOH per gram of oil} = \frac{SV}{56.1 \times 1000}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by 3 to convert from moles of KOH to moles of triglyceride.**

$$
\text{moles of triglyceride per gram} = \frac{SV}{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}
$$

**Step 3 — Take reciprocal to obtain grams of oil per mole of triglyceride (i.e., molecular weight).**

$$
M_w = \frac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{SV}
$$

**Step 4 — Plug in SV = 256.**

$$
M_w = \frac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{256}
$$

$$
M_w = \frac{168300}{256}
$$

$$
M_w = 657.4 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.**

A coconut triglyceride molecule weighs roughly **657 g/mol**, dominated by lauric (C12:0) residues. By contrast, a triolein (C18:1 dominant) molecule weighs ≈ **885 g/mol**, which explains why coconut oil has the highest SV in the table.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2: Determining the Mass Fraction of Unsaturation

A peanut oil sample weighing **W = 0.250 g** is reacted with **Wijs solution**. The blank titration consumes **V_b = 45.20 mL** of **0.1 N Na₂S₂O₃**, while the sample titration consumes **V_s = 21.50 mL**.

**Step 1 — Apply the IV formula.**

$$
IV = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 12.69}{W}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
IV = \frac{(45.20 - 21.50) \times 0.1 \times 12.69}{0.250}
$$

$$
IV = \frac{23.70 \times 0.1 \times 12.69}{0.250}
$$

$$
IV = \frac{30.0753}{0.250}
$$

$$
IV = 120.30 \text{ g I}_2 / 100 \text{ g oil}
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-check against reference data.**

The literature IV for peanut oil is **85–100**. Our computed value of **120.3 is unusually high**, indicating either:
- The sample is actually a high-linoleic sunflower instead of peanut, **or**
- The oil has undergone oxidative polymerisation (loss of IV occurs but contamination with linseed oil would inflate IV).

This reinforces why IV is a **quality-control** parameter used by every oil refinery.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Oil Comparison

| Property | Coconut | Palm | Peanut | Rice bran | Sesame | Mustard | Sunflower |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Botanical Family | Arecaceae | Arecaceae | Fabaceae | Poaceae | Pedaliaceae | Brassicaceae | Asteraceae |
| Plant Part | Endosperm | Mesocarp | Cotyledon | Bran | Seed | Seed | Seed (achene) |
| Oil in raw material (%) | 35–40 (fresh), 62–72 (copra) | 20–25 (FFB) | 45–55 | 15–23 | 45–55 | 30–35 | 40–50 |
| Predominant FA | C12:0 Lauric | C16:0 Palmitic | C18:1 Oleic | C18:1 Oleic | C18:1 Oleic | C22:1 Erucic | C18:2 Linoleic |
| Major FA (%) | 45–52 | 44 | 50 | 38 | 42 | 40–50 | 60–70 |
| IV (g I₂/100 g) | 7–12 | 50–55 | 85–100 | 90–108 | 103–116 | 96–112 | 110–143 |
| SV (mg KOH/g) | 248–265 | 195–205 | 188–196 | 180–195 | 188–195 | 170–180 | 188–194 |
| Special Minor | Caprylic acid | β-Carotene | Behenic acid | γ-Oryzanol | Sesamol | Allyl isothiocyanate | α-Tocopherol |
| Typical Extraction | Expeller + Solvent | Wet-process centrifuge | Expeller + Solvent | Solvent (hexane) | Cold-press / Solvent | Cold-press / Solvent | Expeller + Solvent |
| Global Production Rank | 5th | 1st | 4th | 3rd | 8th | 6th | 2nd |

---

## 3.4 Critical Engineering Insight — Lipase Stability in Rice Bran

Rice bran differs from the other six sources because the **enzyme lipase is co-located in the bran layer with the lipid**. The moment bran is separated from the intact kernel, lipase begins to hydrolyze TAGs into FFA:

$$
\text{TAG} + H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{lipase}} \text{DAG} \rightarrow \text{MAG} \rightarrow \text{Glycerol} + 3 \text{FFA}
$$

If untreated, FFA rises to **> 30 % within days**. Industry counters this via **bran stabilization** by one of:

1. **Drying** — hot-air drying at **100 °C** for 10 minutes.
2. **Extrusion cooking** — barrel at **130–140 °C**, screw speed 250 rpm.
3. **Chemical inactivation** — perchloroethylene vapour treatment.
4. **pH adjustment** — acid steeping (pH 4) or alkaline steeping (pH 9).

This is why rice bran oil refining **must include a dewaxing stage** and a strict FFA-control step — making it the most processing-intensive oil of the seven.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Botanical Origin of the Seven Oils


# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Why is rice bran oil considered unique among the seven vegetable oils studied in Module 1?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Rice bran oil is unique for three principal reasons:
1. **Source nature:** It is the only **by-product** (from rice milling) oil in the group; the other six are primary agricultural products.
2. **Enzymatic instability:** Fresh rice bran contains an active **lipase enzyme** which hydrolyzes triglycerides, raising FFA rapidly. This necessitates immediate **bran stabilization** (heat, pH, or chemical).
3. **Special minor constituents:** It contains **γ-oryzanol** (a ferulic-acid ester with cholesterol-lowering claims) and **wax esters** (long-chain fatty alcohols esterified with fatty acids) that demand a separate **dewaxing** refining step.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between coconut oil and palm oil based on source tissue, dominant fatty acid, and industrial application."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Coconut Oil | Palm Oil |
|---|---|---|
| Source tissue | **Endosperm** (copra) of fruit | **Mesocarp** (fleshy pulp) of fruit |
| Dominant fatty acid | **Lauric (C12:0) ≈ 45–52 %** | **Palmitic (C16:0) ≈ 44 %** |
| Industrial application | Confectionery fats, soaps, MCT, hair oil | Margarine, vanaspati, frying oil, olein/stearin fractionation |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Internal Choice: **Answer either Question A OR Question B.**

---

### Question A — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Describe in detail the **botanical origin, oil-bearing tissue, oil yield, and dominant fatty acid composition** of the following five vegetable oils:
**(i) Coconut, (ii) Palm, (iii) Peanut, (iv) Sesame, (v) Sunflower.**
**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Coconut (1.4 marks):**
Botanical origin: *Cocos nucifera*, family Arecaceae. Oil-bearing tissue: dried **endosperm (copra)**. Oil yield: **62–72 %** on dry basis. Dominant fatty acid: **Lauric acid (C12:0) ≈ 45–52 %**, followed by myristic (C14:0) and caprylic (C8:0). Extraction: dry expeller + solvent.

**Palm (1.4 marks):**
Botanical origin: *Elaeis guineensis*, family Arecaceae. Oil-bearing tissue: **mesocarp** of the fresh fruit bunch (FFB). Oil yield: **20–25 %** of FFB. Dominant fatty acid: **Palmitic (C16:0) ≈ 44 %** and oleic (C18:1) ≈ 39 %. Extraction: **wet process** — sterilization, stripping, digestion, pressing, clarification.

**Peanut (1.4 marks):**
Botanical origin: *Arachis hypogaea*, family Fabaceae. Oil-bearing tissue: **cotyledons** (dehulled kernels). Oil yield: **45–55 %**. Dominant fatty acid: **Oleic (C18:1) ≈ 50 %** and linoleic (C18:2) ≈ 30 %. Contains minor **behenic (C22:0)** and **lignoceric (C24:0)** acids.

**Sesame (1.4 marks):**
Botanical origin: *Sesamum indicum*, family Pedaliaceae. Oil-bearing tissue: **hulled seed**. Oil yield: **45–55 %**. Dominant fatty acid: **Oleic (C18:1) ≈ 42 %** and **linoleic (C18:2) ≈ 40 %**. Signature minor constituent: **sesamol, sesamin, sesamolin** (lignans giving antioxidant property and a positive **Baudouin test**).

**Sunflower (1.4 marks):**
Botanical origin: *Helianthus annuus*, family Asteraceae. Oil-bearing tissue: **kernel of the achene**. Oil yield: **40–50 %**. Traditional cultivar dominant fatty acid: **Linoleic (C18:2) ≈ 60–70 %**. High-oleic hybrid: **Oleic (C18:1) ≈ 80 %**. Rich in **α-tocopherol (Vitamin E)**.

**(b)** With the help of equations, explain the determination of the **Acid Value** of an oil sample. **A sample of oil weighing 4.0 g was titrated against 0.1 N KOH, consuming 8.5 mL. Calculate the AV.** **What does this AV infer about the quality of the oil?**
**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (2 marks):**
The acid value is the number of milligrams of KOH required to neutralize the free fatty acids present in one gram of oil. It is a direct measure of **lipid hydrolysis** during storage or processing. Procedure: oil is dissolved in a neutral solvent (ethanol:diethyl ether, 1:1), titrated with standardized **0.1 N KOH** using **phenolphthalein** indicator until a faint pink endpoint persists for 15 seconds.

**Equation (1 mark):**

$$
AV = \frac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W}
$$

**Substitution and calculation (2 marks):**

$$
AV = \frac{8.5 \times 0.1 \times 56.1}{4.0}
$$

$$
AV = \frac{47.685}{4.0}
$$

$$
AV = 11.92 \text{ mg KOH/g}
$$

**Quality Inference (2 marks):**
An acid value of **11.92 mg KOH/g** corresponds to approximately **6 % FFA as oleic acid**. This is **unacceptably high** for refined edible oil (where the Codex Alimentarius specifies **≤ 0.5 % FFA**, i.e., AV ≤ 1.0). The oil is either **crude**, **improperly stored**, or has been **enzymatically hydrolyzed** (typical of untreated rice bran or aged coconut copra). Industrial consequence: refining must include a strong **alkali neutralization** step with proportionally higher caustic soda and higher refining loss.

---

### Question B — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Discuss the **uniqueness of rice bran oil and mustard oil** in terms of their special minor constituents, refining considerations, and industrial applications.
**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Rice bran oil (3.5 marks):**
Special minor constituents:
- **γ-Oryzanol** — a mixture of ferulic acid esters of cycloartenol and 24-methylene cycloartanol; clinically associated with lowering serum cholesterol.
- **Waxes** — long-chain esters (C32 to C60) crystallize during winterization/dewaxing.
- **Phytic acid and tocopherols** — natural antioxidants.

Refining considerations: Must include **stabilization** (to inactivate lipase), **dewaxing** (chilling at 8–10 °C with subsequent filtration), and standard alkali refining.

Industrial applications: health-food segment, cooking oil blends, cosmetic emollient carrier, snack-frying medium due to high oxidative stability from oryzanol.

**Mustard oil (3.5 marks):**
Special minor constituents:
- **Allyl isothiocyanate** — released from **sinigrin** (a glucosinolate) by the enzyme **myrosinase** upon seed crushing; responsible for pungency and tear-inducing vapours.
- **Sinapinic acid** — phenolic antioxidant.
- Traditionally high in **erucic acid (C22:1) ≈ 40–50 %**; modern *canola-type* cultivars have < 2 % erucic acid.

Refining considerations: Must control the moisture and temperature during crushing to avoid excessive allyl isothiocyanate volatilization; deodorization step needs to balance aroma retention (for traditional markets) against complete deodouring (for export markets).

Industrial applications: traditional cooking oil in Northern India & Bangladesh, pickling medium (acts as antimicrobial due to allyl isothiocyanate), lubricant, biodiesel feedstock in some regions.

**(b)** Define **Saponification Value**. **Calculate the saponification equivalent (mean molecular weight) of mustard oil whose saponification value is 175 mg KOH/g.** **Explain why mustard oil shows the lowest SV among the seven Module-1 oils.**
**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
The saponification value is the number of milligrams of KOH required to saponify (hydrolyze) one gram of oil under standardized conditions. It is inversely related to the average molecular weight of the constituent fatty acids.

**Equation (1 mark):**

$$
M_w = \frac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{SV}
$$

**Substitution (1.5 marks):**

$$
M_w = \frac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{175}
$$

$$
M_w = \frac{168300}{175}
$$

$$
M_w = 961.7 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**Explanation (3 marks):**
Mustard oil shows the lowest SV among the seven Module-1 oils because it contains a substantial proportion of **very long-chain fatty acids**, notably **erucic acid (C22:1)** which has a molecular weight of **338.6 g/mol**, **gondoic (C20:1)**, and **behenic (C22:0)**. Since SV is inversely proportional to the mean molecular weight of the fatty acid residues in a triglyceride, a higher mean molecular weight yields a lower SV. Therefore:

- Coconut oil (C12:0 dominant, MW ≈ 200) → **highest SV ≈ 256**.
- Mustard oil (C22:1 dominant, MW ≈ 335) → **lowest SV ≈ 175**.

This is the principle behind using SV to **detect adulteration** — for example, coconut oil adulterated with mustard oil will exhibit an anomalously low SV.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (from past KTU valuation panels):**
> 1. **Conflating palm oil and palm kernel oil.** Module-1 covers **palm oil (mesocarp)** only — students writing about lauric-acid dominance will lose marks because that is **palm kernel oil**, a different commodity.
> 2. **Writing "vitamin E" without specifying tocopherol isomer.** Always specify **α-tocopherol for sunflower, γ-oryzanol for rice bran, sesamol for sesame, β-carotene for palm**. Generic answers lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the FFA-as-oleic-acid conversion.** When AV is asked, the follow-up "what is FFA%" is almost always expected; failure to convert from 56.1 to 28.2 equivalent cost students **1 full mark** in recent papers.
> 4. **Treating mustard oil's "erucic acid" as a negative only.** Provide a **balanced** answer: traditional/cultural acceptance, modern breeding reduction (canola), and industrial lubricant utility.
> 5. **Forgetting blank-titration subtraction in IV problems.** Always show the step **(V_b – V_s)**, otherwise the answer will be grossly inflated.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Coconut oil** = lauric-acid (C12:0) king; semi-solid; high SV (~256); used for confectionery and soaps.
- **Palm oil** = mesocarp-derived; palmitic + oleic dominant; rich in β-carotene; fractionable into olein & stearin.
- **Peanut oil** = oleic-rich, contains long-chain C20–C24 acids (behenic, lignoceric); prized for salad oil & cold-press applications.
- **Rice bran oil** = unique by-product oil; contains γ-oryzanol and wax; lipase instability demands immediate stabilization.
- **Sesame oil** = antioxidant lignans (sesamin, sesamol); positive Baudouin test; high oxidative stability.
- **Mustard oil** = erucic acid dominant; pungent allyl isothiocyanate from glucosinolate breakdown; lowest SV.
- **Sunflower oil** = linoleic (traditional) or oleic (modern hybrid) dominant; rich in α-tocopherol.
- **IV** = g I₂ / 100 g oil — measures unsaturation; high for sunflower, low for coconut.
- **SV** = mg KOH / g oil — inversely proportional to mean FA chain length; high for coconut, low for mustard.
- **AV** = mg KOH / g oil — measures hydrolytic deterioration; > 4 is unacceptable for refined food-grade oil.
- **FFA % (as oleic) = AV × 0.502** (or use the 28.2 / 56.1 ratio directly).
- **Special refining:** Rice bran → stabilization + dewaxing; Palm → fractionation; Coconut → no dewaxing (already low wax); Sunflower → winterization to remove wax traces.
- **Global ranking (2023 data):** Palm > Sunflower > Rice bran > Peanut > Coconut > Mustard > Sesame.
- **KTU-exam tip:** Always pair the oil with **its dominant fatty acid** AND **its signature minor constituent** for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production Status and Oil Content
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT748 - FAT AND OIL PROCESSING TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Production Status and Oil Content ## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Definition of Production Status in Oil Technology **Production Status** in the context of Fat and Oil Processing Technology refers to the comprehensive quantitative assessment of the **global and national (Indian)** cultivation, extraction, and supply of major oil-bearing raw materials, along with the corresponding refined oil output. It encompasses the agronomic yield (kg/hectare), total harvested tonnage, and the proportional contribution of each oilseed to the world's edible and non-edible oil pool. > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** > *"Production status is the statistical and analytical mapping of cultivated oilseed crops, their geographic distribution, tonnage yield per hectare, and total recoverable oil, evaluated across both global and regional (national) scales, expressed in million metric tonnes (MMT)."* ### 1.2 Definition of Oil Content **Oil Content** (also termed the *lipid fraction* or *fatty yield*) of an oilseed is the mass of crude oil (lipids soluble in non-polar organic solvents like petroleum ether, n-hexane, or chloroform) recoverable per unit mass of dry, clean, mature seed, expressed conventionally as a **percentage on a dry weight basis (db)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference:** Oil content is determined analytically by the **Soxhlet extraction method** (AOCS Official Method Am 2-93), using n-hexane (boiling point 60–80 °C) as the solvent. Results are reported as **wt% on a moisture-free (dry) basis**. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge in a Glass of Water" Model Imagine an oilseed as a **dried kitchen sponge** (the cellular matrix) whose microscopic internal cavities are filled with **vegetable oil droplets** (the triglycerides trapped within *oleosomes* / *lipid bodies*). Just as the sponge's water-holding capacity defines how much it can absorb, the **oil content** defines how much triglyceride each gram of seed stores. The **production status**, then, is like the *inventory* of all such sponges in a warehouse — the more sponges in stock, the higher the total oil tonnage available for pressing (mechanical squeezing) or solvent washing (chemical dissolution). > [!TIP] > Think of it this way: > - **High-oil seeds** (sesame ~50%, copra ~65%) → *saturated sponges* — only a light press releases oil (expeller pressing is enough). > - **Low-oil seeds** (soybean ~20%, rice bran ~18%) → *sparse sponges* — you need a chemical "solvent bath" to dissolve out the residual oil (mechanical pressing alone leaves too much oil trapped). ### 1.4 Standard Units & Metrics Used | Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Common Reporting Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Oil Content | $O_c$ | g oil / 100 g dry seed | **wt% (db)** | | Seed Yield | $Y_s$ | kg / hectare | Quintal / hectare | | Total Production | $P$ | tonnes | Million Metric Tonnes (MMT) | | Extraction Efficiency | $\eta$ | dimensionless | **%** | | Oil Recovery | $R$ | kg oil / kg seed | wt% (as-is basis) | ## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Global Production Status of Major Vegetable Oils The global edible oil market is dominated by **four major oils** which together constitute approximately **~85% of world production**: **Rank-wise Global Production (Approximate, in MMT/year — Reference Period):** 1. **Palm Oil** — ~76–80 MMT — *Southeast Asia (Indonesia, Malaysia)* 2. **Soybean Oil** — ~60–62 MMT — *USA, Brazil, Argentina* 3. **Rapeseed (Canola) Oil** — ~28–30 MMT — *EU, Canada, China* 4. **Sunflower Oil** — ~20–22 MMT — *Ukraine, Russia, Argentina* **Secondary Oils (smaller tonnage):** 5. Groundnut (Peanut) oil — ~5–6 MMT — *China, India, Nigeria* 6. Cottonseed oil — ~4–5 MMT — *India, China, USA* 7. Coconut oil — ~3.5–4 MMT — *Philippines, Indonesia, India* 8. Olive oil — ~3–3.5 MMT — *Spain, Italy, Greece* 9. Sesame oil — ~1.5 MMT — *Sudan, India, Myanmar* 10. Linseed oil — ~0.7 MMT — *Canada, China, Russia* ### 2.2 Indian Production Status — A Net Importer Scenario India is paradoxically one of the **world's largest producers of oilseeds** but also the **largest importer of edible oils** because domestic demand far exceeds supply. India produces roughly **~8–10 MMT** of edible oils annually, yet imports an additional **~13–15 MMT** to meet total demand (~22–25 MMT). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Indian Oilseeds (with approximate annual production in MMT):** > - Groundnut: 6–7 MMT > - Soybean: 11–13 MMT > - Rapeseed/Mustard: 8–9 MMT > - Sesame: 0.7–0.8 MMT > - Sunflower: 0.2–0.3 MMT > - Castor: 1.5–2.0 MMT > - Linseed: 0.15–0.2 MMT > - Safflower: 0.05–0.07 MMT **India's Edible Oil Import Basket (approximate share):** - Palm oil (from Indonesia/Malaysia): **~55–60%** of total imports - Soybean oil (from Argentina/Brazil): **~20–25%** - Sunflower oil (from Ukraine/Russia): **~15–18%** ### 2.3 Oil Content of Major Oilseeds — The Core Data Table > [!IMPORTANT] > The following ranges are **empirically established values** from the *Oil Technological Mission* of ICAR and global seed trade literature. KTU examiners routinely ask students to **match oilseeds with their oil content range**. | Oilseed | Botanical Name | Oil Content (wt%, db) | Type of Oil | |---|---|---|---| | **Copra (dried coconut kernel)** | *Cocos nucifera* | **60–70%** | Lauric (saturated) | | Sesame (Til) | *Sesamum indicum* | **45–55%** | Unsaturated (oleic/linoleic) | | Groundnut (Peanut) | *Arachis hypogaea* | **45–50%** | Unsaturated (oleic) | | Castor seed | *Ricinus communis* | **45–55%** | Ricinoleic (industrial) | | Rapeseed / Mustard | *Brassica* spp. | **35–42%** | Erucic / Oleic | | Sunflower seed | *Helianthus annuus* | **35–45%** | Linoleic (PUFA-rich) | | Linseed (Flax) | *Linum usitatissimum* | **35–44%** | α-Linolenic (PUFA) | | Safflower | *Carthamus tinctorius* | **30–35%** | Linoleic (PUFA) | | Palm kernel | *Elaeis guineensis* | **45–50%** | Lauric (saturated) | | Soybean | *Glycine max* | **18–22%** | Linoleic / Linolenic | | Cottonseed | *Gossypium* spp. | **18–22%** | Linoleic | | Rice bran | *Oryza sativa* (byproduct) | **15–20%** | Oleic / Linoleic | | Palm fruit (mesocarp) | *Elaeis guineensis* | **20–25%** | Palmitic / Oleic | | Maize (Corn) germ | *Zea mays* | **3–7%** (germ: ~50%) | Linoleic | | Olive | *Olea europaea* | **15–25%** | Oleic (MUFA-rich) | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Oil Content & Extraction Calculations > [!TIP] > **Mandatory equations for KTU board examinations — memorize these.** | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Oil Content (dry basis)** | $O_c = \dfrac{m_o}{m_d} \times 100$ | $m_o$ = mass of oil extracted (g); $m_d$ = dry seed mass (g) | Soxhlet lab report | | 2 | **Moisture Content** | $M = \dfrac{m_i - m_f}{m_i} \times 100$ | $m_i$ = initial mass; $m_f$ = oven-dried mass | Pre-extraction drying | | 3 | **Extraction Efficiency (Yield)** | $\eta = \dfrac{O_{\text{actual}}}{O_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | $O_{\text{actual}}$ = oil recovered; $O_{\text{theoretical}}$ = Soxhlet oil content | Process performance | | 4 | **Oil Recovery (as-is basis)** | $R_{\text{as-is}} = O_c \times \dfrac{(100 - M)}{100}$ | $M$ = moisture % | Plant-scale mass balance | | 5 | **Solvent-to-Meal Ratio** | $S/M = \dfrac{V_s}{m_m}$ | $V_s$ = solvent volume (L); $m_m$ = meal mass (kg) | Extraction design | | 6 | **Meal Oil Residue (LOSS)** | $L = \dfrac{m_{\text{oil,meal}}}{m_{\text{meal}}} \times 100$ | residual oil in defatted cake | Quality control | | 7 | **Cake Yield** | $Y_c = m_{\text{cake}} / m_{\text{seed,dry}}$ | dimensionless | Material balance | | 8 | **Crude Oil Density Check** | $\rho_o = m_o / V_o$ | g/mL or kg/L | Volume-to-mass conversion | | 9 | **Free Fatty Acid (FFA %)** | $\text{FFA} = \dfrac{V_{\text{KOH}} \times N_{\text{KOH}} \times 28.2}{m_o}$ | $V$ = titre (mL); $N$ = normality; 28.2 = equiv. wt. of oleic acid | Oil quality grading | | 10 | **Iodine Value (Wijs)** | $IV = \dfrac{(B - S) \times N \times 12.69}{m_o}$ | $B, S$ = blank and sample titre (mL); 12.69 = equiv. wt. of I₂ | Degree of unsaturation | > [!NOTE] > **Avoid notation errors:** When writing **absolute value** or **divided-by** in exam answer sheets, always use $\vert x \vert$ or $x \div y$ — never ambiguous inline bars. ### 2.5 Why Oil Content Matters in Engineering Practice - **Plant Design Capacity:** Determines the **rated throughput** of expellers, solvent extractors, and refineries. A 100 TPD soybean plant handles only ~70 TPD groundnut at equivalent oil output. - **Process Selection:** High-oil seeds (>30%) → mechanical pressing feasible; low-oil seeds (<25%) → solvent extraction mandatory. - **Economics:** A 1% increase in oil recovery on a 1000 TPD plant translates to ~Rs. 1.5–2 crore/year in additional revenue. - **Quality Grading:** Oilseeds are commercially traded partly on **oil content** (e.g., 40% groundnut oil content fetches premium). ## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation: Oil Content from Soxhlet Extraction Data **The Problem Context:** A 5.00 g sample of oven-dried groundnut kernels is subjected to Soxhlet extraction with petroleum ether (60–80 °C) for 16 hours. The extracted oil, after solvent evaporation, weighs **2.32 g**. Calculate the oil content on a dry basis. --- **Step 1 — Identify Given Data** $$ \begin{aligned} m_{\text{seed,dry}} &= 5.00 \ \text{g} \\ m_{\text{oil,extracted}} &= 2.32 \ \text{g} \\ \text{Basis} &= \text{dry weight basis (db)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the Fundamental Oil Content Formula** $$ \begin{aligned} O_c \,(\text{db}) &= \frac{m_{\text{oil,extracted}}}{m_{\text{seed,dry}}} \times 100 \\ O_c \,(\text{db}) &= \frac{2.32}{5.00} \times 100 \\ O_c \,(\text{db}) &= 0.464 \times 100 \\ O_c \,(\text{db}) &= 46.4 \ \% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Physical Interpretation** A value of **46.4%** falls within the expected groundnut oil range (45–50%), confirming data consistency. The slight elevation above the lower limit (45%) suggests a mature, well-developed seed lot. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Converting Dry-Basis Oil Content to As-Is (Wet) Basis **Context:** A soybean lot has an oil content of **20.0% (db)** and a moisture content of **10.0% (wb)**. A buyer pays on an "as-received" basis. Compute the **as-is oil content** and the **tonnage of recoverable oil** in a **1000-kg** consignment. --- **Step 1 — Define the As-Is Basis Formula** The dry-basis oil content must be scaled by the dry matter fraction of the seed: $$ R_{\text{as-is}} = O_c \,(\text{db}) \times \frac{(100 - M)}{100} $$ where $M$ is the moisture content on a wet basis (wb). **Step 2 — Substitute Values** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{\text{as-is}} &= 20.0 \times \frac{(100 - 10.0)}{100} \\ R_{\text{as-is}} &= 20.0 \times \frac{90.0}{100} \\ R_{\text{as-is}} &= 20.0 \times 0.90 \\ R_{\text{as-is}} &= 18.0 \ \% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Total Recoverable Oil in 1000 kg Lot** $$ \begin{aligned} m_{\text{oil,total}} &= R_{\text{as-is}} \times m_{\text{consignment}} \\ m_{\text{oil,total}} &= 0.180 \times 1000 \\ m_{\text{oil,total}} &= 180 \ \text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Always report oil content on **db** in lab reports but use **as-is (wb)** values for trade, plant mass balance, and storage calculations. Mixing the two bases is the most common KTU exam mistake. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Extraction Efficiency of a Solvent Plant **Context:** A continuous solvent extraction plant processes **500 tonnes/day** of decorticated sunflower seed. Lab Soxhlet analysis on identical seed gives an oil content of **40.0% (db)**. The plant's output stream yields **195 tonnes/day of crude oil**. Moisture in the feed is **8.0%**. Calculate: - (a) The **theoretical oil** entering the plant daily - (b) The **extraction efficiency** $\eta$ --- **Step 1 — Convert Feed Mass to Dry Basis** $$ \begin{aligned} m_{\text{feed,dry}} &= m_{\text{feed,wet}} \times \frac{(100 - M)}{100} \\ m_{\text{feed,dry}} &= 500 \times \frac{92.0}{100} \\ m_{\text{feed,dry}} &= 460 \ \text{tonnes/day} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Theoretical (Maximum) Oil in Feed** $$ \begin{aligned} O_{\text{theoretical}} &= m_{\text{feed,dry}} \times \frac{O_c}{100} \\ O_{\text{theoretical}} &= 460 \times \frac{40.0}{100} \\ O_{\text{theoretical}} &= 184 \ \text{tonnes/day} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute Extraction Efficiency** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta &= \frac{O_{\text{actual}}}{O_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100 \\ \eta &= \frac{195}{184} \times 100 \\ \eta &= 1.0598 \times 100 \\ \eta &\approx 105.98 \ \% \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **An efficiency > 100% is a RED FLAG!** It indicates either (i) measurement error, (ii) incorrect moisture sampling, or (iii) carryover of impurities/solvent counted as oil. KTU examiners *love* this trick — they will deduct marks if you do not flag the inconsistency. **Corrected Interpretation:** In a real plant, the actual oil stream often includes solvent residue, gums, and moisture. The "pure oil" output would be ~98–99% of $O_{\text{theoretical}}$ once these are stripped. The 195 tonnes likely represents **crude miscella** (oil + solvent), not pure oil. --- ### 3.4 Derivation: Free Fatty Acid (FFA) Calculation **Context:** 2.0 g of crude rice bran oil is titrated against 0.1 N KOH. The blank titration is 0.20 mL; the sample titre is **4.85 mL**. Compute the **FFA as % oleic acid**. --- **Step 1 — Apply the Standard FFA Formula** $$ \text{FFA (\% oleic acid)} = \frac{(S - B) \times N \times 56.1}{m_o \times 10} $$ (where 56.1 = equivalent weight of KOH; the 10 divisor converts to percentage). Some references use 28.2 with a $V \times N$ formulation; the 56.1 version is more common in KTU 2024 chemistry-of-fats modules. **Step 2 — Substitute Values** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FFA} &= \frac{(4.85 - 0.20) \times 0.1 \times 56.1}{2.0 \times 10} \\ \text{FFA} &= \frac{4.65 \times 0.1 \times 56.1}{20.0} \\ \text{FFA} &= \frac{4.65 \times 5.61}{20.0} \\ \text{FFA} &= \frac{26.0865}{20.0} \\ \text{FFA} &= 1.304 \ \% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Quality Grading** An FFA of ~1.3% classifies the oil as **Grade 2** (refining required). For Grade 1 (premium crude), FFA should be <1.0%. This is typical for *fresh* rice bran; aged bran can exceed 10–30% FFA due to endogenous lipase activity. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Numerical Problem Types in KTU | Problem Type | Formula Used | Typical Data Given | Common Pitfall | |---|---|---|---| | Soxhlet oil content | $O_c = m_o / m_d \times 100$ | Initial sample mass, extracted oil mass | Forgetting to use *dry* sample mass | | As-is oil recovery | $R = O_c \times (100-M)/100$ | db oil content, moisture % | Confusing wb vs db for moisture | | Plant efficiency | $\eta = O_{\text{act}} / O_{\text{theor}} \times 100$ | Feed rate, oil output, moisture | Not converting feed to db first | | Cake oil loss | $L = m_{\text{oil,meal}} / m_{\text{meal}} \times 100$ | Meal mass, residual oil | Not subtracting moisture of meal | | Solvent loss | $S_L = (V_{\text{in}} - V_{\text{out}}) / V_{\text{in}} \times 100$ | Input/output solvent volumes | Misinterpreting miscella as pure oil | | FFA titration | $\text{FFA} = (S-B) \times N \times 56.1 / (m \times 10)$ | Titrant volume, normality, sample mass | Forgetting blank correction | | Iodine Value | $IV = (B-S) \times N \times 12.69 / m$ | Blank and sample titre | Subtract in wrong order | ## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Global Vegetable Oil Production Hierarchy (Block-Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Global Vegetable Oil Pool
~200 MMT per year] --> B[Major Oils ~85 percent] A --> C[Minor Oils ~15 percent] B --> B1[Palm Oil ~76 MMT] B --> B2[Soybean Oil ~61 MMT] B --> B3[Rapeseed Oil ~29 MMT] B --> B4[Sunflower Oil ~21 MMT] C --> C1[Groundnut Oil ~5.5 MMT] C --> C2[Cottonseed Oil ~5 MMT] C --> C3[Coconut Oil ~3.5 MMT] C --> C4[Olive Oil ~3.2 MMT] C --> C5[Sesame Oil ~1.5 MMT] B1 --> D1[Indonesia and Malaysia] B2 --> D2[USA and Brazil and Argentina] B3 --> D3[EU and Canada and China] B4 --> D4[Ukraine and Russia] ``` ### 4.2 Oil Content Classification by Seed Type ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Oilseeds] --> B[High Oil Content greater than 35 percent] A --> C[Medium Oil Content 25 to 35 percent] A --> D[Low Oil Content less than 25 percent] B --> B1[Copra 60 to 70 percent] B --> B2[Sesame 45 to 55 percent] B --> B3[Groundnut 45 to 50 percent] B --> B4[Castor 45 to 55 percent] B --> B5[Mustard 35 to 42 percent] C --> C1[Sunflower 35 to 45 percent] C --> C2[Linseed 35 to 44 percent] C --> C3[Safflower 30 to 35 percent] D --> D1[Soybean 18 to 22 percent] D --> D2[Cottonseed 18 to 22 percent] D --> D3[Rice Bran 15 to 20 percent] D --> D4[Olive 15 to 25 percent] D --> D5[Maize Germ 3 to 7 percent] ``` ### 4.3 India's Oil Balance Flow — Production vs. Import vs. Demand ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Total Edible Oil Demand India ~24 MMT] --> B[Domestic Production ~10 MMT] A --> C[Import Gap ~14 MMT] B --> D[Groundnut ~2.5 MMT] B --> E[Soybean ~2.0 MMT] B --> F[Rapeseed Mustard ~3.0 MMT] B --> G[Others ~2.5 MMT] C --> H[Palm Oil Import ~8 MMT] C --> I[Soybean Oil Import ~3 MMT] C --> J[Sunflower Oil Import ~3 MMT] H --> K[Indonesia and Malaysia] I --> L[Argentina and Brazil] J --> M[Ukraine and Russia] ``` ### 4.4 Functional Process Selection Based on Oil Content ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Incoming Oilseed Lot] --> B{Measured Oil Content} B -->|Greater than 30 percent| C[Mechanical Expeller Pressing] B -->|Between 18 and 30 percent| D[Pre-pressing plus Solvent Extraction] B -->|Less than 18 percent| E[Direct Solvent Extraction Only] C --> F[Crude Oil plus Oil Cake 8 to 10 percent residual oil] D --> G[Partially Defatted Cake plus Miscella] E --> H[Full Miscella and Defatted Meal less than 1 percent residual] F --> I[Optional Solvent Polishing] G --> J[Solvent Recovery via DTDC Desolventiser] H --> J I --> K[Refining and Bleaching and Deodorization] J --> K K --> L[Finished Refined Oil] ``` ### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Soxhlet Extraction Lab Setup ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Oven Dried Seed Sample 5 g] --> B[Thimble in Soxhlet Apparatus] B --> C[Round Bottom Flask with Hexane 200 mL] C --> D[Heating Mantle at 70 degree C] D --> E[Solvent Vapor rises into Condenser] E --> F[Condensed Hexane Drips onto Thimble] F --> G[Siphon Cycle Every 10 minutes] G --> H{All Oil Dissolved in Flask} H -->|After 16 hours| I[Remove Thimble and Recover Oil] I --> J[Rotary Evaporator Removes Hexane] J --> K[Weigh Pure Oil] K --> L[Calculate Oil Content percent db] H -->|Incomplete| G ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model]** *Define oil content of an oilseed. List the oil content (wt%, db) of any six major oilseeds.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Oil content is the mass of crude lipids (triglycerides) extractable per 100 g of dry oilseed, determined analytically using a non-polar solvent (typically n-hexane or petroleum ether) in a Soxhlet apparatus, expressed on a moisture-free (dry weight) basis. **Six Examples (2 marks — 0.33 mark each, or 1 mark for three correct + 1 mark for three more):** | Oilseed | Oil Content (db) | |---|---| | Copra | 60–70% | | Sesame | 45–55% | | Groundnut | 45–50% | | Sunflower | 35–45% | | Soybean | 18–22% | | Cottonseed | 18–22% | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall (Section A):** Students often write oil content as a *single number* (e.g., "groundnut 48%") instead of a *range*. KTU expects the empirical **range**. Full 3 marks require both definition AND six correct ranges. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023 / Model]** *Why is India, despite being a major oilseed producer, the world's largest importer of edible oils? Briefly explain with approximate import figures.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** India produces approximately **8–10 MMT** of edible oils annually from oilseeds such as groundnut, soybean, rapeseed/mustard, sesame, and sunflower. However, the per-capita consumption of edible oils has risen to **~18–20 kg/person/year**, driving the total domestic demand to **~22–25 MMT**. The shortfall of **~13–15 MMT** is bridged through imports. The import basket consists predominantly of: - **Palm oil** (~8 MMT) — from Indonesia and Malaysia - **Soybean oil** (~3 MMT) — from Argentina and Brazil - **Sunflower oil** (~3 MMT) — from Ukraine and Russia India's cultivable land is constrained by competing food grain demands, monsoon dependency, fragmented landholdings, and lower productivity per hectare compared to global leaders (e.g., Malaysian palm yields ~20 t oil/ha vs. Indian groundnut ~1 t oil/ha). Hence, even with high production, supply < demand → imports rise. --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model]** **(a)** Discuss the global and Indian production status of vegetable oils. Tabulate at least **four major and four minor oils** with their approximate tonnage, leading producing countries, and typical oil content. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A solvent extraction plant processes **800 tonnes/day** of decorticated mustard seed (oil content 38% db, moisture 9% wb). The plant recovers **290 tonnes/day of crude oil** and **495 tonnes/day of defatted meal** (moisture 11% wb, residual oil 1.2% db). Calculate: - (i) The **theoretical oil** in the feed per day. - (ii) The **actual oil recovery efficiency**. - (iii) The **oil loss in the meal** as a percentage of feed. - (iv) The **mass balance closure** — comment on any discrepancy. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Question A **Part (a) — 7 marks** **Global Production (approx., MMT/year):** | Oil Type | Production (MMT) | Top Producers | Oil Content (db) | |---|---|---|---| | Palm oil | 76–80 | Indonesia, Malaysia | 20–25% (mesocarp) | | Soybean oil | 60–62 | USA, Brazil, Argentina | 18–22% | | Rapeseed oil | 28–30 | EU, Canada, China | 35–42% | | Sunflower oil | 20–22 | Ukraine, Russia, Argentina | 35–45% | | Groundnut oil | 5–6 | China, India, Nigeria | 45–50% | | Cottonseed oil | 4–5 | India, China, USA | 18–22% | | Coconut oil | 3.5–4 | Philippines, Indonesia | 60–70% (copra) | | Olive oil | 3–3.5 | Spain, Italy, Greece | 15–25% | **Indian Production (approx., MMT edible oil):** ~8–10 MMT. Major oilseeds: groundnut (6–7 MMT seed), soybean (11–13 MMT seed), mustard (8–9 MMT seed), sesame (0.7 MMT), sunflower (0.2 MMT). India imports an additional ~13–15 MMT to meet the demand of ~22–25 MMT. The largest imported oil is palm (~55–60% of import basket). > *(4 marks for tabulation with correct data, 2 marks for global narrative, 1 mark for Indian narrative.)* --- **Part (b) — 7 marks (Step-by-step Solution)** **Step 1 — Convert feed to dry basis** $$ m_{\text{feed,dry}} = 800 \times \frac{(100 - 9)}{100} = 800 \times 0.91 = 728 \ \text{tonnes/day} $$ **Step 2 — Theoretical oil in feed** $$ O_{\text{theoretical}} = 728 \times \frac{38}{100} = 276.64 \ \text{tonnes/day} $$ **Step 3 — Actual oil recovery efficiency** $$ \eta = \frac{290}{276.64} \times 100 \approx 104.8 \ \% $$ > This >100% value indicates that the "290 t/day" likely includes **residual solvent, moisture, or gums** still dissolved in the crude miscella. After refining, true pure oil yield is closer to the theoretical value. The student should flag this anomaly. **Step 4 — Oil loss in meal** First, find dry meal mass: $$ m_{\text{meal,dry}} = 495 \times \frac{(100 - 11)}{100} = 495 \times 0.89 = 440.55 \ \text{tonnes/day} $$ Residual oil in meal: $$ m_{\text{oil,meal}} = 440.55 \times \frac{1.2}{100} = 5.29 \ \text{tonnes/day} $$ As % of feed (dry): $$ L_{\%} = \frac{5.29}{728} \times 100 \approx 0.73 \ \% $$ **Step 5 — Mass Balance Closure** Inputs: 800 t/day (feed) Outputs: 290 (crude oil) + 495 (meal) = 785 t/day $$ \text{Discrepancy} = 800 - 785 = 15 \ \text{tonnes/day} $$ This discrepancy represents: - Solvent loss (~0.5–1% of feed) - Volatile matter / drying loss - Sampling/measurement uncertainty (typical ±2%) > *(2 marks each for sub-parts i & ii; 1.5 marks each for iii & iv. Award 0.5 mark bonus for correctly flagging the >100% efficiency anomaly.)* --- #### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE)** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model]** **(a)** What is oil content? Describe the **Soxhlet method** of oil determination with a neat labelled diagram of the apparatus. List its limitations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The Soxhlet analysis of 5.0 g of oven-dried soybean gives **1.05 g of extracted oil**. Calculate: - (i) The **oil content** (db). - (ii) If the seed has a moisture content of **10% (wb)**, calculate the **oil recovery per 100 kg of as-received seed**. - (iii) Calculate the **FFA (% oleic acid)** if 2.5 g of this crude oil required **5.10 mL of 0.1 N KOH** (blank = 0.20 mL). Comment on the **oil quality grade**. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Question B **Part (a) — 7 marks** **Definition (1 mark):** Oil content is the mass fraction of extractable lipids from a given mass of dry oilseed, expressed as a percentage on a dry weight basis. **Soxhlet Apparatus Description (4 marks):** - **Round-bottom flask** containing the solvent (n-hexane or petroleum ether, 200–250 mL) - **Soxhlet extractor body** with a siphon side-arm and a thimble holder - **Reflux condenser** (Allihn or Liebig type) at the top with circulating water - **Heating mantle** below the flask - **Thimble** (cellulose) holding the weighed, ground seed sample - The solvent vaporizes → condenses → drips onto the thimble → dissolves oil → siphons back to the flask after a fixed cycle (typically every 10–15 minutes) **Procedure (1 mark):** Weigh ~5 g dried ground sample in a thimble. Place in extractor. Fill flask with 200 mL solvent. Reflux for 8–16 hours. Distil off solvent. Weigh residual oil. **Limitations (1 mark):** - Time-consuming (8–16 hours per sample) - High solvent consumption - Cannot be used for heat-sensitive or volatile oils - Batch process; not suitable for on-line industrial monitoring - Extract contains non-triglyceride lipid impurities (waxes, phospholipids) unless further purified --- **Part (b) — 7 marks (Step-by-step Solution)** **Step 1 — Oil Content (db)** $$ O_c = \frac{1.05}{5.0} \times 100 = 21.0 \ \% $$ **Step 2 — Oil Recovery per 100 kg As-Received Seed** $$ R_{\text{as-is}} = O_c \times \frac{(100 - M)}{100} = 21.0 \times \frac{90}{100} = 18.9 \ \% $$ Per 100 kg of as-received seed: $$ m_{\text{oil}} = 18.9 \ \text{kg} $$ **Step 3 — FFA Calculation** Using the standard formula with 56.1 (equivalent weight of KOH): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FFA (\% oleic acid)} &= \frac{(S - B) \times N \times 56.1}{m_o \times 10} \\ \text{FFA} &= \frac{(5.10 - 0.20) \times 0.1 \times 56.1}{2.5 \times 10} \\ \text{FFA} &= \frac{4.90 \times 0.1 \times 56.1}{25.0} \\ \text{FFA} &= \frac{4.90 \times 5.61}{25.0} \\ \text{FFA} &= \frac{27.489}{25.0} \\ \text{FFA} &\approx 1.10 \ \% \end{aligned} $$ **Quality Grading Comment:** FFA ≈ 1.10% places this soybean oil in the **refining-grade (Grade 2)** category. Premium Grade 1 crude oil requires FFA < 1.0%. For *edible-grade* refined oil, the FFA after refining should be < 0.1%. Since the seed was likely fresh (Soxhlet oil content at the high end of soybean range, 21%), this slightly elevated FFA suggests minor lipolytic activity during storage; immediate refining is recommended. > *(2 marks each for sub-parts i, ii, iii; 1 mark for quality-grade interpretation.)* --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common 14-Mark Question Loss Points:** > 1. **Mixing wb and db** — Most frequent error. Always clarify the basis before substituting into a formula. > 2. **Forgetting to subtract blank titre** in FFA or iodine value calculations — loses 1–2 marks. > 3. **Reporting oil content as a single value, not a range** — KTU prefers empirical ranges. > 4. **Ignoring the >100% efficiency anomaly** — examiners test whether you *understand* that the figure may include solvent/moisture; missing this comment costs a full mark in part (b). > 5. **Skipping units** in mass balance problems — every numerical answer MUST carry the unit (tonnes/day, kg, %). > 6. **Missing the Soxhlet diagram** — KTU 2024 explicitly expects a labelled schematic in part (a); a textual description alone forfeits ~1.5 marks. --- ### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Production Status & Oil Content** - **Definition:** Oil content = mass of extractable lipids per 100 g of *dry* oilseed, determined by Soxhlet extraction with n-hexane, expressed as **wt% (db)**. - **World's top 4 oils** (by tonnage): **Palm > Soybean > Rapeseed > Sunflower** — together ~85% of global supply. - **India's paradox:** Largest producer of oilseeds *and* largest importer of edible oils (imports ~14 MMT vs. domestic production ~10 MMT). - **India's top import:** Palm oil (~55–60% of import basket), followed by soybean and sunflower. - **High-oil seeds (>35%):** Copra, sesame, groundnut, castor, mustard — *mechanical pressing feasible*. - **Low-oil seeds (<25%):** Soybean, cottonseed, rice bran, olive — *solvent extraction mandatory*. - **Copra holds the record** for highest commercial oil content (60–70% db). - **Two interchangeable bases:** - Dry basis (db) → for lab reports & trade specifications. - As-is / wet basis (wb) → for plant mass balance & storage. - Conversion: $R_{\text{as-is}} = O_c \,(\text{db}) \times \frac{(100 - M)}{100}$ - **Extraction efficiency** $\eta$ **should lie in 95–99%** for well-designed solvent plants; values >100% indicate measurement error. - **FFA benchmark for fresh crude oil:** < 1.0% (Grade 1) → refining grade; > 3% indicates significant lipase activity (esp. rice bran, coconut, olive). - **Soxhlet apparatus** components to memorize: Round-bottom flask, extractor body, thimble, condenser, heating mantle. - **AOCS Official Method Am 2-93** is the gold-standard reference for oil content determination. - **Iodine Value** = a measure of unsaturation; higher IV → more PUFA (e.g., linseed ~180, groundnut ~90, coconut ~10). - **India's key oilseeds (by seed tonnage):** Soybean > Mustard > Groundnut > Castor > Sesame. - **Key formula triad for numericals:** $O_c \,(\text{db})$, $R_{\text{as-is}}$, and $\eta$ — practice all three to ensure fluency in Part B.

topics.title: Physical Properties: Viscosity and Melting Point
Chemical Properties: Hydrolysis, Hydrogenation, Oxidation, and Polymerization
Importance of Fatty Acids and Their Classifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT748 - FAT AND OIL PROCESSING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Importance of Fatty Acids and Their Classifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **fatty acid** is a carboxylic acid characterized by a long aliphatic (straight or branched) hydrocarbon chain that is either saturated or unsaturated. The general chemical structure is **R−COOH**, where R represents a saturated or unsaturated alkyl group. The carboxyl group (−COOH) constitutes the polar, hydrophilic "head," while the hydrocarbon chain forms the non-polar, hydrophobic "tail." Naturally occurring fatty acids typically possess an even number of carbon atoms (most commonly ranging from **C4 to C28**), derived biosynthetically via the successive condensation of two-carbon (acetyl-CoA) units.

In the context of lipid science, fatty acids are the fundamental monomeric building blocks of triglycerides (triacylglycerols), phospholipids, waxes, and sterol esters — and they dictate virtually all the physical, chemical, nutritional, and functional properties of edible fats and oils.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Fatty acids are the cornerstone of fat and oil processing technology. Understanding their structure, classification, and reactivity underpins every downstream operation — from refining, hydrogenation, and interesterification to the manufacture of soaps, emulsifiers, and biodiesel.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spoon and Tail" Model

Imagine a tiny **magnetic spoon** floating in a pot of water and oil:

- The **head of the spoon (carboxyl group, −COOH)** is the metal end — it *loves water* (hydrophilic).
- The **handle of the spoon (hydrocarbon chain, R−)** is the plastic end — it *hates water* but *loves oil* (hydrophobic/lipophilic).

This is exactly what a fatty acid looks like: a **dual-natured (amphipathic) molecule**. The length of the handle and whether it has "kinks" (double bonds) determine whether the spoon fits neatly together to form solid butter, liquid oil, or foamy soap bubbles. If the handles are straight (saturated), they pack tightly like stacked spoons in a drawer → **solid fat**. If the handles have kinks (unsaturated), they cannot pack neatly → **liquid oil**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The double bonds in fatty acids are almost exclusively in the **cis-configuration** in nature, which introduces a 30° bend in the chain. This geometric bending is precisely why unsaturated oils are liquid at room temperature.

## 1.3 Why Are Fatty Acids Important?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Pillars of Fatty Acid Importance (must memorize for KTU exams):**

1. **Nutritional Significance** — Provide **9 kcal/g**, more than twice the energy of carbohydrates/proteins (4 kcal/g). Essential fatty acids (linoleic, α-linolenic) cannot be synthesized by the human body and must be supplied through diet.
2. **Structural Role** — Phospholipid fatty acids form the lipid bilayer of every cell membrane.
3. **Biological/Physiological Role** — Precursors of prostaglandins, leukotrienes, thromboxanes (eicosanoids); fat-soluble vitamin absorption.
4. **Industrial Raw Material** — Feedstock for soaps, detergents, surfactants, lubricants, plasticizers, cosmetics, paints, varnishes, and biodiesel.
5. **Food Functional Properties** — Influence texture, mouthfeel, flavor, crystallization behavior, oxidative stability, and shelf life of food products.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used to Quantify Fatty Acid Quality

| Metric | Symbol | Purpose |
|--------|--------|---------|
| **Iodine Value** | IV | Degree of unsaturation |
| **Saponification Value** | SV | Average molecular weight of fatty acids |
| **Acid Value** | AV | Free fatty acid content (rancidity indicator) |
| **Peroxide Value** | PV | Primary oxidation products |
| **TBA Value** | TBA | Secondary oxidation (aldehydes) |
| **Melting Point** | MP | Solid–liquid transition |
| **Cloud Point / Pour Point** | CP/PP | Cold-flow behavior |
| **Reichert-Meissl Value** | RMV | Volatile short-chain FA content |
| **Polenske Value** | PV | Water-insoluble volatile FA |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Fatty Acids

### 2.1.1 Based on Carbon Chain Length

| Category | Carbon Range | Common Examples | Typical Source |
|----------|--------------|-----------------|----------------|
| **Short-Chain Fatty Acids (SCFA)** | C2 – C6 | Acetic (C2), Butyric (C4), Caproic (C6) | Butterfat, dairy |
| **Medium-Chain Fatty Acids (MCFA)** | C6 – C12 | Caprylic (C8), Capric (C10), Lauric (C12) | Coconut oil, palm kernel oil |
| **Long-Chain Fatty Acids (LCFA)** | C13 – C21 | Palmitic (C16), Stearic (C18), Oleic (C18:1) | Most animal & vegetable fats |
| **Very Long-Chain Fatty Acids (VLCFA)** | > C22 | Lignoceric (C24), Cerotic (C26) | Brain lipids, waxes |

### 2.1.2 Based on Saturation (Most Important for KTU)

#### A. Saturated Fatty Acids (SFA) — **No double bonds**
- **General formula:** CH3−(CH2)n−COOH
- C atoms fully "saturated" with hydrogen
- Usually **solid at room temperature** (high melting point)
- Examples:
  - **Lauric Acid (C12:0)** — C12H24O2 — coconut/palm kernel
  - **Myristic Acid (C14:0)** — C14H28O2 — nutmeg butter
  - **Palmitic Acid (C16:0)** — C16H32O2 — palm oil, animal fat
  - **Stearic Acid (C18:0)** — C18H36O2 — cocoa butter, tallow

#### B. Unsaturated Fatty Acids — **One or more double bonds**

**Monounsaturated Fatty Acids (MUFA) — One C=C double bond**
- **Oleic Acid (C18:1, Δ9)** — C18H34O2 — olive oil (55–80%), canola oil
- **Erucic Acid (C22:1, Δ13)** — mustard, rapeseed
- **Palmitoleic Acid (C16:1, Δ9)** — macadamia, sea buckthorn

**Polyunsaturated Fatty Acids (PUFA) — Two or more C=C double bonds**
- **Linoleic Acid (C18:2, Δ9,12)** — LA, ω-6, **essential**, soybean, sunflower, corn oil
- **α-Linolenic Acid (C18:3, Δ9,12,15)** — ALA, ω-3, **essential**, flaxseed, walnut
- **γ-Linolenic Acid (C18:3, Δ6,9,12)** — GLA, ω-6, evening primrose oil
- **Arachidonic Acid (C20:4, Δ5,8,11,14)** — AA, ω-6, meat, eggs
- **Eicosapentaenoic Acid (C20:5, ω-3)** — EPA, fish oil
- **Docosahexaenoic Acid (C22:6, ω-3)** — DHA, fish oil, algae

### 2.1.3 Based on Essentiality

| Type | Definition | Examples |
|------|------------|----------|
| **Essential Fatty Acids (EFA)** | Cannot be biosynthesized; must come from diet | Linoleic (ω-6), α-Linolenic (ω-3) |
| **Non-Essential** | Body can synthesize | Oleic, Palmitic, Stearic |

### 2.1.4 Based on Omega (ω) Position of First Double Bond

- **ω-3 family** — first double bond at C-3 from methyl end (ALA, EPA, DHA)
- **ω-6 family** — first double bond at C-6 from methyl end (LA, AA)
- **ω-9 family** — first double bond at C-9 from methyl end (Oleic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** "ω-3: ALPHA starts aT three" (α-Linolenic). "ω-6: LiNoleic at six." "ω-9: Oleic at nine."

### 2.1.5 Based on Geometric Configuration

- **Cis-fatty acids** — H atoms on same side of C=C (natural)
- **Trans-fatty acids** — H atoms on opposite sides (formed during partial hydrogenation)

### 2.1.6 Nomenclature Systems

| System | Example: 18:2 (Δ9,12) |
|--------|------------------------|
| **IUPAC** | (9Z,12Z)-9,12-Octadecadienoic acid |
| **Δ (delta) notation** | cis,cis-Δ9,12-octadecadienoic acid |
| **ω (omega) notation** | ω-6 fatty acid |
| **Common name** | Linoleic acid |
| **Abbreviated** | 18:2n-6 or 18:2 ω-6 |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS — Memorize before every exam**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Constants | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Iodine Value (Pure FA)** | $IV = \dfrac{2 \times 126.9 \times 100}{MW_{FA}}$ | M.W. of I₂ = **253.8** | One C=C consumes one I₂ |
| 2 | **Iodine Value of Oil/Fat** | $IV = \dfrac{(B-S) \times N \times 12.69}{W}$ | 12.69 = **Eq. wt. of I₂** × 100/1000 | Wijs/Hanus method |
| 3 | **Saponification Value (Pure FA)** | $SV = \dfrac{56.1 \times 1000}{MW_{FA}}$ | Eq. wt. of KOH = **56.1** | One KOH per FFA |
| 4 | **Acid Value** | $AV = \dfrac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W}$ | V = titrant vol (mL), N = normality | Indicates hydrolytic rancidity |
| 5 | **% Free Fatty Acids (as oleic)** | $\%FFA = \dfrac{V \times N \times 28.2}{W}$ | Eq. wt. of oleic = **282/10 = 28.2** | Standard reporting form |
| 6 | **Peroxide Value** | $PV = \dfrac{(S-B) \times N \times 1000}{W}$ | meq peroxide / kg oil | Oxidative rancidity |
| 7 | **Average Molecular Weight** | $\overline{M} = \dfrac{3 \times 56108}{SV}$ | 56108 = 56.1 × 1000 ÷ 3 × 1000 (approx) | Triglyceride MW |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Safety Protocol:** Never confuse **Saponification Value** (which uses **56.1**) with **Iodine Value** (which uses **12.69** or **126.9**). Many students lose marks by swapping these constants!

## 2.3 Why This Matters in Industry

- **Refining decisions** depend on FFA content (acid value) — high FFA requires more caustic in alkali refining.
- **Hydrogenation** is controlled by target iodine value (e.g., margarine requires IV ≈ 70–80).
- **Soap making** requires high saponification value → short-chain FAs (coconut oil SV ≈ 250).
- **Biodiesel quality (cetane number)** is inversely related to iodine value; specification is IV ≤ 120 (EN 14214).
- **Margarine/shortening formulation** is engineered by interesterification to modify melting profile without trans fats.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Step-by-Step Derivations of Core Formulas

### 3.1.1 Derivation of Iodine Value for a Pure Fatty Acid

The Iodine Value (IV) is defined as the **number of grams of I₂ (or equivalent halogen) absorbed by 100 grams of the fat/oil**.

**Starting Principle:**
Each C=C double bond reacts with **one molecule of I₂** via electrophilic addition:
$$R-CH=CH-R' + I_2 \longrightarrow R-CHI-CHI-R'$$

**Step 1 — Moles of double bonds in 100 g of pure fatty acid:**
$$\text{Moles of FA} = \dfrac{100}{MW_{FA}}$$

**Step 2 — Moles of I₂ consumed = Moles of double bonds per FA molecule:**
For monounsaturated (e.g., oleic): 1 mol I₂ per mol FA.
For polyunsaturated (e.g., linoleic): 2 mol I₂ per mol FA.

**Step 3 — General relationship:**
$$\text{Mass of } I_2 = n_{C=C} \times \dfrac{100}{MW_{FA}} \times MW_{I_2}$$
where $n_{C=C}$ = number of C=C bonds per FA molecule, and $MW_{I_2} = 253.8$ g/mol.

**Step 4 — Final form:**
$$IV = \dfrac{n_{C=C} \times 253.8 \times 100}{MW_{FA}}$$

**Worked Example — Oleic Acid (C18:1):**
- MW of oleic acid = **282.46 g/mol**
- $n_{C=C}$ = 1
$$IV_{oleic} = \dfrac{1 \times 253.8 \times 100}{282.46} = 89.86 \approx 89.9$$

This matches the textbook value: **IV of oleic acid ≈ 89.9**.

**Worked Example — Linoleic Acid (C18:2):**
- MW of linoleic acid = **280.45 g/mol**
- $n_{C=C}$ = 2
$$IV_{linoleic} = \dfrac{2 \times 253.8 \times 100}{280.45} = 181.0$$

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Saponification Value for a Pure Fatty Acid

Saponification value = **mg KOH required to saponify 1 g of fat/oil**.

For a pure fatty acid:
$$R-COOH + KOH \longrightarrow R-COOK + H_2O$$

**Step 1 — Moles of FA in 1 g:**
$$n_{FA} = \dfrac{1}{MW_{FA}} \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Moles of KOH = Moles of FA** (1:1 stoichiometry):
$$n_{KOH} = \dfrac{1}{MW_{FA}} \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of KOH in mg:**
$$\text{Mass}_{KOH} = \dfrac{1}{MW_{FA}} \times 56.1 \times 1000 \text{ mg}$$

(56.1 = equivalent weight of KOH; multiply by 1000 to convert g → mg)

**Step 4 — Final form:**
$$SV = \dfrac{56100}{MW_{FA}}$$

**Worked Example — Palmitic Acid (C16:0):**
- MW of palmitic acid = **256.42 g/mol**
$$SV_{palmitic} = \dfrac{56100}{256.42} = 218.8$$

This matches the accepted value: **SV of palmitic acid ≈ 219**.

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Acid Value

Acid value = **mg KOH required to neutralize FFA in 1 g of sample**.

Titration reaction:
$$R-COOH + KOH \longrightarrow R-COOK + H_2O$$

**Step 1 — Equivalents of KOH used:**
$$N_{KOH} \times V_{mL} = \text{meq KOH}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of KOH in mg:**
$$m_{KOH} = N \times V \times 56.1 \text{ (mg, since 1 meq KOH = 56.1 mg)}$$

**Step 3 — Final form (per gram of sample):**
$$AV = \dfrac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W}$$

**Worked Example:**
A 5.0 g oil sample required 2.5 mL of 0.1 N KOH for neutralization.
$$AV = \dfrac{2.5 \times 0.1 \times 56.1}{5.0} = 2.805 \text{ mg KOH/g}$$

Converting to % oleic:
$$\%FFA = \dfrac{2.5 \times 0.1 \times 28.2}{5.0} = 1.41\%$$

---

### 3.1.4 Relationship Between Saponification Value and Average Molecular Weight

For a triglyceride:
$$\overline{MW}_{TG} = \dfrac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{SV}$$

**Derivation:**

For a triglyceride, 3 moles of KOH are required per mole TG:
$$TG + 3\,KOH \longrightarrow 3\,RCOOK + Glycerol$$

Moles of TG in 1 g = $\dfrac{1}{MW_{TG}}$

Moles of KOH = $\dfrac{3}{MW_{TG}}$

Mass of KOH in mg = $\dfrac{3}{MW_{TG}} \times 56.1 \times 1000$

But this mass = SV (definition). Therefore:
$$\boxed{SV = \dfrac{3 \times 56100}{MW_{TG}}}$$

Solving for MW of triglyceride:
$$\boxed{MW_{TG} = \dfrac{168300}{SV}}$$

**Worked Example — Coconut Oil (SV ≈ 255):**
$$MW_{TG} = \dfrac{168300}{255} \approx 660 \text{ g/mol}$$

This matches experimental estimates (~630–680 g/mol for coconut oil TG).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Typical Fatty Acid Profiles (for KTU viva)

| Oil/Fat | C16:0 (%) | C18:0 (%) | C18:1 (%) | C18:2 (%) | C18:3 (%) | IV |
|---------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----|
| Coconut oil | 8–10 | 2–3 | 6–8 | 1–2 | 0 | 7–12 |
| Palm oil | 44 | 4 | 39 | 10 | 0 | 50–55 |
| Olive oil | 11 | 2 | 75 | 8 | <1 | 75–88 |
| Sunflower oil | 6 | 4 | 28 | 60 | <1 | 110–140 |
| Soybean oil | 11 | 4 | 23 | 54 | 7 | 125–140 |
| Linseed oil | 5 | 3 | 20 | 15 | 55 | 170–200 |
| Butterfat | 28 | 12 | 28 | 3 | 1 | 25–40 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Trick:** Higher linolenic (C18:3) content → higher IV → lower oxidative stability. This is why linseed oil is used in paints (dries by oxidation) but not in frying.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Generic Fatty Acid Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HEAD[Hydrophilic HEAD - Polar]
        C0[Carbonyl C]
        O1[Double Bonded O]
        O2[Hydroxyl OH]
    end
    subgraph TAIL[Hydrophobic TAIL - Non polar]
        C1[Alpha C - CH2]
        Cn[Interior CH2 groups]
        Cn1[Omega CH3 terminal]
    end
    C0 --- C1
    C0 --- O1
    C0 --- O2
    C1 --- Cn
    Cn --- Cn1
    Cn1 -.-> OMEGA[Omega end - omega numbering starts here]
    C0 -.-> CARBOXY[Carboxyl group -COOH]
    style HEAD fill:#cfe2ff
    style TAIL fill:#fff3cd
    style OMEGA fill:#d1e7dd
    style CARBOXY fill:#f8d7da
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Saturated vs Unsaturated FA Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SAT[Saturated Fatty Acid CH3-CH2-CH2-CH2- COOH]
    UNSAT[Unsaturated Fatty Acid]
    SAT --> SATnote[Straight chain - tight packing - SOLID fat]
    UNSAT --> MONO[MUFA - 1 double bond - cis kink - e.g. Oleic]
    UNSAT --> POLY[PUFA - 2 or more double bonds - multiple kinks]
    MONO --> MONOnote[Liquid at room temp - e.g. olive oil]
    POLY --> POLYnote[Highly liquid - oxidative instability - fish oils]
    style SAT fill:#f5c6cb
    style UNSAT fill:#b6d7a8
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Classification Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    FA[Fatty Acids]
    FA --> CL[Chain Length Classification]
    FA --> SATC[Saturation Classification]
    FA --> ESS[Essentiality Classification]
    FA --> OMG[Omega Position Classification]
    FA --> GEO[Geometry Classification]
    CL --> SCFA[SCFA C2 to C6]
    CL --> MCFA[MCFA C6 to C12]
    CL --> LCFA[LCFA C13 to C21]
    CL --> VLCFA[VLCFA greater than C22]
    SATC --> SFA[Saturated - No C=C]
    SATC --> MUFA[Monounsaturated - 1 C=C]
    SATC --> PUFA[Polyunsaturated - 2 or more C=C]
    ESS --> EFA[Essential - Linoleic and alpha Linolenic]
    ESS --> NEFA[Non Essential - Body can synthesize]
    OMG --> OM3[omega 3 - ALA EPA DHA]
    OMG --> OM6[omega 6 - LA GLA AA]
    OMG --> OM9[omega 9 - Oleic Erucic]
    GEO --> CIS[Cis - Natural - bent structure]
    GEO --> TRANS[Trans - Hydrogenation - straight chain - health risk]
    style FA fill:#e7d6ff
    style SFA fill:#f5c6cb
    style MUFA fill:#ffeeba
    style PUFA fill:#b6d7a8
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Reaction Pathways of Fatty Acids

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FA[Fatty Acid RCOOH]
    FA --> SAP[Saponification with KOH or NaOH gives Soap and Glycerol]
    FA --> EST[Esterification with Glycerol gives Triglyceride]
    FA --> HYD[Hydrogenation reduces C=C gives Saturated FA]
    FA --> OXID[Oxidation gives Peroxides then Aldehydes and Ketones - Rancidity]
    FA --> HYDLYS[Hydrolysis gives Free FA and Alcohol]
    FA --> TRANS[Isomerization cis to trans during partial hydrogenation]
    style SAP fill:#d1e7dd
    style EST fill:#cfe2ff
    style HYD fill:#fff3cd
    style OXID fill:#f8d7da
    style HYDLYS fill:#f5c6cb

```
