# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**"List any three essential fatty acids and justify why they are called 'essential'."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three essential fatty acids are:

1. **Linoleic Acid (LA, C18:2, ω-6)** — Found abundantly in soybean, sunflower, corn, and safflower oils.
2. **α-Linolenic Acid (ALA, C18:3, ω-3)** — Found in flaxseed (linseed), walnut, and chia oils.
3. **Arachidonic Acid (C20:4, ω-6)** — Although obtainable from meat/eggs, infants cannot synthesize it efficiently.

**Justification:** These are called "essential" because the human body **lacks the desaturase enzymes (specifically Δ12 and Δ15 desaturases)** required to introduce double bonds at positions beyond the Δ9 carbon. Therefore, these fatty acids **must be supplied through diet**. Deficiency leads to dermatitis, poor wound healing, impaired immunity, and growth retardation.

*(Award 1 mark per FA + 1 mark for justification + 1 mark for deficiency symptoms = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**"Distinguish between saturated and unsaturated fatty acids. Give two examples of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Saturated FA (SFA) | Unsaturated FA (UFA) |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------|
| C=C bonds | **Absent** | **One (MUFA) or more (PUFA)** |
| Physical state | Usually **solid** at room temp | Usually **liquid** at room temp |
| Source | Animal fats, palm, coconut | Plant oils (sunflower, soy, olive) |
| Melting point | **High** | **Low** |
| Examples | Palmitic (C16:0), Stearic (C18:0) | Oleic (C18:1), Linoleic (C18:2) |

**(1 mark) Saturated:** Palmitic and Stearic acids. **(1 mark) Unsaturated:** Oleic and Linoleic. **(1 mark)** Tabular distinction with correct features.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Module 1, Q1a/1b]**

**(a)** Define fatty acids. Classify fatty acids based on (i) chain length, (ii) degree of saturation, and (iii) essentiality. Give one industrial/biological application of each class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define Iodine Value and Saponification Value. A sample of pure stearic acid (MW = 284.48 g/mol) is analyzed. Calculate the Iodine Value and Saponification Value. Comment on why stearic acid has zero iodine value. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (Question A)

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A fatty acid is a long-chain carboxylic acid with the general formula **R−COOH**, where R is a saturated or unsaturated aliphatic hydrocarbon chain (typically C4–C28). Fatty acids are the building blocks of triglycerides and phospholipids.

**Classification:**

**(i) Chain Length (2 marks):**
| Type | Range | Example | Application |
|------|-------|---------|-------------|
| Short-chain (SCFA) | C2–C6 | Butyric (C4:0) | Dairy flavor, gut health |
| Medium-chain (MCFA) | C6–C12 | Lauric (C12:0) | MCT nutrition, soap-making |
| Long-chain (LCFA) | C13–C21 | Stearic (C18:0) | Candles, cosmetics |
| Very long-chain (VLCFA) | >C22 | Lignoceric (C24:0) | Brain lipids, waxes |

**(ii) Saturation (2 marks):**
- **Saturated (SFA)** — no C=C bonds; e.g., palmitic (C16:0); used in margarine and shortening.
- **Monounsaturated (MUFA)** — one C=C; e.g., oleic (C18:1); used in salad oils.
- **Polyunsaturated (PUFA)** — ≥2 C=C; e.g., linoleic (C18:2); used in nutritional supplements.

**(iii) Essentiality (1 mark):**
- **Essential:** Linoleic (ω-6) and α-linolenic (ω-3) — dietary requirement for membrane integrity and eicosanoid synthesis.
- **Non-essential:** Palmitic, stearic, oleic — body can synthesize.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Iodine Value Definition (1 mark):**
The iodine value (IV) is defined as the **number of grams of iodine (I₂) absorbed by 100 grams of fat/oil**. It is a measure of the degree of unsaturation (number of C=C double bonds).

**Saponification Value Definition (1 mark):**
The saponification value (SV) is defined as the **number of milligrams of KOH required to saponify 1 gram of fat/oil**. It is inversely proportional to the average molecular weight of the fatty acids.

**Calculations (4 marks):**

For **stearic acid (C18:0)**:
- Molecular weight: MW = 284.48 g/mol
- Number of C=C bonds: **0** (saturated)

**Iodine Value calculation:**
$$IV = \dfrac{n_{C=C} \times 253.8 \times 100}{MW_{FA}} = \dfrac{0 \times 253.8 \times 100}{284.48} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Saponification Value calculation:**
$$SV = \dfrac{56.1 \times 1000}{MW_{FA}} = \dfrac{56100}{284.48} = \mathbf{197.2 \approx 197}$$

**Comment (1 mark):**
Stearic acid has **zero iodine value** because it is a fully saturated fatty acid containing **no carbon–carbon double bonds (C=C)**. Since the Wijs/Hanus method depends on the addition of I₂ across each double bond, no double bonds means no iodine consumption, yielding IV = 0.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE*

**(a)** Explain in detail the nutritional and industrial importance of fatty acids. Discuss why polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFA) are considered essential and highlight their role in human health. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fat sample weighing 4.0 g required 3.5 mL of 0.1 N KOH for acid value determination. Calculate (i) Acid Value, and (ii) % Free Fatty Acids (as oleic). If the same sample shows a saponification value of 200, determine the average molecular weight of the triglycerides present. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (Question B)

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Nutritional Importance (3 marks):**
1. **Energy source** — Provides 9 kcal/g (more than double that of carbohydrates).
2. **Essential fatty acids (EFA):** Linoleic (ω-6) and α-linolenic (ω-3) cannot be synthesized by humans and must be supplied through diet. They are precursors of long-chain PUFAs like arachidonic acid, EPA, and DHA — critical for brain development, retinal function, and inflammation regulation.
3. **Fat-soluble vitamin absorption** — Vitamins A, D, E, K require dietary fat for absorption.
4. **Membrane structure** — Phospholipid fatty acids maintain membrane fluidity; unsaturated FAs increase fluidity.

**Industrial Importance (2 marks):**
1. **Soaps and detergents** — Saponification of fats with NaOH (hard soap) or KOH (soft soap).
2. **Biodiesel** — Transesterification of triglycerides with methanol → fatty acid methyl esters (FAME).
3. **Cosmetics, paints, lubricants, plasticizers** — Castor oil (ricinoleic acid), linseed oil (linolenic acid).

**Why PUFAs are Essential and their Health Role (2 marks):**
PUFAs such as linoleic acid (LA, ω-6) and α-linolenic acid (ALA, ω-3) are termed "essential" because mammals lack the desaturase enzymes (Δ12 and Δ15) required to introduce double bonds at these positions. They are precursors of:
- **Eicosanoids** (prostaglandins, leukotrienes, thromboxanes) — regulate inflammation, platelet aggregation, vascular tone.
- **Docosahexaenoic acid (DHA)** — vital for neural and retinal development.
- ω-3 PUFAs are cardioprotective (lower triglycerides), anti-inflammatory, and reduce arrhythmia risk.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- W = 4.0 g
- V = 3.5 mL
- N = 0.1 N
- SV = 200

**(i) Acid Value (3 marks):**
$$AV = \dfrac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W}$$
$$AV = \dfrac{3.5 \times 0.1 \times 56.1}{4.0}$$
$$AV = \dfrac{19.635}{4.0} = \mathbf{4.91 \text{ mg KOH/g}}$$

**(ii) % Free Fatty Acids (2 marks):**
$$\%FFA = \dfrac{V \times N \times 28.2}{W}$$
$$\%FFA = \dfrac{3.5 \times 0.1 \times 28.2}{4.0} = \dfrac{9.87}{4.0} = \mathbf{2.47\%}$$

**(iii) Average Molecular Weight of Triglyceride (2 marks):**
$$MW_{TG} = \dfrac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{SV} = \dfrac{168300}{200} = \mathbf{841.5 \text{ g/mol}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Forgetting to multiply 56.1 by 1000** in the SV formula is the #1 mistake. The SV definition uses **mg** KOH, but KOH equivalent weight is in grams → must convert.
> - **Swapping IV and SV constants** (12.69 vs 56.1). Mnemonic: **"S**aponification uses **S**ix (56.1), **I**odine uses **I**-halves (12.69)."
> - **Forgetting the factor of 100** in IV definition (per 100 g of fat, not per 1 g).
> - **Unit errors** — Always state "mg KOH/g" for AV and "g I₂/100g" for IV.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Importance of Fatty Acids and Their Classifications"**

### Definition
- ☐ Fatty acid = long-chain carboxylic acid (**R−COOH**), typically C4–C28, even-numbered.
- ☐ Amphipathic structure: polar −COOH head + non-polar hydrocarbon tail.

### Classification
- ☐ **By Chain Length:** SCFA (≤C6), MCFA (C6–C12), LCFA (C13–C21), VLCFA (>C22).
- ☐ **By Saturation:** SFA (no C=C), MUFA (1 C=C), PUFA (≥2 C=C).
- ☐ **By Essentiality:** EFA = Linoleic (ω-6) + α-Linolenic (ω-3); body cannot synthesize.
- ☐ **By Omega:** ω-3 (ALA, EPA, DHA), ω-6 (LA, GLA, AA), ω-9 (Oleic).
- ☐ **By Geometry:** Cis (natural, bent) vs Trans (industrial, linear — health risk).

### Importance (5 Pillars)
- ☐ Nutritional (9 kcal/g, EFA for growth)
- ☐ Structural (cell membranes)
- ☐ Biological (eicosanoid precursors, vitamin absorption)
- ☐ Industrial (soap, biodiesel, cosmetics)
- ☐ Functional (texture, oxidative stability)

### Key Constants to Memorize
- ☐ **56.1** = equivalent weight of KOH (for AV and SV)
- ☐ **28.2** = (282/10) for oleic-based % FFA
- ☐ **253.8** = MW of I₂
- ☐ **12.69** = Eq. wt. of I₂ × 100/1000

### Core Formulas
- ☐ $IV = \dfrac{n_{C=C} \times 253.8 \times 100}{MW_{FA}}$
- ☐ $SV = \dfrac{56.1 \times 1000}{MW_{FA}} = \dfrac{56100}{MW_{FA}}$
- ☐ $AV = \dfrac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W}$
- ☐ $\%FFA_{oleic} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 28.2}{W}$
- ☐ $MW_{TG} = \dfrac{168300}{SV}$

### Quick Recall Examples
- ☐ **Oleic acid** (C18:1, ω-9) → major FA in olive oil; IV ≈ 89.9
- ☐ **Linoleic acid** (C18:2, ω-6) → essential; IV ≈ 181
- ☐ **α-Linolenic acid** (C18:3, ω-3) → essential; IV ≈ 273
- ☐ **Palmitic acid** (C16:0) → saturated; IV = 0; SV ≈ 219
- ☐ **Stearic acid** (C18:0) → saturated; IV = 0; SV ≈ 197

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of Oils in Nutrition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT748 - FAT AND OIL PROCESSING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Role of Oils in Nutrition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Nutritional Oils and Fats** are lipid-based biological macronutrients, chemically classified as **acylglycerols (predominantly triacylglycerols, TAGs)**, along with associated minor constituents such as **phospholipids, sterols, tocopherols, and fat-soluble vitamins**. In human and animal nutrition, they are defined as a heterogeneous group of compounds that are **insoluble in water but soluble in organic solvents** (e.g., chloroform, ether, benzene), as per the classical **Bloor's classification of lipids**.

From a nutritional standpoint, edible oils and fats function as:
1. A **concentrated energy substrate** delivering **9.0 kcal/g (37.7 kJ/g)** of metabolizable energy.
2. A vehicle for **Essential Fatty Acids (EFAs)** — linoleic acid (C18:2, ω-6) and α-linolenic acid (C18:3, ω-3) — which the human body cannot synthesize *de novo*.
3. A medium for the absorption of **fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, and K (ADEK)**.
4. Structural components of **biological membranes (phospholipid bilayer)**.
5. Precursors for **eicosanoids, hormones, and bile acids**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term *"Oils"* (typically liquid at room temperature, **~25 °C**) and *"Fats"* (semi-solid/solid at room temperature) are nutritionally interchangeable; the physical state is determined by the **degree of unsaturation** and the **chain length of fatty acids** esterified to the glycerol backbone.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Premium Fuel & Body Architecture" Analogy:**
> Think of dietary oils as the **"premium-grade diesel fuel plus structural steel"** for a complex machine (the human body):
>
> - **As Fuel:** Just as diesel engines extract more energy per litre than petrol engines, fats liberate more than **double the energy per gram (9 kcal/g)** compared to carbohydrates or proteins (**4 kcal/g** each). A small spoonful of oil literally powers hours of metabolic activity.
> - **As Building Material:** The phospholipid bilayer of every cell membrane is like the **reinforced concrete walls** of a building — flexible, strong, and selectively permeable. Without oils, the "walls" crumble.
> - **As Courier Vehicles:** Vitamins A, D, E, K are like VIP passengers. They are hydrophobic ("water-fearing") and cannot travel in the watery bloodstream alone. Oils act as their **luxury limousine**, ferrying them to organs.
> - **As Insulation:** Subcutaneous fat is the body's **"thermal wool"**, protecting vital organs (kidneys, heart) from mechanical shock and cold.
> - **As Chemical Messengers:** Certain fatty acids are converted into hormone-like compounds (**prostaglandins, leukotrienes**) that regulate inflammation, blood pressure, and clotting.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants in Nutrition

| Parameter | Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Energy yield of fat | **9.0 kcal/g (37.7 kJ/g)** |
| Atwater factor for fats | **9.0** |
| Energy yield of carbohydrates | 4.0 kcal/g |
| Energy yield of proteins | 4.0 kcal/g |
| Daily RDA of visible fat (adult) | **20–30 g/day (ICMR-NIN, 2020)** |
| Energy contribution from fats (% of total) | **20–35 %** (Acceptable Macronutrient Distribution Range, IOM) |
| EFA requirement (Linoleic acid) | **~2.5–3 % of total energy** |
| EFA requirement (α-Linolenic acid) | **~0.5–1 % of total energy** |
| Optimal ω-6 : ω-3 ratio | **4 : 1 to 10 : 1** |

---

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Fat and Oil Processing Technology

Understanding the *biological role* of oils is the foundational pillar of Module 1 because it dictates **every downstream processing decision**:
- The need to **preserve EFAs** → drives the adoption of **cold-press extraction** and **refining protocols that minimize trans-isomerization**.
- The presence of **tocopherols** → justifies **minimal refining** to retain natural antioxidants.
- The role in **vitamin transport** → necessitates **fortification programs** (e.g., vitamin A in vanaspati).
- Consumer demand for **heart-healthy oils** → drives **interesterification** and **blending technology**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Oils and Fats from a Nutritional Standpoint

The nutritional role of oils is best understood by classifying them structurally and functionally.

#### 2.1.1 Structural Classification
1. **Simple Lipids**
   - **Acylglycerols (Glycerides):** Mono-, Di-, and **Triacylglycerols (TAGs)** — the dominant fraction (95–98 %) of edible oils.
   - **Waxes:** Esters of long-chain fatty acids with long-chain alcohols (e.g., beeswax, carnauba wax). Nutritionally inert; act as protective coatings in plants.
2. **Compound (Conjugate) Lipids**
   - **Phospholipids:** Phosphatidylcholine (lecithin), phosphatidylethanolamine (cephalin), phosphatidylinositol.
   - **Glycolipids:** Cerebrosides, gangliosides — important in neural tissue.
   - **Lipoproteins:** Chylomicrons, VLDL, LDL, HDL — transport vehicles in blood.
3. **Derived Lipids**
   - **Fatty acids** (saturated, monounsaturated, polyunsaturated).
   - **Sterols:** Cholesterol (animal), phytosterols (plant).
   - **Terpenes & fat-soluble vitamins.**

#### 2.1.2 Nutritional / Functional Classification
| Class | Examples | Key Nutritional Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Saturated Fatty Acids (SFAs)** | Lauric, Myristic, Palmitic, Stearic | Energy; structural fats; *excess linked to LDL cholesterol* |
| **Monounsaturated FAs (MUFAs)** | Oleic (C18:1 ω-9) | Cardioprotective; stabilizes membrane fluidity |
| **Polyunsaturated FAs (PUFAs)** | Linoleic (LA), α-Linolenic (ALA), Arachidonic (AA) | **Essential FAs;** eicosanoid precursors; membrane function |
| **Trans Fatty Acids (TFAs)** | Elaidic acid | *Non-essential;* industrially produced; **deleterious** |
| **Phospholipids** | Lecithin | Emulsification; membrane structure; choline source |
| **Sterols** | Cholesterol, β-sitosterol | Hormone precursor; membrane fluidity modulator |
| **Fat-soluble vitamins** | A, D, E, K | Vision, Ca²⁺ metabolism, antioxidation, coagulation |

---

### 2.2 The Six Pillars of Nutritional Roles of Oils

#### Pillar 1: Concentrated Source of Energy
- The long hydrocarbon chains of fatty acids possess highly reduced C–H and C–C bonds.
- **β-Oxidation** in the mitochondria strips 2-carbon units (acetyl-CoA) per cycle, feeding the **TCA (Krebs) cycle** and the **electron transport chain (ETC)**.
- The **calorific value of 9.0 kcal/g** is roughly **2.25 times** that of carbohydrates (4.0 kcal/g) — making fats the body's preferred "energy reservoir."
- Storage efficiency: Adipose tissue stores ~**9,000 kcal/kg** versus glycogen's hydrated mass storing only ~**1,200 kcal/kg** of effective energy.

#### Pillar 2: Source of Essential Fatty Acids (EFAs)
- The human body **lacks Δ¹² and Δ¹⁵ desaturase enzymes**, so it cannot introduce double bonds at positions 12 and 15 of the fatty acid chain.
- Therefore, **Linoleic Acid (LA, 18:2 ω-6)** and **α-Linolenic Acid (ALA, 18:3 ω-3)** must come from the diet.
- These EFAs are converted to:
  - **Long-chain PUFAs:** Arachidonic acid (AA, 20:4 ω-6), Eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA, 20:5 ω-3), Docosahexaenoic acid (DHA, 22:6 ω-3).
  - **Eicosanoids:** Prostaglandins, thromboxanes, leukotrienes, prostacyclins — *local hormones* regulating inflammation, vasodilation, platelet aggregation.

#### Pillar 3: Vehicle for Fat-Soluble Vitamins (ADEK)
- Vitamins A, D, E, and K are **lipophilic** — they dissolve in oil but not in water.
- Bile salts emulsify dietary fat into **micelles**, enabling vitamin absorption across the intestinal mucosa.
- **Clinical correlation:** Fat malabsorption syndromes (e.g., cystic fibrosis, chronic pancreatitis) cause **deficiency of A, D, E, K**, manifesting as night blindness, rickets, hemolysis, and bleeding diathesis.

#### Pillar 4: Structural and Protective Functions
- **Phospholipid bilayer:** Every cell membrane is a 5–7 nm thick structure composed of phospholipids, cholesterol, and proteins. Phospholipids provide **fluidity, selective permeability, and signal transduction platforms**.
- **Myelin sheath:** 70–80 % lipid by dry weight; critical for nerve impulse conduction. Deficiency impairs CNS function.
- **Thermal insulation:** Subcutaneous adipose tissue (the body's "blubber") minimizes heat loss.
- **Mechanical cushioning:** Perirenal, pericardial, and omental fat pads protect vital organs from physical trauma.

#### Pillar 5: Satiety, Palatability, and Sensory Appeal
- Fats slow gastric emptying, inducing **prolonged satiety**.
- They act as **aroma carriers** — many flavor compounds (esters, aldehydes, ketones) are fat-soluble, so oil-rich foods smell and taste richer.
- **Texture:** Fats impart the mouthfeel (creamy, crispy, flaky) that drives consumer preference.

#### Pillar 6: Structural and Hormonal Precursors
- **Cholesterol** → precursor of steroid hormones (cortisol, aldosterone, sex hormones: estrogen, testosterone) and **vitamin D₃** (cholecalciferol).
- **Phospholipids** → precursors of **second messengers** (e.g., phosphatidylinositol 4,5-bisphosphate → IP₃ + DAG).
- **Sphingolipids** → regulate cell growth, differentiation, and apoptosis.

---

### 2.3 EFA Deficiency and Toxicity — Clinical Outcomes

| Condition | Cause | Clinical Manifestation |
|---|---|---|
| **EFA deficiency** | Prolonged parenteral nutrition without lipids; severe malabsorption | Dermatitis (eczema-like), hair loss, poor wound healing, growth retardation, neurological symptoms |
| **ω-3 deficiency** | Low fish/fish-oil intake | Impaired retinal function, cognitive decline, cardiovascular risk ↑ |
| **Hypervitaminosis A & D** | Excess cod liver oil / supplement | Hepatotoxicity (Vit A), hypercalcemia, nephrocalcinosis (Vit D) |
| **Excess SFA / TFA** | High intake of vanaspati, fried foods, processed snacks | ↑ LDL-C, ↓ HDL-C, atherosclerosis, insulin resistance |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA NOTE (for KTU numericals):** Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` inside markdown tables to avoid breaking table syntax.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{fat} = m \times 9.0$ | Energy from fat (Atwater factor) | kcal, m in g |
| 2 | $E_{CHO} = m \times 4.0$ | Energy from carbohydrates | kcal, m in g |
| 3 | $E_{prot} = m \times 4.0$ | Energy from proteins | kcal, m in g |
| 4 | $TEE = E_{fat} + E_{CHO} + E_{prot}$ | Total Energy Expenditure | kcal/day |
| 5 | $\%E_{fat} = \dfrac{E_{fat}}{TEE} \times 100$ | % Energy from fat | dimensionless |
| 6 | $RDA_{fat} = 0.25 \times TEE \div 9.0$ | Recommended fat (if 25 % of TEE) | g/day |
| 7 | $RDA_{LA} = 0.025 \times TEE \div 9.0$ | LA requirement (~2.5 % E) | g/day |
| 8 | $RDA_{ALA} = 0.005 \times TEE \div 9.0$ | ALA requirement (~0.5 % E) | g/day |
| 9 | $\text{Saponification Value} = \dfrac{56.1 \times 1000}{MW_{fatty acid}}$ | Indirect measure of chain length | mg KOH/g |
| 10 | $\text{Iodine Value} = \dfrac{126.91 \times n_{double\,bonds}}{MW_{FA}} \times 100$ | Degree of unsaturation | g I₂/100 g |
| 11 | $\omega\text{-}6:\omega\text{-}3 = \dfrac{\sum \omega\text{-}6}{\sum \omega\text{-}3}$ | EFA balance ratio | dimensionless |
| 12 | $n\text{-}6/n\text{-}3 \text{ ideal} = 4:1\text{ to }10:1$ | WHO/FAO recommendation | — |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Designing Functional Oils:** A food technologist formulating a "heart-healthy" blend must compute ω-6:ω-3 ratios using **Formula 11** and adjust with seed oils (sunflower, ω-6 rich) vs. fish oil / flaxseed (ω-3 rich).
- **Caloric Labeling (FSSAI / Codex):** Food labels mandate declaration of fat content in grams; engineers use **Formula 1** to convert from analytical data.
- **Stability Predictions:** **Iodine Value (Formula 10)** is a critical refining parameter — high-IV oils (soybean, safflower) need **hydrogenation or interesterification** to improve oxidative stability for frying.
- **Fortification Programs:** Government-mandated **vitamin A & D fortification** of vanaspati/margarine relies on accurate dosing per gram of oil — a direct application of Pillar 3.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Module-1 Specific Derivations:** Since the topic "Role of Oils in Nutrition" is conceptual, the following derivations focus on **quantitative nutritional calculations** — the most frequently asked numerical type in KTU University Examinations for Module 1.

---

### 3.1 Derivative Derivation 1: Energy Contribution of Dietary Fats in a Mixed Diet

**Problem Statement (Worked Example):**
A 25-year-old sedentary male consumes per day:
- Carbohydrates: 350 g
- Proteins: 70 g
- Fats: 80 g

**Derive:**
(i) Total energy intake.
(ii) Percentage of energy from each macronutrient.
(iii) Comment on whether fat intake complies with WHO/AMDR (20–35 % of total energy).

**Step 1: Apply Atwater Factors.**
The Atwater factors (4-9-4) give the average energy released upon complete oxidation of 1 g of carbohydrate, fat, and protein, respectively, in the human body.

**Step 2: Compute individual energy contributions.**

For Carbohydrates:

$$E_{CHO} = m_{CHO} \times 4.0 = 350 \times 4.0 = 1400 \text{ kcal}$$

For Proteins:

$$E_{prot} = m_{prot} \times 4.0 = 70 \times 4.0 = 280 \text{ kcal}$$

For Fats:

$$E_{fat} = m_{fat} \times 9.0 = 80 \times 9.0 = 720 \text{ kcal}$$

**Step 3: Sum to obtain Total Energy Expenditure (TEE).**

$$TEE = E_{CHO} + E_{prot} + E_{fat}$$

$$TEE = 1400 + 280 + 720 = 2400 \text{ kcal/day}$$

**Step 4: Compute percentage contributions.**

$$\%E_{CHO} = \frac{E_{CHO}}{TEE} \times 100 = \frac{1400}{2400} \times 100 = 58.33\,\%$$

$$\%E_{prot} = \frac{280}{2400} \times 100 = 11.67\,\%$$

$$\%E_{fat} = \frac{720}{2400} \times 100 = 30.00\,\%$$

**Step 5: Inference.**
Since $\%E_{fat} = 30\,\%$, the fat intake lies **within the AMDR range of 20–35 %**. The diet is therefore **acceptable** from a macronutrient-distribution standpoint, though qualitative fatty-acid profiling (SFA vs. MUFA vs. PUFA) must follow for a complete nutritional verdict.

---

### 3.2 Derivative Derivation 2: EFA Requirement from Total Energy Intake

**Problem Statement:**
For an adult requiring a total daily energy intake of 2200 kcal, calculate the minimum daily requirement of:
(i) Linoleic acid (LA, 18:2 ω-6) at 2.5 % of total energy.
(ii) α-Linolenic acid (ALA, 18:3 ω-3) at 0.5 % of total energy.

**Step 1: Conceptual Setup.**
The EFA requirement is expressed as a percentage of *total energy*, not of *fat energy*. To convert an *energy percentage* into a *mass in grams*, we must divide the allocated energy by the calorific value of fat (9.0 kcal/g), since EFAs themselves are fatty acids (energy density = 9.0 kcal/g).

**Step 2: Derive the General Formula.**

$$\text{Energy from EFA} = \dfrac{p}{100} \times TEE \text{ (kcal)}$$

$$\text{Mass of EFA (g)} = \dfrac{\dfrac{p}{100} \times TEE}{9.0}$$

where $p$ is the required energy percentage for the EFA.

**Step 3: Compute LA requirement (p = 2.5 %).**

$$\text{Energy}_{LA} = \dfrac{2.5}{100} \times 2200 = 55 \text{ kcal}$$

$$RDA_{LA} = \dfrac{55}{9.0} = 6.11 \text{ g/day}$$

**Step 4: Compute ALA requirement (p = 0.5 %).**

$$\text{Energy}_{ALA} = \dfrac{0.5}{100} \times 2200 = 11 \text{ kcal}$$

$$RDA_{ALA} = \dfrac{11}{9.0} = 1.22 \text{ g/day}$$

**Step 5: Practical Implication.**
A single tablespoon (~14 g) of soybean oil provides approximately **7 g LA** and **1 g ALA** — virtually meeting the EFA requirements. This is why a single-source-vegetable-oil diet typically avoids EFA deficiency.

---

### 3.3 Derivative Derivation 3: ω-6 : ω-3 Ratio Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A blended edible oil contains the following fatty acid profile (per 100 g oil):
- Linoleic acid (LA, ω-6): 48 g
- α-Linolenic acid (ALA, ω-3): 4 g
- Arachidonic acid (AA, ω-6): 1 g

Calculate the **ω-6 : ω-3 ratio** and compare with the WHO/FAO recommendation of **4 : 1 to 10 : 1**.

**Step 1: Sum ω-6 fatty acids.**

$$\sum \omega\text{-}6 = LA + AA = 48 + 1 = 49 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2: Sum ω-3 fatty acids.**

$$\sum \omega\text{-}3 = ALA = 4 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3: Apply the ratio formula.**

$$\dfrac{\omega\text{-}6}{\omega\text{-}3} = \dfrac{49}{4} = 12.25 : 1$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**
The ratio **12.25 : 1** is **higher than the WHO upper limit of 10 : 1**, indicating a **pro-inflammatory bias**. To correct this, the blender should fortify with an ω-3-rich oil such as **flaxseed oil** or **canola oil**.

**Step 5: How much ALA must be added?**
Target ratio = 10 : 1. Let $x$ g of ALA be added.

$$\dfrac{49}{4 + x} = 10 \implies 4 + x = \dfrac{49}{10} = 4.9 \implies x = 0.9 \text{ g}$$

Thus, adding **0.9 g ALA per 100 g oil** brings the blend to the ideal ratio.

---

### 3.4 Derivative Derivation 4: Iodine Value — Predicting Nutritional & Storage Quality

**Problem Statement:**
Calculate the theoretical iodine value of **triolein** (trioleoylglycerol, MW = 885.4 g/mol), given that oleic acid (C18:1, MW = 282.46 g/mol) has one double bond.

**Step 1: Concept.**
The **Iodine Value (IV)** is defined as the grams of I₂ (or its equivalent in halogen) absorbed by 100 g of fat. Each C=C consumes 1 mole of I₂ (MW = 253.81 g/mol).

**Step 2: Stated general formula.**

$$IV = \dfrac{n \times 126.91 \times 100}{MW_{FA}}$$

where $n$ = number of double bonds per FA molecule, and 126.91 is the atomic weight of iodine.

**Step 3: Compute IV of pure oleic acid.**

$$IV_{oleic} = \dfrac{1 \times 126.91 \times 100}{282.46} = 44.93 \text{ g I}_2/100\text{g}$$

**Step 4: Scale to triolein.**
Since triolein has 3 oleic acid residues, but the IV is per 100 g of *fat*, we directly use the molecular weight of triolein and the 3 double bonds:

$$IV_{triolein} = \dfrac{3 \times 126.91 \times 100}{885.4} = 43.0 \text{ g I}_2/100\text{g}$$

**Step 5: Inference.**
- Olive oil (rich in oleic acid) typically has IV ≈ 75–88 g I₂/100 g.
- Triolein alone is *less unsaturated* than natural olive oil, which contains ~ 10 % linoleic acid.
- A high IV indicates greater unsaturation → greater susceptibility to **oxidative rancidity** and thus a shorter shelf-life. This is why **antioxidant fortification (BHA/BHT, or natural tocopherols)** is critical for polyunsaturated oils.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: Matching Oil to Nutritional Need

| Health Objective | Recommended Oil (First Choice) | Reason (Nutritional Logic) | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cardioprotection (↓ LDL, ↑ HDL) | **Olive / Canola / Rice bran** | High MUFA (~70 % oleic acid) | Cost |
| Brain & retinal development (infants) | **Soybean + Fish oil blend** | DHA (ω-3) for neural tissue | Fishy odour |
| Anti-inflammatory diet | **Flaxseed / Chia** | Highest ALA (~55–60 %) | Poor oxidative stability |
| High-temperature frying | **Palm / Groundnut** | High SFA/MUFA → low IV → stable | SFA excess |
| EFA supplementation on budget | **Sunflower / Safflower** | >60 % LA | Severe ω-3 deficit |
| Trans-fat-free vanaspati | **Interesterified palm + soy** | Zero TFAs, plastic fat texture | Costlier than partial hydrogenation |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Logic (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**
> - **Part A:** 3-mark questions, 50–80 words; tested at K1 (Remember) / K2 (Understand) Bloom levels.
> - **Part B:** 14-mark questions, internal choice; tested at K3 (Apply) / K4 (Analyze) / K5 (Evaluate) levels. Each 14-mark question typically has sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question]**
*Define Essential Fatty Acids (EFAs). Name the two EFAs and state the daily adult requirement of each as a percentage of total energy intake.*

**Model Answer:**
Essential Fatty Acids (EFAs) are polyunsaturated fatty acids that **cannot be synthesized by the human body** due to the absence of **Δ¹² and Δ¹⁵ desaturase enzymes** and must therefore be obtained from the diet.
The two EFAs are:
1. **Linoleic Acid (LA, 18:2 ω-6)** — required at **2.5–3.0 % of total daily energy**.
2. **α-Linolenic Acid (ALA, 18:3 ω-3)** — required at **0.5–1.0 % of total daily energy**.

Deficiency causes dermatitis, growth retardation, and impaired neural development. [3 marks]

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Question]**
*List the fat-soluble vitamins and explain why their absorption is dependent on dietary fat intake.*

**Model Answer:**
The fat-soluble vitamins are **A, D, E, and K (ADEK)**.
- **Vitamin A** — vision, epithelial integrity.
- **Vitamin D** — calcium and phosphorus homeostasis.
- **Vitamin E (tocopherols)** — chain-breaking antioxidant.
- **Vitamin K** — γ-carboxylation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, X.

**Reason for fat-dependence:** ADEK are **lipophilic (non-polar)** molecules. They are insoluble in the aqueous environment of the intestinal lumen and cannot cross the **brush-border membrane** of enterocytes unaided. Bile salts emulsify dietary TAGs into **mixed micelles**, solubilizing the vitamins, after which they are absorbed alongside fatty acids via passive diffusion and packaged into **chylomicrons** for lymphatic transport. Fat malabsorption syndromes (e.g., cystic fibrosis, bile-duct obstruction) thus lead to **deficiency of A, D, E, K** despite adequate dietary intake. [3 marks]

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing only the **physiological role** without connecting to **processing implications** → loses 2–3 marks. Always link biology to *oil processing technology* (the course name).
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **phospholipids with triglycerides** in membrane structure → 1 mark lost per instance.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Failing to write the **ω-6 : ω-3 ratio in numerical form** (e.g., "8:1") → deduction of 0.5 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Omitting units in energy calculations (kcal vs. kJ) → 0.5 mark deduction.

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**
*(a)* Discuss the role of dietary oils and fats as a **concentrated energy source** in human nutrition. Derive the energy contribution of fats using the Atwater factor. [7 marks]
*(b)* Explain the structural role of phospholipids in **biological membranes**. Differentiate between phospholipids and triacylglycerols in terms of polarity, function, and nutritional significance. [7 marks]

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Oils as Concentrated Energy Source [7 marks]**

1. **Chemical basis:** The fatty acid chains of TAGs are rich in highly reduced **C–H and C–C bonds**. Complete oxidation in mitochondria liberates far more energy than carbohydrates or proteins. [1 mark]
2. **Atwater Factor:** The energy yield per gram of macronutrient is determined empirically as:
   - Carbohydrates: **4.0 kcal/g (17 kJ/g)**
   - Proteins: **4.0 kcal/g (17 kJ/g)**
   - Fats: **9.0 kcal/g (37 kJ/g)** [1 mark]
3. **Mathematical derivation:**

$$E_{fat} = m_{fat} \times 9.0 \text{ kcal/g}$$

If a person consumes 80 g of oil/day:

$$E_{fat} = 80 \times 9.0 = 720 \text{ kcal/day}$$

This single macronutrient provides **30 %** of a 2400 kcal diet. [2 marks]

4. **β-Oxidation pathway:** Each round of β-oxidation cleaves a 2-carbon **acetyl-CoA** unit, generating **1 FADH₂, 1 NADH, and 1 acetyl-CoA**. Acetyl-CoA enters the **TCA cycle**, and the reduced coenzymes feed the **electron transport chain**, ultimately producing **ATP**. [1 mark]
5. **Storage efficiency:** TAGs are stored anhydrous in adipocytes, yielding ~ **9,000 kcal/kg** of effective energy, compared with glycogen's hydrated mass that delivers only ~ **1,200 kcal/kg** of usable energy. This is why fats are the body's long-term energy reserve. [1 mark]
6. **Processing implication:** In edible-oil refining, **minimizing oxidative damage** preserves the high calorific value, as oxidized polymers have lower metabolizable energy. [1 mark]

**(b) Phospholipids in Biological Membranes [7 marks]**

1. **Definition:** Phospholipids are **amphipathic** lipids with a glycerol backbone esterified to **two fatty acids** (sn-1 and sn-2) and a **phosphate group** (sn-3) bearing a polar head (choline, ethanolamine, serine, inositol). [1 mark]
2. **Phospholipid bilayer architecture:**
   - Two monolayers arranged **tail-to-tail**.
   - Hydrophobic fatty-acid tails face inward.
   - Hydrophilic head groups face the aqueous cytosol and extracellular fluid. [1 mark]
3. **Functions:**
   - **Barrier:** selective permeability to ions and small molecules.
   - **Fluidity:** unsaturated FA tails (e.g., oleic, linoleic) maintain membrane fluidity; cholesterol modulates it.
   - **Signal transduction:** PIP₂ → IP₃ + DAG (second messengers).
   - **Protein anchoring:** peripheral and integral membrane proteins require a lipid environment. [2 marks]
4. **Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Phospholipids | Triacylglycerols (TAGs) |
|---|---|---|
| **Polarity** | Amphipathic (polar + non-polar) | Non-polar |
| **Solubility in water** | Form micelles/emulsions | Insoluble |
| **Primary function** | Membrane structure, emulsification | Energy storage, insulation |
| **Charge** | Charged (zwitterionic at physiological pH) | Neutral |
| **Nutritional role** | Structural, choline source (lecithin) | Caloric, EFAs, vitamin carrier |
| **Body location** | Cell membranes, lipoproteins, bile | Adipose tissue, muscle (intramuscular TAG) | [2 marks]

5. **Processing implication:** Lecithin (phosphatidylcholine) is a **by-product of degumming** crude soybean/sunflower oil. It is recovered and sold as a **natural emulsifier** in the food industry (chocolate, bakery, margarine). [1 mark]

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*(a)* Explain the concept of **Essential Fatty Acids (EFAs)**. Discuss the metabolic conversion of **linoleic acid (ω-6)** and **α-linolenic acid (ω-3)** into long-chain PUFAs and eicosanoids. [7 marks]
*(b)* A sedentary adult male consumes 300 g of carbohydrates, 65 g of protein, and 75 g of fat per day. Calculate the **total energy intake** and the **percentage of energy from each macronutrient**. Comment on whether the fat intake is within the AMDR. [7 marks]

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Essential Fatty Acids and Eicosanoid Biosynthesis [7 marks]**

1. **Definition of EFAs [1 mark]:** EFAs are PUFAs that the human body **cannot synthesize *de novo*** because of the **absence of Δ¹² and Δ¹⁵ desaturases**. The two EFAs are:
   - **Linoleic Acid (LA, 18:2 Δ⁹·¹², ω-6)**
   - **α-Linolenic Acid (ALA, 18:3 Δ⁹·¹²·¹⁵, ω-3)**
2. **ω-6 Pathway (LA → AA) [1.5 marks]:**
   LA → (Δ⁶-desaturase) → γ-Linolenic acid (GLA, 18:3) → (elongase) → Dihomo-γ-linolenic acid (DGLA, 20:3) → (Δ⁵-desaturase) → Arachidonic acid (AA, 20:4).
3. **ω-3 Pathway (ALA → EPA/DHA) [1.5 marks]:**
   ALA → (Δ⁶-desaturase) → Stearidonic acid (SDA, 18:4) → (elongase) → Eicosatetraenoic acid (20:4) → (Δ⁵-desaturase) → EPA (20:5) → (elongase + Δ⁴-desaturase) → DHA (22:6).
4. **Eicosanoid formation [1.5 marks]:**
   - AA → **Series-2 prostaglandins (PGE₂)**, **Series-4 leukotrienes (LTB₄)**, and **thromboxane A₂** → pro-inflammatory, platelet-aggregating, vasoconstrictive.
   - EPA → **Series-3 prostaglandins (PGE₃)**, **Series-5 leukotrienes (LTB₅)**, and **thromboxane A₃** → anti-inflammatory, vasodilatory, anti-aggregating.
   - **DHA** → incorporated directly into **retinal photoreceptors and neural synapses** for visual and cognitive function.
5. **Clinical significance [1.5 marks]:**
   - EFA deficiency causes dermatitis, growth retardation, and impaired wound healing.
   - Imbalance (ω-6 : ω-3 > 10:1) is implicated in cardiovascular disease, type-2 diabetes, and chronic inflammation.
   - **ω-3 supplementation** (fish oil, algal DHA) is recommended for pregnant women, infants, and patients with hypertriglyceridemia.

**(b) Energy Distribution Calculation [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- $m_{CHO}$ = 300 g
- $m_{prot}$ = 65 g
- $m_{fat}$ = 75 g
- Atwater factors: 4, 4, 9 (CHO, Protein, Fat)

**Step 1: Energy from Carbohydrates.**

$$E_{CHO} = 300 \times 4.0 = 1200 \text{ kcal}$$

**Step 2: Energy from Proteins.**

$$E_{prot} = 65 \times 4.0 = 260 \text{ kcal}$$

**Step 3: Energy from Fats.**

$$E_{fat} = 75 \times 9.0 = 675 \text{ kcal}$$

**Step 4: Total Energy.**

$$TEE = 1200 + 260 + 675 = 2135 \text{ kcal/day}$$

**Step 5: Percentage Distribution.**

$$\%E_{CHO} = \frac{1200}{2135} \times 100 = 56.21\,\%$$

$$\%E_{prot} = \frac{260}{2135} \times 100 = 12.18\,\%$$

$$\%E_{fat} = \frac{675}{2135} \times 100 = 31.62\,\%$$

**Step 6: AMDR Compliance Check.**
The AMDR for fat is **20–35 %** of total energy. Since $31.62\,\% \in [20, 35]\,\%$, the fat intake is **within the acceptable range**. The diet passes the macronutrient-distribution test; however, qualitative profiling (SFA ≤ 10 %, TFA < 1 %, adequate ω-3) is still required for a complete nutritional verdict.

**[7 marks awarded: 1 mark per calculation step, 1 mark for AMDR comparison, 1 mark for the inference]**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Oils vs. Fats:** Physical state at **25 °C** — oils are liquid, fats are solid; the *nutritional* classification is identical.
- **Atwater Factors (4-9-4):** Memorize the 4/4/9 values for CHO/Protein/Fat. They form the basis of every energy calculation.
- **EFAs:** Only **Linoleic (LA, 18:2 ω-6)** and **α-Linolenic (ALA, 18:3 ω-3)** are essential. RDA = **2.5 % E** and **0.5 % E** respectively.
- **Fat-Soluble Vitamins = ADEK.** Vitamin A (retinol) = vision; D = calcium; E = antioxidant; K = coagulation.
- **Six Pillars of Nutritional Roles:** Energy, EFAs, Vitamin Carrier, Membrane Structure, Insulation, Hormonal Precursors.
- **Phospholipid Bilayer:** Amphipathic; tails inside, heads outside; provides **fluidity + selective permeability**.
- **ω-6 : ω-3 Ideal Ratio = 4 : 1 to 10 : 1.** Modern Western diets trend toward 15–20 : 1 → pro-inflammatory.
- **Cholesterol:** Not "bad" per se — it is a **precursor of steroid hormones, vitamin D₃, and bile acids**.
- **Trans Fats (TFAs):** Industrially produced via **partial hydrogenation**; implicated in **atherosclerosis, insulin resistance, and cognitive decline**; WHO recommends < **1 % of total energy**.
- **Calorific Efficiency of Storage:** 1 g fat = 9 kcal; 1 g glycogen (hydrated) ≈ 1.2 kcal effective. **Adipose tissue = 9,000 kcal/kg.**
- **Processing–Nutrition Linkage:**
  - **Hydrogenation** → ↑ SFA, possible TFA → nutritional downgrade.
  - **Interesterification** → zero TFA → preferred modern route.
  - **Cold pressing** → retains tocopherols and EFAs → premium nutritional product.
  - **Degumming** → recovers **lecithin** (food-grade emulsifier).
  - **Fortification** → adds **vitamins A & D** to vanaspati/margarine.
- **EFA Deficiency Triad:** Dermatitis + Hair loss + Growth retardation. Suspect in patients on prolonged **TPN (Total Parenteral Nutrition)** without lipid emulsion.
- **Clinical Nutrition Pearls:** Cystic fibrosis → ADEK deficiency; Bile-duct obstruction → fat malabsorption; Refsum's disease → phytanic acid accumulation (strict low-phytol diet required).
- **Recommended Daily Visible Fat Intake (ICMR-NIN 2020):** **20–30 g/day** for a sedentary adult; up to **40–50 g/day** for moderate-to-heavy activity.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 Notes — Role of Oils in Nutrition.**
> *For maximum retention, redo the four derivations in Section 3 and the two 14-mark model answers in Section 5.2 within 24 hours of reading.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact of Fatty Acids on Health
Mechanical Extraction Methods: Ghani, Power Ghani, Rotary, Hydraulic Press, Screw Press, Expellers, Filter Press
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT748 - FAT AND OIL PROCESSING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Extraction Methods: Ghani, Power Ghani, Rotary, Hydraulic Press, Screw Press, Expellers, Filter Press

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mechanical Extraction** is a purely physical (non-chemical, non-solvent) unit operation in fat and oil processing wherein **compressive, shear, and frictional forces** are applied to conditioned oil-bearing seeds to rupture the cellular oil-bearing bodies (oleosomes / spherosomes) and liberate the liquid triglyceride phase, which is then recovered as **crude vegetable oil**. The residual solid by-product is called **oil-cake** (or **expeller-cake** when obtained from continuous presses).

Mathematically stated, the unit operation is governed by the **Schwartzberg Equation** (simplified form) for equilibrium liquid recovery from a compacting bed:

$$Y = Y_\infty \left(1 - e^{-k P^n}\right)$$

where $Y$ is fractional oil recovery, $Y_\infty$ is the asymptotic maximum recovery, $P$ is applied pressure, and $k$ and $n$ are seed-dependent empirical constants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Mechanical extraction is the *primary* recovery step and precedes solvent extraction. It typically recovers **60–80 %** of the available oil, with the remaining 20–40 % recovered later by hexane extraction from the cake.

## 1.2 The Family of Mechanical Extractors

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups mechanical extractors into the following six categories:

| # | Equipment | Energy Source | Mode |
|---|-----------|---------------|------|
| 1 | **Ghani** (Chekku) | Animal / Manual | Batch |
| 2 | **Power Ghani** | Electric Motor | Batch (Improved) |
| 3 | **Rotary Press** | Motor-driven cage | Batch / Semi-continuous |
| 4 | **Hydraulic Press** | Hydraulic fluid | Batch |
| 5 | **Screw Press / Expeller** | Electric motor + gearbox | Continuous |
| 6 | **Filter Press** | Hydraulic / Mechanical | Batch (Clarification) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Filter Press is NOT a primary extractor.** It is grouped with mechanical operations because it uses mechanical pressure (hydraulic/mechanical) to **clarify** the crude oil obtained from the upstream press by removing fines, phosphatides, and broken seed particles. This classification is explicitly followed in the KTU 2024 Scheme module statement.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lemon-and-Screw" Intuition

Imagine you have a **lemon** (the seed) and you want to extract the juice (the oil).

* If you **squeeze it with your fingers** — a *Ghani* (very low force, lots of pulp and peel left behind).
* If you **squeeze it with a nutcracker** — a *Hydraulic Press* (very high force, but you have to reload each lemon one at a time).
* If you **thread the lemon onto a rotating screw inside a barrel** and push it forward — a *Screw Press / Expeller* (continuous, automatic, but some juice is wasted in the squeezed-out pulp, called *cake*).

The **force**, the **mechanism of force application**, and the **continuity** (batch vs continuous) are the three parameters that differentiate every mechanical extractor.

## 1.4 Engineering Constants and Operational Metrics

The following industry-standard operational metrics are mandatory knowledge for KTU examinations:

* **Moisture content of conditioned seed entering press:** **8 – 10 % (w.b.)** (critical for plasticity).
* **Cooking / conditioning temperature:** **80 – 110 °C**.
* **Flake thickness after rolling:** **0.20 – 0.40 mm**.
* **Applied pressure range:** **200 bar** (rotary) to **2000 bar** (hydraulic).
* **Residual oil in cake from expeller:** **5 – 7 %**.
* **Residual oil in cake from solvent extraction:** **0.5 – 1.5 %**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** "**G**hani is **G**eared to **G**ravity" — it is the most primitive. **E**xpeller is **E**ngineered for **E**fficiency — it is the most industrial. The progression in the syllabus is **chronologically and technologically evolutionary**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Stage Theoretical Framework

Every mechanical extractor, regardless of type, performs the same **six fundamental theoretical stages** on the seed material:

### Stage 1 — **Cell Wall Rupture**
The seed is first crushed, cracked, flaked, or rolled to rupture the rigid cellulosic walls that encase the oil bodies. Without this, the oil cannot escape even under enormous pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> Rupturing is **not** done inside the press itself; it is a *pre-treatment* (cracking, flaking, cooking). The press only **collects and expels** the already-freed oil.

### Stage 2 — **Plasticization via Cooking / Conditioning**
The crushed flakes are heated to **80 – 110 °C** to:

* Reduce oil viscosity ($\eta$ drops, oil flows out easier).
* Coagulate proteins (makes cell walls more permeable).
* Adjust moisture to the optimum **8 – 10 %**.
* Inactivate enzymes (lipase, myrosinase) that degrade oil.

### Stage 3 — **Pressure Application**
Force $F$ is applied over area $A$ to generate pressure $P$. This pressure overcomes the **interfacial tension** between oil and ruptured cell wall, and the **capillary pressure** holding oil inside the cell.

### Stage 4 — **Oil Migration and Drainage**
Oil flows through the porous cake matrix. The flow obeys **Darcy's Law** for porous media:

$$Q = -\frac{k A}{\mu} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx}$$

where $Q$ is volumetric flow, $k$ is cake permeability, $\mu$ is oil viscosity, and $dP/dx$ is the pressure gradient along the cake.

### Stage 5 — **Cake Formation and Discharge**
The de-oiled solids form a compressed cake which is discharged either manually (Ghani) or continuously through a cone/conical choke (expeller).

### Stage 6 — **Clarification (Filter Press Stage)**
The crude oil still contains **1 – 15 % solids (fines)**, **0.5 – 3 % gums**, and broken cell-wall material. The filter press removes these to yield a clearer crude oil for refining.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print / Save this table.** It contains every numerical relationship examinable for this topic.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\text{Oil Yield (\%)} = \dfrac{W_o}{W_s} \times 100$ | Oil as % of seed weight processed | %, kg, kg |
| 2 | $\eta_{extraction} = \dfrac{O_a}{O_t} \times 100$ | Extraction efficiency (actual / theoretical oil) | % |
| 3 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pressure generated by a press | Pa or bar |
| 4 | $\dfrac{F_1}{A_1} = \dfrac{F_2}{A_2}$ | Pascal's Law for hydraulic press | N, m² |
| 5 | $MA = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = \dfrac{F_2}{F_1}$ | Mechanical Advantage of hydraulic system | dimensionless |
| 6 | $Q_m = \dfrac{m_{cake}}{t}$ | Mass throughput capacity of a press | kg/h |
| 7 | $\Delta P_{capillary} = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r}$ | Capillary pressure in oil cell (Laplace eqn.) | Pa |
| 8 | $Y = Y_\infty \left(1 - e^{-k P^n}\right)$ | Schwartzberg equilibrium recovery model | dimensionless |
| 9 | $P_{hydraulic} = \rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic head of oil column (design check) | Pa |
| 10 | $\rho_{cake} = \dfrac{m_{cake}}{V_{cake}}$ | Bulk density of expelled cake | kg/m³ |

> **Note on units:** $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 14.5 \text{ psi}$. Always use **SI base units** in numerical problems unless the question specifies otherwise. Use $\vert$ for absolute value in working; in final tables use `\vert` to preserve markdown.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Mechanical extraction is the **economic backbone** of the global edible oil industry:

* **Cold-pressed virgin oils** (olive, coconut, sesame) command **3 – 5×** the price of solvent-extracted refined oils because the process preserves heat-labile antioxidants and avoids solvent residues.
* **Expeller-pressed** oils are a regulated label in the EU and US — they guarantee no chemical solvent was used in primary recovery.
* In **developing nations** (Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka), the Ghani / Power Ghani supports the **rural micro-enterprise coconut oil sector** — over **50,000** Power Ghanis are operational in India alone.
* **Filter press** clarification reduces downstream refining costs by lowering the load on centrifugal separators and bleaching earth.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pascal's Law Applied to the Hydraulic Press

The hydraulic press is mathematically the most elegant extractor, so KTU examiners routinely test it. We derive the relationship between applied effort force and the load force the seed cake experiences.

### Given
* Master cylinder cross-sectional area: $A_1$ (m²)
* Slave (ram) cylinder cross-sectional area: $A_2$ (m²), with $A_2 \gg A_1$
* Effort force applied on master piston: $F_1$ (N)
* Load force delivered to seed cake: $F_2$ (N)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The pressure transmitted through an incompressible hydraulic fluid is equal at every point in the connected fluid column (this is the *statement* of Pascal's Law).

Therefore, the pressure generated under the master piston equals the pressure exerted by the slave piston on the cake:

$$P_1 = P_2$$

By definition of pressure $P = F/A$:

$$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

Cross-multiplying to obtain the load force:

$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

The **Mechanical Advantage (MA)** is therefore:

$$MA = \frac{F_2}{F_1} = \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

If the slave piston is **20 times** the area of the master piston ($A_2 / A_1 = 20$), then a 100 N effort yields a **2000 N** load on the cake. The trade-off is that the master piston must travel 20 times the distance of the slave piston (conservation of work / energy).

> [!NOTE]
> The work input equals the work output (ideal, frictionless case): $F_1 \cdot d_1 = F_2 \cdot d_2$. This is why the press is *slow* but *powerful* — a classical engineering compromise.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worked Example: Oil Yield and Extraction Efficiency

### Problem
A batch of **1000 kg** of cleaned groundnut kernels (theoretical oil content = **45 %**) is processed in a Power Ghani. The recovered crude oil weighs **380 kg**, and the expelled cake weighs **610 kg** (the remaining 10 kg is loss / fines). Calculate:

* (i) Oil yield (% by weight of seed)
* (ii) Extraction efficiency (%)
* (iii) Residual oil in cake (%), assuming theoretical oil = 45 % of kernel weight.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Oil yield:**

$$\text{Oil Yield (\%)} = \frac{W_o}{W_s} \times 100 = \frac{380}{1000} \times 100 = 38.0\%$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical oil in feed:**

$$O_t = 1000 \times 0.45 = 450 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Extraction efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{O_a}{O_t} \times 100 = \frac{380}{450} \times 100 = 84.44\%$$

**Step 4 — Residual oil in cake:**

$$O_{cake} = O_t - O_a = 450 - 380 = 70 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{Residual oil \%} = \frac{70}{610} \times 100 = 11.48\%$$

> [!TIP]
> A **Power Ghani** typically leaves **10 – 12 %** residual oil in cake, which is why a single extraction is rarely sufficient. Industrially, the cake is sent to a **second expeller pass** or to **solvent extraction** to recover the remaining oil.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capillary Pressure in an Intact Oil Cell

Before the cell wall is ruptured, oil is held inside the cell by **capillary forces**. The pressure required to overcome this is given by the **Laplace-Young equation** for a cylindrical capillary:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r}$$

where $\sigma$ is the oil-water interfacial tension (typically **30 mN/m** for vegetable oils), $\theta$ is the contact angle (approx. **0°** for oil on cellulose wall, so $\cos\theta = 1$), and $r$ is the effective cell pore radius (approx. **5 µm**).

### Numerical Evaluation

$$\Delta P = \frac{2 \times 0.030 \times 1}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.060}{5 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\Delta P = 12{,}000 \text{ Pa} = 0.12 \text{ bar}$$

This is a *very small* pressure — explaining why **even gentle pressing of pre-ruptured flakes** releases oil. The high pressures (200–2000 bar) in industrial presses are needed to overcome the **flow resistance through the cake matrix**, not the cell wall itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **favourite KTU question** — the examiner tests whether the student understands that cell wall rupture (done in pre-treatment) is *not* the rate-limiting step in the press.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

The following matrix is essential for the 14-mark comparison questions in the KTU exam.

| Parameter | Ghani | Power Ghani | Rotary Press | Hydraulic Press | Screw / Expeller | Filter Press |
|-----------|-------|-------------|--------------|-----------------|------------------|--------------|
| **Capacity (kg/h)** | 5 – 10 | 30 – 80 | 200 – 500 | 50 – 200 (batch) | 1000 – 10,000 | 50 – 500 (batch) |
| **Residual oil in cake (%)** | 15 – 18 | 10 – 12 | 8 – 10 | 6 – 8 | 5 – 7 | N/A (not extractor) |
| **Mode** | Batch | Batch | Batch | Batch | **Continuous** | Batch |
| **Oil temperature** | Ambient | Ambient | 40 – 60 °C | Ambient | 60 – 90 °C | 30 – 50 °C |
| **Pressure (bar)** | Gravity + impact | Mechanical | 200 – 400 | 1000 – 2000 | 500 – 1500 | 5 – 15 |
| **Main seed(s)** | Coconut, groundnut | Coconut, mustard | Copra, palm kernel | Olive, palm | Soy, rape, groundnut | Crude oil clarification |
| **Investment (rel.)** | Very low | Low | Medium | High | Very high | Medium |
| **Labour intensity** | Very high | High | Medium | High | Low | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Mechanical Extraction Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cleaned Oilseed] --> B[Dehulling and Cracking]
    B --> C[Flaking / Rolling Mill]
    C --> D[Cooking / Conditioning at 80-110 deg C]
    D --> E{Press Selection}
    E --> E1[Ghani]
    E --> E2[Power Ghani]
    E --> E3[Rotary Press]
    E --> E4[Hydraulic Press]
    E --> E5[Screw Press / Expeller]
    E1 --> F[Crude Oil Stream]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    E1 --> G[Oil Cake]
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    E4 --> G
    E5 --> G
    F --> H[Filter Press Clarification]
    H --> I[Clarified Crude Oil to Refining]
    G --> J[S Solvent Extraction for Residual Oil]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Functional Mapping of the Six Extractors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEG[Legend]
        direction LR
        L1[Batch Process]
        L2[Continuous Process]
        L3[Clarification Stage]
    end

    subgraph GRP1[Primitive Batch Extractors]
        direction TB
        N1[Ghani: Mortar and Pestle with Animal Drive]
        N2[Power Ghani: Motorised Mortar with Stirring Rods]
    end

    subgraph GRP2[Industrial Batch Extractors]
        direction TB
        N3[Rotary Press: Rotating Caged Cage]
        N4[Hydraulic Press: Pascal Cylinder System]
    end

    subgraph GRP3[Continuous Industrial Extractors]
        direction TB
        N5[Screw Press and Expeller: Worm Shaft inside Barrel]
    end

    subgraph GRP4[Downstream Mechanical Stage]
        direction TB
        N6[Filter Press: Plate and Frame Clarifier]
    end

    A[Prepared Flakes] --> N1
    A --> N2
    A --> N3
    A --> N4
    A --> N5
    N1 --> B1[Crude Oil + Cake]
    N2 --> B1
    N3 --> B1
    N4 --> B1
    N5 --> B1
    B1 --> N6
    N6 --> C1[Clarified Crude Oil]
    N6 --> C2[Filter Cake with Fines]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Working Principle of an Expeller (Continuous Screw Press)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hopper with Conditioned Flakes] --> B[Horizontal Rotating Worm Shaft]
    B --> C[Barrel Cage with Drainage Slots]
    C --> D[Conical Choke Restriction]
    D --> E[Discharged Oil Cake Pellet]
    C --> F[Collected Crude Oil Draining Through Slots]
    F --> G[Crude Oil Sump]
    G --> H[Filter Press Clarification]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for the schematic:** The worm shaft is a **continuously tapering screw**. As the cake is conveyed forward, the *available volume decreases*, the *pressure rises*, and oil is *squeezed out* through the drainage slots of the barrel cage. The remaining solids are extruded through the conical choke as a dry cake pellet.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] 

**Define mechanical extraction. State any four mechanical methods used in oilseed processing.**

**Model Answer:**

Mechanical extraction is the physical recovery of oil from conditioned oilseeds by the application of compressive, shear, and frictional forces, without the use of solvents, yielding crude oil and a solid by-product called oil-cake.

Four mechanical methods are: **(i) Ghani**, **(ii) Power Ghani**, **(iii) Hydraulic Press**, and **(iv) Screw Press / Expeller**. Rotary Press and Filter Press are also classified under this umbrella.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuator Note (3-mark pitfall):** Do **not** include solvent extraction in your list. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for confusing mechanical with solvent-based methods. Filter press is *clarification*, not extraction — mention it only if the question says "presses used in oil processing."

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between batch and continuous mechanical extraction with one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Batch | Continuous |
|---------|-------|------------|
| **Operation** | One charge at a time | Steady feed, steady discharge |
| **Labour** | High (loading, unloading) | Low (automated) |
| **Capacity** | Low (5–500 kg/batch) | High (1–10 tonnes/h) |
| **Example** | Ghani, Hydraulic Press | Screw Press / Expeller |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Rotary presses are *semi-continuous* — students often mis-classify them. State "semi-continuous (cage rotates, but cake is discharged in shots)" to be safe.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> The KTU 2024 ESE pattern for this module provides an **OR option** between two 14-mark questions. Both alternatives are given below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working principle of a Hydraulic Press** used for oil extraction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Power Ghani** processes **500 kg** of mustard seeds per batch. The mustard contains **38 %** oil by weight. The press recovers **160 kg** of crude oil. Calculate the **oil yield (%)**, **extraction efficiency (%)**, and the **residual oil in the cake (%)** if the cake weighs **330 kg**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**Construction (3 marks):**
A hydraulic press consists of a **master cylinder** (small diameter piston) connected via an **incompressible hydraulic fluid** (oil) to a **slave cylinder** (large diameter ram). The slave ram presses the seed cake (held in a perforated cage or filter cloth) against a fixed head. A high-pressure pump supplies the fluid, and pressure gauges monitor the line.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
By **Pascal's Law**, pressure applied on the master cylinder is transmitted undiminished to the slave cylinder. Because $A_2 \gg A_1$, the force is amplified:

$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

For example, with a 1:15 area ratio, a 1000 N hand effort produces a 15,000 N pressing force on the seed cake. Oil drains through the perforated cage; the de-oiled cake is manually ejected.

**Applications and limitations (1 mark):**
Used for olive, palm, and coconut oils in batch operations. Limitation: slow, batch-wise, requires skilled operator, not suited for high-capacity industrial lines.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Given:** $W_s = 500$ kg, $O_t = 38\%$ of $W_s$, $W_o = 160$ kg, $W_{cake} = 330$ kg.

**Step 1 — Theoretical oil in feed:**

$$O_t = 500 \times 0.38 = 190 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Oil Yield (% by weight of seed):**

$$\text{Oil Yield} = \frac{W_o}{W_s} \times 100 = \frac{160}{500} \times 100 = 32.0\%$$

**Step 3 — Extraction Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{O_a}{O_t} \times 100 = \frac{160}{190} \times 100 = 84.21\%$$

**Step 4 — Residual oil in cake:**

$$O_{cake} = O_t - O_a = 190 - 160 = 30 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{Residual oil \%} = \frac{30}{330} \times 100 = 9.09\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuator Note (7-mark numerical pitfall):** Students commonly write "Oil Yield" and "Extraction Efficiency" interchangeably. They are **NOT the same**. Oil yield is on the *seed weight* basis; efficiency is on the *oil content* basis. Mixing them costs 2 marks immediately.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **Ghani**, **Power Ghani**, and **Rotary Press** with respect to construction, capacity, oil recovery, and applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat diagram the **working of a Screw Press (Expeller)**. State any **three advantages** and **three limitations** of the screw press. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

**Ghani (2 marks):** A traditional Indian device consisting of a **wooden or stone mortar** with a **vertical pestle** (locally called *kolhu* or *ghani*). Driven by a bullock walking in circles, the pestle grinds and crushes the seed while a stirrer (the *onti*) prevents the paste from rising. Oil seeps out through a side outlet. Capacity: **5 – 10 kg/batch**, residual oil: **15 – 18 %**. Used in rural India for coconut, groundnut, and sesame.

**Power Ghani (2 marks):** A motorised version of the Ghani. The bullock is replaced by a **1–5 HP electric motor** driving the pestle at **30–60 rpm**. Capacity rises to **30–80 kg/batch**, residual oil drops to **10–12 %**. Construction is similar but the mortar is usually cast iron. Used in small and micro-enterprises, especially in Kerala (coconut oil) and Gujarat (groundnut oil).

**Rotary Press (2 marks):** A **cylindrical perforated cage** containing the seed, with **rotating wedges or pistons** inside that compress the cake as the cage turns. Capacity: **200–500 kg/h**, residual oil: **8–10 %**. Used primarily for **copra (coconut) and palm kernel** processing. Semi-continuous in operation.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The progression is one of **mechanisation** — animal → motor → motor + rotation. Each step improves capacity, oil recovery, and reduces manual drudgery.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**Diagram (3 marks):** A horizontal **worm shaft** (tapered screw) rotates inside a **horizontal barrel cage** with drainage slots. Conditioned flakes enter from a **hopper** at one end, are conveyed forward by the worm, and are progressively compressed as the screw depth decreases. Oil drains through the slots into a collection trough; the dry cake is discharged at the far end through an **adjustable conical choke** that controls back-pressure and thus oil recovery.

**Working in steps (2 marks):**
1. Flakes enter the barrel from the hopper.
2. Worm rotation conveys and compresses the mass.
3. Pressure builds up due to the tapered screw and the choke.
4. Oil is squeezed out through the cage slots.
5. Cake exits through the choke as a hard pellet.

**Three Advantages (1 mark):**
* Continuous operation — high capacity (1–10 t/h).
* No solvent required — safer, cheaper, "cold-pressed" labelling possible.
* Adjustable choke allows operator to tune residual oil in cake.

**Three Limitations (1 mark):**
* High initial capital cost and skilled maintenance.
* High wear on the screw and barrel cage due to friction — frequent replacement.
* Cannot achieve residual oil below ~5 %; solvent extraction is still required downstream.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuator Note:** When asked to "explain with a diagram," students often draw a *flowchart* instead of a *labelled cross-section of the expeller*. Examiners expect: **hopper, worm shaft, barrel cage with slots, drainage trough, conical choke, cake discharge** — at minimum. Missing the **conical choke** in the diagram costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **The Last-Minute Revision Checklist (print and carry):**

* Mechanical extraction is the **primary oil recovery** step; solvent extraction handles the residual.
* **Six extractors** in syllabus: Ghani, Power Ghani, Rotary, Hydraulic, Screw/Expeller, Filter Press.
* **Filter press is a clarification device, NOT an extractor** — placed at the end of the mechanical line.
* **Ghani** = animal-driven, traditional, residual oil 15–18 %, low capacity.
* **Power Ghani** = motorised Ghani, residual oil 10–12 %, micro-enterprise.
* **Rotary Press** = rotating cage, residual oil 8–10 %, used for copra and palm kernel.
* **Hydraulic Press** = Pascal's Law, $F_2 = F_1 \cdot (A_2/A_1)$, very high pressure (1000–2000 bar), batch, used for olive and palm.
* **Expeller / Screw Press** = continuous, worm shaft + barrel cage + conical choke, residual oil 5–7 %, the *industrial workhorse*.
* **Conditioning (cooking at 80–110 °C)** to 8–10 % moisture is the critical pre-treatment — *not* done in the press.
* **Oil Yield** (%) = $W_o / W_s \times 100$ — on seed basis.
* **Extraction Efficiency** (%) = $O_a / O_t \times 100$ — on oil-content basis. **Never confuse the two.**
* **Pascal's Law** applies *only* to the Hydraulic Press — do not write it for the Screw Press.
* **Darcy's Law** governs the oil flow through the cake matrix in *every* press.
* The **Schwartzberg equation** $Y = Y_\infty (1 - e^{-kP^n})$ is the model answer for "recovery vs pressure" in 14-mark derivations.
* Always state **units** in numerical answers: kg, %, bar, °C.
* Always state **assumptions** in derivations: incompressible fluid, no leakage, isothermal.

> [!TIP]
> **Final exam mantra:** *"Ghani grinds, Power Ghani powers, Rotary rotates, Hydraulic hydrostatically amplifies, Screw squeezes, Filter filters."* — recite this in the exam hall for a 2-minute mental reset before writing any answer on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Operation and Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT748 - FAT AND OIL PROCESSING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principles of Operation and Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Fat and Oil Processing Technology**, the **Principles of Operation and Maintenance** refer to the systematic set of engineering guidelines, standard operating procedures (SOPs), preventive interventions, and performance optimization strategies that govern the day-to-day functioning of an oilseed processing plant. These principles ensure that all unit operations — from cleaning, cracking, flaking, cooking, pressing, solvent extraction, refining, hydrogenation, and deodorization — deliver the desired throughput, product quality, and safety levels throughout the plant's designed operational life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Operation principles encompass **equipment functioning**, **process control parameters**, **start-up and shut-down routines**, and **maintenance philosophies** that span mechanical, electrical, instrumentation, and process safety domains.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **commercial kitchen in a five-star hotel**. The chefs (operators) follow recipes (SOPs), the equipment (ovens, mixers, fryers) must be cleaned daily (preventive maintenance), and any breakdown (e.g., a clogged burner) must be repaired immediately (corrective maintenance). Without scheduled cleaning of grease traps, the kitchen would shut down due to fire hazards. Similarly, in an oil mill, residual oil cakes left uncleaned can cause microbial contamination, fire risks, and downstream filtration problems. **Operation** is the art of *running* the process correctly; **Maintenance** is the science of *keeping* it running safely and efficiently.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Metrics in Plant Maintenance:**
> - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** **> 720 hours** for critical equipment in well-maintained plants.
> - **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** Ideally **≤ 8 hours** for non-critical and **≤ 2 hours** for critical breakdown recovery.
> - **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE):** World-class benchmark = **≥ 85%**.

### 1.3 Scope of Operation and Maintenance in Fat & Oil Industry

| Domain | Operation Aspect | Maintenance Aspect |
|--------|------------------|--------------------|
| **Mechanical** | Equipment start-up sequencing | Bearing lubrication, gear alignment |
| **Process** | Temperature, pressure, flow control | Calibration of sensors, valve servicing |
| **Electrical** | Motor load monitoring | Insulation testing, winding inspection |
| **Instrumentation** | DCS/PLC panel reading | Loop checking, transmitter calibration |
| **Safety** | Permit-to-work systems | Pressure relief valve testing, fire system checks |
| **Sanitation** | Clean-in-Place (CIP) cycles | Gasket replacement, pipeline pigging |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principles of Operation

The operation of any fat/oil processing plant is governed by the **TRIAGE principle**: **T**emperature, **R**atio (solvent-to-meal), **I**nteraction time, **A**gitation, and **G**radient (pressure/moisture). These five parameters must be precisely controlled at each unit operation:

1. **Seed Preparation Stage**
   - **Cleaning:** Removal of foreign matter using magnetic separators, vibrating screens, and destoners.
   - **Dehulling:** Cracking and aspiration; hull content in oilseed meals must be **< 5%**.
   - **Crushing/Flaking:** Flake thickness maintained at **0.25–0.40 mm** for optimum oil yield.
   - **Cooking (Conditioning):** Temperature raised to **80–110 °C** with moisture control at **9–11%** to rupture cell walls.

2. **Oil Extraction Stage**
   - **Mechanical Pressing:** Expeller pressure ranges from **200–700 bar** depending on seed type.
   - **Solvent Extraction:** Solvent-to-meal ratio maintained at **0.8–1.2 : 1**, temperature at **50–60 °C**, using **hexane** (boiling point **68.7 °C**).
   - **DT (Desolventizer-Toaster):** Operates under vacuum and indirect steam to recover solvent from meal.

3. **Refining Stage**
   - **Degumming:** Hot water at **70–80 °C** added at **2–3%** of oil weight.
   - **Neutralization:** Caustic soda concentration **8–20°Bé (Baumé)** based on free fatty acid (FFA) content.
   - **Bleaching:** Earth dosage of **0.5–2.0%** at **90–120 °C** under vacuum.
   - **Deodorization:** High temperature **220–260 °C** at vacuum **≤ 5 mbar**.

### 2.2 Principles of Maintenance

The maintenance philosophy in fat/oil processing rests on four pillars:

**A) Preventive Maintenance (PM):**
Scheduled, time-based interventions aimed at preventing failure. Includes routine lubrication, filter replacement, and inspection of wear-prone parts such as expeller worms and screw conveyor flights.

**B) Predictive Maintenance (PdM):**
Condition-based monitoring using tools like **vibration analysis**, **thermography**, **oil analysis**, and **ultrasonic thickness testing**. PdM reduces unplanned downtime by **30–50%** compared to pure reactive approaches.

**C) Corrective Maintenance (CM):**
Restoration of equipment to working condition after a failure has been detected. Often associated with **breakdown maintenance**.

**D) Total Productive Maintenance (TPM):**
A holistic Japanese-origin philosophy that involves every employee — from operators to top management — in maintenance activities. TPM aims for **Zero Defects, Zero Breakdowns, Zero Accidents** (the "Three Zeros").

> [!NOTE]
> **The 5S Methodology** (foundation of TPM):
> **Seiri** (Sort) → **Seiton** (Set in order) → **Seiso** (Shine) → **Seiketsu** (Standardize) → **Shitsuke** (Sustain).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---------------------|------------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | **Oil Yield Efficiency** | $\eta_{oil} = \dfrac{m_{oil}}{m_{seed}} \times 100$ | 18–22 (soybean); 40–45 (rapeseed) | % |
| 2 | **Solvent-to-Meal Ratio** | $R_{S/M} = \dfrac{m_{solvent}}{m_{meal}}$ | 0.8 – 1.2 | dimensionless |
| 3 | **Residual Oil in Cake** | $C_{oil} = \dfrac{m_{oil,residue}}{m_{cake}} \times 100$ | ≤ 0.5 – 1.0 | % |
| 4 | **Mean Time Between Failures** | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{total,operating}}{N_{failures}}$ | ≥ 720 | hours |
| 5 | **Mean Time To Repair** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum T_{repair,i}}{N_{failures}}$ | ≤ 8 (general), ≤ 2 (critical) | hours |
| 6 | **Plant Availability** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100$ | ≥ 95 | % |
| 7 | **Overall Equipment Effectiveness** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | ≥ 85 (world class) | % |
| 8 | **Free Fatty Acid (FFA)** | $FFA\% = \dfrac{V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \times 28.2}{m_{sample}}$ | < 2 (crude), < 0.1 (refined) | % as oleic |
| 9 | **Moisture Reduction Ratio** | $MRR = \dfrac{M_i - M_f}{M_i} \times 100$ | 11 → 2 (flaking), 50 → 11 (DT) | % |
| 10 | **Steam Economy (Deodorizer)** | $SE = \dfrac{m_{oil,processed}}{m_{steam}}$ | ≥ 100 (modern plants) | kg/kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Tip:** Always report FFA in **% as oleic acid equivalent**. Reporting in mg KOH/g will be considered incomplete unless the conversion factor is shown.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

The operational principles dictate product specifications such as **iodine value**, **saponification value**, **peroxide value**, and **color (Lovibond)**. Maintenance, on the other hand, governs **plant uptime**, **productivity**, **energy consumption per ton**, and **workplace safety**. In large solvent extraction plants, even a **5%** improvement in solvent recovery translates into savings worth **₹50–80 lakhs annually** (for a 500 TPD plant). Hence, robust operation & maintenance (O&M) practices are central to plant economics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)

**Step 1 — Define the three components of OEE:**

OEE is the product of **Availability (A)**, **Performance (P)**, and **Quality (Q)**.

$$
OEE = A \times P \times Q
$$

**Step 2 — Define Availability:**

Availability accounts for *uptime vs. planned production time*. Planned Production Time (PPT) = 24 hours × number of working days.

$$
A = \dfrac{Operating\ Time}{Planned\ Production\ Time} \times 100
$$

Operating Time = Planned Production Time − Downtime (both scheduled and unscheduled).

**Step 3 — Define Performance:**

Performance captures *speed losses*, i.e., the equipment running slower than its designed standard cycle time (SCT).

$$
P = \dfrac{Ideal\ Cycle\ Time \times Total\ Pieces\ Produced}{Operating\ Time} \times 100
$$

Ideal Cycle Time = reciprocal of rated throughput (pieces/hour or tons/hour).

**Step 4 — Define Quality:**

Quality accounts for *good pieces produced vs. total pieces produced*, including rejected output.

$$
Q = \dfrac{Good\ Pieces}{Total\ Pieces\ Produced} \times 100
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Illustration:**

Consider a solvent extraction plant:
- Planned production time = 30 days × 24 h = **720 hours**
- Unplanned downtime = **25 hours**
- Total material processed = **1,800 tons**
- Rated throughput (Ideal Cycle Time inverse) = **2.5 tons/hour**
- Defective product rejected = **45 tons**

Solving:

$$
A = \dfrac{720 - 25}{720} \times 100 = \dfrac{695}{720} \times 100 = 96.53\%
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{2.5 \times 1800}{695} \times 100 = \dfrac{4500}{695} \times 100 = 647.48\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget that Performance can exceed **100%** if the equipment runs faster than its design specification. This must be capped at 100% in standard OEE reporting. Hence the corrected value is **100%**.

$$
Q = \dfrac{1800 - 45}{1800} \times 100 = \dfrac{1755}{1800} \times 100 = 97.50\%
$$

Substituting back:

$$
OEE = 96.53\% \times 100\% \times 97.50\% = 94.12\%
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Failure Modes in Fat/Oil Processing Equipment

| Equipment | Dominant Failure Mode | Root Cause | Maintenance Strategy |
|-----------|----------------------|------------|----------------------|
| **Expeller** | Worm shaft wear | Abrasive solids in meal | Vibration analysis, hardness testing |
| **DT (Desolventizer-Toaster)** | Indirect steam coil leakage | Corrosion from acidic condensate | Hydrostatic testing every 6 months |
| **Centrifuge** | Bowl imbalance | Solids accumulation | Daily bowl cleaning, annual dynamic balancing |
| **Deodorizer** | Heating coil coking | High-temperature breakdown | Scheduled decoking every 12 months |
| **Hydrogenation Reactor** | Catalyst poisoning | Sulfur in feedstock | Sulfur guard bed replacement |
| **Conveyor** | Belt slippage | Misalignment, low tension | Tracking & tension checks weekly |
| **Solvent Pump** | Seal leakage | Mechanical seal wear | Seal replacement every 4,000 hours |

### 3.3 Derivation of FFA Calculation (Oleic Acid Equivalent)
