# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section provides a **model KTU examination pattern** covering Part A (short-answer) and Part B (long-answer with internal choice) per the 2024 Scheme.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

> **Q:** Define **Virgin Coconut Oil**. State **any two** APCC specifications for Grade A VCO.

#### Model Answer:
- **Definition:** Virgin Coconut Oil is the oil obtained from the **fresh, mature kernel of the coconut** by **mechanical or natural means**, with or without the use of heat, **without undergoing chemical refining, bleaching, or deodorization** (APCC, 2003).
- **APCC specifications (any two):**
  1. **Moisture content:** ≤ 0.2 %
  2. **Free Fatty Acid (as lauric acid):** ≤ 0.5 %
  3. **Peroxide Value:** ≤ 3 meq O₂/kg
  4. **Water-insoluble impurities:** ≤ 0.05 %
  5. **Saponification value:** 250–260 mg KOH/g

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> **Q:** What is the **purpose of sterilization** in palm oil processing? Mention **any two objectives**.

#### Model Answer:
Sterilization is the treatment of Fresh Fruit Bunches (FFB) with **saturated steam at 3 bar (~130 °C) for 90 minutes**. Its purposes are:
1. **Inactivation of lipase enzyme**, preventing FFA rise.
2. **Loosening of fruits** from the spikelets for easy threshing.
3. **Coagulation of mucilaginous proteins**, reducing emulsification during pressing.
4. **Softening of mesocarp**, increasing oil yield during pressing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Writing only "to kill bacteria" earns **zero** credit. Examiners specifically look for *lipase inactivation* and *fruit loosening* as the two cardinal objectives.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice — 14 Marks each)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — KTU Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

> **Q (a)** With a **neat flow diagram**, describe the **complete process of Crude Palm Oil (CPO) production** from Fresh Fruit Bunches. Discuss the function of each unit operation. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** Explain **dry fractionation** of refined palm oil. How is **RBD Palm Olein** different from **RBD Palm Stearin** in terms of fatty acid composition, iodine value, and applications? **(7 Marks)**

#### MODEL ANSWER — Part (a)

The CPO production flow consists of the following sequential operations:

1. **FFB Reception:** Bunches are received, weighed, and graded by ripeness index. FFA in fresh bunches must be < 5 %.

2. **Sterilization:** FFB are loaded into **horizontal sterilizer cages** and treated with saturated steam at **3 bar (≈130 °C) for 90 minutes**. This inactivates lipase, loosens fruits, coagulates proteins, and softens the mesocarp.

3. **Threshing:** Sterilized bunches are passed through a **rotating drum thresher** that strips the fruits from the empty fruit bunch (EFB). EFB is conveyed to the boiler as fuel.

4. **Digestion:** The stripped fruits are mashed in a **steam-heated digester** at **80–95 °C** for 20–30 minutes to rupture oil-bearing cells.

5. **Pressing:** The digested mash is fed to a **continuous screw press**, which squeezes out CPO + press liquor + press cake. Modern presses recover **70–75 %** of available oil.

6. **Clarification:** The crude oil-water mixture is fed to a **vertical clarifier** where oil floats to the top, sludge settles, and water drains. **Disk-stack centrifuges** polish the oil in modern plants.

7. **Vacuum Drying:** Wet oil is dried under vacuum to < 0.1 % moisture to prevent hydrolysis during storage.

8. **Storage:** Dried CPO is stored at **50–55 °C** in stainless-steel tanks pending refining.

[Draw flow diagram similar to Section 4.1]

#### MODEL ANSWER — Part (b)

**Dry fractionation** is the **solvent-free crystallization and separation** of palm oil into higher-melting (**stearin**) and lower-melting (**olein**) fractions. It is performed by:

1. **Slow cooling** of RBD palm oil from ~ 70 °C to **18–22 °C** under controlled shear.
2. **Crystallization** of the higher-melting triglycerides (mostly tripalmitin and dipalmitoyl-oleoyl-glycerol) into solid crystals.
3. **Filtration** through a **membrane filter press** that separates liquid olein from solid stearin.

**Differences between Olein and Stearin:**

| Parameter | RBD Palm Olein | RBD Palm Stearin |
|---|---|---|
| State at 25 °C | Liquid | Solid |
| Iodine Value | ~ 56 | ~ 48 |
| Major triglycerides | POP, POO, OOO | PPP, PPS, POP |
| Palmitic acid | ~ 40 % | ~ 50 % |
| Oleic acid | ~ 43 % | ~ 36 % |
| Application | Cooking oil, frying | Margarine, shortening, cocoa-butter substitute (CBS) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **dry fractionation** (crystallization + filtration, no solvent) with **solvent fractionation** (acetone or hexane used). Marks are lost if the **distinction is not clearly stated**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam — KTU Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

> **Q (a)** Describe the **fermentation method** and the **enzymatic method** of **Virgin Coconut Oil (VCO)** production with flow diagrams. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** Write **short notes** on:
> (i) Rice Bran Oil and the importance of **heat stabilization**.
> (ii) Extra Virgin Olive Oil (EVOO) — extraction and quality parameters. **(7 Marks)**

#### MODEL ANSWER — Part (a)

**Fermentation Method:**
- Fresh mature kernel is deshelled, pared, washed, and **comminuted**.
- Meat is mixed with water in **1:1 ratio** and pressed to obtain **coconut milk**.
- Milk is filtered through cheesecloth.
- **Natural fermentation** at ambient temperature for **24–48 hours** allows native microflora to break the oil-protein emulsion.
- Three phases form: oil (top), cream, and skim milk (bottom).
- Phase separation + gentle heating at 50 °C + centrifugation at 9000 rpm yields crude VCO.
- Final **vacuum drying** brings moisture to < 0.2 %.

**Enzymatic Method:**
- Coconut milk is prepared as above.
- **Enzymes** (protease, cellulase, pectinase) are added at **0.1–0.5 % (w/w)** and incubated at **50–60 °C for 2–4 hours**.
- Enzymes hydrolyze the **protein–oil complex** stabilizing the emulsion.
- Centrifugation yields oil of high clarity; subsequent vacuum drying produces APCC-grade VCO.

**Comparison:**

| Feature | Fermentation | Enzymatic |
|---|---|---|
| Cost | Low (no enzyme cost) | Higher (enzyme cost) |
| Time | 24–48 h | 2–4 h |
| Yield | 80–85 % | 88–92 % |
| Microbial risk | Higher (depends on hygiene) | Lower (controlled) |
| Energy | Ambient temp | 50–60 °C |
| Process control | Difficult | Easy |

#### MODEL ANSWER — Part (b)

**(i) Rice Bran Oil — Heat Stabilization:**
- Rice bran contains **active lipase** that rapidly hydrolyzes the oil.
- Within hours of milling, FFA can rise from 5 % to 60 %.
- **Heat stabilization** inactivates lipase: bran is treated at **130 °C for 1–2 minutes** via extrusion or PFA (Perfatty Acid) addition.
- Stabilized bran can be safely stored for weeks before solvent extraction with **hexane**.
- Crude RBO is then dewaxed (winterization at 5 °C) and refined.
- Nutraceutical content: **γ-Oryzanol (1–2 %)**, tocotrienols, phytosterols, ferulic acid.

**(ii) Extra Virgin Olive Oil (EVOO):**
- EVOO is obtained from olive drupes **solely by mechanical means** (cold press or centrifugation) at temperatures **below 27 °C**.
- No chemical refining, no solvent extraction.
- **Free acidity (as oleic acid):** ≤ 0.8 g/100 g.
- **Peroxide value:** ≤ 20 meq O₂/kg.
- Rich in **oleic acid (~75 %)**, polyphenols (hydroxytyrosol), squalene, and α-tocopherol.
- Color: golden yellow with green tints (chlorophyll + pheophytin).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write *cold-pressed* without specifying **< 27 °C** — losing 0.5 marks. Also, omitting the **mechanical means** clause in EVOO definition is a common trap.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **The Top 7 Mark-Deduction Triggers in Special Oils Module:**
> 1. **Confusing VCO with RBD coconut oil** — they differ in processing, FFA, color, and price.
> 2. **Writing "sterilization kills bacteria"** as the sole purpose — must mention lipase inactivation.
> 3. **Calling dry fractionation a "solvent process"** — it is NOT.
> 4. **Omitting temperature thresholds** (e.g., 27 °C for EVOO, 130 °C for sterilization, 50–60 °C for enzymatic VCO).
> 5. **Forgetting the moisture specification** for VCO (≤ 0.2 %).
> 6. **Confusing mesocarp (palm oil) with kernel (PKO)** — they are two distinct oils.
> 7. **Neglecting to draw flow diagrams in Part B** — diagrams carry up to **3 marks** in 7-mark sub-questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Special Oils (Module 2)**

### 🔹 Palm Oil
- Source: **Mesocarp of oil palm fruit**.
- Sterilization: **3 bar, 130 °C, 90 min**.
- Digestion: **80–95 °C**.
- Press recovery: **70–75 %**.
- Refining: Full **RBD** (degum → neutralize → bleach → deodorize).
- Fractionation: **Dry** (no solvent); produces **Olein (IV ~56)** and **Stearin (IV ~48)**.
- OER ≈ **20–23 % of FFB**; KER ≈ **5–6 %**.
- Major FA: **Palmitic 44 %**, Oleic 40 %, Linoleic 10 %.

### 🔹 Virgin Coconut Oil
- Source: **Fresh mature coconut kernel**.
- APCC spec: **FFA ≤ 0.5 %**, PV ≤ 3, Moisture ≤ 0.2 %.
- Five approved routes: **Cold press, Fermentation, Centrifugation, Enzymatic, Quick drying**.
- Major FA: **Lauric 48 %**, Myristic 18 %, Caprylic + Capric 8 %.
- **No chemical refining allowed**.
- Yields: 12–18 % on fresh-kernel basis.

### 🔹 Rice Bran Oil
- Source: **Rice bran** (pericarp + germ).
- **Heat stabilization at 130 °C** is mandatory to inactivate lipase.
- Extraction: Solvent (hexane).
- **Dewaxing / winterization** at 5 °C to remove waxes.
- Nutraceuticals: **γ-Oryzanol, tocotrienols, phytosterols**.

### 🔹 Olive Oil (EVOO)
- Cold press at **< 27 °C**.
- No chemical refining.
- Acidity (as oleic) ≤ **0.8 %**.
- Major FA: **Oleic ~75 %**.
- Polyphenols: hydroxytyrosol, oleuropein.

### 🔹 Quick Numerical Values to Memorize
- Sterilization: **3 bar / 130 °C / 90 min**.
- Digestion: **80–95 °C**.
- VCO moisture: **0.2 %**.
- EVOO temperature cap: **27 °C**.
- Steam-to-FFB: **0.18 kg/kg**.
- OER: **20–23 %**.
- Lauric acid in VCO: **~48 %**.
- Palmitic acid in palm: **~44 %**.

### 🔹 Acronyms for Quick Recall
- **FFB** = Fresh Fruit Bunch
- **CPO** = Crude Palm Oil
- **PKO** = Palm Kernel Oil
- **RBD** = Refined, Bleached, Deodorized
- **EFB** = Empty Fruit Bunch
- **POME** = Palm Oil Mill Effluent
- **VCO** = Virgin Coconut Oil
- **EVOO** = Extra Virgin Olive Oil
- **RBO** = Rice Bran Oil
- **APCC** = Asian Pacific Coconut Community
- **OER** = Oil Extraction Rate
- **KER** = Kernel Extraction Rate

### 🔹 14-Mark Question Themes (Practice These)
1. CPO production with flow diagram.
2. VCO production methods — compare any two.
3. Dry fractionation of palm oil.
4. Sterilization — purpose, conditions, consequences of under/over-treatment.
5. Refining comparison: RBD palm oil vs VCO.
6. Rice bran oil — lipase problem and heat stabilization.
7. EVOO — definition, processing, and quality parameters.

> **Final Tip:** Always close your 14-mark answer with a **flow diagram** (3 marks) and a **comparison table** (2 marks). These are the highest-leverage elements in KTU's valuation key.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Refining Processes: Objectives and Characterization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT748 - FAT AND OIL PROCESSING TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Refining Processes: Objectives and Characterization # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Refining** in the context of fats and oils processing is defined as the systematic removal of non-glyceride impurities (both saponifiable and unsaponifiable) from crude vegetable oils and animal fats to produce an edible-grade product that meets the standards of the **Codex Alimentarius Commission**, **FSSAI**, and **BIS (IS 1448)** specifications for human consumption. According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, refining encompasses the **purification of crude oils** to eliminate: - **Free Fatty Acids (FFA)** — formed by enzymatic/chemical hydrolysis of triglycerides - **Phospholipids (Gums)** — hydratable and non-hydratable phosphatides - **Pigments** — chlorophylls, carotenoids, gossypol (in cottonseed oil) - **Waxes** — long-chain esters, alcohols, hydrocarbons - **Trace metals** — iron, copper, nickel (pro-oxidants) - **Odour and flavour volatiles** - **Oxidation products** — peroxides, aldehydes, ketones - **Moisture** and **insoluble impurities (MIU)** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Refining is one of the **three mandatory stages** (along with Degumming and Bleaching) preceding **Deodorization** in the standard edible oil processing sequence: > **Crude Oil → Degumming → Refining → Bleaching → Deodorization → RBD Oil** ## 1.2 Refining Loss — Definition The mass of material removed from crude oil during refining, expressed as a percentage of the crude oil charge, is called **Refining Loss (L)**. It is a critical economic parameter. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Think of crude oil as a freshly tapped **palm juice or coconut toddy**. Just as toddy contains unwanted fibrous debris, suspended pulp, sour fermented acids, and an earthy smell that need to be strained and boiled away before it becomes a clean syrup, **crude vegetable oil** comes loaded with impurities — free acids that taste sour (FFA), sticky phosphorous compounds (gums), bitter pigments, and metals that make it spoil fast. **Refining is the "purification kitchen" of the oil industry** — like passing cloudy apple juice through a fine filter, neutralizing vinegar with baking soda, and then passing steam through the juice to strip off unwanted volatile aromas. Each step removes a specific impurity, just as each refining sub-process targets a particular class of contaminants. The **objective** is simple: **keep the good oil (triglycerides) and throw away only the bad stuff** — with minimum loss of the valuable neutral oil. > [!NOTE] > **Key Performance Indicator (KPI):** The economic success of refining is measured by **Refining Factor (RF)** and **Loss on Refining**. A well-designed refinery achieves **RF = 1.0 to 1.5** — meaning losses are only marginally above the theoretical chemical requirement. ## 1.4 Standards & Quality Benchmarks | Parameter | Refined Oil Limit (FSSAI/BIS) | Crude Oil Range | |---|---|---| | FFA (as oleic acid) | **≤ 0.25%** | 0.5 – 15% | | Phosphorus | **≤ 10 ppm** | 50 – 1500 ppm | | Moisture | **≤ 0.1%** | 0.1 – 1.0% | | Color (Lovibond 1" cell) | **≤ 3.0 R** | 5 – 70 R | | Peroxide Value | **≤ 10 meq/kg** | Variable | | Iron | **≤ 0.5 ppm** | 1 – 5 ppm | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Refining — Six Engineering Objectives The KTU 2024 curriculum specifies **six core objectives** that refining must accomplish. Each is engineered to address a specific defect or quality concern: ### Objective 1: **Removal of Free Fatty Acids (FFA)** - **Why:** FFA causes rancidity, off-flavor, smoke at low temperatures, and increases soap formation during deodorization. - **How:** Alkali neutralization (chemical refining) converts FFA → soap → removed in soapstock, OR high-temperature steam stripping (physical refining) volatilizes FFA. ### Objective 2: **Removal of Phosphatides (Degumming complement)** - **Why:** Phosphatides cause dark coloration during deodorization ("fixed colour"), catalyze oxidation, and foul heat exchangers. - **How:** Water/acid degumming precedes refining; residual phosphorus is precipitated as calcium/magnesium salts. ### Objective 3: **Removal of Colour Bodies** - **Why:** Carotenoids (β-carotene in palm), chlorophylls (in canola), and gossypol (cottonseed) make oil visually unappealing. - **How:** Some pigments are heat-labile (thermal bleaching in deodorization); others need adsorbent bleaching. ### Objective 4: **Removal of Waxes and High-Melting Glycerides** - **Why:** Cause cloudiness in cold-stored oils (sunflower, rice bran, corn oil). - **How:** Winterization (fractional crystallization) — not strictly refining but part of post-refining polish. ### Objective 5: **Removal of Oxidation Products** - **Why:** Peroxides, aldehydes (hexanal, nonenal), and ketones are toxic and produce off-flavors. - **How:** Adsorption on bleaching earth + steam stripping in deodorization. ### Objective 6: **Removal of Trace Metals (Iron, Copper, Manganese)** - **Why:** These are **pro-oxidants** — even 0.1 ppm copper shortens shelf life dramatically (catalyzes hydroperoxide decomposition). - **How:** Citric acid chelation (forms metal-citrate complexes) + bleaching earth adsorption. ## 2.2 Characterization of Crude Oils — The Refining Profile A complete refining profile (also called **RBD Evaluation** in industry) characterizes crude oil by **eleven critical parameters**: ### A. Chemical Parameters | Parameter | Symbol | Method | Typical Range | |---|---|---|---| | Free Fatty Acid | FFA | AOCS Ca 5a-40 | 0.5 – 20% | | Phosphorus (Phosphatides) | P | AOCS Ca 12-55 | 50 – 1500 ppm | | Moisture | M | AOCS Ca 2c-25 | 0.05 – 1.5% | | Insoluble Impurities | I | AOCS Ca 3a-46 | 0.01 – 0.5% | | Soap Content | – | AOCS Cc 17-79 | 0 – 50 ppm (post-refining) | | Oxidative Stability Index | OSI | Rancimat | 2 – 25 h | ### B. Physical Parameters | Parameter | Method | Significance | |---|---|---| | Colour (Lovibond) | AOCS Cc 13b-45 | Pigment load | | Refractive Index | AOCS Cc 7-25 | Purity, chain length | | Density | Pycnometer | Adulteration check | | Melting Point (Slip Point) | AOCS Cc 3-25 | Fats only | ### C. Performance Parameters | Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Method | |---|---|---| | Saponification Value | SV | Titrimetric (KOH) | | Iodine Value | IV | Wijs / Hanus | | Unsaponifiable Matter | USM | Ether extraction | | Peroxide Value | PV | Iodometric | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Critical Note for Tables:** All absolute values and conditioning are typeset using `\vert` and `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{FFA\% (as oleic)} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 28.2}{W}$ | Acid value × 0.503 conversion; $V$=titrant vol, $N$=normality, $W$=sample wt | % | | 2 | $\text{Acid Value (AV)} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W}$ | mg KOH per g oil | mg KOH/g | | 3 | $\text{Refining Loss (L)} = \dfrac{W_c - W_r}{W_c} \times 100$ | $W_c$=crude wt, $W_r$=refined wt | % | | 4 | $L_{\text{chem}} = \dfrac{\text{FFA\%} \times M_{\text{NaOH}}}{M_{\text{oleic}}} \times \text{excess}$ | Theoretical loss from neutralization | % | | 5 | $L_{\text{mech}} = \dfrac{\text{Neutral Oil}}{W_c} \times 100$ | Mechanical entrainment loss | % | | 6 | $L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{chem}} + L_{\text{mech}}$ | Total refining loss | % | | 7 | $\text{Soapstock \%} = \dfrac{100 \times W_{\text{soap}}}{W_c}$ | Mass balance check | % | | 8 | $\text{Neutral Oil Loss (NOL)} = \dfrac{W_{\text{soap}} \times N_o}{W_c}$ | $N_o$ = neutral oil in soap | % | | 9 | $\text{Refining Factor (RF)} = \dfrac{L_{\text{actual}}}{L_{\text{chemical}}}$ | RF=1.0 ideal, RF>1.5 indicates over-loss | – | | 10 | $\text{SV} = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N_{KOH} \times 56.1}{W}$ | Saponification value | mg KOH/g | | 11 | $\text{IV} = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N_{\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3} \times 12.69}{W}$ | Iodine value (Wijs) | g I₂/100 g | | 12 | $\text{PV} = \dfrac{(V_s - V_b) \times N \times 1000}{W}$ | Peroxide value | meq O₂/kg | | 13 | $\text{RM (Refining Material)} = 0.142 \times \text{FFA\%} + \text{excess alkali}$ | Total alkali dose | % | | 14 | $\text{MIU} = M + I + U$ (volatile & insoluble matter) | Moisture + Insoluble + Unsaponifiable | % | ## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Refining Characterization Characterization data drives **three critical industrial decisions**: 1. **Process Selection** — high FFA (>3%) crude oil is preferentially routed to **physical refining** (lower chemical cost); low FFA oils go to **chemical refining** (better colour). 2. **Capacity & Equipment Sizing** — gum/phosphorus load dictates the size of degumming reactors and wastewater treatment. 3. **Economic Pricing** — crude oil is traded on a **NOL + FFA + Phosphorus basis**; traders discount for high FFA (>2% deduction per % FFA). ## 2.5 Types of Refining — Quick Comparison | Process | Principle | Best For | Typical Loss | |---|---|---|---| | **Chemical (Alkali) Refining** | NaOH neutralization of FFA | Low-FFA, light oils (soy, canola) | 1.5 × FFA | | **Physical (Steam) Refining** | Steam stripping at high T, low P | High-FFA oils (palm, rice bran, coconut) | 1.2 × FFA | | **Miscella Refining** | Refining in hexane miscella | Miscella bleaching pre-step | 0.8 – 1.2 × FFA | | **Re-esterification** | Catalyzed FFA → triglyceride | Specialty, low loss | < 1.0 × FFA | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Free Fatty Acid (FFA) Formula The percentage FFA expressed as oleic acid is derived from the acid-base titration stoichiometry: **Step 1 — Write the balanced neutralization equation:** $$\text{R-COOH} + \text{KOH} \longrightarrow \text{R-COOK} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ One mole of fatty acid reacts with **one mole of KOH** (1:1 stoichiometry). **Step 2 — Express moles of KOH consumed:** $$\text{moles of KOH} = V \times N$$ where $V$ is in litres and $N$ is normality (eq/L). Since each mole of KOH provides one equivalent of OH⁻, this equals the moles of FFA. **Step 3 — Convert moles of FFA to mass of oleic acid:** The molecular weight of oleic acid is **282.46 g/mol**. Therefore: $$\text{Mass of oleic acid (g)} = V \times N \times 282.46$$ **Step 4 — Normalize to percent of sample:** $$\text{FFA\%} = \dfrac{\text{Mass of oleic acid}}{\text{Mass of sample (g)}} \times 100$$ $$\text{FFA\%} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 282.46}{W} \times \dfrac{100}{1000}$$ (since normality is in eq/L, multiply by 1000 to convert mL → L) **Step 5 — Final expression (KTU convention):** $$\boxed{\text{FFA\% (as oleic acid)} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 28.2}{W}}$$ where the 28.2 = 282.46/10 captures the conventional conversion factor. > [!NOTE] > **Conversion:** $\text{FFA\%} = \dfrac{\text{Acid Value}}{1.99} \approx \dfrac{\text{AV}}{2}$ ## 3.2 Derivation of Refining Loss Components **Step 1 — Define total refining loss $L_T$:** The mass balance for an alkali refining batch is: $$W_{\text{crude}} = W_{\text{refined}} + W_{\text{soapstock}} + W_{\text{misc losses}}$$ Therefore: $$L_T = \dfrac{W_{\text{crude}} - W_{\text{refined}}}{W_{\text{crude}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{W_{\text{soapstock}}}{W_{\text{crude}}} \times 100$$ **Step 2 — Decompose soapstock into two phases:** Soapstock contains: - **Soap phase:** the FFA-alkali salts (intentional chemical loss, $L_{\text{chem}}$) - **Entrained neutral oil phase:** trapped triglycerides (mechanical loss, $L_{\text{mech}}$) $$L_T = L_{\text{chem}} + L_{\text{mech}}$$ **Step 3 — Derive $L_{\text{chem}}$ from stoichiometry:** For 100 g of oil containing $F$% FFA (as oleic acid): $$\text{Mass of oleic acid} = F \text{ g}$$ Moles of oleic acid = $F / 282.46$ Moles of NaOH required = $F / 282.46$ Mass of NaOH (theoretical) = $\dfrac{F}{282.46} \times 40 = 0.1416 \times F \approx 0.142 \times F$ In practice, an **excess alkali** ($E$%, typically 0.1 – 0.5%) is added to ensure complete neutralization: $$\text{Total NaOH used} = 0.142 \times F + E \text{ (g per 100 g oil)}$$ **Step 4 — Convert NaOH to sodium oleate (soap):** Molecular weight of sodium oleate = **304.44 g/mol** $$\text{Soap formed} = \dfrac{F \times 304.44}{282.46} = 1.077 \times F$$ Therefore, **chemical loss** expressed as soap (theoretical): $$\boxed{L_{\text{chem}} = 1.077 \times F + E \times \dfrac{304.44}{40}}$$ For practical KTU calculations, the simplified form is: $$L_{\text{chem}} \approx 1.08 \times F + 0.05$$ **Step 5 — Mechanical loss $L_{\text{mech}}$:** This depends on the **separation efficiency** of the centrifuge. Typically $L_{\text{mech}} = 0.3$ to $1.5\%$, depending on: - Soapstock density (controlled by excess alkali) - Centrifuge bowl design - Operating temperature ($70$ – $90^\circ\text{C}$) - Wash water quality ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Standard) **Problem:** A batch of 5000 kg crude soybean oil is refined with alkali. The crude oil has: - FFA (as oleic) = **2.5%** - Excess alkali = **0.20%** of oil weight - Mechanical (entrainment) loss = **0.6%** Calculate: 1. Theoretical alkali requirement 2. Chemical refining loss 3. Total refining loss 4. Refining Factor (RF) 5. Mass of soapstock formed **Solution — Step by step:** **Step (i) Alkali required:** $$\text{NaOH} = 0.142 \times 2.5 + 0.20 = 0.355 + 0.20 = 0.555\%$$ Total NaOH = $\dfrac{0.555}{100} \times 5000 = 27.75 \text{ kg}$ **Step (ii) Chemical loss:** $$L_{\text{chem}} = 1.08 \times 2.5 + 0.05 = 2.70 + 0.05 = 2.75\%$$ **Step (iii) Total loss:** $$L_T = L_{\text{chem}} + L_{\text{mech}} = 2.75 + 0.60 = 3.35\%$$ **Step (iv) Refining Factor:** $$RF = \dfrac{L_{\text{actual}}}{L_{\text{chemical}}} = \dfrac{3.35}{2.75} = 1.218$$ This is in the desirable range (1.0 – 1.5). ✓ **Step (v) Mass of soapstock:** $$W_{\text{soap}} = 0.0335 \times 5000 = 167.5 \text{ kg}$$ **Step (vi) Mass of refined oil:** $$W_{\text{refined}} = 5000 - 167.5 = 4832.5 \text{ kg}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Chemical vs. Physical Refining | Parameter | Chemical Refining | Physical Refining | Decision Threshold | |---|---|---|---| | FFA in crude | < 3% preferred | > 3% preferred | FFA = 2 – 4% (crossover) | | Phosphorus | < 200 ppm required | < 50 ppm required | P-dependent | | Colour body removal | Excellent | Moderate | Chemical wins | | Capital cost | Lower | Higher (vacuum, steam) | – | | Operating cost | Higher (chemical + wastewater) | Lower (only steam) | Volume-dependent | | Wastewater | High BOD/COD | Negligible | Environmental KPI | | Refining loss | 1.5 × FFA + 0.5% | 1.2 × FFA | Physical wins at high FFA | | Final oil quality | Slight soap residues | Trace metals + odour | Chemical wins for flavour | | Process time | 30 – 45 min | 60 – 90 min | – | ## 3.5 Mass Balance Around the Refining Centrifuge For 100 kg/h crude oil feed at 2.5% FFA: ``` INPUTS OUTPUTS ───────────── ───────────── Crude Oil : 100.00 kg/h Soapstock : 3.35 kg/h NaOH (12% soln): 4.63 kg/h Wash water : 5.00 kg/h Process water : 5.00 kg/h Refined oil : 96.65 kg/h Vapour/evap : 4.63 kg/h ───────────── ───────────── Total in : 109.63 kg/h Total out : 109.63 kg/h ``` Closure check: $\sum \text{IN} = \sum \text{OUT}$ ✓ # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Define Free Fatty Acids (FFA) in crude oil. Why is FFA removal the primary objective of alkali refining?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Free Fatty Acids (FFA) are carboxylic acid molecules (e.g., oleic, palmitic, stearic) that exist in a free, uncombined state in crude oils. They are formed by the enzymatic (lipase) or hydrolytic cleavage of triglycerides during seed storage, oilseed damage, or improper post-harvest handling. > **Why primary objective (1.5 marks):** > 1. FFA causes **off-flavor and rancidity**, making oil unpalatable. > 2. FFA **catalyzes oxidative degradation**, reducing shelf life. > 3. FFA causes **smoke and foaming** at high temperatures (during frying and deodorization). > 4. FFA **consumes additional alkali** during refining, increasing chemical cost and refining loss. > 5. Residual FFA in refined oil is legally restricted to **< 0.25%** under FSSAI. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: List any six objectives of refining of edible oils.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The six objectives of refining are: > 1. **Removal of Free Fatty Acids (FFA)** — neutralization. > 2. **Removal of Phosphatides (Gums)** — improves heat stability. > 3. **Removal of Pigments** — improves colour (carotenoids, chlorophylls). > 4. **Removal of Waxes and High-Melting Triglycerides** — prevents cloudiness. > 5. **Removal of Oxidation Products** — peroxides and aldehydes. > 6. **Removal of Trace Metals** — iron, copper (pro-oxidant control). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Module-3 Internal Choice:** Students answer **either Question A or Question B** from Module 3. Each question is structured as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. --- ### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks) #### Part (a): *Discuss the various objectives of refining of edible oils in detail. How does refining differ from degumming and bleaching? (7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **1. Objectives of Refining (5 marks):** | S.No. | Objective | Mechanism | Importance | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | FFA removal | NaOH neutralization or steam stripping | Reduces rancidity, off-flavor | | 2 | Phosphatide removal | Acid + water hydration | Prevents dark colour, fouling | | 3 | Pigment removal | Adsorption / heat decomposition | Improves visual appeal | | 4 | Wax removal | Winterization, filtration | Prevents cloudiness | | 5 | Metal chelation | Citric acid + bleaching earth | Extends shelf life | | 6 | Odour removal | Steam stripping in deodorization | Flavour stabilization | | 7 | Moisture removal | Vacuum drying | Reduces hydrolysis | **2. Difference Table — Refining vs. Degumming vs. Bleaching (2 marks):** | Aspect | Degumming | Refining | Bleaching | |---|---|---|---| | Target | Phosphatides | FFA + residual gums | Pigments, metals | | Reagent | Water/H₃PO₄ | NaOH | Bleaching earth | | Output | Hydrated gums | Soapstock | Spent earth + colour bodies | | Position in sequence | 1st | 2nd | 3rd | --- #### Part (b): *Define Refining Loss. Derive the chemical refining loss for a crude oil containing 3.2% FFA with 0.25% excess alkali. Assume mechanical loss of 0.7%. Calculate the Refining Factor. (7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Define Refining Loss (1 mark):** > Refining loss $L_T$ is the total mass of material removed from crude oil during refining, expressed as a percentage of crude oil charge: > $$L_T = \dfrac{W_{\text{crude}} - W_{\text{refined}}}{W_{\text{crude}}} \times 100$$ **Step 2 — Identify the components (1 mark):** $$L_T = L_{\text{chem}} + L_{\text{mech}}$$ **Step 3 — Derive chemical loss using the standard formula (2 marks):** $$\boxed{L_{\text{chem}} = 1.08 \times \text{FFA\%} + 0.05 \text{ (approx.)}}$$ Substituting FFA = 3.2%: $$L_{\text{chem}} = 1.08 \times 3.2 + 0.05 = 3.456 + 0.05 = 3.51\%$$ **Step 4 — Account for excess alkali explicitly (1 mark):** Excess alkali contributes to additional soap formation: $$L_{\text{excess}} = E \times \dfrac{304.44}{40 \times 100} = \dfrac{0.25 \times 304.44}{4000} = 0.019\%$$ (This is small and is usually subsumed in the constant 0.05.) **Step 5 — Compute total loss (1 mark):** $$L_T = 3.51 + 0.70 = 4.21\%$$ **Step 6 — Refining Factor (1 mark):** $$RF = \dfrac{L_T}{L_{\text{chem}}} = \dfrac{4.21}{3.51} = 1.199$$ **Conclusion:** RF = 1.20 is well within the acceptable range of **1.0 – 1.5**, indicating an efficient refinery operation. --- ### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* #### Part (a): *Explain the characterization of crude vegetable oils for refining purposes. List the major chemical and physical parameters evaluated. (7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **1. Introduction (1 mark):** > Characterization of crude oil is essential to determine the refining route, estimate refining loss, select reagents, and predict final oil quality. The complete refining profile is established before processing. **2. Chemical Parameters (3 marks):** | Parameter | Typical Range | Significance | |---|---|---| | Free Fatty Acid (FFA) | 0.5 – 15% | Determines alkali dose | | Phosphorus (Phosphatides) | 50 – 1500 ppm | Determines degumming intensity | | Moisture (M) | 0.05 – 1.5% | Affects hydrolysis | | Insoluble Impurities (I) | 0.01 – 0.5% | Pre-filtration required | | Unsaponifiable Matter (U) | 0.3 – 5% | Quality and regulatory | | Trace metals (Fe, Cu, Mn) | 0.1 – 5 ppm | Pro-oxidant risk | **3. Physical Parameters (2 marks):** - **Colour (Lovibond)** — indicates pigment load. - **Density / Refractive Index** — chain length, purity check. - **Melting/Slip Point** — important for fats. - **Viscosity** — affects flow and separation. **4. Performance Parameters (1 mark):** - **Saponification Value (SV)** — average molecular weight of fatty acids. - **Iodine Value (IV)** — degree of unsaturation. - **Peroxide Value (PV)** — primary oxidation status. - **Oxidative Stability Index (OSI)** — Rancimat, shelf-life indicator. --- #### Part (b): *Compare chemical (alkali) refining and physical (steam) refining of edible oils. Under what circumstances is each preferred? (7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Feature | Chemical (Alkali) Refining | Physical (Steam) Refining | |---|---|---| | **Principle** | NaOH neutralization of FFA → soap | Steam stripping at 240–260°C, 2–5 mbar | | **FFA removal** | Converted to soap → separated | Volatilized as fatty acid vapour | | **Best crude** | Low FFA (<3%), light oils | High FFA (>3%), palm, rice bran, coconut | | **Phosphorus required** | < 200 ppm | < 50 ppm (strict) | | **Refining loss** | 1.5 × FFA + 0.5% | 1.2 × FFA | | **Capital cost** | Lower (centrifuge + reactor) | Higher (vacuum, steam ejector) | | **Operating cost** | High (NaOH + wastewater) | Low (only steam) | | **Wastewater** | High BOD/COD | Negligible | | **Final colour** | Excellent | Moderate (needs more bleaching) | | **Process time** | 30 – 45 min | 60 – 90 min | | **Trace metals** | Removed in soap | Need chelation (citric acid) | **Selection Criteria (3 marks):** **Prefer Chemical Refining when:** - FFA < 3% (e.g., refined soy, canola, sunflower). - High-quality colour required (e.g., salad oil). - Phosphorus < 200 ppm can be assured. - Smaller plant scale. **Prefer Physical Refining when:** - FFA > 3% (e.g., crude palm oil with 5–8% FFA, rice bran with 10–30% FFA). - Wastewater disposal is restricted. - Energy/steam is cheap. - Phosphorus can be reduced to < 50 ppm via acid degumming. **Conclusion:** Modern high-throughput refineries increasingly adopt **physical refining + acid degumming + silica treatment** for environmental and economic reasons. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **FFA Formula Sign Error:** Students often use $282.46$ instead of $28.2$ (the correct factor when V is in mL). KTU awards **0 marks** if the unit inconsistency is not handled. > 2. **RF Misinterpretation:** A low RF (close to 1.0) is *good*, not bad. Many students write "low RF means poor refining" — this is **wrong**. > 3. **Confusing Refining Loss Components:** $L_{\text{chem}}$ is the *intentional* (stoichiometric) loss; $L_{\text{mech}}$ is the *unavoidable* centrifugal entrainment. Merging them loses 2 marks. > 4. **Forgetting Excess Alkali Contribution:** Always add $E$ explicitly when given in the problem. > 5. **Mixing up Phosphorus and Phosphatides:** 1 ppm P ≈ 30 ppm phosphatides (using MW ratio). Many students forget this conversion. > 6. **Unit confusion for Iodine Value:** Wijs uses N/10 (0.1 N) thiosulfate; factor becomes **12.69** (not 126.9). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Bullets for Last-Minute Revision** - **Refining** = removal of non-glyceride impurities from crude oils to meet FSSAI/BIS edible-grade standards. - **Six core objectives:** FFA ↓, Phosphatides ↓, Pigments ↓, Waxes ↓, Oxidation products ↓, Trace metals ↓. - **Crude oil characterization** involves **11 parameters**: FFA, P, M, I, MIU, colour, RI, density, SV, IV, PV. - **FFA% (as oleic)** $= \dfrac{V \times N \times 28.2}{W}$ — universal KTU formula. - **Acid Value** = 1.99 × FFA% — keep this conversion memorized. - **Refining Loss** $L_T = L_{\text{chem}} + L_{\text{mech}}$. - **Chemical Loss** $= 1.08 \times \text{FFA\%} + 0.05$ (simplified KTU form). - **Refining Factor (RF)** = $L_{\text{actual}} / L_{\text{chem}}$; ideal range = **1.0 – 1.5**. - **Chemical refining** for low FFA (<3%); **physical refining** for high FFA (>3%). - **Physical refining** requires **P < 50 ppm** (after acid degumming). - **MIU = M + I + U** is a standard industry acronym in oil trading. - **Phosphorus → Phosphatides:** multiply by **30** for conversion (MW ratio). - **Bleaching earth** also removes residual soaps, trace metals, and oxidation products — complementary to refining. - **Mass balance check** always: $\sum \text{IN} = \sum \text{OUT}$ (use to validate soapstock weight). - **Excess alkali** (0.1 – 0.5%) is standard practice — it ensures complete neutralization and prevents hydrolytic reversion. - **KTU exam tip:** When asked about refining loss, always state the **components** (chemical + mechanical), not just a single number — examiners allocate 2 marks for proper decomposition. - **Industry benchmarks:** Soy refined loss ≈ 2.5–3.5%; Palm refined loss ≈ 3.5–6%; Rice bran ≈ 8–15% (due to wax and high FFA). - **Codex limit for refined oil:** FFA ≤ 0.25% (as oleic); phosphorus ≤ 10 ppm. - **RF = 1.0 is theoretically perfect** but operationally impossible; aim for 1.1 – 1.3. ---

topics.title: Refining Techniques: Degumming, Deacidification (Acid Refining), Bleaching, Deodorization, Winterization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT748 - FAT AND OIL PROCESSING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Refining Techniques: Degumming, Deacidification (Acid Refining), Bleaching, Deodorization, Winterization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Oil Refining?

**Refining** is a series of purification processes applied to **crude vegetable oils** (and animal fats) to remove unwanted non-glyceride components such as phospholipids (gums), free fatty acids (FFA), pigments, waxes, odoriferous compounds, and trace metals. The goal is to produce an edible-grade oil that is **bland, stable, light-colored, and safe** for human consumption, while maximizing the yield of neutral oil (triglycerides).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** *"Refining is the purification of crude oils to remove objectionable constituents like phospholipids, FFA, pigments, waxes, and odoriferous matter with minimum damage to the neutral oil (triglycerides) and minimum loss of desirable constituents."*

### The Five Major Refining Stages (KTU Module 3 Focus)

| # | Stage | Primary Target Impurity | Key Reagent |
|---|-------|------------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Degumming** | Phospholipids, gums, mucilaginous matter | Water / Phosphoric acid |
| 2 | **Deacidification (Acid/Alkali Refining)** | Free Fatty Acids (FFA) | NaOH (Caustic Soda) |
| 3 | **Bleaching** | Pigments, soap traces, peroxides, metals | Bleaching earth (activated clay) |
| 4 | **Deodorization** | Volatile odor/flavor compounds, residual FFA | Steam under high vacuum |
| 5 | **Winterization** | High-melting waxes & saturated TAGs | Controlled cold crystallization |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The House Cleaning Model"

Imagine your **crude oil as a freshly harvested apple juice** straight from the orchard. It contains:
- Cloudy suspended particles (like **phospholipids** in oil) → must be removed by settling/filtering → **Degumming**
- Sour taste due to natural acids (like **FFA** in oil) → must be neutralized → **Alkali Refining**
- Brownish color (like **pigments** in oil) → must be bleached → **Bleaching**
- Fermented smell (like **odoriferous volatiles** in oil) → must be steam-stripped → **Deodorization**
- Wax that solidifies on cooling (like **waxes** in oil) → must be filtered cold → **Winterization**

> [!NOTE]
> **Just like juice clarification makes it palatable and shelf-stable, refining makes crude oil edible, neutral in taste, light in color, and resistant to oxidative rancidity.**

## 1.3 Why Refining is Essential — The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Quality Targets for Refined Edible Oil (KTU Board Exam Favourite):**
> - **FFA (as oleic acid):** < 0.1 %
> - **Phosphorus content:** < 5 ppm (degummed)
> - **Color (Lovibond):** < 2.5 R (refined, bleached)
> - **Peroxide Value:** < 1 meq/kg (after deodorization)
> - **Moisture & Volatiles:** < 0.1 %

Each refining step targets a specific defect, and skipping any one compromises oil quality, shelf life, and downstream processability (especially during hydrogenation and interesterification).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DEGUMMING — Removal of Phospholipids (Gums)

### 2.1.1 Background
Crude vegetable oils (soybean, sunflower, rapeseed, groundnut) contain **0.5–3.0 %** phospholipids — **phosphatidylcholine (PC), phosphatidylethanolamine (PE), phosphatidylinositol (PI), and phosphatidic acid (PA)**. These are also called **gums** or **mucilaginous matter**.

**Hydratable gums (PC, PE)** swell and become insoluble when mixed with water. **Non-hydratable gums (PA, Ca/Mg salts of PE)** require acid pretreatment.

### 2.1.2 Process Steps (Water Degumming)
1. Heat crude oil to **60–80 °C**.
2. Add **2–3 % water** (or 0.05–0.1 % **85 % phosphoric acid** for non-hydratable gums).
3. Mix intensely for **15–30 min** with high-shear agitator.
4. Hydrated gums precipitate and form a separate phase (gum sludge).
5. Separate by **centrifugation** (typically at ~ 6000 rpm).
6. Dry the degummed oil under vacuum to < 0.1 % moisture.

### 2.1.3 Chemical Reaction (Acid Degumming)
$$\text{PA-Ca} + 2\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \longrightarrow \text{PA} + \text{Ca(H}_2\text{PO}_4)_2$$

The acid liberates bound phospholipids into their free (hydratable) form.

### 2.1.4 Types of Degumming
- **Water Degumming** — used for soy, sunflower, groundnut (high hydratable gums).
- **Acid Degumming (Superdegumming / TOP degumming)** — used for oils rich in non-hydratable gums (palm, rapeseed).
- **Enzymatic Degumming (EnzyMax®, Lurgi)** — uses phospholipase A1/A2 enzymes; very low phosphorus residual (< 5 ppm).
- **Membrane Degumming** — ultrafiltration through polymeric membranes; emerging green technology.

---

## 2.2 DEACIDIFICATION (ACID / ALKALI REFINING)

### 2.2.1 Objective
Reduce **Free Fatty Acids (FFA)** from 1–10 % (in crude oil) to < 0.1 % (refined oil) by reacting with **sodium hydroxide (NaOH)** to form **soapstock** which is separated by centrifugation.

### 2.2.2 Saponification Reaction
$$\text{R-COOH} + \text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{R-COONa} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

(Where R-COOH is a free fatty acid, e.g., oleic acid.)

### 2.2.3 Process Steps (Batch Alkali Refining)
1. **Pre-treat** degummed oil with 0.05–0.1 % phosphoric acid to precipitate residual gums.
2. Heat oil to **50–70 °C**.
3. Add calculated quantity of **NaOH solution (8–14 °Bé, i.e., 5–10 % w/w)**.
4. Mix for **10–20 min** in a **neutralizer** with slow agitation to allow **neutralization** of FFA and **saponification** of partial glycerides.
5. Heat to **70–80 °C** to break emulsion and coagulate soapstock.
6. Separate soapstock by **centrifugation** (primary centrifugal separator).
7. **Wash** the oil with hot soft water (10–20 % of oil volume) to remove residual soap.
8. **Dry** the oil under vacuum (3–5 kPa) at 90–100 °C to < 0.1 % moisture.

### 2.2.4 Chemical/Physical Refining Alternative
- **Physical Refining:** Steam-stripping FFA under high vacuum at 220–260 °C (preferred for **palm, coconut, and lauric oils** with high FFA and low phosphatides). Saves chemicals; gives higher yield.
- **Chemical Refining:** Alkali + centrifugation (preferred for soybean, sunflower, groundnut).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic: "ChemRef for Lecithin-Rich, PhyRef for Lauric-Rich"** — Chemical refining suits oils rich in phospholipids; physical refining suits oils rich in lauric acid (C12:0).

---

## 2.3 BLEACHING — Removal of Pigments

### 2.3.1 Objective
Remove **carotenoids, chlorophylls, xanthophylls, gossypol (in cottonseed), and trace metals** to obtain a light, marketable color.

### 2.3.2 Adsorbent: Bleaching Earth (Activated Bentonite/Fuller's Earth)
- **Natural bleaching earth:** hydrated aluminum silicates.
- **Acid-activated bleaching earth:** treated with H₂SO₄ to increase surface area (200–400 m²/g) and acidity.
- Typical dosage: **0.5–2.0 %** of oil weight (depending on oil quality and target color).

### 2.3.3 Process Steps
1. Dry refined oil to < 0.1 % moisture.
2. Heat to **90–110 °C** under vacuum (50–100 mbar).
3. Add bleaching earth (and optionally 0.05–0.1 % activated carbon for hard-to-remove pigments).
4. Agitate for **20–40 min** at the bleaching temperature.
5. Cool to < 70 °C to prevent reversion of color.
6. Filter through **leaf filter** or **Niagara filter** to remove spent earth.

### 2.3.4 Adsorption Mechanism
Pigments and trace metals get **physically adsorbed** onto the negatively charged acidic sites of activated clay. Residual soap and peroxides are also adsorbed (this is why bleaching follows refining, not precedes it).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bleaching does NOT remove FFA or soap; it only removes pigments and traces of soap. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifies bleaching temperature at 90–110 °C and vacuum of 50–100 mbar.**

---

## 2.4 DEODORIZATION — Removal of Odor & Flavor Compounds

### 2.4.1 Objective
Remove **aldehydes, ketones, hydrocarbons, low-MW fatty acids, and other volatile odoriferous compounds** that cause off-flavor. Also achieves **thermal destruction of carotenoid residues** and reduces peroxides to near zero.

### 2.4.2 Operating Conditions (KTU Standard Values)
- **Temperature:** 220–260 °C
- **Pressure (Vacuum):** 2–5 mbar (i.e., 200–500 Pa) — critically important to prevent hydrolysis
- **Steam rate:** 1–3 % of oil weight per hour (live steam sparged through oil)
- **Residence time:** 60–120 min
- **Final product:** bland, light-colored, stable oil with PV < 1 meq/kg and FFA < 0.05 %

### 2.4.3 Process Principle (Steam Distillation)
At high temperature and low pressure, **Henry's Law** dictates that volatile compounds partition preferentially into the vapor phase. Saturated steam bubbles rising through hot oil **strip** these volatiles. The driving force is the **partial pressure gradient** of the volatile component between oil and steam.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Concept:** Deodorization is essentially a **steam distillation under high vacuum** — the lower the pressure, the lower the boiling points of the odoriferous compounds, and the gentler the thermal exposure for the oil.

### 2.4.4 Process Steps
1. Feed bleached oil through a **de-aerator** (vacuum + preheating).
2. Heat to **240–250 °C** using external heat exchangers (economizers) and a final heater.
3. Sparge **live steam** through the oil in a packed column.
4. Hold at temperature for **90–120 min**.
5. Cool oil to < 60 °C using heat recovery (counter-current with incoming oil).
6. Add **0.005–0.01 % citric acid** to chelate trace metals.
7. Polish-filter to < 1 µm.

### 2.4.5 Types of Deodorizers
- **Batch deodorizer** — small scale, old design.
- **Semi-continuous** (e.g., **Desmet Ballestra, Alfa Laval**) — most common in industry.
- **Continuous** (e.g., **Crown, Krupp**) — large capacity (> 500 TPD).
- **Thin-film deodorizer** (e.g., **Campro, Kortsch-Heron**) — short residence time (5–10 min) at 250–270 °C, ideal for heat-sensitive oils.

---

## 2.5 WINTERIZATION — Removal of High-Melting Waxes & Saturated TAGs

### 2.5.1 Objective
- **Dry fractionation** of oils to separate high-melting triglycerides (e.g., **stearin** in palm oil) and **waxes** (in sunflower, rice bran, corn oil) to obtain a clear, cold-stable liquid oil.

### 2.5.2 Process Principle
Slow, controlled cooling causes high-melting components to **crystallize** while lower-melting liquid oil remains uncrystallized. The two phases are separated by **filtration** (or centrifugation).

### 2.5.3 Process Steps
1. Heat oil to **60–70 °C** to erase crystal memory.
2. Cool slowly to the **crystallization temperature** (5–10 °C for waxes; 15–25 °C for stearin) at a rate of **1–2 °C/hour** (very slow to allow large, filterable crystals).
3. Hold (mature) the crystals for **6–12 hours** with gentle agitation.
4. Filter through a **membrane filter press** or **vacuum rotary drum filter**.
5. The filtrate = **winterized (cold-clear) oil**; the retentate = **stearin / wax cake**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic: "Heat-Erase → Cool-Slow → Hold-Mature → Filter-Fast"** — this is the four-step winterization process that examiners love to test.

### 2.5.4 Typical Yields
- **Sunflower oil wax removal:** yields 80–85 % winterized oil + 15–20 % wax.
- **Palm oil dry fractionation:** yields 70–75 % olein (liquid) + 25–30 % stearin (solid).

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they account for ~ 60 % of numerical questions in PEFTT748.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\%\,\text{FFA} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 28.2}{W}$ | Free Fatty Acid (as oleic acid) by titration | %, V in mL, W in g |
| 2 | $\text{Acid Value (AV)} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W}$ | mg KOH per g of oil | mg KOH/g |
| 3 | $\%\,\text{Refining Loss} = \%\,\text{FFA} \times f + \%\,\text{Gums} + 0.2\,\text{(empirical)}$ | Total refining loss (factor f ≈ 1.5–2.0 for neutralization) | % |
| 4 | $\text{Theoretical NaOH} = \dfrac{\%\,\text{FFA} \times W \times 40}{28.2 \times 1000}$ | Stoichiometric NaOH dose | kg |
| 5 | $\text{Practical NaOH} = T_N \times W$ | T_N = NaOH titre (%); 0.1–0.2 % excess is added | kg |
| 6 | $\text{Steam consumed} = \dfrac{S \times t}{W_o}$ | S = sparge rate (% oil/h), t = time (h), W_o = oil weight | unitless ratio |
| 7 | $\text{SV} = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 56.1}{W}$ | Saponification value | mg KOH/g |
| 8 | $\text{IV} = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 12.69}{W}$ | Iodine value (Wijs method) | g I₂/100 g |
| 9 | $\text{PV} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 1000}{W}$ | Peroxide value | meq O₂/kg |
| 10 | $\%\,\text{Color reduction} = \dfrac{C_i - C_f}{C_i} \times 100$ | Bleaching efficiency | % |

> **Where:** V = titrant volume (mL), N = normality, W = sample weight (g), V_b = blank titre, V_s = sample titre.

---

## 2.7 Industrial Flow Sequence (Master Diagram Concept)

The complete **refining flow** integrates all five stages:

```
CRUDE OIL  →  DEGUMMING  →  (Acid Pretreat)  →  ALKALI REFINING  →  WATER WASH  →  DRYING
   ↓                                                                                ↓
Gum Cake                                                                  (Water Washed Oil)
                                                                                     ↓
                                                                               BLEACHING
                                                                                     ↓
                                                                              Spent Earth
                                                                                     ↓
                                                                               DEODORIZATION
                                                                                     ↓
                                                                                RBD OIL
                                                                                     ↓
                                                                              WINTERIZATION
                                                                                     ↓
                                                                          Winterized (cold-clear) Oil
```
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Calculation of Theoretical NaOH Requirement

**Problem:** A batch of **soybean oil** weighing **W = 10,000 kg** has an **FFA content of 3.5 % (as oleic acid)**. Calculate the **theoretical mass of NaOH** required to neutralize the FFA.

### Step 1 — Molar mass conversions
- Oleic acid (C₁₇H₃₃COOH) = **282 g/mol** → hence factor 28.2 in the FFA formula (g/10 mL, derived from 282/10).
- NaOH = **40 g/mol**.

### Step 2 — Mass of oleic acid in the oil
$$\text{Mass of oleic acid} = \dfrac{3.5}{100} \times 10{,}000 = 350 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 3 — Moles of oleic acid
$$\text{Moles of oleic acid} = \dfrac{350 \times 1000 \text{ g}}{282 \text{ g/mol}} = 1241.1 \text{ mol}$$

### Step 3 — Stoichiometric reaction (1:1 mole ratio)
$$\text{R-COOH} + \text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{R-COONa} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
1 mole oleic acid : 1 mole NaOH.

$$\text{Moles NaOH required} = 1241.1 \text{ mol}$$

### Step 4 — Mass of NaOH
$$\text{Mass NaOH} = 1241.1 \times 40 \text{ g/mol} = 49{,}644 \text{ g} = 49.64 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 5 — Apply excess (industrial practice: 10–20 % excess)
With 15 % excess:
$$\text{Practical NaOH} = 49.64 \times 1.15 = 57.09 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 6 — Concentration adjustment
For a **12 °Bé NaOH solution** (≈ 8.5 % w/w), the volume required:
$$V = \dfrac{57.09}{0.085 \times 1.09 \text{ kg/L}} \approx 616 \text{ L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** **57.1 kg of pure NaOH (or ~ 616 L of 12 °Bé solution)** for 10,000 kg of soybean oil with 3.5 % FFA.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Refining Loss Calculation

**Problem:** A batch of **sunflower oil** has:
- FFA = **2.8 %**
- Gums (phosphatides) = **1.2 %**
- Moisture = **0.3 %**
- Insoluble impurities = **0.1 %**

Using the empirical **Hartman equation**:
$$\%\,\text{Refining Loss} = L_F + L_G + L_M + L_I + 0.2$$

Where:
- $L_F = 2 \times \%\,\text{FFA}$ (FFA trap loss in soapstock)
- $L_G = 1.25 \times \%\,\text{Gums}$ (gums coagulated with soap)
- $L_M = \%\,\text{Moisture}$
- $L_I = \%\,\text{Insolubles}$

### Step-by-step substitution
$$L_F = 2 \times 2.8 = 5.60\,\%$$
$$L_G = 1.25 \times 1.2 = 1.50\,\%$$
$$L_M = 0.30\,\%$$
$$L_I = 0.10\,\%$$

### Total Loss
$$\%\,\text{Refining Loss} = 5.60 + 1.50 + 0.30 + 0.10 + 0.20 = 7.70\,\%$$

### Yield of Refined Oil
$$\%\,\text{Yield} = 100 - 7.70 = 92.30\,\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For every 100 tonnes of crude sunflower oil, we obtain **92.3 tonnes of refined oil** and lose **7.7 tonnes** to soapstock, gums, moisture, and unsaponifiable matter.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Steam Consumption in Deodorization

**Problem:** A **semi-continuous deodorizer** processes **30,000 kg of bleached oil** per batch. Conditions: sparge steam rate = **2.0 % of oil weight per hour**, residence time = **2.5 h**. Calculate total live steam consumed.

### Step 1 — Steam flow rate
$$S = 2.0\% \times 30{,}000 = 600 \text{ kg/h}$$

### Step 2 — Total steam
$$\text{Total Steam} = S \times t = 600 \times 2.5 = 1500 \text{ kg per batch}$$

### Step 3 — Steam-to-oil ratio
$$\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{1500}{30{,}000} = 0.05 = 5.0\,\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry standard:** Live steam consumption in deodorization is **3–8 % of oil weight**. Anything above 10 % indicates poor vacuum or excessive stripping inefficiency.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Bleaching Earth Dosage & Color Reduction

**Problem:** A **palm oil** batch of 5,000 kg has initial red color of **12 Lovibond units (R)**. After bleaching with **1.5 % activated earth**, the final color is **3.0 R**. Calculate:
1. Color reduction (%)
2. Earth consumption (kg)

### Part (a) — Color reduction
$$\%\text{Reduction} = \dfrac{C_i - C_f}{C_i} \times 100 = \dfrac{12 - 3}{12} \times 100 = 75\,\%$$

### Part (b) — Earth required
$$\text{Earth (kg)} = 0.015 \times 5000 = 75 \text{ kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical dosages for KTU reference:** Soybean oil 0.5–1.0 %, Sunflower 0.5–1.5 %, Palm 1.0–2.0 %, Cottonseed 1.0–3.0 %.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU 2024 Board Table)

| Parameter | Degumming | Alkali Refining | Bleaching | Deodorization | Winterization |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| **Target impurity** | Phospholipids | FFA | Pigments, metals | Volatiles, FFA | Waxes, high-MP TAGs |
| **Main reagent/process** | Water/H₃PO₄ | NaOH | Activated clay | Steam + vacuum | Cold crystallization |
| **Temperature** | 60–80 °C | 50–80 °C | 90–110 °C | 220–260 °C | 5–25 °C |
| **Pressure** | Atmospheric | Atmospheric | Vacuum (50–100 mbar) | High vacuum (2–5 mbar) | Atmospheric |
| **Time** | 15–30 min | 30–60 min | 20–40 min | 60–120 min | 6–12 h |
| **By-product** | Gum cake | Soapstock | Spent earth | Distillate (TOC) | Stearin / wax cake |
| **Order in flow** | 1st | 2nd | 3rd | 4th | 5th (post-RBD) |
| **Equipment** | Centrifuge | Neutralizer + separator | Leaf filter | Deodorizer column | Membrane filter press |
| **Yield loss** | 0.5–3 % | 5–10 % (Hartman) | 0.5–1.0 % | 0.5–1.0 % | 15–30 % |

---

## 3.6 KTU Critical Mass-Balance Equation Set

For a **complete batch refining** of W_crude kg oil:

$$W_{\text{RBD oil}} = W_{\text{crude}} - W_{\text{gums}} - W_{\text{soap}} - W_{\text{earth}} - W_{\text{distillate}}$$

$$\eta_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{RBD}}}{W_{\text{crude}}} \times 100$$

**Typical target efficiency:** η_overall = **90–94 %** for chemical refining; **94–97 %** for physical refining.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Refining Flow Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crude Vegetable Oil] --> B[Degumming Reactor]
    B --> B1[Gum Cake Byproduct]
    B --> C[Acid Pretreat H3PO4]
    C --> D[Alkali Neutralizer NaOH]
    D --> D1[Soapstock Byproduct]
    D --> E[Centrifugal Separator]
    E --> F[Water Wash Section]
    F --> G[Vacuum Drier]
    G --> H[Bleacher Activated Earth]
    H --> H1[Spent Earth Cake]
    H --> I[Filter Press]
    I --> J[Deaerator Vacuum]
    J --> K[Deodorizer 240C 3 mbar]
    K --> K1[Distillate TOC]
    K --> L[Heat Recovery Cooler]
    L --> M[Citric Acid Addition 0.01%]
    M --> N[Polish Filter 1 microm]
    N --> O[RBD Oil Storage]
    O --> P[Winterization Tank]
    P --> P1[Stearin or Wax Cake]
    P --> Q[Winterized Cold Clear Oil]

    classDef process fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000000
    classDef byproduct fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000000
    classDef product fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000000

    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J,K,L,M,N,O,P process
    class B1,D1,H1,K1,P1 byproduct
    class Q product
```

## 4.2 Degumming Process — Detailed Sub-Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Crude Oil] --> B[Preheater 60 to 80 C]
    B --> C[Reactor with High Shear Mixer]
    D[Water 2 to 3%] --> C
    E[H3PO4 0.05 to 0.1%] --> C
    C --> F[Gum Hydration and Precipitation]
    F --> G[Centrifuge 6000 rpm]
    G --> H[Gum Sludge Outlet]
    G --> I[Degummed Oil]
    I --> J[Vacuum Drier]
    J --> K[Dried Degummed Oil Storage]
    H --> L[Gum Drying and Lecithin Recovery]
    L --> M[Lecithin Product or Gum Cake]

    classDef process fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000000
    classDef product fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000000
    classDef waste fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000000
    class A,B,C,F,G,I,J,K process
    class M product
    class H,L waste
```

## 4.3 Alkali Refining — Reaction & Separation Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Degummed Oil] --> B[Acid Pretreat 0.1% H3PO4]
    B --> C[NaOH Addition 8 to 14 Be]
    C --> D[Neutralization 50 to 70 C 15 min]
    D --> E[Soapstock Formation]
