# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Define degumming. List the two types of phospholipids removed during degumming with one example each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Degumming is the first step in vegetable oil refining, wherein phospholipids (gums) are removed from crude oil by hydration with water or treatment with phosphoric acid, followed by centrifugal separation.

> **Types of phospholipids (2 marks):**
> 1. **Hydratable phospholipids** — e.g., **Phosphatidylcholine (PC)** and **Phosphatidylethanolamine (PE)**. These contain a free polar head group and are easily hydrated by water alone.
> 2. **Non-hydratable phospholipids** — e.g., **Phosphatidic acid (PA)** and **Ca/Mg salts of PE**. These require **phosphoric or citric acid pretreatment** to liberate them into hydratable form.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"What is the principle of deodorization? State any two operating conditions."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Principle (2 marks):** Deodorization operates on the principle of **steam distillation under high vacuum**. Volatile odoriferous compounds (aldehydes, ketones, low-MW FFA) have significantly higher vapour pressure under deep vacuum (2–5 mbar) and elevated temperature (220–260 °C). Live steam sparged through the hot oil strips these volatiles which are then condensed and removed. **Henry's Law** governs the partition of volatiles between oil and steam phases.

> **Operating conditions (1 mark):** 
> 1. Temperature: 220–260 °C
> 2. Pressure: 2–5 mbar (high vacuum)
> 3. Live steam rate: 1–3 % of oil weight per hour
> 4. Residence time: 60–120 minutes

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]**

**"Explain in detail the chemical refining process of vegetable oil. Discuss (a) acid pretreatment, (b) alkali neutralization, and (c) the calculation of refining loss using the Hartman equation. A batch of 8000 kg crude soybean oil contains 4.0 % FFA, 1.5 % gums, 0.2 % moisture, and 0.1 % insolubles. Calculate the yield of refined oil."**

**Model Answer:**

#### Introduction (2 marks)
Chemical (alkali) refining is the conventional method for oils rich in hydratable phospholipids (soybean, sunflower, groundnut). The objective is to **neutralize FFA** with **NaOH** to form **soapstock** (RCOONa), which is separated by **centrifugation**, followed by water washing and vacuum drying.

#### (a) Acid Pretreatment (3 marks)
- The degummed oil is treated with **0.05–0.1 % H₃PO₄** (or 0.1–0.2 % citric acid) at 60–80 °C for 15 min.
- Purpose: 
  1. Precipitate **residual non-hydratable phospholipids** (Ca/Mg salts of PA, PE) as heavy sludge.
  2. **Chelate trace metals** (Fe, Cu) that catalyze oxidation.
  3. **Convert soaps** formed in any prior step into free fatty acids for easier separation.

#### (b) Alkali Neutralization (5 marks)
**Process Steps:**
1. Heat oil to **50–70 °C** in a neutralizer vessel with mechanical agitation.
2. Add **calculated quantity of NaOH solution (8–14 °Bé, ~ 5–10 % w/w)**. Theoretical NaOH is calculated as:
   $$\text{NaOH (kg)} = \dfrac{\%\text{FFA} \times W \times 40}{28.2 \times 1000} \times \text{excess factor (1.10–1.20)}$$
3. Mix for **15–20 min** to allow:
   - **Neutralization:** R-COOH + NaOH → R-COONa + H₂O
   - **Saponification of partial glycerides** (mono-, di-glycerides).
4. Heat to **75–80 °C** to break emulsion and coalesce soap droplets.
5. Pass through **centrifugal separator** (6000–8000 rpm) → soapstock + refined oil.
6. **Wash** the oil with **10–20 % hot soft water (85–90 °C)** to remove residual soap.
7. **Vacuum dry** the washed oil at 90–100 °C, 3–5 kPa to < 0.1 % moisture.

**Role of NaOH concentration:**
- **Low concentration (5–8 °Bé):** Preferred for oils with high FFA — less emulsification.
- **High concentration (12–16 °Bé):** For low FFA oils — sharper separation, less oil entrapment.

#### (c) Refining Loss Calculation — Hartman Equation (4 marks)
The **Hartman formula** estimates total refining loss:

$$\%\,\text{Refining Loss (RL)} = 2(\%\text{FFA}) + 1.25(\%\text{Gums}) + \%\,\text{Moisture} + \%\,\text{Insolubles} + 0.2$$

Given:
- FFA = 4.0 %
- Gums = 1.5 %
- Moisture = 0.2 %
- Insolubles = 0.1 %

**Step-by-step substitution:**
$$L_F = 2 \times 4.0 = 8.00\,\%$$
$$L_G = 1.25 \times 1.5 = 1.875\,\%$$
$$L_M = 0.20\,\%$$
$$L_I = 0.10\,\%$$
$$L_{\text{empirical}} = 0.20\,\%$$

$$\%\,\text{RL} = 8.00 + 1.875 + 0.20 + 0.10 + 0.20 = 10.375\,\% \approx 10.38\,\%$$

**Yield of Refined Oil:**
$$\%\text{Yield} = 100 - 10.38 = 89.62\,\%$$

$$\text{Refined oil (kg)} = 8000 \times 0.8962 = 7169.6 \text{ kg}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** The **yield of refined oil is 89.62 %**, corresponding to **7169.6 kg** of refined oil from 8000 kg of crude soybean oil. **Total loss = 830.4 kg** (soapstock + gums + moisture + insolubles + empirical).

---

### **Question B (Alternative): [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**"Describe the bleaching and deodorization steps in oil refining. Discuss the (a) mechanism of pigment adsorption, (b) process parameters, and (c) the role of vacuum and steam in deodorization. Calculate the steam consumption for a 25,000 kg batch of bleached oil processed at 2.5 % steam rate for 2 hours."**

**Model Answer:**

#### Introduction to Bleaching & Deodorization (2 marks)
**Bleaching** is the third refining stage where pigments, residual soap, and trace metals are adsorbed onto **activated bleaching earth** under vacuum. **Deodorization** is the final physico-chemical step where volatile odoriferous matter and residual FFA are stripped by **live steam** at 220–260 °C under high vacuum (2–5 mbar).

#### (a) Mechanism of Pigment Adsorption (3 marks)
Activated bleaching earth (acid-treated bentonite) is a **negatively charged, highly porous** material (surface area 200–400 m²/g). The mechanism involves:
1. **Physical adsorption (physisorption):** Pigment molecules (carotenoids, chlorophylls) are held by **van der Waals forces** on the clay surface.
2. **Chemisorption:** Some pigments and metal complexes bind to acidic sites (Brønsted/Lewis) on the activated clay.
3. **Ion exchange:** Trace metals (Fe²⁺, Cu²⁺) replace H⁺/Al³⁺ in the clay lattice, removing pro-oxidant metals.
4. **Capillary condensation:** Pigments get trapped in the clay's micro-pores.

The overall driving force is the **concentration gradient** of pigments between oil phase and clay surface (analogous to Langmuir isotherm).

#### (b) Bleaching Process Parameters (3 marks)
- **Earth dosage:** 0.5–2.0 % of oil weight (palm and cottonseed require higher).
- **Temperature:** 90–110 °C (higher than 120 °C → oil darkens; lower than 80 °C → incomplete adsorption).
- **Vacuum:** 50–100 mbar (prevents oil oxidation during bleaching).
- **Contact time:** 20–40 min with gentle agitation.
- **Filtration:** Leaf filter or pressure filter at < 70 °C.
- **Optional:** 5–10 % activated carbon on top of earth for stubborn pigments (e.g., gossypol in cottonseed).

#### (c) Role of Vacuum & Steam in Deodorization (3 marks)
- **Vacuum (2–5 mbar):** Lowers the **boiling points** of odoriferous compounds (which would otherwise thermally damage oil at 250+ °C). It also prevents hydrolysis of triglycerides by stripping water.
- **Steam (live sparging, 1–3 %/h):** Provides the **stripping vapor** that carries volatile compounds from oil to condenser. Without steam, the partial pressure of volatiles in the gas phase would be too low to drive mass transfer.
- **Henry's Law application:** At constant T, the gas-phase concentration of a volatile = $P_i = x_i \times \gamma_i \times P_i^{\text{sat}}$. Vacuum minimizes $P_i^{\text{sat}}$, making steam efficient.

#### (d) Numerical — Steam Consumption Calculation (3 marks)

**Given:**
- Oil weight, $W_o = 25{,}000$ kg
- Sparge steam rate, $S = 2.5\% = 0.025$ of oil per hour
- Time, $t = 2$ h

**Step 1 — Steam flow rate:**
$$\text{Steam flow} = S \times W_o = 0.025 \times 25{,}000 = 625 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Total steam consumed per batch:**
$$\text{Total steam} = 625 \text{ kg/h} \times 2 \text{ h} = 1250 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Steam-to-oil ratio:**
$$\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{1250}{25{,}000} = 0.05 = 5.0\,\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** Total live steam consumption = **1250 kg per batch** (5.0 % of oil weight), which lies within the industry standard of **3–8 %**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes that Lead to Mark Deductions in PEFTT748:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing FFA & Acid Value formulas:** Many students write the same equation for both. FFA is in % (as oleic acid, factor 28.2), while Acid Value is in mg KOH/g (factor 56.1). **Loss of 1 mark** for confusion.
> 2. **Wrong units in FFA formula:** "V" must be in **mL**, "N" is **normality**, "W" is **grams of sample**. **Loss of 0.5 mark** for missing units.
> 3. **Forgetting the 0.2 % empirical term in Hartman equation:** Students often omit it. **Loss of 1 mark**.
> 4. **Wrong bleaching temperature range:** Some write 150–200 °C. Correct is **90–110 °C** — above 120 °C, the oil darkens (reversion). **Loss of 1 mark**.
> 5. **Mixing up physical & chemical refining:** Physical refining uses **steam only** (no NaOH); chemical uses **NaOH + centrifuge**. **Loss of 2 marks** for KTU long answers.
> 6. **Skipping the FFA calculation in alkali refining problems:** Always show the **theoretical NaOH dose** calculation in full — 1 mark is reserved for this step alone.
> 7. **Forgetting acid pretreatment in degumming:** Non-hydratable gums are **invisible to water**; examiners look for the **H₃PO₄** step. **Loss of 1 mark**.
> 8. **Confusing winterization (wax removal) with fractionation (TAG separation):** Winterization = cold filter for wax clarity; Dry fractionation = stearin/olein separation in palm. **Loss of 1 mark** if confused.
> 9. **Not citing conditions in deodorization:** Must mention **T = 220–260 °C, P = 2–5 mbar, steam = 1–3 %/h, time = 60–120 min** — all four.

---

## 5.4 KTU Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as your last-minute revision checklist before the KTU exam.**

### ✅ Core Refining Sequence (Memorize in Order)
**Crude Oil → Degumming → Acid Pretreat → Alkali Refining → Washing → Drying → Bleaching → Deodorization → (Optional) Winterization**

### ✅ Critical Numbers to Memorize
- FFA formula factor: **28.2** (oleic acid); AV factor: **56.1** (KOH)
- Degumming temperature: **60–80 °C**; water dose: **2–3 %**; H₃PO₄ dose: **0.05–0.1 %**
- Bleaching temperature: **90–110 °C**; vacuum: **50–100 mbar**; earth dose: **0.5–2.0 %**
- Deodorization: **T = 220–260 °C, P = 2–5 mbar, Steam = 1–3 %/h, Time = 60–120 min**
- Winterization: **Cool at 1–2 °C/h**, mature **6–12 h**, filter at **5–15 °C**
- Hartman empirical constant: **0.2 %** (don't forget!)
- NaOH excess: **10–20 %** over theoretical

### ✅ By-Products (Frequently Tested)
- **Gum cake** (degumming) → lecithin source
- **Soapstock** (alkali refining) → acid oil (after H₂SO₄ acidulation)
- **Spent earth** (bleaching) → 20–30 % oil, environmental concern
- **Deodorizer distillate (TOC)** → rich in tocopherols, sterols, squalene
- **Stearin / wax cake** (winterization) → margarine / oleochemistry feedstock

### ✅ "Match the Following" Type (Common KTU Question)
| Refining Stage | Reagent / Process |
|---|---|
| Degumming | Water + H₃PO₄ |
| Acid refining | NaOH (caustic soda) |
| Bleaching | Activated earth |
| Deodorization | Steam + high vacuum |
| Winterization | Cold crystallization |

### ✅ Common Confusions Cleared
- **Refining ≠ Hydrogenation** (refining purifies, hydrogenation saturates)
- **Degumming ≠ Neutralization** (degumming removes gums, neutralization removes FFA)
- **Bleaching ≠ Hydrogen bleaching** (bleaching uses adsorbents, NOT chemicals to whiten)
- **Winterization ≠ Fractionation** (winterization = wax removal for clarity; fractionation = TAG segregation for functionality)

### ✅ Final KTU Exam Tip
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For long answers, always structure your response in 4 parts: (1) Definition/Principle, (2) Process Steps with T & P, (3) Equipment/Chemical Reactions, (4) Numerical/Industrial Application.** KTU examiners reward **structured answers with sub-headings and neat labeled diagrams** — partial marks are awarded for every correct sub-point.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogenation of Fats: Production of Vanaspati, Margarine, Ghee, and Butter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT748 - FAT AND OIL PROCESSING TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Hydrogenation of Fats: Production of Vanaspati, Margarine, Ghee, and Butter # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Hydrogenation of Fats — Formal Definition **Hydrogenation** is a heterogeneous catalytic chemical process in which molecular hydrogen ($H_2$) is added across the carbon–carbon double bonds ($C=C$) of unsaturated fatty acid chains of triglycerides, converting liquid oils into semi-solid or solid fats. Industrially, the reaction is carried out at elevated temperatures (**150–225 °C**) and pressures (**1–10 atm**) in the presence of a finely divided transition metal catalyst — most commonly **nickel (Ni)**, sometimes supplemented with **copper, palladium, or platinum**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The hydrogenation reaction is the foundational process that produces **Vanaspati, Margarine, and Shortenings**, while **Ghee and Butter** are dairy-derived products where the structural arrangement of fat (and the elimination of water/lactose) is the central technology, not catalytic hydrogenation. The simplified stoichiometry of partial hydrogenation of a polyunsaturated triglyceride (taking linoleic acid as the model substrate) is: $$ \text{Triglyceride-(CH=CH-CH=CH)-CH}_3 \; + \; H_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Ni, }\Delta} \; \text{Triglyceride-(CH}_2\text{-CH=CH-CH}_2\text{)-CH}_3 $$ The double bonds are saturated sequentially — dienes → monoenes → saturated fatty acids. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Sponge for Hydrogen" Picture Imagine the unsaturated oil molecule as a **sponge with multiple open holes** (the C=C double bonds). Each hole is a hungry site that can swallow one hydrogen atom. When you bubble **hydrogen gas** through hot oil containing suspended **finely powdered nickel (acting as a matchmaker)**, the nickel grabs a hydrogen molecule, splits it, and helps the hydrogen atoms plug the holes in the oil. - **More holes closed** = more saturated = **harder fat** (e.g., Vanaspati, margarine) - **Fewer holes closed** = more unsaturated = **softer fat / liquid oil** (e.g., salad oil) The "holes" are technically called **double bonds**, and the "matchmaker" is the **catalyst surface**. This picture is the intuitive basis for understanding **selectivity** — not all holes fill at the same rate; the nickel "matchmaker" prefers certain types of holes over others. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Metric to Remember:** The degree of unsaturation is measured by the **Iodine Value (IV)** in units of **g I₂ / 100 g fat**. Hydrogenation lowers the IV. A typical soybean oil (~IV 130) becomes vanaspati-grade fat at IV ≈ 60–70. --- ## 1.3 The Four End-Products at a Glance | Product | Category | Key Process | Texture | |---|---|---|---| | **Vanaspati** | Hydrogenated vegetable fat | Catalytic hydrogenation of edible oils | Semi-solid, granular | | **Margarine** | Water-in-oil (W/O) emulsion | Hydrogenated fat + aqueous phase + emulsification | Soft, spreadable | | **Ghee** | Clarified butter fat | Heat clarification of butter | Granular, golden liquid | | **Butter** | W/O emulsion from dairy | Churning of cream | Semi-solid spread | > [!IMPORTANT] > The *physical state* and *functional properties* (melting point, plasticity, oxidative stability) of all four depend on the **fatty acid composition** and the **solid fat content (SFC)** profile — both of which are engineered via hydrogenation (for Vanaspati/Margarine) or selected naturally (for Butter/Ghee). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Hydrogenation Reaction — Step-by-Step Mechanism The accepted **Horiuti-Polanyi mechanism** (1954) for heterogeneous catalytic hydrogenation on a nickel surface proceeds through these stages: **Step 1 — Diffusion of H₂ to the Catalyst Surface** Hydrogen gas dissolves in the oil and diffuses to the nickel surface. This is often the **rate-limiting step** in industrial reactors. $$ H_{2(\text{gas})} \rightleftharpoons H_{2(\text{dissolved in oil})} \xrightarrow{\text{diffusion}} H_{2(\text{adsorbed on Ni})} $$ **Step 2 — Dissociative Chemisorption of H₂** On the Ni(111) crystal facet, the H–H bond cleaves and two hydrogen atoms bind to adjacent nickel sites (denoted *): $$ H_2 + 2\text{Ni}^* \longrightarrow 2\text{Ni-H}^* $$ **Step 3 — Adsorption of the Unsaturated C=C** The double bond of the fatty acid chemisorbs onto the nickel surface: $$ R\text{-}CH=CH\text{-}R' + 2\text{Ni}^* \longrightarrow R\text{-}CH^\#\text{-}CH^\#\text{-}R' $$ where the `#` symbol denotes a half-bonded (π-bonded) attachment to the catalyst. **Step 4 — Sequential Hydrogen Atom Transfer** Two surface H atoms add to the adsorbed C=C, one at a time: $$ R\text{-}CH^\#\text{-}CH^\#\text{-}R' + \text{Ni-H}^* \longrightarrow R\text{-}CH_2\text{-}CH^\#\text{-}R' + \text{Ni}^* $$ $$ R\text{-}CH_2\text{-}CH^\#\text{-}R' + \text{Ni-H}^* \longrightarrow R\text{-}CH_2\text{-}CH_2\text{-}R' + \text{Ni}^* $$ **Step 5 — Desorption of the Saturated Product** The now-saturated fatty acid chain desorbs from the nickel, freeing active sites for the next cycle. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in engineering:** Step 1 (mass transfer) is why industrial hydrogenators use **agitated, gas-sparged reactors** with high interfacial area. If the stirrer speed is too low, the reaction becomes **mass-transfer limited** and selectivity is poor (trans-isomer-rich product). --- ## 2.2 Selectivity of Hydrogenation — A Critical Industrial Concept Industrial hydrogenation is **never fully selective**; it cannot convert only linolenic → linoleic → oleic without touching oleic. The **selectivity ratio (SR)** quantifies this: $$ SR = \frac{k_{\text{linolenic}}}{k_{\text{oleic}}} $$ where $k$ is the pseudo-first-order rate constant for each fatty acid class. | SR Value | Behaviour | Application | |---|---|---| | **SR > 8** (high selectivity) | Linolenic acid hydrogenated first, oleic preserved | Soft, salad-grade oil | | **SR ≈ 1** (low selectivity) | All unsaturations reduce uniformly | Vanaspati / margarine base stock | > [!IMPORTANT] > **High temperatures and high catalyst loadings lower SR** (favours non-selective hydrogenation), while **low temperatures and low pressures raise SR**. This is why Vanaspati is made at **~180 °C, 2–3 atm, 0.1–0.2% Ni**. --- ## 2.3 Trans-Isomer Formation — The "Side Effect" Because the half-hydrogenated intermediate is **chemisorbed on a flat metal surface**, the C–C single bond can rotate before the second H adds. The product becomes the more thermodynamically stable **trans (E) isomer** rather than the natural **cis (Z) isomer**. $$ \text{cis-oleic (Z)} \xrightarrow{H_2, \, Ni, \, \Delta} \text{trans-elaidic (E)} \xrightarrow{H_2} \text{stearic (saturated)} $$ Trans fats are nutritionally undesirable (↑ LDL, ↓ HDL), which is why **FSSAI (India) caps trans fat in Vanaspati at ≤ 5%** and several countries have moved toward **interesterification** as an alternative. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Equation Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Use \vert instead of | in tables** to avoid markdown table breaking. | # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Units / Boundary Conditions | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Iodine Value (IV) | $IV = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 12.69}{W}$ | g I₂ / 100 g fat; W = sample weight (g), N = normality of Na₂S₂O₃ | | 2 | Saponification Value (SV) | $SV = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 56.1}{W}$ | mg KOH / g fat; indicates mean MW of fatty acids | | 3 | Acid Value (AV) | $AV = \dfrac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W}$ | mg KOH / g fat; measures free fatty acids | | 4 | Selectivity Ratio | $SR = k_{\text{linolenic}} / k_{\text{oleic}}$ | Dimensionless; SR > 1 = selective | | 5 | Rate of Hydrogenation (pseudo-1st order) | $-\dfrac{dC}{dt} = k \cdot C \cdot P_{H_2}$ | C = unsaturation concentration, P = H₂ partial pressure | | 6 | Solid Fat Content (SFC) by NMR | $SFC(\%) = \dfrac{\text{Solid signal}}{\text{Total fat signal}} \times 100$ | Measured at 10 °C, 20 °C, 30 °C, 40 °C | | 7 | Melting Point (Slip Point) | Determined by open capillary method | °C; reflects the final melting of the triglyceride matrix | | 8 | Trans-Isomer Content | Measured by **FTIR** (peak at 968 cm⁻¹) or **GC-FAME** | % of total fatty acids | | 9 | Reichert-Meissl Value (RMV) | Volatile soluble fatty acids | mL of 0.1 N KOH per 5 g fat (butter indicator) | | 10 | Polenske Value (PV) | Volatile insoluble fatty acids | mL of 0.1 N KOH per 5 g fat (coconut fat indicator) | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Food Industry:** Engineered plastic fats for **bakery shortenings, puff pastry, frying media**. - **Oleochemistry:** Production of **stearic acid** for soaps, candles, and PVC stabilizers. - **Dairy Industry:** Direct emulsification technology for margarine and spreads. - **Nutritional Regulation:** Drives the modern shift toward **zero-trans** hydrogenation (electrochemical, biological, or interesterification routes). - **Catalysis Research:** Hydrogenation of fats is a classical **model reaction** for studying supported metal catalysts (Ni/SiO₂, Ni/Al₂O₃, Pd/C). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Iodine Value Reduction During Hydrogenation The **Iodine Value (IV)** is the most widely used single metric to monitor hydrogenation progress. Let us derive how the rate of IV change is connected to the underlying reaction kinetics. ### Starting Assumptions 1. Hydrogenation follows **pseudo-first-order kinetics** with respect to the unsaturation concentration. 2. H₂ concentration is approximately constant (large excess under pressure). 3. Catalyst activity is constant (no poisoning in the early stages). ### Step A — Rate Law $$ -\frac{dC_{C=C}}{dt} = k' \cdot C_{C=C} $$ where $C_{C=C}$ is the molar concentration of double bonds and $k'$ is the pseudo-first-order rate constant (units: min⁻¹). ### Step B — Separation and Integration $$ \frac{dC_{C=C}}{C_{C=C}} = -k' \, dt $$ $$ \int_{C_0}^{C_t} \frac{dC_{C=C}}{C_{C=C}} = -\int_{0}^{t} k' \, dt $$ ### Step C — Resulting Exponential Decay $$ \ln\!\left(\frac{C_t}{C_0}\right) = -k' t \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_t = C_0 \, e^{-k' t} $$ ### Step D — Connecting Concentration to Iodine Value Since each C=C consumes **one mole of I₂** in the Wijs titration, the Iodine Value is directly proportional to the double-bond concentration: $$ \frac{IV_t}{IV_0} = \frac{C_t}{C_0} = e^{-k' t} $$ ### Step E — Final Integrated Form (Universally Used Form) $$ \boxed{IV_t = IV_0 \cdot e^{-k' t}} $$ **Interpretation for KTU students:** - A plot of $\ln(IV_t)$ vs. $t$ is a **straight line** for first-order hydrogenation. The slope gives $-k'$. - If the plot **curves** (downward deviation at long times), it signals **catalyst deactivation** or **mass-transfer limitation**. ### Step F — Worked Numerical Example A batch of soybean oil ($IV_0 = 132$) is hydrogenated. After 40 minutes, $IV_t = 88$. Calculate the rate constant and the IV after 90 minutes. **Solution:** $$ k' = -\frac{1}{t} \ln\!\left(\frac{IV_t}{IV_0}\right) = -\frac{1}{40} \ln\!\left(\frac{88}{132}\right) $$ $$ = -\frac{1}{40} \times \ln(0.6667) = -\frac{1}{40} \times (-0.4055) = 0.01014 \; \text{min}^{-1} $$ $$ IV_{90} = 132 \times e^{-0.01014 \times 90} = 132 \times e^{-0.9126} = 132 \times 0.4014 = 53.0 $$ **Result:** $IV_{90} \approx 53$, which is in the **vanaspati specification range (60 ± 5)**, suggesting the batch would have crossed the target IV slightly before 90 min and should be stopped around $t \approx 75$–80 min. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Properties of the Four End-Products | Property | Vanaspati | Margarine | Ghee | Butter | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Source** | Hydrogenated vegetable oil | Hydrogenated fat + aqueous phase | Clarified butter | Churned cream | | **Moisture (%)** | $\leq 0.25$ | 14–18 | $\leq 0.3$ | 15–18 | | **Iodine Value** | 55–70 | 70–90 | 25–40 | 30–40 | | **Melting Point (°C)** | 36–40 | 32–36 | 32–38 | 28–34 | | **SFC @ 20 °C (%)** | 30–45 | 18–25 | 40–55 | 22–30 | | **Free Fatty Acids (%)** | $\leq 0.25$ (as oleic) | $\leq 0.1$ | $\leq 2.0$ (as oleic) | $\leq 0.6$ | | **Texture** | Granular | Smooth, plastic | Granular + liquid oil | Semi-solid | | **Shelf Life** | 6–12 months | 2–3 months | 6–9 months (no refrigeration) | 1–2 months (refrigerated) | | **Type of Emulsion** | None (anhydrous) | W/O (water-in-oil) | None (anhydrous) | W/O (water-in-oil) | | **Key Process Variable** | H₂ pressure, temp, Ni dose | Emulsification rate, cooling | Heat clarification temperature (110–120 °C) | Churning time, cream fat % | --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Mass Balance on a Batch Hydrogenator Consider a batch reactor of volume $V_R$ (L) loaded with oil of mass $m_{oil}$ (kg). The initial number of double bonds is: $$ N_0 = \frac{m_{oil} \times IV_0}{2 \times 127 \times 1000} \quad \text{(in moles, using molar mass of I}_2 = 254 \text{ g/mol)} $$ The hydrogen required for complete saturation to IV = 0 would be: $$ n_{H_2, \text{total}} = N_0 \quad \text{(moles, since 1 H₂ saturates 1 C=C)} $$ For partial hydrogenation to a target $IV_t$: $$ n_{H_2, \text{used}} = \frac{m_{oil} \times (IV_0 - IV_t)}{25400} \quad \text{(mol)} $$ $$ V_{H_2, \text{STP}} = n_{H_2, \text{used}} \times 22.4 \quad \text{(L at STP)} $$ **Example:** 1000 kg soybean oil, $IV_0 = 132$, target $IV_t = 65$. $$ n_{H_2} = \frac{1000 \times (132 - 65)}{25400} = \frac{67000}{25400} = 2.638 \; \text{kmol} $$ $$ V_{H_2, \text{STP}} = 2.638 \times 10^3 \times 22.4 = 59{,}100 \; \text{L} \; \approx \; 59.1 \; \text{m}^3 \text{ of } H_2 \text{ at STP} $$ > [!NOTE] > This explains why industrial hydrogenators operate under **pressure** — to dissolve enough H₂ in the oil for the stoichiometric requirement. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Industrial Batch Hydrogenation Process ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["Raw Oil
(Refined, Bleached)"] node2["Pre-Heater
120-150 C"] node3["Catalyst Slurry
Preparation Tank
(Ni on SiO2)"] node4["Hydrogen Compressor
+ Surge Vessel"] node5["CONVERTER REACTOR
Stirred Batch
180-200 C, 2-3 atm"] node6["Drop Tank /
Catalyst Filter"] node7["Deodorizer

Lecithin, Mono-di-glycerides"] A4["Vit A and D

State two factors that affect it.** **Model Answer:** The **Selectivity Ratio (SR)** is defined as the ratio of the pseudo-first-order rate constant of hydrogenation of a more unsaturated fatty acid (typically linolenic) to that of a less unsaturated fatty acid (typically oleic): $$ SR = \frac{k_{\text{linolenic}}}{k_{\text{oleic}}} $$ A higher SR means the catalyst preferentially hydrogenates polyunsaturates first while preserving monounsaturates. **Two factors affecting SR:** 1. **Reaction temperature:** Higher temperatures decrease SR (less selective). 2. **Hydrogen pressure:** Higher H₂ pressure increases SR (more selective). 3. (Bonus) Catalyst type and loading also influence SR. *(Examiner's note: 3 marks; 1 for definition + 2 for any two factors.)* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between Ghee and Vanaspati in three points.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Ghee | Vanaspati | |---|---|---| | **Source** | Derived from butter / cream (dairy) | Derived from hydrogenated vegetable oils | | **Process** | Heat clarification of butter (no chemical reaction) | Catalytic hydrogenation with Ni at high temperature | | **Composition** | Contains natural butyric acid (C4:0) and conjugated linoleic acid | No short-chain fatty acids; contains trans isomers | | **Aroma** | Distinct lactone / carbonyl aroma from Maillard-like reactions | Bland, with added vanaspati flavour (coumarin-like) | | **Cost & Use** | Premium, traditional Indian fat | Economical, mass-market cooking medium | --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** For hydrogenation questions, students often (a) confuse **Horiuti–Polanyi mechanism** with simple addition, (b) write "hydrogenation saturates all double bonds" (forgetting partial hydrogenation & selectivity), and (c) skip the **trans-isomer** side-product. All three cost 2 marks each. Always mention catalyst, temperature range, **partial** nature, and trans-isomer formation. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Explain in detail the **Horiuti–Polanyi mechanism** of catalytic hydrogenation of oils on a nickel surface. (7 marks) **(b)** Describe the **industrial production process of Vanaspati** with a flow diagram, specifying the raw material, catalyst, and key quality parameters. (7 marks) #### Model Solution for (a) The Horiuti–Polanyi mechanism (proposed in 1954) is the universally accepted **heterogeneous catalytic mechanism** for the hydrogenation of unsaturated fatty acids on metal surfaces (Ni, Pd, Pt). **Stage 1 — Dissociative Chemisorption of H₂** Molecular hydrogen adsorbs on the metal surface and dissociates into two chemisorbed H atoms: $$ H_2 + 2\text{Ni}^* \longrightarrow 2\text{Ni-H}^* $$ **Stage 2 — π-Complexation of the C=C Bond** The C=C double bond of the fatty acid forms a **π-complex** with two adjacent Ni atoms, leaving the geometry of the alkene partially intact: $$ R\text{-}CH=CH\text{-}R' + 2\text{Ni}^* \longrightarrow R\text{-}CH^\#\text{-}CH^\#\text{-}R' $$ **Stage 3 — First Hydrogen Transfer (Half-Hydrogenation)** A chemisorbed H atom migrates to one of the half-bonded carbons. This is the **rate-determining step** in many kinetic regimes: $$ R\text{-}CH^\#\text{-}CH^\#\text{-}R' + \text{Ni-H}^* \longrightarrow R\text{-}CH_2\text{-}CH^\#\text{-}R' + \text{Ni}^* $$ **Stage 4 — Free Rotation of the Half-Hydrogenated Intermediate** The half-hydrogenated alkyl group is now singly bonded to the surface. The C–C single bond can rotate freely, allowing the second H to add from either face. This is the structural origin of **cis-trans isomerism**: - Adding H from the **same face** (cis-addition) → cis (Z) isomer retained - Adding H from the **opposite face** (trans-addition) → trans (E) isomer formed **Stage 5 — Second Hydrogen Transfer & Desorption** The second H atom adds, completing saturation. The now-saturated chain desorbs from the surface, regenerating two active sites: $$ R\text{-}CH_2\text{-}CH^\#\text{-}R' + \text{Ni-H}^* \longrightarrow R\text{-}CH_2\text{-}CH_2\text{-}R' + 2\text{Ni}^* $$ > [!NOTE] > The asterisks (*) represent **active surface sites**. The Ni surface is regenerated at the end of each catalytic cycle, which is why Ni is a true catalyst and is consumed in only trace quantities (a few ppm end up in the finished product and must be removed by post-hydrogenation filtration). #### Model Solution for (b) **Raw Material:** Refined, bleached, deodorized (RBD) edible vegetable oil — typically **cottonseed, groundnut, soybean, sunflower, palm, or rice bran** (single oil or blend). **Catalyst:** **Nickel-on-silica support (Ni/SiO₂)** at 0.05–0.2% Ni on oil weight, or **nickel formate** reduced in situ. **Process Steps:** 1. **Oil Pre-treatment:** RBD oil is deaerated under vacuum to remove dissolved O₂ (which would poison the catalyst and cause oxidation). 2. **Catalyst Addition:** Dry Ni catalyst is added and dispersed. 3. **Hydrogen Sparging:** Pure H₂ (>99.5%) is bubbled through the oil at 1–5 bar and **180–200 °C** under vigorous agitation. 4. **Hydrogenation:** The reaction is monitored by **refractive index (RI)** or **Iodine Value (IV)**. Reaction is stopped when the target IV (60–70) is reached. 5. **Catalyst Filtration:** The hot fat is filtered through a **plate-and-frame filter press** with diatomaceous earth to reduce residual Ni to **< 5 ppm**. 6. **Deodorization:** Steam-stripped at 220–250 °C, 2–5 mbar to remove volatile off-flavours. 7. **Additives:** **Vitamin A (15 IU/g)** and **D (1–2 IU/g)**, **β-carotene** for colour, **butylated hydroxyanisole (BHA) or BHT** as antioxidant, and permitted flavouring agents. 8. **Crystallisation & Packaging:** Poured into moulds or block formers, cooled, and packed. **Key Quality Parameters (as per FSSAI):** | Parameter | Specification | |---|---| | Iodine Value | 60–70 | | Melting Point | 36–40 °C | | Free Fatty Acids (as oleic) | $\leq 0.25\%$ | | Moisture | $\leq 0.25\%$ | | Trans-fatty acids | $\leq 5\%$ | | Nickel (residual) | $\leq 5$ ppm | --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Discuss the **manufacture of Margarine** with a flow sheet. Highlight the role of emulsifiers and the scraped surface heat exchanger. (7 marks) **(b)** Explain the **production of Ghee from butter**. How does the RMV (Reichert–Meissl Value) help confirm its dairy origin? (7 marks) #### Model Solution for (a) **Margarine Definition:** Margarine is a **water-in-oil (W/O) emulsion** of approximately **80% fat and 16% water**, designed to mimic the plasticity, colour, and mouthfeel of butter. It was invented in 1869 by the French chemist **Hippolyte Mèges-Mouriès** under a commission by Napoleon III. **Raw Materials:** - **Fat phase (80%):** Hydrogenated vegetable oil + liquid oil blend; minor milk fat sometimes added. - **Aqueous phase (16%):** Water, salt, milk solids, preservatives, flavour. - **Emulsifiers (0.5–2%):** **Lecithin** (from soy/sunflower), **monoglycerides, diglycerides**, polyglycerol esters. - **Additives:** β-carotene (yellow), vitamin A & D, potassium sorbate (preservative), BHT/BHA (antioxidant), butter flavour, starter distillate. **Manufacturing Steps:** 1. **Preparation of Fat Phase:** Hydrogenated fat (IV ≈ 70–80) is melted at 50–60 °C. Liquid oil and emulsifiers are added. 2. **Preparation of Aqueous Phase:** Water, salt, milk solids, preservatives are dissolved and pasteurized at 75 °C. 3. **Emulsification:** The aqueous phase is slowly added to the fat phase under high-speed stirring, forming a **W/O emulsion**. 4. **Scraped Surface Heat Exchanger (SSHE) — "Votator":** This is the **heart of the margarine process**. The emulsion passes through a cooled, rotating cylinder fitted with **spring-loaded scraper blades** that: - Cool the emulsion from 30–40 °C to 5–15 °C within 1–2 minutes (rapid cooling). - **Scrape off solidified fat** from the wall, ensuring uniform heat transfer. - Induce **controlled crystallisation** of the higher-melting triglycerides first, giving the desired **β′-crystal network** for plasticity. 5. **Pin Worker / Resting Tube:** The partially crystallised margarine passes through a **pin worker** (which breaks up large crystals and works the emulsion to a smooth texture) and then a **resting tube** (15–25 minutes) for crystal maturation. 6. **Packaging:** Filled into tubs or wrappers at 5–10 °C. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Role of emulsifiers in margarine:** Emulsifiers preferentially reside at the W/O interface, **stabilising the water droplets** and preventing coalescence during storage. Monoglycerides also interact with the fat crystals to **delay polymorphic transitions** (β′ → β), keeping the product smooth and plastic. #### Model Solution for (b) **Production of Ghee from Butter — Steps:** 1. **Starting material:** Butter (or directly, cream/white butter). 2. **Melting:** Butter is heated in a **stainless-steel double-jacketed pan** to 60–70 °C until it melts completely. 3. **Controlled Heating:** Temperature is slowly raised to **110–120 °C**. The moisture (16% in butter) boils off as steam, producing characteristic frothing and crackling. 4. **Dehydration Stage:** Vigorous bubbling continues for 2–3 hours. The endpoint is reached when: - The crackling sound ceases (all water evaporated). - The temperature reaches ~120 °C. - The colour turns golden yellow. - The characteristic **ghee aroma** (lactones, methyl ketones, aldehydes) develops from non-enzymatic browning and phospholipid breakdown. 5. **Sedimentation:** Heating is stopped; the pan is allowed to cool. The **ghee residue (curds and brown particles)** settles at the bottom. 6. **Decantation / Filtration:** The clear golden supernatant is decanted or filtered through muslin cloth. 7. **Packaging:** Filled into airtight metal or glass containers. **Yield:** ~85–88% from butter; ~92–95% from cream (desi-style direct cream method). **Reichert–Meissl Value (RMV) — Why It Confirms Dairy Origin:** The **RMV** measures the amount of **volatile, water-soluble fatty acids** (chiefly **butyric acid C4:0** and **caproic acid C6:0**) distillable from 5 g of fat under standard conditions. These short-chain fatty acids are **virtually absent in vegetable oils** but are **characteristic of milk fat (butter, ghee)**. **Procedure (Brief):** Fat is saponified with KOH, acidified with H₂SO₄, and the volatile fatty acids are steam-distilled and titrated with 0.1 N KOH. $$ \text{RMV (mL of 0.1 N KOH for 5 g fat)} = 26 - 32 \quad \text{(for pure ghee)} $$ | Fat Type | Typical RMV | |---|---| | Pure ghee / butter fat | 26 – 32 | | Coconut oil | 5 – 8 | | Palm oil | 0.5 – 2 | | Vanaspati / Hydrogenated veg oil | 0.2 – 1.0 | > [!NOTE] > **A high RMV (> 20) is a strong forensic confirmation that the fat is of dairy origin.** Adulteration of ghee with vanaspati can be detected by **drop in RMV combined with appearance of trans-fatty acids and β-sitosterol** (a phytosterol absent in milk fat). This makes RMV a standard **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) test** under IS: 16326 for ghee authenticity. --- ## 5.3 KTU Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this section and revise just before the exam.** - [x] **Hydrogenation** is the addition of $H_2$ across $C=C$ bonds in unsaturated oils, catalysed by **Ni** at **150–225 °C** and **1–10 atm**. - [x] **Iodine Value (IV)** decreases with hydrogenation; Wijs titration is the standard method. - [x] The **Horiuti–Polanyi mechanism** has **5 stages**: H₂ dissociation, π-complexation, half-hydrogenation, free rotation (→ trans isomers), second H transfer & desorption. - [x] **Selectivity Ratio (SR)** = $k_{\text{linolenic}} / k_{\text{oleic}}$; high SR preserved with low T and high P. - [x] **Partial hydrogenation** is the industrial norm — fully hardened fat (IV → 0) is used only for stearic acid production. - [x] **Vanaspati** is Indian hydrogenated vegetable fat, IV 60–70, with vitamin A, D, antioxidants, and β-carotene; FSSAI trans limit ≤ 5%. - [x] **Margarine** is a W/O emulsion of 80% fat and 16% water, made via **Votator/SSHE** with emulsifiers (lecithin, mono-/diglycerides). - [x] **Polymorphism** — margarines are crystallised in the **β′ form** for smooth texture (β form = grainy). - [x] **Ghee** is **clarified butter fat**, ~99.5% milk fat, made by **heat clarification at 110–120 °C** until moisture is fully removed. - [x] **Ghee aroma** comes from lactones, methyl ketones, and aldehydes formed during heating. - [x] **Butter** is a W/O emulsion (80% fat) made by **churning pasteurised cream** at 8–12 °C. - [x] **Reichert–Meissl Value (RMV > 20)** is the classic test confirming dairy origin of ghee; vegetable oils have RMV < 2. - [x] **Polenske Value (PV)** measures volatile **insoluble** fatty acids (e.g., lauric fats); complements RMV. - [x] **Trans-fatty acids** are an unintended side-product of partial hydrogenation and a key regulatory concern. - [x] **Interesterification** is the modern alternative to hydrogenation to produce zero-trans plastic fats. - [x] Industrial hydrogenators are **stirred batch reactors** with **deodorization + Ni filtration** as downstream steps to reduce Ni to < 5 ppm. - [x] **Saponification Value (SV)** reflects mean fatty acid MW — high for butter, low for hardened peanut oil. - [x] **Solid Fat Content (SFC)** measured by **pulsed NMR** at multiple temperatures is the most informative single profile for plastic fats.

topics.title: Specialty Oils and Products: Conjugated Linoleic Acid (CLA), Gamma Linolenic Acid (GLA), Oils from Microorganisms, Lecithin, Transgenic Oils, Fish Oil
Production and Processing of Non-Edible Oils: Castor Oil, Linseed Oil, Vegetable Waxes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT748 - FAT AND OIL PROCESSING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Production and Processing of Non-Edible Oils: Castor Oil, Linseed Oil, Vegetable Waxes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What Are Non-Edible Oils?

**Non-edible oils** are lipid extracts obtained from plant seeds, kernels, or beans that are **not fit for direct human consumption** due to the presence of toxic constituents (e.g., ricin in castor), strong purgative effects (castor), rapid oxidation tendency (linseed), or non-nutritive functional use (waxes). They are, however, **commercially valuable** for industrial applications such as lubricants, paints, cosmetics, coatings, pharmaceuticals, and biodiesel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** *Non-edible oils are triglyceride-based lipids derived from oilseeds that, owing to their toxic, purgative, or rapid-polymerizing nature, are channeled exclusively to industrial, pharmaceutical, or cosmetic end-uses rather than dietary applications.*

### 1.2 The Three Classes Covered in This Module

| Class | Source | Major Use | Toxicity |
|-------|--------|-----------|----------|
| **Castor Oil** | *Ricinus communis* seeds | Lubricants, cosmetics, pharma | Contains **ricin** (must be deactivated) |
| **Linseed Oil** | *Linum usitatissimum* seeds | Paints, varnishes, linoleum | Drying oil, rancidifies fast |
| **Vegetable Waxes** | Palm leaves, candelilla shrub, rice bran | Polishes, coatings, candles | Generally inert, GRAS-grade |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine three "unusual fruits" on a tree.
> - **Castor bean** = a fruit containing a *toxic seed* (ricin) wrapped in a *healing oil*. We discard the poison and keep the oil for engines and cosmetics.
> - **Linseed** = a fruit whose oil *hardens in air like paint drying on a wall*. We use this "self-drying" property to make varnishes.
> - **Vegetable wax** = a fruit that gives *solid candles* rather than liquid oil, used for polishing cars and coating pills.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industry Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **Iodine Value (IV):** Measure of unsaturation (g I₂/100 g oil)
> - **Saponification Value (SV):** Measure of average molecular weight (mg KOH/g oil)
> - **Acid Value (AV):** Measure of free fatty acids (mg KOH/g oil)
> - **Viscosity at 25 °C:** Castor oil ≈ **950–1000 cP** (extremely high)
> - **Hydroxyl Value:** Unique to castor oil due to **ricinoleic acid** ≈ **160–170 mg KOH/g**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Castor Oil — Detailed Profile

#### 2.1.1 Botanical & Chemical Background
Castor oil is obtained from the seeds of *Ricinus communis*, family **Euphorbiaceae**. The seed kernel contains **45–55 % oil** and a deadly glycoprotein toxin called **ricin** (LD₅₀ ≈ 2–20 µg/kg in humans).

**Typical Fatty Acid Composition of Castor Oil:**

| Fatty Acid | Carbon Chain | % Composition |
|------------|--------------|---------------|
| **Ricinoleic acid** (12-OH oleic) | C18:1, OH | **85–90 %** |
| Oleic acid | C18:1 | 3–6 % |
| Linoleic acid | C18:2 | 1–5 % |
| Stearic acid | C18:0 | 1–3 % |
| Palmitic acid | C16:0 | 1–2 % |
| Dihydroxystearic acid | C18:0, 2OH | trace |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why castor oil is unique:** The presence of a **hydroxyl group (-OH) at C-12** of ricinoleic acid makes castor oil the only commercially available **naturally hydroxylated triglyceride**. This gives it high polarity, alcohol solubility, and a fixed hydroxyl position for chemical modification (e.g., dehydration to "dehydrated castor oil" or production of **11-aminoundecanoic acid** for nylon-11).

#### 2.1.2 Production Process of Castor Oil

The castor oil processing route is depicted below in bullet form:

1. **Seed Cleaning** — removal of dust, stones, broken seeds.
2. **Decorticating / Dehulling** — mechanical removal of the hull (high fiber).
3. **Cooking / Conditioning** — seeds heated to **80–100 °C** to rupture cell walls, reduce moisture to **< 5 %**, lower oil viscosity.
4. **Mechanical Expelling** — screw press or hydraulic press → yields **crude castor oil (35–42 %)** and **expeller cake (residual oil ~ 8–10 %)**.
5. **Solvent Extraction** — expeller cake treated with **n-hexane** in extractors → **miscella** (oil + solvent).
6. **Solvent Recovery** — distillation of miscella at **60–80 °C** under vacuum.
7. **Refining of Crude Oil:**
   - Degumming (phosphatides removal with water/phosphoric acid)
   - Neutralization (alkali refining with NaOH to remove FFA)
   - Bleaching (with activated earth/carbon)
   - Deodorization (steam stripping at 200–250 °C, < 5 mm Hg)
8. **Ricin Detoxification** — castor cake must be detoxified by:
   - Steam autoclaving at **120 °C / 15 psi for 60 min**, OR
   - Ammoniation (treatment with NH₃ → destroys ricin protein).

> [!NOTE]
> **Cold-pressed castor oil** (used in pharmaceuticals/cosmetics) bypasses cooking, retains natural antioxidants, but yield drops to **30–35 %**.

#### 2.1.3 Industrial Applications of Castor Oil

- **Lubricants:** aircraft, high-speed racing, hydraulic fluids (high viscosity index).
- **Cosmetics:** lipsticks, hair oils, soaps (sodium ricinoleate is transparent).
- **Pharmaceuticals:** laxative (USP grade), vehicle for injectables.
- **Chemicals:** sebacic acid, undecylenic acid, nylon-11 (Rilsan).
- **Biodiesel:** after transesterification → **methyl ricinoleate**.

---

### 2.2 Linseed (Flaxseed) Oil — Detailed Profile

#### 2.2.1 Botanical & Chemical Background
Linseed oil is extracted from the seeds of *Linum usitatissimum* (family Linaceae). Seed contains **35–45 % oil**.

**Typical Fatty Acid Composition of Linseed Oil:**

| Fatty Acid | % Composition |
|------------|---------------|
| **α-Linolenic acid (C18:3)** | **50–60 %** |
| Linoleic acid (C18:2) | 12–18 % |
| Oleic acid (C18:1) | 10–22 % |
| Stearic acid (C18:0) | 2–4 % |
| Palmitic acid (C16:0) | 4–6 % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drying Oil Concept:** Linseed oil is classified as a **drying oil** because its high content of **polyunsaturated fatty acids (especially linolenic acid with three double bonds)** undergoes oxidative polymerization when exposed to air, forming a tough, elastic film. This property is exploited in paints and varnishes.

#### 2.2.2 Production Process of Linseed Oil

The production sequence is similar to castor oil but optimized for **low-temperature extraction** to prevent premature oxidation:

1. **Seed Cleaning & Drying** → moisture < **8 %**.
2. **Crushing / Flaking** → flakes 0.2–0.3 mm thick for efficient solvent contact.
3. **Cooking (optional, mild)** → 60–80 °C.
4. **Pre-pressing (expeller)** → crude oil (28–32 %).
5. **Solvent Extraction (n-hexane)** → reduces residual oil in cake to < **1 %**.
6. **Refining:**
   - Degumming
   - **Alkali Neutralization** — critical because linseed has high FFA (3–5 %).
   - Bleaching (Fuller's earth)
   - **Deodorization** — done at **lower temperatures (160–180 °C)** to avoid polymerization in deodorizer.
7. **Optional: Stand Oilization** — heating linseed oil at **280–320 °C under inert atmosphere (N₂/CO₂)** to polymerize and produce **stand oil** (used in lithographic inks).

#### 2.2.3 Industrial Applications of Linseed Oil

| Application | Mechanism |
|-------------|-----------|
| **Paints & Varnishes** | Oxidative polymerization forms film |
| **Linoleum flooring** | Oxidized linseed oil + cork dust + limestone |
| **Printing ink** | Viscous, glossy stand oil as vehicle |
| **Wood finish / Putty** | Penetrates wood, seals surface |
| **Bakery / Supplement** | Edible-grade cold-pressed (α-linolenic is ω-3) |

---

### 2.3 Vegetable Waxes — Detailed Profile

#### 2.3.1 What Are Vegetable Waxes?

**Vegetable waxes** are **esters of long-chain fatty acids (C₁₆–C₃₄) with long-chain monohydric alcohols (C₁₆–C₃₆)**, often accompanied by hydrocarbons, free fatty alcohols, ketones, and sterols. Unlike oils (triglycerides), waxes are **single esters**, making them harder, more brittle, and higher-melting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Waxes vs. Fats:** Triglycerides (fats/oils) = glycerol + 3 fatty acids. Waxes = long-chain alcohol + 1 fatty acid. This structural difference explains why waxes are solids at room temperature and chemically more inert.

#### 2.3.2 Major Commercial Vegetable Waxes

| Wax | Botanical Source | Melting Point (°C) | Saponification Value |
|-----|------------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Carnauba wax** | *Copernicia prunifera* (palm leaves, Brazil) | **82–86** | 78–95 |
| **Candelilla wax** | *Euphorbia cerifera* / *Pedilanthus* (Mexico) | 68–73 | 43–65 |
| **Rice bran wax** | *Oryza sativa* bran | 75–80 | 75–120 |
| **Soy wax** | *Glycine max* (hydrogenated) | 50–55 | 75–95 |
| **Ouricury wax** | *Syagrus coronata* | 79–84 | 88–100 |
| **Jojoba wax** | *Simmondsia chinensis* (actually liquid ester) | 6–10 | 90–100 |

#### 2.3.3 Production of Vegetable Waxes

The general extraction methodology:

1. **Carnauba wax** — palm leaves are cut, dried, beaten to release wax powder; wax is **melted with hot water**, filtered, and solidified into blocks. Yield: **4–5 %** of dry leaf weight.
2. **Candelilla wax** — entire shrubs are **boiled in water with sulfuric acid (0.1–0.2 %)**; molten wax floats and is skimmed. Yield: **1–2 %** of dry plant weight.
3. **Rice bran wax** — extracted from crude rice bran oil by **winterization + solvent fractionation**. Yield: **3–5 %** of crude oil.
4. **Soy wax** — produced by **hydrogenation of refined soybean oil** (Ni catalyst, 150–180 °C, 5–10 bar H₂) until iodine value drops below 20.

#### 2.3.4 Industrial Applications of Vegetable Waxes

| Application | Wax Used |
|-------------|----------|
| **Car/floor polishes** | Carnauba (high gloss) |
| **Chewing gum base** | Candelilla, rice bran |
| **Candles** | Soy wax (clean burn) |
| **Cosmetic lip balms** | Carnauba + Candelilla |
| **Pharmaceutical tablet coating** | Carnauba (glidant) |
| **Food coating (cheese, fruit)** | Carnauba (E903), Candelilla (E902) |
| **Electronics / insulation** | Ouricury wax |

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas appear most frequently in KTU End-Semester Examinations (ESE) under Module 3.

| Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use |
|---------|---------------|-------------|
| $IV = \dfrac{(B - S) \times N \times 12.69}{W}$ | B=blank titre, S=sample titre, N=normality of Na₂S₂O₃, W=sample weight (g) | Calculate unsaturation degree |
| $SV = \dfrac{(B - S) \times N \times 56.1}{W}$ | B=blank, S=sample, N=normality of KOH, W=sample weight | Average mol. weight of triglycerides |
| $AV = \dfrac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W}$ | V=titre, N=normality of KOH, W=sample | Free fatty acid content |
| $OHV = SV - AV$ | Approximation | Hydroxyl value for castor oil |
| $\% \text{ Yield} = \dfrac{\text{Oil obtained (g)}}{\text{Seed taken (g)}} \times 100$ | Mass balance | Extraction efficiency |
| $\text{Refining Loss (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{FFA \%}}{100} \times \text{factor} + \text{gums} + \text{color pigments}$ | Empirical | Neutralization loss in alkali refining |
| $\text{RMU} = \dfrac{SV}{2 \times IV} \times \dfrac{M_{FA}}{1000}$ | Ratio of mol weights | Cross-check unsaturation |

> [!NOTE]
> In the markdown table above, all "or" / absolute value symbols have been written using mathematical notation; the table does not use the pipe character.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Why These Non-Edible Oils Matter |
|----------|---------------------------------|
| **Automotive lubrication** | Castor oil's viscosity index ≈ 220; mineral oils ≈ 90–100. Critical for racing engines. |
| **Renewable biodiesel** | Castor methyl ester has **cetane number ≈ 60–65**, far above petro-diesel (~45). |
| **Polymer industry** | Castor → sebacic acid → nylon-6,10. Linseed → "bodied oil" for paints. |
| **Cosmetics** | Vegetable waxes are **vegan alternatives** to beeswax and paraffin. |
| **Pharma** | Castor oil is **US FDA-approved** as a stimulant laxative and excipient. |

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Castor Oil Yield Mass Balance

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A castor seed lot weighing **1000 kg** is processed. After mechanical pressing, **380 kg** of crude castor oil is obtained. The expeller cake (mass **= 600 kg**) still contains **8 % oil by weight**. This cake is then solvent-extracted, recovering **95 %** of its residual oil. Calculate:
1. Total oil recovered (kg)
2. Overall extraction efficiency (%)
3. Residual oil left in final cake (%)

#### Step 1 — Oil Recovered by Mechanical Pressing
Mechanical pressing already gave us **380 kg** of crude oil. This is direct from the data.

#### Step 2 — Oil Remaining in Cake After Pressing
Cake mass after pressing = 1000 − 380 = 620 kg (note: some seed moisture, hull etc. is lost; we assume negligible for simplification).

Residual oil in cake:
$$
\text{Oil}_{\text{residual}} = 620 \times \dfrac{8}{100} = 49.6 \ \text{kg}
$$

#### Step 3 — Oil Recovered by Solvent Extraction
Solvent extraction recovers 95 % of residual oil:
$$
\text{Oil}_{\text{from solvent}} = 49.6 \times \dfrac{95}{100} = 47.12 \ \text{kg}
$$

#### Step 4 — Total Oil Recovered
$$
\text{Oil}_{\text{total}} = 380 + 47.12 = 427.12 \ \text{kg}
$$

#### Step 5 — Overall Extraction Efficiency
Assuming the seed originally contained **48 % oil** (typical castor):
$$
\text{Total oil in seed} = 1000 \times 0.48 = 480 \ \text{kg}
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{extraction}} = \dfrac{427.12}{480} \times 100 = 88.98 \ \%
$$

#### Step 6 — Residual Oil in Final Spent Cake
Oil remaining in final cake after solvent extraction:
$$
\text{Oil}_{\text{left}} = 49.6 - 47.12 = 2.48 \ \text{kg}
$$

Final cake mass (after solvent removal):
$$
\text{Cake}_{\text{final}} = 620 - 47.12 = 572.88 \ \text{kg}
$$

Residual oil percentage in final cake:
$$
\% \text{ oil in cake} = \dfrac{2.48}{572.88} \times 100 = 0.43 \ \%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Summary:** Total oil = **427.12 kg**; Efficiency = **88.98 %**; Residual oil in cake = **0.43 %**. Industry target is residual oil < **1 %** ✓.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Iodine Value of Linseed Oil

**Problem Statement:**
A 0.25 g sample of linseed oil is treated with **25 mL of 0.1 N Wijs solution (ICl in acetic acid)**. After **30 minutes** in the dark, **10 mL of 10 % KI** is added. The liberated iodine is titrated with **0.1 N Na₂S₂O₃**, requiring **18.5 mL**. A blank titration consumed **35.0 mL** of the same Na₂S₂O₃. Calculate the Iodine Value.

#### Step 1 — Recall the Formula
$$
IV = \dfrac{(V_{B} - V_{S}) \times N \times 12.69}{W}
$$

Where:
- $V_B$ = blank titre = **35.0 mL**
- $V_S$ = sample titre = **18.5 mL**
- $N$ = normality of Na₂S₂O₃ = **0.1 N**
- $W$ = sample weight = **0.25 g**
- 12.69 = equivalent weight of iodine ÷ 10

#### Step 2 — Substitute
$$
IV = \dfrac{(35.0 - 18.5) \times 0.1 \times 12.69}{0.25}
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute Numerator
$$
(35.0 - 18.5) = 16.5
$$
$$
16.5 \times 0.1 = 1.65
$$
$$
1.65 \times 12.69 = 20.9385
$$

#### Step 4 — Final Division
$$
IV = \dfrac{20.9385}{0.25} = 83.75 \ \dfrac{g \ I_{2}}{100 \ g \ oil}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** The computed value (83.75) is **far below** the expected linseed oil IV (175–200). This indicates either:
> - **Underdosing** of Wijs solution (insufficient halogenation), OR
> - The sample may be a **hydrogenated/partially polymerized** linseed oil (stand oil has lower IV).
> In an exam, students must **comment on the deviation** from typical values to earn full marks.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Saponification Value of Castor Oil

**Problem Statement:**
**2.0 g** of castor oil is refluxed with **25 mL of 0.5 N alcoholic KOH** for **1 hour**. After cooling, the excess KOH is back-titrated with **0.5 N HCl**, requiring **8.4 mL**. Blank titration consumed **25.0 mL**. Compute SV.

#### Step 1 — Formula
$$
SV = \dfrac{(V_B - V_S) \times N \times 56.1}{W}
$$

#### Step 2 — Substitute
$$
SV = \dfrac{(25.0 - 8.4) \times 0.5 \times 56.1}{2.0}
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute
$$
(25.0 - 8.4) = 16.6
$$
$$
16.6 \times 0.5 = 8.3
$$
$$
8.3 \times 56.1 = 465.63
$$
$$
SV = \dfrac{465.63}{2.0} = 232.82 \ \dfrac{mg \ KOH}{g \ oil}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Discussion:** Typical SV of castor oil = **177–187 mg KOH/g**. The computed value 232.82 is **higher** because ricinoleic acid contains an **-OH group** that also consumes KOH. Therefore, the more rigorous formula splits into **ester value + hydroxyl value**. For exam purposes, report the apparent SV and comment on the deviation.

---

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**List the major fatty acid composition of castor oil and explain why castor oil is uniquely soluble in alcohol compared to other vegetable oils.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Castor oil contains primarily **ricinoleic acid (85–90 %)**, with smaller amounts of oleic, linoleic, stearic, and palmitic acids (1 mark). The ricinoleic acid carries a **hydroxyl group at C-12**, making the molecule **polar** (1 mark). This polarity allows castor oil to dissolve in **ethyl alcohol**, unlike other vegetable oils which are non-polar and only soluble in non-polar solvents like hexane (1 mark).

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**What is a drying oil? Why is linseed oil classified as one, and what property of its fatty acids is responsible?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A drying oil is one that forms a **tough, elastic, solid film** when exposed to air due to **oxidative polymerization** of its polyunsaturated fatty acids (1 mark). Linseed oil is classified as a drying oil because of its high content of **α-linolenic acid (C18:3, 50–60 %)**, which has **three double bonds** that absorb oxygen and cross-link into a film (1 mark). This property is exploited in **paints, varnishes, and linoleum** (1 mark).

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Describe in detail the production process of castor oil from castor seeds, including detoxification of the residual cake.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Seed Preparation (1 mark):**
Castor seeds (*Ricinus communis*) are cleaned to remove dirt, stones, and broken seeds, then dried to moisture < **5 %** to prevent mold growth.

**(ii) Decortication (1 mark):**
The hard outer hull is removed mechanically (dehulling) because the hull is high in fiber and pigments and lowers oil quality. After dehulling, kernels (45–55 % oil) are separated.

**(iii) Cooking / Conditioning (1 mark):**
Kernels are cooked at **80–100 °C** for 15–30 minutes to rupture cell walls, lower oil viscosity, and coagulate proteins. This improves oil yield during pressing.

**(iv) Mechanical Expelling (1 mark):**
Cooked kernels are passed through **screw presses (expellers)**. Crude oil (yield 35–42 %) is separated from the **expeller cake** which still retains 8–10 % oil.

**(v) Solvent Extraction (1 mark):**
The expeller cake is treated with **n-hexane at 55–65 °C** in continuous extractors. The resulting **miscella** (oil + hexane) is distilled to recover oil and recycle solvent. Final residual oil in spent cake is brought below **1 %**.

**(vi) Refining (1 mark):**
Crude oil is **degummed** (with water/phosphoric acid), **neutralized** (NaOH to remove free fatty acids), **bleached** (activated earth/carbon at 90–110 °C), and **deodorized** (steam stripping at 200–250 °C, < 5 mm Hg vacuum).

**(vii) Detoxification of Castor Cake (1 mark):**
Castor cake contains the toxic glycoprotein **ricin** (LD₅₀ ≈ 2 µg/kg). It is detoxified by:
- **Steam autoclaving** at 120 °C / 15 psi for 60 minutes, OR
- **Ammoniation** with ammonia gas to denature the ricin protein.
After detoxification, the cake becomes safe for use as **organic fertilizer or animal feed supplement**.

**(b) [7 Marks] Discuss the major industrial applications of castor oil with emphasis on its role in polymer and lubricant industries.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Cosmetics and Pharmaceuticals (1 mark):**
Castor oil is used as a **laxative (USP grade)**, in **lipsticks** (gives glossy film), **hair oils**, and as a vehicle for injectables (e.g., paclitaxel). Sodium ricinoleate is the basis of **transparent soaps**.

**(ii) Lubricants (2 marks):**
Castor oil has a **viscosity index of ~220** (mineral oil = 90–100), making it superior for **high-speed bearings, jet engines, and racing cars**. It is also used in **hydraulic fluids** and **greases** because of its polarity (good metal adhesion). However, it is being replaced by **synthetic esters** in aviation due to better low-temperature behavior.

**(iii) Polymer Industry (2 marks):**
- **Nylon-11 (Rilsan):** Castor oil → ricinoleic acid → methyl ricinoleate → cracking at 300 °C → **11-undecenoic acid** → **11-aminoundecanoic acid** → polycondensation → Nylon-11.
- **Sebacic acid:** Castor oil fused with NaOH at 250 °C → sebacic acid (C₁₀) → used in Nylon-6,10 and plasticizers.
- **Polyurethane:** Hydroxyl group of ricinoleic acid reacts with isocyanates to form polyurethane foams and elastomers.

**(iv) Biodiesel (1 mark):**
Transesterification of castor oil with methanol (NaOH catalyst, 60 °C) gives **methyl ricinoleate**, with **cetane number 60–65** (vs. 45 for petro-diesel) and excellent low-temperature flow due to the inherent OH group.

**(v) Coatings and Surfactants (1 mark):**
**Dehydrated castor oil (DCO)** — produced by removing water from ricinoleic acid at 250 °C with H₂SO₄ catalyst — yields conjugated dienes used in **alkyd resins, paints, and varnishes**. Sulfonated castor oil (**Turkey Red Oil**) is used as a **textile surfactant** and leather softener.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Describe in detail the production process and industrial applications of vegetable waxes, with special reference to carnauba and candelilla wax.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) What are vegetable waxes? (1 mark):**
Vegetable waxes are **esters of long-chain fatty acids (C₁₆–C₃₄) with long-chain monohydric alcohols (C₁₆–C₃₆)**, often accompanied by hydrocarbons, free alcohols, and sterols. Unlike triglyceride oils, waxes are **single esters** — making them **hard, brittle, and high-melting**.

**(ii) Carnauba Wax Production (2 marks):**
- Source: leaves of *Copernicia prunifera* palm, native to **Brazil**.
- Leaves are **cut, sun-dried for several days**, then **mechanically beaten** to release the waxy powder (ceriferous coating on leaves).
- The powder is **melted with hot water at ~95 °C**, the molten wax floats, is **skimmed off, filtered through cloth**, and cast into blocks.
- **Yield:** 4–5 % of dry leaf weight.
- **Refining:** Optionally bleached with hydrogen peroxide or sunlight.

**(iii) Candelilla Wax Production (2 marks):**
- Source: shrubs of *Euphorbia cerifera* and *Pedilanthus pavonis*, native to **Mexico and Texas**.
- Entire plants are harvested, then **boiled in water containing 0.1–0.2 % H₂SO₄** for several hours.
- The acid ruptures plant cells and helps wax coalesce; molten wax **rises and is skimmed**.
- The crude wax is further **clarified by re-melting and filtration**.
- **Yield:** 1–2 % of dry plant weight.

**(iv) Industrial Applications (2 marks):**
| Wax | Application | Reason |
|-----|-------------|--------|
| Carnauba | **Car polishes, lipstick, tablet coating** | Highest melting point (82–86 °C), glossy |
| Candelilla | **Chewing gum base, food coating (E902)** | Good plasticizer, food-grade |
| Rice bran | **Cosmetics, candles** | Emollient, vegan |
| Soy wax | **Candles (clean burn), chocolate coating** | Inexpensive, renewable |

**(b) [7 Marks] Compare the chemical composition and physicochemical properties of castor oil, linseed oil, and carnauba wax. Why is linseed oil preferred for paints while castor oil is preferred for lubricants?**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Chemical Composition (2 marks):**
- **Castor oil:** ~85–90 % **ricinoleic acid** (12-hydroxy oleic), with oleic, linoleic, stearic, palmitic acids as minor components. **Hydroxyl value 160–170 mg KOH/g** is unique.
- **Linseed oil:** ~50–60 % **α-linolenic acid** (C18:3), 12–18 % linoleic, 10–22 % oleic. Polyunsaturated, **no hydroxyl group**.
- **Carnauba wax:** ~85 % **wax esters** (C₂₄–C₃₀ fatty acid esterified with C₂₆–C₃₀ alcohols), plus 10–15 % **free alcohols, hydrocarbons, and resinous matter**. **No glycerol, no double bonds.**

**(ii) Physicochemical Comparison (2 marks):**
| Property | Castor Oil | Linseed Oil | Carnauba Wax |
|----------|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| Iodine value | 82–90 | 175–200 | 7–14 |
| Saponification value | 177–187 | 188–196 | 78–95 |
| Viscosity (25 °C) | 950–1000 cP | 40–60 cP | Solid |
| Melting point | Liquid | Liquid | 82–86 °C |
| Polarity | High (OH group) | Low | Moderate (ester) |
| Solubility in alcohol | Yes | No | Partial (hot) |

**(iii) Why Linseed Oil is Preferred for Paints (1.5 marks):**
Linseed oil is rich in **α-linolenic acid with three C=C double bonds**, which undergo **oxidative polymerization** when exposed to atmospheric oxygen. The conjugated peroxides and hydroperoxides formed react to cross-link the triglycerides into a **tough, continuous film** — the basis of "drying" in oil-based paints. Castor oil's **single OH and limited double bonds** do not allow rapid film formation, making it unsuitable for paints.

**(iv) Why Castor Oil is Preferred for Lubricants (1.5 marks):**
Castor oil has an exceptionally **high viscosity index (~220)**, meaning its viscosity changes minimally with temperature — critical for bearings and engines. The **polar hydroxyl group** allows it to **adhere tenaciously to metal surfaces**, providing superior lubrication. Additionally, it is **non-corrosive** and has **high flash point (~230 °C)**. Linseed oil is unsuitable for lubricants because it **oxidizes and polymerizes in use**, gumming up machinery.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Examiner's Insight):**
> 1. **Confusing castor cake detoxification with oil refining** — these are two separate operations. Don't merge them in a single paragraph. (Loses 1 mark.)
> 2. **Stating ricin is removed from the oil** — wrong! Ricin is in the **cake/meal**, not the oil. Oil contains only ricinoleic acid, which is non-toxic. (Loses 1–2 marks.)
> 3. **Mixing up iodine and saponification values** — remember: **IV high = more unsaturation** (linseed); **SV high = lower mol. weight** (more ester bonds per gram).
> 4. **Forgetting the hydroxyl value** when calculating saponification value for castor oil — KOH also reacts with -OH group, leading to **apparent SV > true SV**.
> 5. **Drawing wax structure with glycerol** — waxes do **NOT** contain glycerol. They are **monoesters of long-chain alcohols**.
> 6. **Forgetting to state yields and typical percentages** (45–55 % for castor, 35–45 % for linseed, 4–5 % for carnauba) — KTU expects quantitative recall.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3, Non-Edible Oils**

- **Castor oil** = from *Ricinus communis* seeds; **85–90 % ricinoleic acid**; **only commercially available hydroxylated oil**; soluble in alcohol; **high viscosity index (~220)**; key feedstock for **Nylon-11, sebacic acid, Turkey Red Oil**.
- **Castor cake detoxification** = essential because of **ricin toxin**; methods = **autoclaving (120 °C, 15 psi, 60 min)** OR **ammoniation**.
- **Linseed oil** = from *Linum usitatissimum*; **50–60 % α-linolenic acid**; classified as a **drying oil**; forms film by **oxidative polymerization**; used in **paints, varnishes, linoleum, printing ink**.
- **Iodine value benchmark:** Linseed (175–200) ≫ Castor (82–90) > Carnauba wax (7–14).
- **Vegetable waxes** = **esters of long-chain fatty acids + long-chain monohydric alcohols** (NOT triglycerides); hard, brittle, high melting.
- **Carnauba wax** = from palm leaves (Brazil); MP **82–86 °C**; **highest commercial natural wax hardness**; E903 in food.
- **Candelilla wax** = from Mexican shrubs; produced by **boiling in dilute H₂SO₄**; E902 in food.
- **Rice bran wax** = by-product of rice bran oil refining; **winterization + solvent fractionation**.
- **Soy wax** = hydrogenated soybean oil; vegan candle alternative.
- **Turkey Red Oil** = sulfonated castor oil; textile surfactant.
- **Dehydrated castor oil (DCO)** = formed by removing H₂O from ricinoleic acid; gives conjugated dienes for **alkyd resins**.
- **Stand oil** = polymerized linseed oil (280–320 °C, inert gas); used in **lithographic inks**.
- **Beeswax substitute** = carnauba + candelilla blend (vegan polishes).
- **Ricin** LD₅₀ ≈ 2 µg/kg (more toxic than cyanide by weight) — reason castor must be **mechanically/chemically processed**, not eaten raw.
- **Refining losses** increase with FFA: alkali refining loss (%) ≈ FFA (%) × factor + gums + pigments.
- **KTU exam focus:** fatty acid composition, drying mechanism, detoxification steps, IV/SV calculations, wax extraction methods.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Packaging and Storage: Requirements and Properties of Packaging Materials (Tinplate, Semi-Rigid, Glass, Flexible Pouches)
Changes During Storage: Causes of Rancidity, Atmospheric Oxidation, Enzyme Action, Free Fatty Acids, Color Changes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT748 - FAT AND OIL PROCESSING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Changes During Storage: Causes of Rancidity, Atmospheric Oxidation, Enzyme Action, Free Fatty Acids, Color Changes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Rancidity

**Rancidity** is the comprehensive term used in lipid chemistry to describe the development of undesirable flavors, odors, colors, and textural changes in fats and oils during storage. These changes are primarily driven by oxidative and hydrolytic degradation of triglycerides and their constituent fatty acids. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Fat and Oil Processing Technology*, rancidity is broadly classified into three fundamental types:

| Type of Rancidity | Primary Mechanism | Key Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| **Oxidative Rancidity** | Atmospheric O₂ attack on unsaturated fatty acids | Oxygen + Heat + Light + Metals |
| **Hydrolytic Rancidity** | Enzymatic/chemical hydrolysis of triglycerides | Lipase enzyme + Moisture |
| **Ketonic Rancidity** | β-oxidation & methyl ketone formation | Mold metabolism |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):** *Rancidity is the deterioration of fats and oils resulting from oxidative, hydrolytic, or microbial action, leading to off-flavors, off-odors, and nutritionally unsafe compounds such as peroxides, hydroperoxides, aldehydes, and ketones.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a fresh bottle of sunflower oil in your kitchen as a **neatly arranged stack of fresh apples** on a counter:

- The **unsaturated fatty acids** (linoleic, linolenic) are like the **exposed apple flesh** — they are reactive and prone to browning when exposed to air.
- **Antioxidants** in oil (tocopherols) are like the **lemon juice** sprinkled on apples — they slow down the reaction.
- **Heat and light** in storage are like leaving the apples in direct sunlight — they accelerate spoilage.
- The **smell of rancid oil** is similar to the smell of a paint can left open — both are products of oxidation.

> [!NOTE]
> The **induction period** (the time before rapid oxidation begins) is analogous to the time it takes for a cut apple to start visibly browning. Oils rich in natural antioxidants (like olive oil with its polyphenols) have a much longer induction period.

## 1.3 Standard Industry Metrics

- **Peroxide Value (PV):** **5–10 meq O₂/kg** (fresh refined oil), exceeding **20 meq O₂/kg** indicates rancidity onset.
- **Free Fatty Acids (FFA):** Expressed as **% oleic acid**; below **0.1%** is premium grade, above **2%** is industrial.
- **Shelf Life Standard:** Typically **6–12 months** for refined oils stored below **25 °C** in dark, sealed containers.
- **Oxidative Stability Index (OSI):** Measured at **110 °C** in commercial accelerated tests.

> [!WARNING]
> Always remember: **Rancidity ≠ Saponification**. Rancidity is storage deterioration, while saponification is an industrial alkaline hydrolysis for soap making (covered in Module 3).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Oxidative Rancidity — The Free Radical Chain Mechanism

This is the **most frequently tested topic in KTU University Exams** for fat and oil technology. The reaction follows three classical phases:

### Phase 1: Initiation

$$RH + O_2 \xrightarrow{h\nu, \, \Delta, \, \text{metal}} R^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}OOH$$

- **RH** = unsaturated fatty acid (oleic, linoleic, linolenic)
- **R•** = alkyl free radical (carbon-centered, highly reactive)
- **Catalysts:** Heat (>60 °C), UV light, transition metals (Fe²⁺, Cu⁺), haem proteins

### Phase 2: Propagation

$$R^{\bullet} + O_2 \rightarrow ROO^{\bullet}$$

$$ROO^{\bullet} + RH \rightarrow ROOH + R^{\bullet}$$

- **ROOH** = hydroperoxide (the primary oxidation product)
- This step **propagates** the chain — one radical creates another.

### Phase 3: Termination

$$R^{\bullet} + R^{\bullet} \rightarrow RR$$
$$R^{\bullet} + ROO^{\bullet} \rightarrow ROOR$$
$$ROO^{\bullet} + ROO^{\bullet} \rightarrow ROOR + O_2$$

- Termination products include **dimers, polymers, aldehydes, ketones**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The hydroperoxides (ROOH) themselves are unstable and decompose into **secondary oxidation products** such as aldehydes (hexanal, malonaldehyde) and ketones, which are responsible for the actual **off-odors and off-flavors** in rancid oils.

## 2.2 Hydrolytic Rancidity — Enzyme Action

**Lipases** (triacylglycerol acyl hydrolases, E.C. 3.1.1.3) catalyze the breakdown of triglycerides:

$$Triglyceride + 3 H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Lipase}} Glycerol + 3 \, Free \, Fatty \, Acids$$

- Optimal lipase activity: **pH 7.0, 30–40 °C**.
- Lipases in oilseeds (e.g., castor, mustard, coconut) are activated when moisture content exceeds the **critical threshold of 0.5–1.0%**.
- The released **FFAs** have lower molecular weight and stronger odors (butyric acid in butter = rancid butter smell).

> [!NOTE]
> **Microbial lipases** from *Aspergillus*, *Penicillium*, and *Pseudomonas* species are major contributors to hydrolytic rancidity in stored oilseeds and crude oils. This is why **moisture control** and **microbial load management** are critical in oil mill operations.

## 2.3 Ketonic Rancidity (Mold Rancidity)

A specific form of hydrolytic + oxidative rancidity where mold enzymes (e.g., from *Aspergillus niger*) cause **β-oxidation** of saturated fatty acids (C12–C18) followed by **decarboxylation** to form methyl ketones:

$$R-CH_2-CO-CH_2-COOH \rightarrow R-CH_2-CO-CH_3 + CO_2$$

The methyl ketone (2-heptanone, 2-nonanone) imparts a characteristic **fruity, musty, candle-like odor** to coconut oil and palm kernel oil.

## 2.4 Color Changes During Storage

Color changes arise from:

1. **Carotenoid degradation** (β-carotene, lutein) — natural pigments oxidized to colorless compounds.
2. **Maillard browning** — non-enzymatic reaction between reducing sugars and amino groups (relevant in oilseed meal storage).
3. **Metal-complex formation** — Fe²⁺/Cu⁺ chelation with polyphenols produces dark colors.
4. **Polymerization** — high-temperature storage produces colored polymers (darkening in frying oils).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Lovibond color index** is the standard industrial measure. Refined oils typically have Lovibond values of **Y=10, R=1.0** (1-inch cell). Increased color darkening is a quality indicator of poor storage.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Table** — These formulas are essential for KTU University Exam Part B questions.

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Reagent / Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Peroxide Value (PV)** | $PV = \dfrac{(V_{sample} - V_{blank}) \times N_{Na_2S_2O_3} \times 1000}{W}$ | **meq O₂/kg oil** | Iodometric (Wijs method) |
| **Acid Value (AV)** | $AV = \dfrac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W}$ | **mg KOH/g oil** | KOH titration |
| **Free Fatty Acids (% oleic)** | $FFA\,\% = \dfrac{V \times N \times 28.2}{W}$ | **% as oleic acid** | Standard alkali titration |
| **TBA Number** | $TBA = 1.1 \times A_{532\,nm}$ | **mg malonaldehyde/kg** | 2-Thiobarbituric acid test |
| **Iodine Value (IV)** | $IV = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 12.69}{W}$ | **g I₂/100 g oil** | Wijs solution |
| **Saponification Value (SV)** | $SV = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 56.1}{W}$ | **mg KOH/g oil** | KOH reflux |
| **Kries Test (Qualitative)** | Visual: red-orange color | **Positive = Oxidized** | FeCl₃ + KSCN reaction |
| **Oxidative Stability Index (OSI)** | Time to reach induction point | **hours at 110 °C** | Rancimat apparatus |
| **Conjugated Dienes** | $\Delta K = A_{232} - A_{300}$ | **specific absorbance** | UV spectrophotometry |
| **p-Anisidine Value (p-AV)** | $p\text{-}AV = \dfrac{25 \times (1.2 A_s - A_b)}{W}$ | **dimensionless** | p-Anisidine reagent |

> [!WARNING]
> The constant **56.1** in AV and SV formulas comes from the **molecular weight of KOH divided by its equivalent factor (MW/1 = 56.1)**. For FFA, the constant **28.2** is **56.1/2** because oleic acid is monobasic with equivalent weight = 282/1 = 282, expressed as % (×100/1000 = 28.2). **Memorize these derivations** — KTU examiners award partial marks for showing the logic.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Packaging design:** Selection of oxygen-impermeable films (PET/AL/PE laminates) to inhibit oxidative rancidity.
- **Nitrogen blanketing:** Used in bulk oil storage tanks to displace O₂ and extend induction period.
- **Antioxidant dosing:** BHA, BHT, TBHQ, and natural tocopherols are added at **100–200 ppm** to commercial oils.
- **Quality control:** PV, FFA, and p-anisidine value are **mandatory release parameters** in refineries (FSSAI, Codex Alimentarius standards).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Peroxide Value (PV) Formula

### Step 1: Reaction Principle
