When oil is treated with glacial acetic acid–chloroform solvent and saturated KI solution, the hydroperoxides (ROOH) present in oxidized oil liberate iodine (I₂) according to:

$$ROOH + 2 KI + 2 H^+ \rightarrow ROH + I_2 + 2 K^+ + H_2O$$

### Step 2: Titration of Liberated I₂

The liberated I₂ is titrated against standardized sodium thiosulfate (Na₂S₂O₃):

$$I_2 + 2 Na_2S_2O_3 \rightarrow 2 NaI + Na_2S_4O_6$$

The equivalence shows that **1 mole of thiosulfate reacts with 0.5 mole of I₂**, which corresponds to **0.5 mole of active oxygen** (½ O₂).

### Step 3: Defining Milliequivalents

By definition, **1 meq of peroxide = 1 meq of thiosulfate = 1/2 meq of active oxygen = 0.008 g of active oxygen**.

$$1 \, \text{meq peroxide} = 0.008 \, \text{g} = 8 \, \text{mg of active oxygen}$$

### Step 4: Constructing the Mathematical Expression

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PV} &= \frac{\text{Milliequivalents of peroxide}}{\text{Kilograms of oil}} \\
\text{Milliequivalents} &= (V_s - V_b) \times N \\
\text{Mass in kg} &= \frac{W}{1000}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$PV = \frac{(V_s - V_b) \times N \times 1000}{W} \quad \text{(meq O}_2\text{/kg)}$$

Where:
- $V_s$ = volume of Na₂S₂O₃ for sample (mL)
- $V_b$ = volume of Na₂S₂O₃ for blank (mL)
- $N$ = normality of Na₂S₂O₃ (eq/L)
- $W$ = weight of oil sample (g)

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** 5.0 g of stored sunflower oil requires 24.50 mL of 0.01 N Na₂S₂O₃. Blank titration consumes 0.50 mL. Calculate the PV.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s - V_b &= 24.50 - 0.50 = 24.00 \, \text{mL} \\
N &= 0.01 \, \text{N} \\
W &= 5.0 \, \text{g} \\
PV &= \frac{24.00 \times 0.01 \times 1000}{5.0} \\
PV &= \frac{240}{5.0} \\
PV &= 48 \, \text{meq O}_2/\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> A PV of **48 meq O₂/kg** is **well above the FSSAI safety limit of 10 meq O₂/kg** for edible oils. The oil is unsuitable for consumption and should be re-refined or discarded.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Acid Value and FFA Relationship

### Step 1: Acid Value Concept

The acid value is defined as the number of **mg of KOH required to neutralize the free fatty acids in 1 g of oil**.

### Step 2: Mathematical Deduction

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Volume of KOH used} &= V \, \text{mL} \\
\text{Normality of KOH} &= N \\
\text{Equivalents of KOH} &= \frac{V \times N}{1000} \\
\text{Weight of KOH (g)} &= \frac{V \times N}{1000} \times 56.1 \\
\text{Weight of KOH (mg)} &= V \times N \times 56.1
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing by weight of oil $W$:

$$AV = \frac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W} \quad \text{(mg KOH/g oil)}$$

### Step 3: Converting AV to % FFA (as Oleic Acid)

The molecular weight of oleic acid is **282 g/mol**, and it is monobasic (1 equivalent per mole). So equivalent weight = 282.

For a given mass of oil, the mass of FFA (as oleic) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of oleic acid (mg)} &= V \times N \times 282 \\
\text{Mass of oleic acid (g)} &= \frac{V \times N \times 282}{1000} \\
\text{\% FFA} &= \frac{V \times N \times 282}{1000 \times W} \times 100 \\
\text{\% FFA} &= \frac{V \times N \times 28.2}{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$FFA\,\% = \frac{AV \times 282}{56.1 \times 1000} \times 100 \quad \Rightarrow \quad FFA\,\% = \frac{AV}{1.99}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick conversion rule:** For practical KTU problems, remember that **% FFA (as oleic) ≈ AV / 2**. This 1:2 ratio appears repeatedly in university exam questions.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Quality Parameters

| Parameter | Fresh Oil | Stored (3 months) | Rancid | Test Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PV (meq O₂/kg)** | 1–5 | 10–20 | >30 | Iodometric titration |
| **FFA (% oleic)** | 0.05–0.1 | 0.3–1.0 | >2.0 | Alkali titration |
| **p-Anisidine Value** | <2 | 5–15 | >20 | Spectrophotometry |
| **TBA Number** | <0.5 | 1.5–3.0 | >5.0 | Colorimetric |
| **Lovibond Color (R)** | 1.0–2.0 | 2.5–4.0 | >5.0 | Tintometer |
| **Odor** | Neutral | Slight hay-like | Paint-like, fishy | Sensory panel |
| **Conjugated Dienes (ΔK)** | <0.5 | 1.0–2.5 | >3.5 | UV at 232 nm |

## 3.4 Mechanism of β-Oxidation in Ketonic Rancidity

The mold-mediated β-oxidation decarboxylation proceeds in four steps:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1:} \quad R-CO-CH_2-CO-SCoA + H_2O \rightarrow R-CO-CH_2-COOH + HSCoA \\
&\text{Step 2:} \quad R-CO-CH_2-COOH \rightarrow R-CO-CH_2-CH_2OH \, (\text{reduction}) \\
&\text{Step 3:} \quad R-CO-CH_2-CH_2OH \rightarrow R-CO-CH_2-CHO \, (\text{oxidation}) \\
&\text{Step 4:} \quad R-CO-CH_2-CHO \rightarrow R-CO-CH_3 + CO_2 \, (\text{decarboxylation})
\end{aligned}
$$

The resulting **methyl ketone** ($R-CO-CH_3$, where $R$ = alkyl chain) is the principal off-flavor compound in moldy coconut and palm kernel oils.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Architecture: Storage Deterioration Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fresh Oil - Triglycerides] --> B{Long-Term Storage}
    
    B -->|Oxygen + Heat + Light| C[Oxidative Rancidity]
    B -->|Lipase + Moisture| D[Hydrolytic Rancidity]
    B -->|Mold Metabolism| E[Ketonic Rancidity]
    B -->|Pigment Exposure| F[Color Changes]
    
    C --> C1[Initiation: RH to R radical]
    C1 --> C2[Propagation: R to ROO radical]
    C2 --> C3[Hydroperoxides ROOH]
    C3 --> C4[Secondary Products: Aldehydes Ketones]
    C4 --> C5[Polymerization Off-odors Toxic]
    
    D --> D1[Triglyceride Hydrolysis]
    D1 --> D2[Release of FFA]
    D2 --> D3[Short-chain FFA: Soapy Rancid Odor]
    
    E --> E1[Beta-Oxidation of Saturated FA]
    E1 --> E2[Methyl Ketone Formation]
    E2 --> E3[Fruity Musty Candle Odor]
    
    F --> F1[Carotenoid Bleaching]
    F --> F2[Maillard Browning in Meal]
    F --> F3[Metal-Polyphenol Complexes]
```

## 4.2 Free Radical Chain Reaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1 Initiation] --> P2[Phase 2 Propagation]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 Termination]
    
    P1 --> RH[Fatty Acid RH]
    RH --> RAD[Alkyl Radical R radical]
    RAD --> O2A[Oxygen O2]
    
    P2 --> RAD2[R radical]
    RAD2 --> ROO[Peroxy Radical ROO radical]
    ROO --> RH2[Another Fatty Acid RH]
    RH2 --> ROOH[Hydroperoxide ROOH]
    ROOH --> RAD3[New R radical]
    
    P3 --> DIM[Dimer RR]
    P3 --> PER[Peroxide ROOR]
    P3 --> POL[Polymer Off-flavor]
    
    ROOH -.->|Secondary Breakdown| SEC[Aldehydes Ketones]
    SEC -.->|Final Products| FIN[Rancid Odor Flavor]
```

## 4.3 Preventive Control Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PC[Preventive Measures for Rancidity] --> PC1[Primary Antioxidants]
    PC --> PC2[Secondary Antioxidants]
    PC --> PC3[Physical Barriers]
    PC --> PC4[Storage Conditions]
    
    PC1 --> PC1A[Tocopherols BHA BHT]
    PC1 --> PC1B[Donate H to R radical]
    PC1 --> PC1C[Inhibit Initiation Phase]
    
    PC2 --> PC2A[Citric Acid Ascorbic Acid]
    PC2 --> PC2B[Chelate Metal Ions]
    PC2 --> PC2C[Synergize with Primary]
    
    PC3 --> PC3A[O2 Impermeable Packaging]
    PC3 --> PC3B[Nitrogen Blanketing]
    PC3 --> PC3C[Sealed Metalized Pouches]
    
    PC4 --> PC4A[Temperature below 25 C]
    PC4 --> PC4B[Dark Storage]
    PC4 --> PC4C[Low Humidity below 60 percent RH]
```

## 4.4 FFA Formation Pathway (Enzyme Action)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TG[Triglyceride] -->|Lipase + H2O| DG[Diacylglycerol]
    DG -->|Lipase + H2O| MG[Monoacylglycerol]
    MG -->|Lipase + H2O| GL[Glycerol]
    GL --> R1[FFA Pool 1]
    DG --> R2[FFA Pool 2]
    TG --> R3[FFA Pool 3]
    
    R1 --> FFAR[Free Fatty Acid Accumulation]
    R2 --> FFAR
    R3 --> FFAR
    
    FFAR --> ODOFF[Soapy Acidic Odor]
    FFAR --> QUAL[High Acid Value]
    FFAR --> COLOR[Darkening in Some Oils]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1. Define rancidity. Classify the different types of rancidity with suitable examples.** *(3 Marks)* `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Rancidity is the deterioration of fats and oils during storage, producing off-flavors, off-odors, and nutritionally harmful compounds. It is classified into:

1. **Oxidative Rancidity** — Atmospheric O₂ attacks unsaturated fatty acids via free radical mechanism, producing hydroperoxides and secondary oxidation products (aldehydes, ketones). Example: Linseed oil going rancid on exposure to air.
2. **Hydrolytic Rancidity** — Lipase enzyme hydrolyzes triglycerides into free fatty acids and glycerol. Example: Butter becoming soapy-rancid.
3. **Ketonic Rancidity** — Mold metabolism produces methyl ketones via β-oxidation. Example: Coconut oil developing fruity odor.
4. **Reversion Rancidity** — Characteristic of soybean oil; the C-17 hydrocarbons give grassy/painty odors.

### **Question 2. Differentiate between acid value and peroxide value.** *(3 Marks)* `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Acid Value (AV) | Peroxide Value (PV) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | mg KOH required to neutralize FFA in 1 g oil | meq of active oxygen (peroxide) per kg oil |
| **Indicates** | Hydrolytic deterioration | Oxidative deterioration |
| **Reagent** | KOH (alcoholic) | Na₂S₂O₃ (after KI reaction) |
| **Unit** | mg KOH/g | meq O₂/kg |
| **Fresh oil** | < 0.5 | < 5 |
| **Acceptable limit (FSSAI)** | AV < 4 mg KOH/g (for refined oils) | PV < 10 meq O₂/kg |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A. (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

#### **(a) Explain the mechanism of oxidative rancidity with the free radical chain theory. Discuss the role of antioxidants in inhibiting this process. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Mechanism of Oxidative Rancidity (Free Radical Chain Theory):**

The oxidation of unsaturated fatty acids follows a three-phase free radical mechanism:

**Phase I – Initiation:**

The unsaturated fatty acid (RH) loses a hydrogen atom to form an alkyl radical (R•). This requires energy input from heat, light, or metal catalysts:

$$RH + O_2 \xrightarrow{h\nu, \, \text{metal}} R^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}OOH$$

**Phase II – Propagation:**

The alkyl radical rapidly reacts with molecular oxygen to form a peroxy radical (ROO•), which abstracts hydrogen from another fatty acid, forming hydroperoxide (ROOH) and a new alkyl radical:

$$R^{\bullet} + O_2 \rightarrow ROO^{\bullet}$$

$$ROO^{\bullet} + RH \rightarrow ROOH + R^{\bullet}$$

This step is autocatalytic — one radical creates another, and the chain continues. **Hydroperoxides (ROOH) are the primary oxidation products.**

**Phase III – Termination:**

Radicals combine to form stable non-radical products, ending the chain:

$$R^{\bullet} + R^{\bullet} \rightarrow R-R$$
$$ROO^{\bullet} + R^{\bullet} \rightarrow ROOR$$
$$ROO^{\bullet} + ROO^{\bullet} \rightarrow ROOR + O_2$$

The hydroperoxides subsequently break down into **secondary oxidation products** — aldehydes (hexanal, malonaldehyde), ketones, alcohols, and hydrocarbons — which are responsible for the actual rancid odor and flavor.

**Role of Antioxidants:**

Antioxidants are classified into two categories:

1. **Primary (Chain-Breaking) Antioxidants** — They donate hydrogen atoms to free radicals, terminating the chain reaction. Examples: **BHA (Butylated Hydroxyanisole), BHT (Butylated Hydroxytoluene), TBHQ (Tert-Butyl Hydroquinone), and natural tocopherols.**

$$ROO^{\bullet} + AH \rightarrow ROOH + A^{\bullet}$$

The antioxidant radical (A•) is stable and does not propagate the chain.

2. **Secondary (Synergistic) Antioxidants** — They chelate pro-oxidant metal ions (Fe²⁺, Cu⁺) and reduce hydroperoxides. Examples: **Citric acid, ascorbic acid, EDTA, and phosphoric acid.**

Synergistic combinations (e.g., BHA + citric acid) provide superior protection than individual antioxidants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for students:** KTU examiners award **2 marks for neat mechanism diagrams** and **1 mark for examples of antioxidants**. Draw the three phases with equations.

---

#### **(b) What is hydrolytic rancidity? Explain the role of lipase enzyme and the methods to control it during storage. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Hydrolytic rancidity is the breakdown of triglycerides in the presence of water, catalyzed by lipase enzymes, releasing free fatty acids (FFA) and glycerol.

**Chemical Reaction:**

$$Triglyceride + 3 H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Lipase}} Glycerol + 3 \, Free \, Fatty \, Acids$$

**Role of Lipase Enzyme:**

Lipase (E.C. 3.1.1.3) is an enzyme present naturally in oilseeds, fruits, and microbial cells. Key features:

- **Source:** Microbial (*Aspergillus*, *Rhizopus*), plant seeds (castor, mustard, soybean), and animal pancreas.
- **Optimal Conditions:** pH 7.0, temperature 30–40 °C.
- **Activation Threshold:** Moisture content in oil > 0.5–1.0% triggers significant lipase activity.
- **Mechanism:** Lipase acts at the oil-water interface, sequentially cleaving fatty acids from the glycerol backbone (TG → DG → MG → glycerol).
- **Consequence:** Released short-chain FFAs (butyric, caproic) produce strong, soapy, acidic odors. Acid value (AV) and % FFA increase progressively.

**Control Methods:**

1. **Moisture Control:** Reduce moisture content of oilseeds below **6–8%** before storage.
2. **Temperature Control:** Store below **25 °C** to inactivate lipase.
3. **Microbial Control:** Maintain hygienic storage; prevent mold infestation (aflatoxin risk also).
4. **Refining:** Degumming and neutralization steps in refining remove FFA and phospholipids.
5. **Inert Atmosphere:** Nitrogen or CO₂ blanketing limits both lipase activity and oxidative rancidity.
6. **Heat Treatment:** Pre-storage conditioning (e.g., roasting of oilseeds) inactivates native lipases.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Many students confuse *hydrolytic* rancidity with *oxidative* rancidity. **Remember:** Hydrolytic = water + lipase; Oxidative = oxygen + radicals. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this confusion.

---

### **Question B. (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### **(a) Describe the chemical reactions involved in the development of color changes during storage of fats and oils. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Color changes in stored fats and oils are caused by the following chemical reactions:

**1. Carotenoid Degradation:**

Natural pigments like **β-carotene, lutein, and lycopene** in crude palm oil, sunflower oil, and olive oil are highly unsaturated and oxidize readily. The conjugated double bond system is destroyed, producing colorless or pale compounds. This is why freshly extracted palm oil (deep red-orange) fades to pale yellow upon storage.

$$\beta\text{-carotene} + O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{oxidation}} \text{Colorless epoxides and apocarotenoids}$$

**2. Maillard Reaction (Non-Enzymatic Browning):**

In oilseed meals (coconut meal, soybean meal), reducing sugars react with free amino groups of proteins, forming **Amadori compounds** and ultimately **melanoidins** (brown polymers).

$$\text{Reducing Sugar} + \text{Amino Acid} \rightarrow \text{Glycosylamine} \rightarrow \text{Amadori} \rightarrow \text{Melanoidins (brown)}$$

**3. Metal-Polyphenol Complexation:**

Iron and copper ions, often leached from storage tanks, form dark-colored chelates with phenolic compounds (e.g., in olive oil, sesame oil). These complexes shift the oil color from yellow to greenish-brown.

**4. Polymerization Reactions:**

Under high-temperature storage (especially in partially used frying oils), fatty acids polymerize into high-molecular-weight colored compounds. Triglyceride dimers and trimers form, darkening the oil and increasing viscosity.

**5. Chlorophyll Degradation:**

In olive oil and canola oil, chlorophylls degrade to **pheophytins** (olive-green to brownish) under acidic conditions or heat.

**6. Anthocyanin and Flavonoid Oxidation:**

In rice bran oil and sesame oil, polyphenolic compounds oxidize to **quinones**, producing dark brown hues.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** Color changes are indicators of **multiple deterioration pathways simultaneously** — oxidation, hydrolysis, and Maillard browning often co-occur in stored oilseed meals.

---

#### **(b) Discuss the chemical principles and procedures for determining Peroxide Value (PV) and Acid Value (AV) of an oil sample. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

### **1. Peroxide Value (PV) — Iodometric Method:**

**Principle:**
Hydroperoxides (ROOH) in oxidized oil react with potassium iodide (KI) in acidic medium, liberating iodine (I₂). The liberated I₂ is titrated with standardized sodium thiosulfate (Na₂S₂O₃).

**Reactions:**

$$ROOH + 2 KI + 2 H^+ \rightarrow ROH + I_2 + H_2O + 2 K^+$$

$$I_2 + 2 Na_2S_2O_3 \rightarrow 2 NaI + Na_2S_4O_6$$

**Procedure:**
1. Weigh **5.0 g of oil** into a 250 mL conical flask.
2. Add **30 mL of acetic acid–chloroform (3:2 v/v)** solvent.
3. Add **0.5 mL of saturated KI solution**; shake for 1 minute; stand in dark for 5 minutes.
4. Add **30 mL distilled water**.
5. Titrate with **0.01 N Na₂S₂O₃** until pale yellow.
6. Add **0.5 mL starch indicator (1%)**; continue titration until blue color disappears.
7. Record volume $V_s$. Repeat without oil for blank $V_b$.

**Calculation:**

$$PV = \frac{(V_s - V_b) \times N \times 1000}{W} \quad \text{(meq O}_2\text{/kg)}$$

**Significance:** PV indicates **primary oxidation**. Values **< 5** are excellent, **5–10** good, **10–20** fair, **> 30** rancid.

### **2. Acid Value (AV) — Alkali Titration Method:**

**Principle:**
Free fatty acids in oil are neutralized by standardized KOH solution, using phenolphthalein as indicator.

**Reaction:**

$$R-COOH + KOH \rightarrow R-COOK + H_2O$$

**Procedure:**
1. Weigh **5.0 g of oil** into a 250 mL flask.
2. Add **50 mL of neutralized ethanol–diethyl ether (1:1)** solvent.
3. Heat gently to dissolve oil; add 2–3 drops of **phenolphthalein indicator**.
4. Titrate with **0.1 N KOH** until persistent pink color appears (15 sec).
5. Record volume $V$. Run blank if needed.

**Calculation:**

$$AV = \frac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W} \quad \text{(mg KOH/g oil)}$$

**Significance:** AV measures **hydrolytic deterioration**. For refined oils, AV < 0.5 is excellent, < 2 acceptable, > 4 poor quality.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Using **wrong normality** of thiosulfate (must be 0.01 N for PV).
> - **Common Mistake #2:** **Forgetting to subtract blank** value in PV calculation (loses 1 mark).
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing **AV (56.1 constant)** with **% FFA (28.2 constant)**.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Not mentioning **indicator used** (starch for PV, phenolphthalein for AV) — examiners deduct **0.5 mark** each.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam Preparation**

### 🔑 Must-Know Definitions
- **Rancidity:** Spoilage of fats/oils producing off-flavors via oxidation, hydrolysis, or microbial action.
- **PV:** meq O₂/kg of oil (iodometric method).
- **AV:** mg KOH/g of oil (alkali titration).
- **FFA:** % as oleic acid; related to AV by $FFA\,\% \approx AV/2$.
- **TBA Number:** mg malonaldehyde/kg (secondary oxidation indicator).
- **p-Anisidine Value:** Indicates aldehyde content (secondary oxidation).
- **Kries Test:** Qualitative test — red color = oxidized oil.

### 🧪 Mechanism Mastery
- **Initiation:** $RH \rightarrow R^{\bullet} + H^{\bullet}$
- **Propagation:** $R^{\bullet} + O_2 \rightarrow ROO^{\bullet} \rightarrow ROOH$
- **Termination:** Radical combination → dimers/polymers.
- **Hydrolysis:** Lipase + H₂O → FFA + glycerol.
- **Ketonic:** β-oxidation → methyl ketones (2-heptanone).

### 📊 Numerical Constants (Memorize!)
- 56.1 = MW of KOH (AV formula)
- 28.2 = 56.1/2 (FFA as oleic, %)
- 12.69 = atomic weight of iodine × 10/equivalent (IV formula)
- 1.1 = TBA conversion factor (A × 1.1)
- 1000 = unit conversion factor in PV formula
- 282 = MW of oleic acid

### 🛡️ Control Methods Summary
- **Primary antioxidants:** BHA, BHT, TBHQ, tocopherols (chain breakers).
- **Secondary antioxidants:** Citric acid, ascorbic acid (metal chelators).
- **Physical methods:** N₂ blanketing, vacuum packaging, dark storage.
- **Chemical methods:** Refining (degumming, neutralization, bleaching).
- **Storage conditions:** T < 25 °C, RH < 60%, moisture in seeds < 8%.

### ⚠️ KTU Exam Toppers' Tricks
- **Always draw a block diagram** for mechanisms (gets 1–2 extra marks).
- **Use subheadings** like "Principle → Procedure → Calculation → Significance."
- **Write units** in every numerical answer.
- **Mention indicators and reagents** in titration-based questions.
- **Cross-link** concepts: e.g., "PV measures primary oxidation; p-AV measures secondary; TOTOX = 2PV + p-AV is the comprehensive indicator."

### 🔬 Industrial Relevance
- FSSAI standards: PV < 10 meq O₂/kg, FFA < 3% (refined oils).
- Codex Alimentarius: PV < 10, p-AV < 4 (refined oils).
- Most common natural antioxidant: **α-tocopherol** (Vitamin E) — present in sunflower, safflower oils.
- Most potent synthetic antioxidant: **TBHQ** — added at **100–200 ppm** in commercial oils.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Applications: Soap, Candles, Paints, and Varnishes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT748 - FAT AND OIL PROCESSING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Industrial Applications: Soap, Candles, Paints, and Varnishes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Industrial Applications of Fats and Oils — Formal Definition

Fats and oils are triglyceride esters of fatty acids that serve as one of the most versatile renewable feedstocks in the chemical process industry. In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme for **PEFTT748**, the term *Industrial Applications* refers to the downstream conversion of refined fats, oils, and their fatty acid derivatives into four major non-food commercial products:

1. **Soap** — surface-active alkali salts of long-chain fatty acids used for cleaning.
2. **Candles** — shaped combustible solids made from fatty acid derivatives and hydrocarbons used for illumination.
3. **Paints** — pigmented surface coatings in which drying oils act as the film-forming binder.
4. **Varnishes** — transparent resin-oil coatings that form a protective and decorative film upon drying.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 specifically emphasizes the chemistry, raw-material selection, and manufacturing flow of these four product categories. Students must be able to relate the **fatty acid chain length, degree of unsaturation, and iodine value** of the parent oil to the end-use performance of the finished product.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Swiss Army Knife of the Chemical Industry"

Think of fats and oils as a **Swiss Army knife**. The same blade (a triglyceride molecule) can be:
- **Flipped open as a cleaning tool** → soap (when reacted with alkali, the glycerol backbone is cut away, and the fatty acid chain becomes a surfactant that lifts grease off your hands).
- **Folded into a fuel stick** → candles (when the fatty acids are hydrogenated or fractionated into stearin, they burn slowly with a steady flame).
- **Stretched as a paint binder** → paints (when unsaturated oils absorb oxygen and polymerize into a tough, glossy film that holds pigment particles together on a wall).
- **Polished into a clear coat** → varnishes (when the oil is cooked with natural or synthetic resins to form a transparent, water-resistant wood finish).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the same starting material behaves so differently:** The *degree of unsaturation* (number of C=C double bonds) of the fatty acid tail dictates the application. **Saturated fats** (low iodine value, e.g., coconut, palm) make hard soap and good candles. **Polyunsaturated oils** (high iodine value, e.g., linseed, tung) absorb oxygen aggressively and form tough films — ideal for paints and varnishes.

## 1.3 The Four Application Categories at a Glance

| Application | Primary Feedstock Type | Key Chemical Transformation | End-Use Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| Soap | Saturated & unsaturated oils/fats (coconut, palm, tallow) | Saponification with NaOH/KOH | Surface activity (detergency) |
| Candles | Stearin, paraffin wax, hydrogenated oils | Moulding / extrusion | Combustion with steady flame |
| Paints | Drying oils (linseed, tung, dehydrated castor) | Oxidative polymerization | Film formation, pigment binding |
| Varnishes | Drying/semi-drying oils + natural/synthetic resins | Resin-oil cooking + solvent thinning | Transparent, glossy protective film |

**Standard Metrics (must memorize):**
- **Saponification Value** = mg KOH required to saponify **1 g** of fat.
- **Iodine Value** = g of I₂ absorbed by **100 g** of fat.
- **Acid Value** = mg KOH required to neutralize free fatty acids in **1 g** of fat.
- **Oil Absorption Value (OAV)** of pigment = g of oil required to wet **100 g** of pigment (typically **15–60 g/100 g**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SOAP — Chemistry, Classification, and Manufacturing Logic

### 2.1.1 Saponification Reaction (Core Chemistry)

When a triglyceride (fat/oil) is boiled with a strong alkali (NaOH for hard bar soap, KOH for soft/liquid soap), the ester bonds are hydrolyzed, releasing glycerol and forming the alkali salt of the fatty acid — which **is** soap.

The general reversible reaction is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{NaOH} &\longrightarrow 3\,\text{RCOONa} + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 \\
\text{(Fat)} \quad\quad\quad\;\; (\text{Caustic Soda}) &\quad\quad (\text{Soap}) \quad\quad\;\; (\text{Glycerol})
\end{aligned}
$$

where R = long-chain alkyl group (C₁₁ to C₁₇) of the fatty acid.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU frequently asked concept:** Sodium salts of fatty acids give **hard bar soap**; potassium salts give **soft, gel-like soap** (green soap, shaving cream base).

### 2.1.2 Steps in Kettle (Boiling) Process of Soap Manufacture

1. **Charging the kettle** — melted fat + caustic lye (≈ 8–12% NaOH) are added.
2. **Saponification stage** — mixture is boiled with open steam; emulsion forms and gradually thickens.
3. **Grain formation / Salting out** — common salt (NaCl) is sprinkled in; soap becomes insoluble and floats as granular "grain" over the lower lye layer (which contains glycerol and impurities).
4. **Glycerol recovery** — lower lye is withdrawn, acidified, and distilled to recover pure glycerol (a valuable by-product).
5. **Strong change / Fitting** — soap is boiled again with water and alkali to complete saponification and adjust texture.
6. **Settling and framing** — purified soap is poured into frames, cooled, cut, and stamped.

### 2.1.3 Cold Process vs. Hot Process

- **Cold process** — used for premium toilet soaps; fat and lye are mixed at ≈ 40–50 °C; reaction is slower; preserves glycerin in the bar (gives a milder feel).
- **Hot process / Kettle process** — used for industrial laundry soaps; faster, more complete saponification, salt-out purification.

## 2.2 CANDLES — Wax Chemistry and Manufacture

### 2.2.1 Classification of Candle Waxes

- **Paraffin wax** — petroleum-derived hydrocarbon (C₂₀–C₄₀), most common.
- **Stearin (stearic acid / palmitic acid blend)** — derived from hydrolyzed fat; gives an opaque, slow-burning, non-drip candle.
- **Beeswax** — natural wax from honeycombs; expensive, aromatic.
- **Hydrogenated oils** — fully saturated vegetable oils (e.g., hydrogenated soybean oil) can substitute paraffin.
- **Microcrystalline wax** — finer crystal structure; improves flexibility and gloss.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why stearin improves candles:** Stearin has a higher melting point (≈ 69 °C) and crystallizes in shiny, interlocking plates. This reduces the molten pool, eliminates dripping, and imparts a smooth, glossy finish — exactly what premium "dinner-candle" quality demands.

### 2.2.2 Candle Manufacturing Steps (Moulding Method)

1. Wax is melted in a steam-jacketed kettle (≈ 80–90 °C).
2. Dye and fragrance are blended in.
3. Pre-waxed wicks are centered in moulds.
4. Molten wax is poured into moulds and allowed to cool slowly.
5. Cured candles are withdrawn, trimmed, and polished.

## 2.3 PAINTS — Composition and Function of Drying Oils

### 2.3.1 Generic Paint Formulation (Percentage by Mass)

- **Binder / Vehicle (Drying oil)** — 20–35 %
- **Pigment (colour + hiding power)** — 15–40 %
- **Solvent / Thinner (mineral turpentine, white spirit)** — 25–40 %
- **Driers (metal salts — Co, Mn, Pb naphthenates)** — 0.5–2 %
- **Additives** (anti-skin, anti-settling, biocides) — 1–5 %

### 2.3.2 Mechanism of Film Formation (Oxidative Polymerization)

Drying oils contain polyunsaturated fatty acids (linolenic, linoleic, eleostearic). Upon exposure to atmospheric O₂, the C=C double bonds undergo:
- **Oxygen uptake** at the α-methylene position (between two double bonds).
- **Crosslinking** via peroxide bridges to form a 3-D polymer network.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical implication:** Linseed oil (Iodine Value ≈ 170–190) dries fastest. Tung oil (IV ≈ 160) dries to a water-resistant, matte film. Soybean oil (IV ≈ 125) is too slow for use as a stand-alone binder and is usually blended with a fast-drying oil.

## 2.4 VARNISHES — Resin-Oil Cooking

### 2.4.1 Definition and Distinction from Paint

A **varnish** is a transparent, pigment-free coating consisting of a **resin** dissolved in a **drying oil** and thinned with a **solvent**. It provides gloss, hardness, and protection without changing the substrate colour — used on wood, metal, and as a final clear coat over painted surfaces.

### 2.4.2 Component Roles

- **Resin** (natural: rosin, copal, dammar, shellac; synthetic: alkyd, phenolic, epoxy) — provides hardness, gloss, and chemical resistance.
- **Drying oil** (linseed, tung, dehydrated castor) — provides flexibility, adhesion, and film-forming continuity.
- **Solvent** (mineral turpentine, naphtha, xylene) — controls viscosity and drying rate.
- **Driers** (Co, Mn, Pb salts) — accelerate oxidative cure.

### 2.4.3 The "Oil Length" Concept (CRITICAL for KTU)

**Oil length** is the most important varnish formulation parameter. It is defined as:

$$
\text{Oil Length (\%)} = \frac{\text{Mass of drying oil}}{\text{Mass of (oil + resin)}} \times 100
$$

| Oil Length Range | Type | Application | Film Character |
|---|---|---|---|
| **< 25 %** | Short-oil varnish | Indoor surfaces, quick dry | Hard, brittle, gloss |
| **25 – 50 %** | Medium-oil varnish | General purpose, wood finishes | Balanced hardness and flexibility |
| **> 50 %** | Long-oil varnish | Exterior surfaces, marine use | Flexible, weather-resistant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Numerical problems often ask you to calculate oil length or to reformulate a varnish to a target oil length. Always keep mass balance clean and round only at the final step.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the four formulas below. They cover ≥ 70% of numerical questions asked on this topic.

| Formula | Expression | Used For | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Saponification Value | $SV = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \cdot N \cdot 56.1}{W}$ | Soap yield estimation | mg KOH / g |
| Acid Value | $AV = \dfrac{V \cdot N \cdot 56.1}{W}$ | FFA content in oil | mg KOH / g |
| Iodine Value (Wijs) | $IV = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \cdot N \cdot 12.69}{W}$ | Degree of unsaturation | g I₂ / 100 g |
| Oil Length of Varnish | $OL\,\% = \dfrac{m_{\text{oil}}}{m_{\text{oil}} + m_{\text{resin}}} \times 100$ | Varnish formulation | % (dimensionless) |
| Oil Absorption of Pigment | $OAV = \dfrac{m_{\text{oil}}}{m_{\text{pigment}}} \times 100$ | Paint consistency | g oil / 100 g pigment |
| Degree of Cure (Drying Time) | $t \propto \dfrac{1}{[\text{O}_2]\,\cdot\,[\text{drier}]}$ | Quality control of paint films | hours |

> Where **V_b** = blank titre value, **V_s** = sample titre value, **N** = normality of titrant (KOH or Na₂S₂O₃), **W** = mass of sample in grams, and **56.1** = equivalent weight of KOH (or NaOH), **12.69** = equivalent weight of iodine / 10.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Estimating Glycerol Yield from Soap Manufacture

**Problem (KTU-style):** A soap kettle is charged with **1000 kg** of a triglyceride oil. The saponification value of the oil is **205 mg KOH/g**. Calculate the theoretical yield of glycerol obtained if 90% of the triglyceride is saponified.

### Step 1: Convert saponification value to mean molecular weight of the fat

The saponification value is inversely proportional to the molecular weight of the fat. From the reaction stoichiometry, 1 mole of fat requires 3 moles of KOH (molecular weight 56.1 g/mol).

$$
\begin{aligned}
SV &= \frac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{M_{\text{fat}}} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow M_{\text{fat}} &= \frac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{SV} \\[4pt]
M_{\text{fat}} &= \frac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{205} \\[4pt]
M_{\text{fat}} &= \frac{168300}{205} \\[4pt]
M_{\text{fat}} &\approx 821.0 \;\text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Calculate moles of fat charged

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{fat}} &= \frac{m}{M_{\text{fat}}} \\[4pt]
n_{\text{fat}} &= \frac{1000 \times 1000 \;\text{g}}{821.0 \;\text{g/mol}} \\[4pt]
n_{\text{fat}} &\approx 1218.0 \;\text{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Stoichiometry — 1 mole of fat produces 1 mole of glycerol

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{glycerol, theoretical}} &= 1218.0 \;\text{mol} \\[4pt]
m_{\text{glycerol, theoretical}} &= n \times M_{\text{glycerol}} \\[4pt]
m_{\text{glycerol, theoretical}} &= 1218.0 \times 92.0 \;\text{g} \\[4pt]
m_{\text{glycerol, theoretical}} &\approx 112056 \;\text{g} \approx 112.06 \;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Apply 90% conversion

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{glycerol, actual}} &= 112.06 \times 0.90 \\[4pt]
m_{\text{glycerol, actual}} &\approx 100.85 \;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Theoretical glycerol yield ≈ **100.85 kg** per 1000 kg of oil charged.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic explained:** Each triglyceride contains one glycerol "backbone" (C₃H₅ moiety, MW ≈ 41) plus three fatty acid chains. When hydrolyzed, the glycerol is released and immediately picks up 3 H atoms from the surrounding water to become free glycerol (C₃H₅(OH)₃, MW = **92 g/mol**). The saponification value tells you the *molecular weight* of the fat indirectly — higher SV means smaller, shorter-chain fats (like coconut oil, SV ≈ 250), while lower SV means longer-chain fats (like rapeseed oil, SV ≈ 175).

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Varnish Oil Length Reformulation

**Problem (KTU-style):** A varnish is formulated with **40 kg** of linseed oil and **20 kg** of phenolic resin. The technical team wants to convert this into a **long-oil varnish (60% oil length)** for an exterior wood-finish application. How much additional linseed oil must be added?

### Step 1: Calculate the existing oil length

$$
\begin{aligned}
OL_{\text{old}} &= \frac{m_{\text{oil}}}{m_{\text{oil}} + m_{\text{resin}}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
OL_{\text{old}} &= \frac{40}{40 + 20} \times 100 \\[4pt]
OL_{\text{old}} &= \frac{40}{60} \times 100 \\[4pt]
OL_{\text{old}} &\approx 66.67\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Hold on — that's already > 60%.** Let's verify the requirement: the target is **60% oil length**. Since 66.67% > 60%, the existing varnish is *more* long-oil than required. The team actually needs to **reduce the oil length**, not increase it. The question is mis-stated. Let me reformulate correctly:

### Step 1 (Corrected): Calculate the existing oil length

The existing oil length is **66.67%**, which is already in the long-oil range.

### Step 2: Determine how much resin to add to reach exactly 60% oil length

Let $x$ = additional resin mass to be added (kg). Total oil stays at 40 kg; total resin becomes $(20 + x)$ kg.

$$
\begin{aligned}
OL_{\text{new}} &= \frac{40}{40 + (20 + x)} \times 100 = 60 \\[4pt]
\frac{40}{60 + x} \times 100 &= 60 \\[4pt]
\frac{4000}{60 + x} &= 60 \\[4pt]
4000 &= 60(60 + x) \\[4pt]
4000 &= 3600 + 60x \\[4pt]
4000 - 3600 &= 60x \\[4pt]
400 &= 60x \\[4pt]
x &= 6.67 \;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Verify

$$
\begin{aligned}
OL_{\text{verify}} &= \frac{40}{40 + (20 + 6.67)} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{40}{66.67} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&\approx 60.0\,\% \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Add **6.67 kg of phenolic resin** (do not add more oil). The final batch composition is **40 kg oil + 26.67 kg resin**, giving exactly **60% oil length**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** When asked to "convert" a varnish to a different oil length, students reflexively add the component they think is deficient. Always recheck whether the existing oil length is *above* or *below* the target before deciding which component to add.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Oil for the Right Application

| Oil Source | Iodine Value (g I₂/100 g) | Saturation | Best Suited Application | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Coconut oil | **7 – 12** | Highly saturated | Hard bar soap, premium toilet soap | High SV → rich lather; saturated → white, hard bar |
| Palm oil | **50 – 55** | Moderately saturated | Laundry soap, candles (stearin source) | Balanced chain length; crystallizes well |
| Tallow (animal) | **40 – 48** | Saturated | Hard soap, tallow candle | Stearin-rich; pale colour after refining |
| Linseed oil | **170 – 190** | Highly polyunsaturated | Paints, varnishes, linoleum | Three C=C bonds → fast oxidative drying |
| Tung oil | **160 – 170** | Polyunsaturated (conjugated) | Water-resistant varnish, marine paint | Conjugated double bonds → rapid polymerization |
| Soybean oil | **120 – 130** | Moderately unsaturated | Alkyd resin manufacture, blended paints | Two C=C bonds → moderate drying, good flexibility |
| Castor oil (dehydrated) | **125 – 140** | Conjugated after dehydration | Heat-resistant varnish, PU intermediates | Ricinoleic acid → drying oil after dehydration |
| Olive oil | **80 – 88** | Mostly monounsaturated | Castile soap (mild, green) | Oleic acid → mild soap; not a drying oil |
| Rapeseed (canola) | **110 – 125** | Monounsaturated (erucic-poor) | Biodiesel, soft soap | Erucic acid → soft soap; not a drying oil |
| Palm olein | **55 – 60** | Liquid fraction of palm | Liquid hand soap | Stays liquid at room temperature |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Soap Manufacturing (Kettle Process)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Fat Charge] --> B[Melting and Filtration]
    B --> C[Kettle with Steam Coils]
    C --> D[Saponification with NaOH 8 to 12 percent]
    D --> E[Grain Formation with NaCl]
    E --> F[Salting Out: Soap Grain Floats]
    F --> G[Glycerol Lye Withdrawal]
    G --> H[Glycerol Purification and Distillation]
    F --> I[Strong Change Boiling with Water]
    I --> J[Settling and Fitting]
    J --> K[Framing and Cooling]
    K --> L[Cutting and Stamping]
    L --> M[Finished Bar Soap]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style M fill:#90EE90
    style H fill:#ADD8E6
    style D fill:#FFB6C1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **A → D:** Feedstock preparation and primary reaction.
- **D → E → F:** Phase separation — soap precipitates as "grain" on salting out.
- **F → G → H:** By-product recovery stream (glycerol is more valuable per kg than crude soap).
- **F → I → J → M:** Main product stream (purified soap) to finished bar.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Paint / Varnish Manufacturing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Drying Oil Charge] --> M1[Oil Reactor with Stirrer and Heater]
    R2[Resin Charge Natural or Synthetic] --> M1
    M1 --> CK[Resin Oil Cooking at 200 to 280 degree C]
    CK --> CT[Cook Test for String and Clarity]
    CT --> TH[Thinning with Mineral Turpentine]
    R3[Pigment and Filler Charge] --> MM[Pigment Dispersion Mill Ball Mill or Sand Mill]
    MM --> BL[Let Down and Mixing with Vehicle]
    TH --> BL
    R4[Driers and Additives] --> BL
    BL --> ST[Straining and Quality Check]
    ST --> CN[Canning and Labeling]
    CN --> FG[Finished Paint or Varnish]

    style R1 fill:#FFE4B5
    style R2 fill:#FFE4B5
    style R3 fill:#FFE4B5
    style R4 fill:#FFE4B5
    style FG fill:#90EE90
    style CK fill:#FFB6C1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **R1 + R2 → M1 → CK:** The "hot" or "cooking" stage — the chemistry of the vehicle is set here.
- **CT:** Critical quality control checkpoint — varnish string length is tested manually.
- **R3 → MM → BL:** The "cold" or "let-down" stage — pigments are dispersed and blended into the vehicle.
- **TH → BL:** The cooked resin-oil is thinned *before* being mixed with the pigment paste, to ensure proper dispersion.

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Selection Logic for Oil vs. Resin vs. Wax

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Fat or Oil Feedstock] --> Q1{Saturated or Unsaturated?}
    Q1 -->|Saturated IV below 50| Q2{Chain Length and Appearance}
    Q1 -->|Unsaturated IV above 100| Q3{Drying Oil Potential?}

    Q2 -->|Hard wax like palm or tallow| SOAP1[Soap Hard Bar]
    Q2 -->|Fractionate to stearin| CAND[Candles Premium Dinner Type]
    Q2 -->|Coconut rich lauric| SOAP2[Toilet Soap High Suds]

    Q3 -->|Dries to film quickly IV above 160| PAINT[Paints and Varnishes]
    Q3 -->|Moderate IV around 120| ALKYD[Alkyd Resin for Enamel]
    Q3 -->|Low drying tendency IV below 100| LUB[Lubricant or Plasticizer]

    style START fill:#FFE4B5
    style SOAP1 fill:#90EE90
    style SOAP2 fill:#90EE90
    style CAND fill:#90EE90
    style PAINT fill:#90EE90
    style ALKYD fill:#90EE90
    style LUB fill:#ADD8E6
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The single decision point `Q1` (saturated vs unsaturated) is the most critical industrial sorting criterion.
- **Saturated branch:** leads to cleaning and illumination products (soap, candles).
- **Unsaturated branch:** leads to coating products (paint, varnish, alkyd).
- **Oil with IV between 100 and 160:** too slow for paints, too valuable for lubricants → route to **alkyd resins** (the workhorse of the modern enamel industry).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **saturated** and **unsaturated** drying oils. Give **two examples** of each and state which type is preferred for paint manufacture. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

- **Saturated drying oils** contain predominantly single C–C bonds; they do **not** absorb oxygen appreciably and therefore do not form films. *Examples:* coconut oil, palm oil.
- **Unsaturated drying oils** contain C=C double bonds (mono-, di-, or poly-unsaturated). They react with atmospheric O₂ to undergo oxidative polymerization, forming a tough, continuous film. *Examples:* linseed oil (polyunsaturated), tung oil (conjugated polyunsaturated).
- **Paint manufacture requires unsaturated drying oils** — particularly polyunsaturated oils such as **linseed oil (IV ≈ 180)** or **tung oil (IV ≈ 165)** — because the multiple C=C bonds are essential for crosslinking and film formation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's pitfall:** Students often confuse "drying" (solvent evaporation) with "curing" (chemical film formation). Drying oils "dry" by *chemical cure*, not merely by solvent loss. State this distinction clearly to earn full marks.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define **saponification value**. What does a **high saponification value** indicate about the parent fat? *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

- **Definition:** The saponification value (SV) is the number of milligrams of **KOH** required to completely saponify **1 gram** of the fat.
- **Interpretation of a high SV:** A high SV (e.g., coconut oil SV ≈ 250) means a **smaller mean molecular weight**, i.e., the fatty acid chains are **shorter** (more lauric/myristic, fewer stearic/oleic).
- **Practical consequence:** Short-chain fats yield soap with **richer lather** and better solubility — hence the use of coconut and palm kernel oils in **toilet and shaving soaps**.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 End-Semester Examination (ESE) regulations, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice between two questions on the same module. Both Question A and Question B below are independent and carry full marks.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]** Discuss in detail the **raw materials, chemistry, and manufacturing process of toilet soap** by the **kettle (boiling) process**. Highlight the role of **(a)** caustic alkali, **(b)** common salt, and **(c)** the **oil length** concept does not apply here — instead, explain the **TFM (Total Fatty Matter)** concept. *(14 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Raw materials (4 marks)**
- **Fat blend:** typically **80% tallow** (saturated, gives hardness) + **20% coconut oil** (saturated short-chain, gives lather). Palm oil and palm stearin are modern substitutes.
- **Caustic alkali:** **NaOH** (sodium hydroxide) for hard bar soap; concentration 8–12% w/w in the kettle.
- **Salt (NaCl):** used for "salting out" — precipitates soap from the glycerol-lye mixture.
- **Water:** for lye preparation and reaction medium.
- **Additives:** perfume, colour, antioxidants (BHT), optical brighteners, glycerin (for transparency), rosin (for lather stability).

**(b) Chemistry of saponification (4 marks)**
- Reaction: $\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{NaOH} \rightarrow 3\,\text{RCOONa} + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3$
- **Reversible** in early stages; complete saponification requires **excess alkali** and heat.
- Side reaction: hydrolysis of free fatty acids (FFA) in the fat to form additional soap.

**(c) Manufacturing steps of kettle process (4 marks)**
1. **Charging:** fat is melted and filtered into the kettle.
2. **Saponification:** dilute NaOH is added gradually; steam is bubbled; emulsion forms.
3. **Graining:** NaCl is sprinkled in; soap separates as "grain"; lower lye (containing glycerol) is drawn off.
4. **Strong change:** grain is re-boiled with fresh alkali and water to complete saponification.
5. **Fitting and settling:** soap is adjusted to the right texture and allowed to settle.
6. **Framing and finishing:** molten soap is poured into frames, cooled, cut into bars, and stamped.

**(d) Total Fatty Matter (TFM) concept (2 marks)**
- TFM = mass of fatty acid + mass of soap (anhydrous basis) per 100 g of finished soap.
- **Premium toilet soap** requires TFM ≥ **76%** (BIS standard IS 2888).
- **Grade-1 soap:** TFM ≥ 76%; **Grade-2:** 60–75%; **Grade-3:** ≤ 60% (often laundry soap).
- Higher TFM = less water and filler = better quality, milder feel, longer-lasting bar.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common loss of marks (1–2 marks):** Students frequently forget to write the **stoichiometric reaction** and the **TFM standard values**. Examiners specifically look for these two — write them clearly.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]** Explain the **composition, function of each component, and manufacturing process of an exterior long-oil varnish**. Discuss the **oil length** concept with a numerical example. *(14 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Definition and classification (2 marks)**
- Varnish is a **transparent, pigment-free** coating of **resin + drying oil + solvent + driers**.
- Classified by oil length: short (<25%), medium (25–50%), **long (>50%)**.
- Exterior long-oil varnish uses linseed or tung oil; designed for **flexibility, weather resistance, and UV durability**.

**(b) Composition and role of each component (4 marks)**
- **Drying oil (linseed oil, 55–65%):** imparts flexibility, adhesion, and forms the crosslinked film.
- **Resin (natural copal / synthetic alkyd / phenolic, 35–45%):** imparts hardness, gloss, and chemical resistance.
- **Solvent (mineral turpentine, 30–40% of total):** controls viscosity and evaporation rate.
- **Driers (Co, Mn, Pb naphthenates, 0.5–2%):** accelerate oxidative cure.
- **Additives** (anti-skin agent MEKO, UV stabilizers): prevent skinning in the can and yellowing on exposure.

**(c) Manufacturing process (4 marks)**
1. **Oil heating:** linseed oil is heated to 200–250 °C in a stainless-steel reactor.
2. **Resin addition:** copal/alkyd resin is added gradually; temperature rises to 270–290 °C ("cooking").
3. **Cook test:** a drop is cooled on a glass plate and pulled — the **string length** indicates the molecular weight of the polymer.
4. **Cooling and thinning:** cooked mass is cooled to 150 °C, and mineral turpentine is added slowly with stirring.
5. **Drier addition:** cobalt/manganese/lead naphthenates are added at ≤ 80 °C.
6. **Filtration and packaging:** strained through cheesecloth or bag filter; packed in airtight containers.

**(d) Numerical example — oil length (4 marks)**

*Given:* A varnish is to be formulated with **55 kg of linseed oil** and **45 kg of phenolic resin**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
OL &= \frac{m_{\text{oil}}}{m_{\text{oil}} + m_{\text{resin}}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
OL &= \frac{55}{55 + 45} \times 100 \\[4pt]
OL &= \frac{55}{100} \times 100 \\[4pt]
OL &= 55\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Since **55% > 50%**, this is a **long-oil varnish**, suitable for **exterior wood finishes, marine coatings, and structural steel primers** where flexibility and weather resistance are required.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common loss of marks:** Students often confuse the **denominator** of the oil-length formula. It is **(oil + resin)**, not the *total batch mass* (which includes solvent and driers). The oil length is defined on the **non-volatile vehicle basis** — solvent and driers are excluded from the denominator.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4**

- ☐ **Soap** is the alkali salt of a fatty acid; **NaOH → hard bar**, **KOH → soft/liquid soap**.
- ☐ Saponification releases **1 mole of glycerol per mole of triglyceride** (MW = **92 g/mol**).
- ☐ **Kettle process stages:** charge → saponify → grain → salt out → strong change → fit → frame → cut.
- ☐ **TFM** (Total Fatty Matter) ≥ **76%** = Grade-1 toilet soap (BIS IS 2888).
- ☐ **Candles** are made primarily from **paraffin wax + stearin**; stearin improves gloss and prevents dripping.
- ☐ **Paints** = pigment + drying oil + solvent + driers + additives. Drying oil acts as the **binder / vehicle**.
- ☐ **Drying oil film formation** is via **oxidative polymerization** at C=C double bonds (α-methylene oxidation).
- ☐ **Linseed oil (IV ≈ 180)** is the workhorse drying oil; **tung oil** gives water-resistant films; **soybean oil** is too slow alone.
- ☐ **Varnish** = resin + drying oil + solvent + driers (no pigment) — gives a **transparent** film.
- ☐ **Oil length** = mass of oil / (mass of oil + mass of resin) × 100. **< 25%** short, **25–50%** medium, **> 50%** long.
- ☐ Always remember the **four key formulas**: SV, AV, IV, OL — and the constants **56.1** (KOH equiv. wt.) and **12.69** (I₂/10 equiv. wt.).
- ☐ **Long-oil varnish** is for **exterior** use (flexible, weather-proof); **short-oil** is for **interior** (hard, glossy, fast-drying).
- ☐ When asked to **adjust oil length**, first check whether the *current* value is above or below the *target* before deciding to add oil or resin.
- ☐ **Memorize the analytical matrix** (Section 3.3) — KTU loves to ask "which oil is best suited for which application?"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Regulations: ISI and AGMARK Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT748 - FAT AND OIL PROCESSING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quality Regulations: ISI and AGMARK Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Quality Regulations in Fats & Oils?

**Quality Regulations** are legally enforceable standards, specifications, and grading systems established by national and international bodies to ensure that edible fats and oils meet defined criteria of purity, safety, composition, and labelling before reaching consumers. In India, the two foundational pillars of food quality regulation are the **ISI (Indian Standards Institution)** standards, now administered by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**, and the **AGMARK (Agricultural Marketing)** standards administered under the **Agricultural Produce (Grading and Marking) Act, 1937**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Quality Regulations* refer to the codified set of mandatory and voluntary standards — chemical, physical, microbiological, and packaging — that govern the production, distribution, sale, and export of oils, fats, and oilseed products. These are enforced by statutory bodies like **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)**, **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)**, and the **Directorate of Marketing and Inspection (DMI)** for AGMARK.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you walk into a restaurant and order "pure ghee." Without standards, how would you know if what you receive is genuinely pure, free of adulterants, and safe to consume? **Quality regulations act like a "trust seal" backed by law.** They are like the **grading system for diamonds or the hall-marking of gold** — a certified assurance that the product inside the tin matches what is promised on the label. **ISI** is the technical quality specification (like the recipe and purity criteria), while **AGMARK** is the *commercial grade* label (like "Grade A," "Special," "General") that helps consumers identify quality tiers at a glance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Statutory Bodies at a Glance (Must Remember for KTU):**
> - **BIS** (Bureau of Indian Standards) — formerly ISI, sets IS standards (e.g., IS 5430 for refined groundnut oil).
> - **AGMARK** — Grades agricultural commodities including ghee, mustard oil, and oilseeds.
> - **FSSAI** — Apex food safety regulator under the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006.
> - **Codex Alimentarius** — International FAO/WHO food standards body.

### 1.3 Evolution and Authority

The **Indian Standards Institution (ISI)** was established in **1947** and was renamed the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** in **1987** under the Bureau of Indian Standards Act, **1986**. BIS operates under the Ministry of Consumer Affairs, Food and Public Distribution, Government of India. The use of the **ISI mark** is voluntary for most food items but becomes mandatory when notified by the central government.

The **AGMARK** system, older than ISI, was introduced through the **Agricultural Produce (Grading and Marking) Act, 1937**, and is administered by the **Directorate of Marketing and Inspection (DMI)** under the Ministry of Agriculture. AGMARK certification is also voluntary unless made compulsory by law.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Reference:**
> - **BIS Act, 1986** → governs ISI mark.
> - **Agricultural Produce (Grading and Marking) Act, 1937 (amended 1986)** → governs AGMARK.
> - **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006** → governs FSSAI; supersedes the Prevention of Food Adulteration Act (PFA), 1954.

### 1.4 Why Quality Regulations Matter in Fat & Oil Technology

The fat and oil industry is uniquely vulnerable to adulteration (e.g., mineral oil in edible oil, argemone oil in mustard oil, Vanaspati mislabelling as ghee) due to high commercial value and the technical difficulty of visual detection. Standards thus serve **three critical functions**:

1. **Consumer Protection** — prevents adulteration and ensures food safety.
2. **Trade Facilitation** — harmonizes specifications for domestic and export markets.
3. **Industry Self-Discipline** — provides benchmarks for process control, refining, packaging, and shelf-life claims.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ISI (BIS) Standards — Structure and Operation

The **Bureau of Indian Standards** formulates standards through a consensus process involving industry experts, scientific institutions, government bodies, and consumer organizations. Each standard is identified by an alphanumeric code: **IS [Number]: [Year]**, where the number is a sequential identifier and the year denotes the latest revision.

For fats and oils, BIS has issued dedicated standards such as:
- **IS 5430: 1999 (Reaffirmed 2014)** — Refined Groundnut Oil specifications.
- **IS 4276: 1988** — Refined Cottonseed Oil.
- **IS 14832: 2000** — Refined Palm Oil.
- **IS 4275: 1977** — Refined Soybean Oil.
- **IS 3491: 1992** — Vanaspati (partially hydrogenated vegetable oil).
- **IS 3490: 1992** — Refined Rapeseed/Mustard Oil.
- **IS 3508: 1989** — Refined Coconut Oil.
- **IS 4932: 1978** — Refined Sunflower Oil.
- **IS 13685: 1994** — Refined Rice Bran Oil.
- **IS 14543: 1998** — Refined Palmolein.
- **IS 17962: 2022** — Refined Olive Oil (newer entry).
- **IS 1169: 1958** — Hydrogenated Oil (Vanaspati) — for industrial use.

### 2.2 BIS Certification Process — Operational Logic

The BIS certification process for the ISI mark follows these structured steps:

1. **Application** — Manufacturer submits a formal application with product details.
2. **Document Review** — BIS scrutinizes the manufacturing process, raw material sourcing, and quality control protocols.
3. **Factory Inspection** — BIS officers conduct on-site audits of the production facility.
4. **Sample Testing** — Finished product samples are drawn and tested in BIS-recognized or in-house labs against the relevant IS specification.
5. **Grant of License** — If compliant, a license to use the ISI mark is issued (typically valid for 1–5 years).
6. **Surveillance Audits** — Periodic re-inspection ensures continued compliance.
7. **Renewal / Cancellation** — License may be suspended or revoked upon non-compliance.

### 2.3 Quality Parameters Mandated by BIS for Edible Oils

Each BIS standard for a refined edible oil specifies the following critical parameters:

- **Moisture and Insoluble Impurities (%)**
- **Free Fatty Acid (FFA) content**, expressed as oleic acid equivalent (%).
- **Iodine Value (Wijs method)**, g I₂/100 g — indicates unsaturation.
- **Saponification Value** — average molecular weight indicator.
- **Unsaponifiable Matter (%)**
- **Refractive Index at 40 °C**
- **Colour in a 1-inch Lovibond cell (Y + R)**
- **Peroxide Value (meq O₂/kg)** — oxidative deterioration.
- **Test for Argemone Oil** (must be absent in mustard oil).
- **Test for Mineral Oil** (must be absent).
- **Test for Rancidity** (Kreis test or peroxide value).
- **Heavy metals** (lead, arsenic, cadmium — in ppm).
- **Aflatoxin levels** (for groundnut and unrefined oils).
- **Pesticide residue limits.**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Must-Know Numerical Limits (Sample — Refined Groundnut Oil, IS 5430):**
> - **FFA (as oleic acid): max 0.10 %**
> - **Iodine Value: 85–99 g I₂/100 g**
> - **Saponification Value: 188–196**
> - **Unsaponifiable Matter: max 1.0 %**
> - **Moisture & Insoluble Impurities: max 0.10 %**
> - **Colour: max 10 Y / 1.0 R (Lovibond 1-inch cell)**
> - **Test for Argemone Oil: Negative**

### 2.4 AGMARK Standards — Theory and Operation

**AGMARK** is a certification mark applied to agricultural commodities, including certain edible oils and ghee. Its purpose is to certify the **grade** of the product (e.g., "Special," "Grade 1," "Grade 2," "General"), and it is enforced by the **Directorate of Marketing and Inspection (DMI)** with state-level **AGMARK Laboratories**.

AGMARK covers a focused set of commodities under the Grading and Marking Rules. For fats and oils, key AGMARK-graded commodities include:

- **Ghee** (cow ghee, buffalo ghee, mixed ghee) — AGMARK rules exist for grading.
- **Mustard Oil** — Kachhi Ghani and Expeller varieties.
- **Oilseeds** — groundnut, sesame, linseed, rapeseed, castor seed.
- **Edible Oils** — in selected cases.

### 2.5 AGMARK Grade Designations

For each commodity, AGMARK prescribes **grade designations** based on physico-chemical and sensory quality. For example:

- **Ghee AGMARK grades**: Special, Grade I, Grade II, General.
- **Mustard Oil**: Grade I, Grade II (with free fatty acid limits).
- **Groundnut (in shell/kernel)**: Special, Grade I, Grade II, Grade III.

The grading criteria typically include:
- **Physical characteristics** (colour, texture, appearance).
- **Moisture content** (max %).
- **FFA content** (max %).
- **Iodine value / saponification value** (range).
- **Refractive index** (specific range).
- **Butyro-refractometer reading (BR reading)** at 40 °C — for ghee.
- **Reichert-Meissl value and Polenske value** — for ghee to detect adulteration with animal fats or vanaspati.
- **Mineral oil / argemone oil tests** — must be absent.
- **Sediment / insoluble impurities** (max %).
- **Microbiological limits** (especially for ghee).

### 2.6 Comparative Distinction — ISI vs AGMARK

| **Attribute** | **ISI / BIS Standard** | **AGMARK Standard** |
|---|---|---|
| **Statutory Act** | BIS Act, 1986 | Agricultural Produce (Grading and Marking) Act, 1937 |
| **Administering Body** | Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) | Directorate of Marketing & Inspection (DMI) |
| **Coverage Scope** | Broad — industrial + consumer goods including all edible oils | Limited to agricultural commodities (ghee, mustard oil, oilseeds) |
| **Marking Style** | ISI Mark (ISI logo + licence number) | AGMARK label with grade designation + signature of authorised packer |
| **Voluntary / Mandatory** | Voluntary (mandatory when notified) | Voluntary (mandatory when notified) |
| **Focus** | Detailed specification of physico-chemical quality parameters | Grade designation based on overall quality level |
| **Period of Origin** | 1947 (as ISI); renamed BIS in 1987 | 1937 (oldest quality certification in India) |

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Definition** | **Units** | **Significance** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Free Fatty Acid (FFA) | $\text{FFA (\% as oleic acid)} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 28.2}{W}$ | % | Measures hydrolytic rancidity |
| Acid Value (AV) | $\text{AV} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W}$ | mg KOH/g | Direct measure of acidity |
| Iodine Value (IV) | $\text{IV} = \dfrac{(B - S) \times N \times 12.69}{W}$ | g I₂/100 g | Degree of unsaturation |
| Saponification Value (SV) | $\text{SV} = \dfrac{(B - S) \times N \times 56.1}{W}$ | mg KOH/g | Average M.W. indicator |
| Peroxide Value (PV) | $\text{PV (meq O}_2\text{/kg)} = \dfrac{(S - B) \times N \times 1000}{W}$ | meq O₂/kg | Oxidative deterioration |
| Unsaponifiable Matter | $\text{UM (\%)} = \dfrac{W_R}{W_S} \times 100$ | % | Sterols, tocopherols, hydrocarbons |
| Reichert-Meissl Value (RMV) | Distillation-based titration of steam-volatile fatty acids | mL 0.1 N KOH per 5 g | Detects butterfat in ghee |
| Polenske Value (PV) | Titration of water-insoluble steam-volatile fatty acids | mL 0.1 N KOH per 5 g | Detects coconut/palm kernel fat |
| Kirschner Value | Further separation and titration of soluble silver salts | mL 0.1 N KOH per 5 g | Differentiates butterfat from other fats |
| B.R. Reading | Refractive index reading on butyro-refractometer at 40 °C | dimensionless | Rapid purity check for ghee |
| Moisture & Volatile Matter | Hot air oven method, 105 °C | % | Shelf-life determinant |

> **Where:** $V$ = titrant volume (mL); $N$ = normality; $W$ = sample weight (g); $B$ = blank reading; $S$ = sample reading; $W_R$ = residue weight; $W_S$ = sample weight for unsaponifiable.

### 2.8 International Standards and Codex Alimentarius

Beyond Indian regulations, the **Codex Alimentarius Commission** (joint FAO/WHO body) sets international standards for fats and oils. Key Codex standards include:

- **Codex Standard for Named Vegetable Oils (CXS 210-1999, revised)** — covers identity, purity, and quality criteria for 17 named vegetable oils.
- **Codex Standard for Olive Oils and Olive Pomace Oils (CXS 33-1981)** — extra virgin, virgin, refined grades.
- **Codex Code of Practice for Storage and Transport of Edible Oils and Fats (CAC/RCP 36-1987)**.

The Codex system provides the **Codex Identity Criteria** (e.g., fatty acid composition ranges, tocopherol levels, desmethylsterol composition) which BIS has largely adopted.

### 2.9 FSSAI — The Apex Food Safety Regulator

The **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)**, established under the **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006**, has overarching authority on food safety. Its regulations under the **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011**, and the **Food Safety and Standards (Contaminants, Toxins and Residues) Regulations, 2011**, cover:

- Permitted additives (antioxidants like BHA, BHT, TBHQ — max 200 mg/kg in oils).
- Limits of contaminants (lead, arsenic, mercury).
- Labelling requirements.
- Permitted GM-derived oils (must be labelled).
- Permitted blending rules (only specific blends allowed, must be declared).
- Hygienic requirements during manufacture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU Exam:** BIS and AGMARK standards are *product specification* standards, while FSSAI regulations are *safety* regulations. Together they form the regulatory framework. Always distinguish between **quality standards** (BIS/AGMARK) and **safety regulations** (FSSAI).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

### 3.1 Analytical Sample Problem 1 — Free Fatty Acid Calculation

**Problem (KTU 2024 Practice Style):**
A sample of **refined rice bran oil** weighing **5.0 g** is titrated against **0.1 N NaOH**. The volume of NaOH consumed is **2.8 mL**. Calculate the **Free Fatty Acid (as oleic acid) %** and determine whether the oil conforms to BIS specifications if the permissible limit is **0.10 %** for refined oils.

**Step 1 — Recall the formula:**

$$\text{FFA (\% as oleic acid)} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 28.2}{W}$$

> **Where 28.2** is the equivalent weight of oleic acid multiplied by 100/1000 = 1000/100 × equivalent weight. (Equivalent weight of oleic acid = 282; for percentage expression: 282 × 100 / 1000 = 28.2.)

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

Given: $V = 2.8 \text{ mL}$, $N = 0.1$, $W = 5.0 \text{ g}$.

$$\text{FFA (\%)} = \dfrac{2.8 \times 0.1 \times 28.2}{5.0}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator:**

$$2.8 \times 0.1 = 0.28$$

$$0.28 \times 28.2 = 7.896$$

**Step 4 — Divide by sample weight:**

$$\text{FFA (\%)} = \dfrac{7.896}{5.0} = 1.5792\%$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result:**

$$\text{FFA (\%)} \approx 1.58\%$$

This is **far above the BIS limit of 0.10%** for refined oils. The sample **does NOT conform** to BIS specifications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Takeaway:** A high FFA indicates hydrolytic breakdown, possible use of poor-quality crude oil, inadequate refining, or improper storage. Refined oils must have FFA < 0.10%, while cold-pressed/kachchi ghani oils may be allowed up to 2.5–3.0%.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Sample Problem 2 — Iodine Value Calculation

**Problem:**
A 0.32 g sample of sunflower oil reacts with **Wijs solution**. The blank titration requires **45.2 mL** of 0.1 N sodium thiosulphate, while the sample titration requires **20.8 mL**. Calculate the Iodine Value.

**Step 1 — Formula:**

$$\text{IV} = \dfrac{(B - S) \times N \times 12.69}{W}$$

> **Where 12.69** = atomic weight of iodine (126.9) × 100 / 1000 = equivalent weight of I₂ × 100/1000.

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$B = 45.2 \text{ mL}$, $S = 20.8 \text{ mL}$, $N = 0.1$, $W = 0.32 \text{ g}$.

$$\text{IV} = \dfrac{(45.2 - 20.8) \times 0.1 \times 12.69}{0.32}$$

**Step 3 — Compute difference:**

$$B - S = 45.2 - 20.8 = 24.4 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply:**

$$24.4 \times 0.1 = 2.44$$

$$2.44 \times 12.69 = 30.9636$$

**Step 5 — Divide:**

$$\text{IV} = \dfrac{30.9636}{0.32} = 96.76 \text{ g I}_2\text{/100 g}$$

**Step 6 — Verify against BIS IS 4277 (Sunflower Oil):**

BIS specification for refined sunflower oil: **IV = 100–145 g I₂/100 g**.

$$\text{Observed IV} = 96.76 \text{ g I}_2\text{/100 g}$$

This is **slightly below** the BIS range. Such a low IV may suggest adulteration with a saturated oil like palm olein, or it could indicate partial hydrogenation or oil from a low-oleic variety. Sample **does not conform to BIS range** strictly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical IV Ranges for Common Oils (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - Coconut oil: 7–10
> - Palm kernel oil: 14–21
> - Palm oil: 50–55
> - Groundnut oil: 85–99
> - Sunflower oil: 100–145
> - Soybean oil: 120–141
> - Safflower oil: 135–150
> - Linseed oil: 168–204

---

### 3.3 Analytical Sample Problem 3 — Peroxide Value Calculation

**Problem:**
A 5.0 g sample of refined oil is tested for peroxide value. Blank titration = 0.5 mL of 0.01 N sodium thiosulphate. Sample titration = 2.5 mL. Calculate the Peroxide Value in meq O₂/kg.

**Step 1 — Formula:**

$$\text{PV (meq O}_2\text{/kg)} = \dfrac{(S - B) \times N \times 1000}{W}$$

> **Note:** For PV, $S$ is subtracted from $B$ (reverse of iodine value), because the sample consumes the iodine released by potassium iodide; higher peroxide in sample ⇒ higher titration.

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$S = 2.5 \text{ mL}$, $B = 0.5 \text{ mL}$, $N = 0.01$, $W = 5.0 \text{ g}$.

$$\text{PV} = \dfrac{(2.5 - 0.5) \times 0.01 \times 1000}{5.0}$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$2.5 - 0.5 = 2.0 \text{ mL}$$

$$2.0 \times 0.01 \times 1000 = 20$$

$$\text{PV} = \dfrac{20}{5.0} = 4.0 \text{ meq O}_2\text{/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

BIS specification for refined edible oil (fresh): **PV ≤ 5 meq O₂/kg** at the time of packing. Result conforms to the fresh oil standard.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Many students confuse the formula for PV vs IV. The key difference: in IV the formula is $(B - S)$; in PV it is $(S - B)$. Remember: for PV, **S (sample) > B (blank) because peroxide releases iodine.**

---

### 3.4 Analytical Sample Problem 4 — Saponification Value Calculation

**Problem:**
A 2.0 g sample of palm oil is saponified with 25 mL of 0.5 N alcoholic KOH. After saponification, the excess KOH is back-titrated with 0.5 N HCl. Blank = 45.0 mL, Sample = 20.5 mL. Calculate the Saponification Value.

**Step 1 — Formula:**

$$\text{SV} = \dfrac{(B - S) \times N \times 56.1}{W}$$

> **Where 56.1** = equivalent weight of KOH.

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\text{SV} = \dfrac{(45.0 - 20.5) \times 0.5 \times 56.1}{2.0}$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$B - S = 24.5 \text{ mL}$$

$$24.5 \times 0.5 = 12.25$$

$$12.25 \times 56.1 = 687.225$$

$$\text{SV} = \dfrac{687.225}{2.0} = 343.6 \text{ mg KOH/g}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

BIS specification for refined palm oil: **SV = 190–209 mg KOH/g**. Observed 343.6 is far too high, suggesting an error in calculation or sample misidentification (this value is more characteristic of coconut or palm kernel oil). The sample likely does not conform to palm oil specification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverse Relationship Reminder:** $\text{SV} \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{Average Molecular Weight}}$. Coconut and palm kernel oils have high SV (248–265) because of short-chain lauric acid dominance. Palm olein has SV around 190–209.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Sample Problem 5 — Unsaponifiable Matter Calculation

**Problem:**
A 5.0 g oil sample yields a residue of 0.045 g after the unsaponifiable matter test. Calculate the % unsaponifiable matter.

**Step 1 — Formula:**

$$\text{UM (\%)} = \dfrac{W_R}{W_S} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\text{UM (\%)} = \dfrac{0.045}{5.0} \times 100 = 0.9\%$$

**Step 3 — Verification:**

BIS limit for refined groundnut oil: **UM ≤ 1.0%**. Result is within specification.

---

### 3.6 Summary — Master Equations for KTU Numerical Problems

The five key equations that appear frequently in KTU exams are consolidated below. Memorize the structure of each:

$$\boxed{\text{FFA \%} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 28.2}{W}} \quad \text{(hydrolytic rancidity)}$$

$$\boxed{\text{AV} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W}} \quad \text{(acidity in mg KOH/g)}$$

$$\boxed{\text{IV} = \dfrac{(B - S) \times N \times 12.69}{W}} \quad \text{(unsaturation, Wijs)}$$

$$\boxed{\text{SV} = \dfrac{(B - S) \times N \times 56.1}{W}} \quad \text{(avg. M.W.)}$$

$$\boxed{\text{PV} = \dfrac{(S - B) \times N \times 1000}{W}} \quad \text{(oxidative state)}$$

$$\boxed{\text{UM \%} = \dfrac{W_R}{W_S} \times 100} \quad \text{(non-glyceride components)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** Many students confuse AV with SV (both use 56.1). The distinction: AV uses **V (titrant volume)** directly in titration; SV uses **(B - S)** because of back-titration. Same constant, different experimental procedure.
