## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Style]**
**Define ISI mark and AGMARK. State two differences between them.**

**Model Answer:**
The **ISI mark** is a certification mark issued by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** under the BIS Act, 1986, certifying that a product conforms to the relevant **Indian Standard (IS)** specification. It is indicated on the product package along with the licence number.

**AGMARK** is a certification mark applied under the **Agricultural Produce (Grading and Marking) Act, 1937**, certifying the **grade designation** of agricultural commodities. It is administered by the **Directorate of Marketing and Inspection (DMI)**.

**Two differences:**

1. **Scope:** BIS standards cover all industrial and consumer goods (broad), whereas AGMARK is restricted to specific agricultural commodities (narrow).
2. **Focus:** BIS specifies detailed physico-chemical parameters with numerical limits; AGMARK assigns commercial grade designations (Special, Grade I, II, etc.) based on a holistic quality assessment.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Style]**
**List the BIS-prescribed quality parameters for any one refined edible oil of your choice.**

**Model Answer (Refined Groundnut Oil — IS 5430:1999):**

1. Moisture and insoluble impurities — **max 0.10%**
2. Free fatty acid (as oleic acid) — **max 0.10%**
3. Iodine value (Wijs) — **85–99 g I₂/100 g**
4. Saponification value — **188–196 mg KOH/g**
5. Unsaponifiable matter — **max 1.0%**
6. Refractive index at 40 °C — **1.4670–1.4690**
7. Colour in 1-inch Lovibond cell — **max 10 Y + 1.0 R**
8. Test for argemone oil — **negative**
9. Test for mineral oil — **negative**
10. Peroxide value at packing — **max 5 meq O₂/kg**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (Option 1) — [KTU University Exam Style 2024 Scheme, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **ISI (BIS) certification procedure** for granting the ISI mark to a manufacturer of refined edible oil. Describe the role of BIS in maintaining quality. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **AGMARK grading system for ghee**. List the grade designations and the major quality parameters used for grading. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Q.A(a):**

The **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** operates under the BIS Act, 1986, and is responsible for formulating standards and granting the **ISI mark** (now called the Standard Mark) to manufacturers complying with Indian Standards.

**Certification Procedure — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1 — Application Submission:**
The manufacturer submits a formal application to the regional BIS office, enclosing the product composition, manufacturing process flowchart, in-house quality control procedures, list of testing equipment, and copies of the relevant IS standard.

**Step 2 — Document Scrutiny:**
BIS officers review the documents for completeness and compliance with general requirements (Good Manufacturing Practices, in-house lab infrastructure, etc.).

**Step 3 — Factory Inspection:**
A BIS inspection team conducts an on-site audit to verify the production facilities, hygienic conditions, in-process controls, and testing capabilities. Non-conformities are recorded and must be closed before proceeding.

**Step 4 — Sample Drawing and Testing:**
BIS inspectors draw finished product samples, which are tested in BIS-recognized laboratories against every parameter prescribed in the relevant IS standard (e.g., IS 5430 for groundnut oil).

**Step 5 — Grant of Licence:**
If all parameters are within specified limits, BIS issues a **licence to use the ISI mark**, valid for a specified period (typically 1–5 years). The licence number must be printed on every unit of packaging.

**Step 6 — Surveillance Audits:**
BIS conducts periodic (usually annual) surveillance audits including fresh sample drawing and testing to ensure continued compliance.

**Step 7 — Renewal / Cancellation:**
The licence is renewed upon satisfactory surveillance. Non-compliance leads to **suspension** (temporary) or **cancellation** (permanent withdrawal of the right to use the ISI mark).

**Role of BIS in Quality Maintenance:**

- Sets unified national standards eliminating regional variation.
- Provides consumer assurance of quality, safety, and authenticity.
- Facilitates inter-state and export trade through harmonized specifications.
- Prevents adulteration through mandatory testing.
- Acts as a self-discipline tool for industry.

---

**Model Answer for Q.A(b):**

**AGMARK Grading of Ghee:**

**Statutory Basis:** AGMARK grading of ghee is governed by the **Ghee Grading and Marking Rules** framed under the Agricultural Produce (Grading and Marking) Act, 1937 (as amended in 1986). The Directorate of Marketing and Inspection (DMI) administers the scheme through authorised chemists and laboratories.

**Grade Designations (Standard AGMARK Grades for Ghee):**

1. **Special**
2. **Grade I**
3. **Grade II**
4. **General**

These grades are assigned based on the analytical, sensory, and physico-chemical parameters of the ghee sample.

**Major Quality Parameters for AGMARK Grading:**

| **Parameter** | **Special** | **Grade I** | **Grade II** | **General** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Moisture (%) | max 0.3 | max 0.3 | max 0.3 | max 0.3 |
| FFA (as oleic acid, %) | max 1.5 | max 2.5 | max 3.5 | max 5.0 |
| BR Reading at 40 °C | 40.0–43.0 | 40.0–43.0 | 40.0–43.0 | 40.0–43.0 |
| Reichert-Meissl Value (min) | 28.0 | 26.0 | 24.0 | 22.0 |
| Polenske Value | up to 2.0 | up to 2.0 | up to 2.0 | up to 2.0 |
| Test for Vanaspati | Negative | Negative | Negative | Negative |
| Test for Mineral Oil | Negative | Negative | Negative | Negative |
| Colour, Odour, Flavour | Pleasant, characteristic | Pleasant | Pleasant | Pleasant |

> **Reichert-Meissl Value (RMV)** is a critical test — it detects adulteration of ghee with **vanaspati** or other non-butter fats, as RMV is naturally high (≥ 28) in pure butterfat due to short-chain fatty acids (butyric, caproic).

**AGMARK Marking Convention:**
Each pack bears the AGMARK label with:
- The grade designation.
- The signature of the authorised packer.
- The batch/lot number.
- The location of the packing station.

---

**Question B (Option 2 — Internal Choice) — [KTU University Exam Style 2024 Scheme, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Discuss the **role of FSSAI** in regulating the safety and quality of edible oils and fats in India. Compare its objectives with those of BIS. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A sample of refined soybean oil weighing **5.6 g** is titrated with **0.1 N NaOH** and consumes **3.2 mL**. Calculate the **FFA (%)** as oleic acid. State whether the sample conforms to BIS limits. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Q.B(a):**

**Role of FSSAI:**

The **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)** was established under the **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006**, and is the apex regulatory body for food safety in India. It operates under the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare.

**Key Functions Relevant to Edible Oils and Fats:**

1. **Setting Safety Standards:** FSSAI prescribes safety parameters (heavy metal limits, pesticide residues, microbiological criteria) under the **Food Safety and Standards (Contaminants, Toxins and Residues) Regulations, 2011**.
2. **Permitted Additives:** Lists antioxidants (BHA, BHT, TBHQ, propyl gallate) with maximum permitted levels — for fats and oils, generally **max 200 mg/kg** (single or in combination).
3. **Product Standards:** Defines identity and composition for various oil and fat products through the **Food Products Standards and Food Additives Regulations, 2011** (e.g., blending rules, hydrogenation rules).
4. **Labelling Requirements:** Mandates declaration of name, net weight, batch number, date of manufacture, "Best Before" date, FSSAI logo and licence number, source of oil, and whether blended or hydrogenated.
5. **Licensing and Registration:** All Food Business Operators (FBOs) must obtain FSSAI registration or licence.
6. **Enforcement and Surveillance:** Through state food safety commissioners, conducts inspections and collects samples for testing.
7. **Recall and Withdrawal:** Powers to order recall of unsafe products.

**Comparison — FSSAI vs BIS:**

| **Aspect** | **FSSAI** | **BIS** |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Focus** | Food safety, public health | Product quality and standardization |
| **Coverage** | All food articles (broader) | Industrial and consumer goods |
| **Legal Basis** | Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 | BIS Act, 1986 |
| **Nature of Standard** | Mandatory for all food products | Voluntary unless notified |
| **Licence** | FSSAI Registration / Licence for all FBOs | BIS licence for ISI mark usage |
| **Penalty** | Prosecution, fines, imprisonment under FSS Act | Suspension/cancellation of BIS licence |
| **Adulteration Action** | Direct enforcement (seizure, prosecution) | Indirect (revoke certification) |

> Both bodies work in tandem: BIS sets the **quality benchmarks** (IS codes), while FSSAI enforces **safety regulations** and ensures compliance with both IS standards and its own safety rules.

---

**Model Answer for Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- Sample weight, $W = 5.6 \text{ g}$
- Titrant normality, $N = 0.1$
- Titrant volume, $V = 3.2 \text{ mL}$

**Formula:**

$$\text{FFA (\% as oleic acid)} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 28.2}{W}$$

**Substitution:**

$$\text{FFA (\%)} = \dfrac{3.2 \times 0.1 \times 28.2}{5.6}$$

**Calculation:**

$$3.2 \times 0.1 = 0.32$$

$$0.32 \times 28.2 = 9.024$$

$$\text{FFA (\%)} = \dfrac{9.024}{5.6} = 1.611\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{FFA (\%)} \approx 1.61\%}$$

**Conformity with BIS Limits:**

BIS specification for **refined soybean oil (IS 4275)**:
- FFA (as oleic acid): **max 0.10%** for refined grade.

**Conclusion:**
The observed FFA of **1.61%** far exceeds the BIS limit of **0.10%**. The sample **does NOT conform** to the IS specification for refined soybean oil. Such a high FFA suggests one or more of the following defects:
- Use of poor-quality crude oil with high initial FFA.
- Inadequate or improper refining (failure of alkali neutralisation stage).
- Hydrolytic deterioration during storage (moisture ingress, microbial lipase activity).
- Possible adulteration with a cheaper, higher-FFA oil.

For the sample to conform, the FFA must be reduced to ≤ 0.10% by **refining** (typically chemical refining with caustic soda, or physical refining with steam stripping under vacuum).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing BIS with ISI:** Many students write "ISI mark is given by Bureau of Indian Standards" but score only partial credit. Correct statement: *ISI mark is the certification mark, BIS is the issuing body since 1987; before that, ISI itself was the body.*
>
> 2. **Mixing FFA and Acid Value Formulas:** The constants differ (28.2 for oleic % vs 56.1 for KOH equivalent). Students who write the wrong constant lose the entire numerical mark.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Units in Iodine Value:** IV must be reported as **g I₂/100 g** (not as g/g or mg). KTU examiners specifically deduct for missing units.
>
> 4. **Confusing Reichert-Meissl Value with Polenske Value:** RMV detects butterfat adulteration (soluble volatile fatty acids); Polenske detects coconut/palm kernel fat (insoluble volatile fatty acids). Writing them interchangeably costs marks.
>
> 5. **Stating AGMARK is mandatory:** AGMARK is **voluntary** unless specifically notified. Saying "AGMARK is compulsory for ghee" is wrong unless quoted under a specific notification context.
>
> 6. **Missing the BIS numbering format:** Always write as **"IS [Number]: [Year]"** (e.g., IS 5430:1999). Skipping the year is a minor deduction but matters for full marks.
>
> 7. **Not differentiating Quality vs Safety standards:** Confusing BIS (quality) with FSSAI (safety) shows incomplete understanding and leads to deduction in 14-mark answers.

---

- IS 3508 → Coconut oil
- IS 3490 → Mustard/Rapeseed oil
- IS 4932 → Sunflower oil
- IS 4277 → Safflower oil
- IS 13685 → Rice bran oil
- IS 3491 → Vanaspati
- IS 17962 → Olive oil (refined)

**C. AGMARK Commodities for Fats & Oils:**
- Ghee (Special, Grade I, Grade II, General)
- Mustard oil (Kachhi Ghani, Expeller — Grade I, II)
- Groundnut (in shell and kernel)
- Sesame (til) seeds

**D. Master Formulae (Memorize Constants):**
- FFA (%) = (V × N × 28.2) / W
- AV = (V × N × 56.1) / W
- IV = (B − S) × N × 12.69 / W
- SV = (B − S) × N × 56.1 / W
- PV = (S − B) × N × 1000 / W
- UM (%) = (W_R / W_S) × 100

**E. Adulteration Tests to Remember:**
- **Argemone oil in mustard oil** — Nitric acid test (reddish-brown colour).
- **Mineral oil in edible oil** — Fluorescence under UV light / petroleum ether test.
- **Vanaspati in ghee** — RMV (drops below 28), Polenske value (rises above 2.0), B.R. reading mismatch.
- **Cottonseed oil in other oils** — Halphen test (pink-red colour).
- **Animal fat in vegetable oil** — Boemer test, sterol composition (cholesterol vs phytosterols).

**F. Key Distinctions for 14-Mark Answers:**
- BIS = Quality, AGMARK = Grade, FSSAI = Safety.
- IV measures unsaturation; SV measures average molecular weight.
- RMV distinguishes butter fat from non-butter fat.
- Polenske value distinguishes coconut / palm kernel from other fats.

**G. Numerical Interpretation Skill:**
Always convert → compute → compare with BIS limit → comment on conformity. KTU examiners award the final "conformity comment" mark separately from the calculation.

**H. One-Line Definitions (Quick Recall):**
- **Iodine Value:** Grams of iodine absorbed by 100 g of fat.
- **Saponification Value:** Milligrams of KOH required to saponify 1 g of fat.
- **Acid Value:** Milligrams of KOH required to neutralise free fatty acids in 1 g of fat.
- **Peroxide Value:** Milliequivalents of active oxygen (as peroxide) per kg of fat.
- **Reichert-Meissl Value:** mL of 0.1 N KOH required to neutralise steam-volatile water-soluble fatty acids from 5 g of fat.
- **B.R. Reading:** Refractive index of fat expressed on butyro-refractometer scale at 40 °C.

> **Final Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, structure your answer as:
> 1. Definition/Scope (2 marks) →
> 2. Statutory basis (2 marks) →
> 3. Procedure / Parameters / Grade designations (5 marks) →
> 4. Interpretation or numerical (3 marks) →
> 5. Conclusion / Comparison (2 marks).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Aspects and HACCP Standards in Oil Industries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT748 - FAT AND OIL PROCESSING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Safety Aspects and HACCP Standards in Oil Industries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Industrial Safety in Oil Industries — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Safety in Oil Processing** refers to the systematic application of engineering principles, technical knowledge, and managerial practices to identify, evaluate, eliminate, or control hazards arising from the handling, processing, storage, and transport of fats, oils, and associated solvents (like hexane), to protect workers, equipment, the environment, and product integrity.

Edible oil processing plants (solvent extraction, refining, hydrogenation, deodorization) deal with:
- **Flammable solvents** (n-Hexane with flash point **−22 °C**, acetone)
- **High-temperature operations** (deodorizer at 220–260 °C; hydrogenation at 160–200 °C)
- **High-pressure hydrogen gas** (typically **2–6 bar** in hardening reactors)
- **Caustic chemicals** (NaOH for refining, concentration **10–22 °Bé**)
- **Hot oils and pumps** (thermal burns)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (CO4):** The student must be able to identify safety hazards in solvent extraction, hydrogenation, and refining plants and apply HACCP principles to ensure food-grade oil quality.

## 1.2 HACCP — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HACCP (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points)** is a science-based, systematic, preventive food-safety management system that addresses biological, chemical, and physical hazards by anticipating and preventing them, rather than relying on end-product inspection. It is mandated by the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 4-2003)** and adopted globally under FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India) regulations.

**HACCP acronym decoded:**
- **H**azard — A biological, chemical, or physical agent that may cause an adverse health effect.
- **A**nalysis — Comparison with risk severity and occurrence.
- **C**ritical — Essential to eliminate or prevent the hazard.
- **C**ontrol — Action to regulate the CCP.
- **P**oint — Step in the process where control is applied.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Driving a Car with HACCP & Safety in Mind"**
>
> Imagine driving a car from Kottayam to Bengaluru. You don't simply drive and check the brakes at the destination (this is "end-product testing"). Instead:
> - **Pre-planning (Hazard Analysis):** Check tyre pressure, fuel, weather.
> - **Critical Control Points (CCP):** Speed limit zones, blind curves, toll booths — places you **must** apply control to avoid an accident.
> - **Monitoring:** Watching the speedometer, mirrors, fuel gauge.
> - **Corrective Action:** If fuel runs low, you refuel; if rain starts, you slow down.
> - **Verification & Records:** Service log, GPS data, license renewal.
>
> Similarly, in oil processing, HACCP prevents hazards *before* they reach the consumer. Industrial safety is the "seat-belt" — protecting the worker. HACCP is the "GPS + traffic rules" — protecting the consumer.

## 1.4 Classification of Hazards in Oil Industries

| Hazard Category | Example in Oil Plant | Severity |
|---|---|---|
| **Fire/Explosion** | Hexane vapour leak → spark | **Catastrophic** |
| **Chemical Burn** | NaOH splash in refinery | High |
| **Asphyxiation** | Hexane vapour displaces O₂ | High |
| **Thermal Burn** | Hot oil at 200 °C from deodorizer | Medium–High |
| **Mechanical** | Rotating expeller shafts, hammer mills | Medium |
| **Ergonomic** | Manual handling of 25 kg oil tins | Medium |
| **Ergonomic-Chemical** | Pesticide residues in crude oil | Medium |
| **Physical Contamination** | Metal fragments from sieves/centrifuge | Medium |
| **Biological** | Microbial growth in stored oil | Low–Medium |
| **Noise** | Hammer mills (>85 dB) | Medium |

## 1.5 Common Safety Metrics in Oil Plants

- **Flash Point (FP):** Lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off enough vapour to form an **ignitable mixture** with air.
- **Fire Point:** Temperature at which sustained combustion occurs (typically **5–10 °C above FP**).
- **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT):** Spontaneous ignition without external flame (hexane **225 °C**).
- **Lower/Upper Explosive Limit (LEL/UEL):** Concentration range of vapour in air that can explode (hexane LEL = **1.1 %**, UEL = **7.5 %**).
- **TWA (Time-Weighted Average):** 8-hour exposure limit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industrial Standards Governing Oil Plant Safety:**
> - **IS 1986 (Indian Standard)** — Solvent extraction plant safety code.
> - **NFPA 36** (National Fire Protection Association) — Solvent extraction plants.
> - **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.106** — Flammable liquids.
> - **FSSAI 2006** (India) — Mandatory HACCP for food businesses.
> - **Codex Alimentarius CAC/RCP 1-1969 Rev. 4 (2003)** — International HACCP framework.
> - **ISO 22000:2018** — Food Safety Management System integrating HACCP.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Seven Principles of HACCP (Codex Alimentarius)

HACCP is built on **seven sequential principles**. Every KTU examination question on HACCP is tested against these.

### Principle 1 — Conduct Hazard Analysis
Identify potential biological (**B**), chemical (**C**), and physical (**P**) hazards at every process step. Two parameters are used:

$$R = S \times P$$

Where:
- $R$ = **Risk score**
- $S$ = **Severity** (1 = Negligible, 4 = Catastrophic)
- $P$ = **Probability of occurrence** (1 = Rare, 4 = Frequent)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** $R$ ranking decides whether a step becomes a CCP. Steps with $R \geq 9$ are almost always upgraded to CCPs.

### Principle 2 — Determine Critical Control Points (CCPs)
A **CCP Decision Tree** is used (Codex four-question tree):

1. Do preventive control measures exist for the identified hazard? **Yes → Q2**, No → Not a CCP (modify process).
2. Is this step specifically designed to eliminate or reduce the likely occurrence of the hazard? **Yes → CCP**, No → Q3.
3. Could contamination with the identified hazard occur in excess of acceptable levels, or could it increase to unacceptable levels? **Yes → Q4**, No → Not a CCP.
4. Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the identified hazard? **Yes → Not a CCP**, No → **CCP**.

### Principle 3 — Establish Critical Limits
Maximum/minimum value to which a CCP must be controlled. Examples:
- Phosphoric acid dosing in degumming: **0.05–0.1 %** of oil weight.
- Degumming temperature: **60–80 °C**.
- Refining temperature: **70–90 °C**.
- Moisture in finished oil: **≤ 0.1 %**.
- PV (Peroxide Value) of refined oil: **≤ 5 meq O₂/kg**.
- Hexane residue in meal: **≤ 500 ppm** (FSSAI).

### Principle 4 — Establish Monitoring Procedures
Continuous or periodic observation to confirm CCP stays within critical limits.
- **Frequency** example: Temperature logged every **30 minutes**; PV tested every **4 hours**.

### Principle 5 — Establish Corrective Actions
Specific actions when monitoring indicates deviation from a critical limit.
- Example: If deodorizer temperature exceeds 260 °C → stop steam supply, divert oil to re-blending tank.

### Principle 6 — Establish Verification Procedures
Confirm that the HACCP system is working effectively. Includes:
- Calibration of thermometers, refractometers.
- Internal audits (semi-annual).
- Microbiological testing of finished oil.

### Principle 7 — Establish Record-Keeping & Documentation
- HACCP plan, CCP monitoring logs, corrective action reports, verification records, calibration records.
- Mandatory retention: **minimum 2 years** (FSSAI) or product shelf-life + 1 year, whichever is greater.

## 2.2 Prerequisites for HACCP (PRPs — Prerequisite Programmes)

Before HACCP can be applied, the following must be in place (per Codex):

| PRP | Oil Industry Application |
|---|---|
| **Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP)** | Sanitary plant design, equipment hygiene |
| **Sanitation Standard Operating Procedures (SSOPs)** | Cleaning of centrifuges, deodorizers |
| **Pest Control** | Insect/rodent control in storage godowns |
| **Personal Hygiene** | Worker health, gloves, head-cover |
| **Maintenance & Calibration** | Thermometer, flow-meter accuracy |
| **Traceability & Recall** | Lot coding of finished oil |
| **Allergen Management** | Cross-contamination of sesame, groundnut allergens |

## 2.3 Safety Engineering for Solvent Extraction Plants

### 2.3.1 Properties of n-Hexane (the most-used solvent)

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Molecular formula | $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{14}$ |
| Molecular weight | **86.18 g/mol** |
| Density at 20 °C | **0.659 g/cm³** |
| Flash point | **−22 °C** (closed cup) |
| Auto-ignition temperature | **225 °C** |
| LEL / UEL | **1.1 % / 7.5 %** (by volume) |
| Vapour density (air = 1) | **2.97** (heavier than air — sinks to floor) |
| TLV-TWA | **50 ppm (176 mg/m³)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because hexane vapour is **2.97× heavier than air**, it pools in pits, trenches, and lower floors. This is the #1 cause of explosion accidents in oil extraction plants. Plant flooring must be **non-absorbent, impermeable, and slope-drained** (minimum 1:100 gradient) to safe pits.

### 2.3.2 Quantitative Fire/Explosion Formulas

The **Bunsen-type explosion equation** for solvent concentration:

$$C_{vap} = \frac{P \times M}{R \times T} \times 10^{6} \;\; \text{(in ppm)}$$

Where:
- $C_{vap}$ = Vapour concentration in ppm.
- $P$ = Partial vapour pressure of solvent at temperature $T$ (Pa).
- $M$ = Molar mass (kg/mol).
- $R$ = Universal gas constant **8.314 J/(mol·K)**.
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K).

The **stochiometric air-fuel ratio (AFR)** for hexane combustion:

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{14} + 9.5\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 6\,\text{CO}_2 + 7\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{9.5 \times 32}{86.18} \times \frac{100}{21} \approx 53.8 \;\;\text{kg air/kg hexane}$$

### 2.3.3 Vent Sizing for Explosion Relief (NFPA 68 method)

$$A_v = \frac{C \times A_s}{\sqrt{P_{red}}}$$

Where:
- $A_v$ = Vent area (m²).
- $A_s$ = Internal surface area of enclosure (m²).
- $P_{red}$ = Reduced explosion pressure (typically **0.2 bar gauge** for oil plants).
- $C$ = Gas constant for hexane dust/vapour ≈ **0.21** (dimensionless).

## 2.4 Safety in Hydrogenation Plant

Hydrogenation is among the **most critical** operations because of:
1. **High-pressure H₂ gas** (2–6 bar → sometimes 50 bar in industrial hardenings).
2. **Catalyst dust (Ni)** — pyrophoric when dry.
3. **High temperature** (160–200 °C).

### Critical Safety Devices

| Device | Function |
|---|---|
| **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV)** | Vents excess H₂ if pressure exceeds design limit |
| **Rupture Disc** | One-time burst protection for over-pressure |
| **Flame Arrestor** | Prevents flashback from vent stack to vessel |
| **Emergency Shut-Down (ESD) Valve** | Pneumatic/Hydraulic block valve, fail-safe |
| **Inert Gas Purge (N₂)** | Removes residual H₂ before maintenance |
| **Catalyst Quench Tank** | Safe submersion of spent Ni catalyst under water |

## 2.5 Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) Matrix

| Hazard | PPE |
|---|---|
| Hexane splash | Chemical-resistant gloves (Nitrile), goggles, apron |
| Hot oil burn | Heat-resistant gauntlets, face shield, aluminized suit |
| Caustic NaOH | PVC gloves, chemical goggles, face shield |
| Dust (meal/cake) | FFP2/N95 respirator |
| Noise | Earplugs + earmuffs (>100 dB) |
| Falling objects | Safety helmet (ISI marked) |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Term | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = S \times P$ | Risk scoring for hazard analysis | dimensionless |
| 2 | $C_{vap} = \dfrac{P M}{R T} \times 10^{6}$ | Vapour concentration from ideal gas law | ppm |
| 3 | $AFR = \dfrac{O_2 \times 32}{M_{fuel}} \times \dfrac{100}{21}$ | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio | kg air/kg fuel |
| 4 | $A_v = \dfrac{C \cdot A_s}{\sqrt{P_{red}}}$ | Explosion vent area | m² |
| 5 | $P_{max} \le 1.1 \times MAWP$ | Operating pressure rule | bar |
| 6 | $LFL = \dfrac{1}{9} \times \text{APC}$ | Lower flammable limit (rule of thumb) | % |
| 7 | $E_{vap} = m \cdot L_v$ | Energy to vaporise hexane spill (mass × latent heat) | kJ |
| 8 | $C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ | Atmospheric dispersion decay (k=decay constant) | ppm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** These formulas are the heart of safety audit reports, PHA (Process Hazard Analysis) studies, and OSHA compliance documentation. A KTU graduate entering a solvent extraction plant as a Safety Officer will use these daily.

## 2.7 HACCP Plan for a Typical Refined Oil Plant (Module-High-Yield)

| Process Step | Hazard | CCP? | Critical Limit | Monitoring | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Receipt of crude oil | Pesticide residue, aflatoxin | **Yes (CCP-1)** | Aflatoxin ≤ 20 µg/kg | Lab testing (HPLC) | Reject lot |
| Degumming | Phospholipids → gum | No | — | — | — |
| Neutralization (Caustic) | Soap formation | No | Excess NaOH ≤ 0.1 % | pH titration | Re-refine |
| **Bleaching** | Heavy metals, pigments | **Yes (CCP-2)** | Earth dosage 0.5–2 %, temp 90–110 °C | Filter test, colour Lovibond | Re-bleach |
| **Deodorization** | Free fatty acids, oxidation | **Yes (CCP-3)** | Temp 220–260 °C, vacuum 3–6 mbar, time 60–90 min | Thermocouple, vacuum gauge | Re-process |
| Filtration (polishing) | Trace metals from earth | **Yes (CCP-4)** | Filter ≤ 5 µm; metal ≤ 0.1 ppm | Periodic ICP-MS | Replace filter |
| Packaging | Cross-contamination | No | — | Visual check | Quarantine batch |

## 2.8 HACCP vs Conventional Quality Control

| Aspect | Conventional QC | HACCP |
|---|---|---|
| Approach | Reactive (end-product test) | **Preventive** (process control) |
| Focus | Final product inspection | **Process hazards** |
| Sampling | Random/statistical | **Continuous monitoring at CCPs** |
| Documentation | Test reports | **Full HACCP plan + records** |
| Responsibility | QC lab | **Entire team (PDCA cycle)** |
| Cost | Rejection of bad batches | **Lower long-term cost** |
| Legal | Voluntary in many regions | **Mandatory under FSSAI** |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Vapour Concentration of Hexane at Storage Temperature

**Problem Statement (KTU Exam Style):**
A hexane storage tank is at 30 °C. Calculate the vapour concentration of hexane in ppm above the liquid surface, given the vapour pressure of hexane at 30 °C = **22.6 kPa** (0.226 bar).

> [!NOTE]
> This is a standard KTU numerical. Use the **Ideal Gas Law** in ppm form.

### Step 1: List known values
- $P = 22.6\;\text{kPa} = 22600\;\text{Pa}$
- $M = 86.18\;\text{g/mol} = 0.08618\;\text{kg/mol}$
- $R = 8.314\;\text{J/(mol·K)}$
- $T = 30\;\text{°C} = 303\;\text{K}$

### Step 2: Convert partial pressure to mol/m³ using ideal gas law

$$PV = nRT \Rightarrow \frac{n}{V} = \frac{P}{RT}$$

$$\frac{n}{V} = \frac{22600}{8.314 \times 303}$$

$$\frac{n}{V} = \frac{22600}{2519.1} = 8.972\;\text{mol/m}^3$$

### Step 3: Convert to mass concentration (g/m³)

$$C_{mass} = \frac{n}{V} \times M = 8.972 \times 86.18$$

$$C_{mass} = 773.2\;\text{g/m}^3$$

### Step 4: Convert to ppm by volume

For an ideal gas, ppm by volume equals mole fraction × 10⁶.

$$C_{vap} = \frac{n_{hexane}/V}{n_{total}/V} \times 10^6$$

Assuming total pressure $P_{total} = 101.325\;\text{kPa}$:

$$C_{vap} = \frac{P_{hexane}}{P_{total}} \times 10^6$$

$$C_{vap} = \frac{22.6}{101.325} \times 10^6$$

$$C_{vap} = 0.2230 \times 10^6 = 2.23 \times 10^5\;\text{ppm}$$

### Step 5: Compare with LEL

$$LEL_{hexane} = 1.1\% = 11000\;\text{ppm}$$

$$\frac{C_{vap}}{LEL} = \frac{223000}{11000} = 20.3 \times LEL$$

### Conclusion
The vapour concentration is **20.3× above the Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)**. This is **extremely hazardous**. The storage tank must be sealed, nitrogen blanketed, and located outdoors with explosion-vent panels.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students forget to convert temperature to Kelvin or use R = 0.0821 (which is for L·atm). Always use **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** when pressure is in Pa.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air Requirement for Hexane Combustion

### Step 1: Write balanced combustion equation

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{14} + 9.5\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 6\,\text{CO}_2 + 7\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** For each mole of hexane, 9.5 moles of $\text{O}_2$ are required. Air contains only 21 % O₂ by volume (and roughly 23 % by mass).

### Step 2: Calculate mass of O₂ per kg of hexane

$$\text{Mass of O}_2 = 9.5 \times 32 = 304\;\text{g O}_2 / \text{mol hexane}$$

$$\text{Mass of hexane} = 86.18\;\text{g/mol}$$

$$O_2\;\text{required} = \frac{304}{86.18} = 3.527\;\text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg hexane}$$

### Step 3: Convert O₂ mass to air mass

Air composition by mass: O₂ = 23.2 %, N₂ = 76.8 %.

$$\text{Mass of air} = \frac{3.527}{0.232} = 15.20\;\text{kg air/kg hexane}$$

### Step 4: Cross-check using volume basis

$$\text{Volume of air at STP} = \frac{9.5}{0.21} = 45.24\;\text{mol air/mol hexane}$$

$$\text{Volume ratio} = \frac{45.24}{1} = 45.24\;\text{m}^3 \text{air/m}^3 \text{hexane}$$

### Conclusion
Approximately **15.2 kg air is required to burn 1 kg hexane**, equivalent to ~45 volumes of air per volume of hexane vapour. Real-world combustion uses **200–400 % excess air** for safety.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Risk Score and CCP Decision

**Scenario:** In a hydrogenation reactor, consider two hazards:
1. **Hazard A:** High H₂ pressure → Severity = 4 (catastrophic), Probability = 3 (occasional).
2. **Hazard B:** Trace nickel in finished oil → Severity = 2 (minor health), Probability = 2 (rare).

### Step 1: Compute Risk Scores

$$R_A = S_A \times P_A = 4 \times 3 = 12$$

$$R_B = S_B \times P_B = 2 \times 2 = 4$$

### Step 2: Apply HACCP Decision Tree

**Hazard A:**
- Q1: Does preventive control exist? **Yes** (PRV).
- Q2: Is the step specifically designed to eliminate the hazard? **Yes** (PRV caps pressure).
- → **CCP** ✔
- Critical limit: P ≤ 6 bar.
- Monitoring: Pressure transducer every 10 sec.

**Hazard B:**
- Q1: Preventive control? **Yes** (post-refining filtration).
- Q2: Step eliminates hazard? **No** (only reduces).
- Q3: Could contamination exceed acceptable level? **No** (final filtration <0.1 ppm).
- Q4: Will a subsequent step eliminate? **Yes** (bleaching + polishing filter).
- → **NOT a CCP** ✗ (controlled by PRP — Good Manufacturing Practice).

### Conclusion
Hazard A is a CCP; Hazard B is controlled by standard GMP. The KTU exam expects you to show the decision-tree logic clearly.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Explosion Vent Area for a Solvent Recovery Room

**Given:**
- Room dimensions: 8 m × 6 m × 4 m = $192\;\text{m}^3$
- Internal surface area $A_s = 2(8\times6 + 6\times4 + 8\times4) = 2(48 + 24 + 32) = 208\;\text{m}^2$
- Reduced pressure $P_{red} = 0.2\;\text{bar}$
- $C = 0.21$ (hexane)

### Step 1: Apply NFPA 68 formula

$$A_v = \frac{C \cdot A_s}{\sqrt{P_{red}}}$$

$$A_v = \frac{0.21 \times 208}{\sqrt{0.2}}$$

$$A_v = \frac{43.68}{0.4472}$$

$$A_v = 97.7\;\text{m}^2$$

### Step 2: Convert to vent panel dimensions

If using square panels of 1 m × 1 m, total panels = 98 panels distributed across the roof.

### Conclusion
Such a room requires **98 m² of vent area** — clearly impractical. This is why extraction plants are **open-sided sheds** with natural cross-ventilation rather than closed rooms.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: HACCP Application in Different Oil Industry Operations

| Operation | Primary Hazard | Critical Limit | Monitoring Frequency | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Solvent Extraction** | Hexane residue in meal | ≤ 500 ppm | Every batch (GC) | Quarterly lab cross-check |
| **Refining (Neutralization)** | Excess NaOH → soap | Free fatty acid ≤ 0.1 % | Every 2 hr | Soap content titration |
| **Bleaching** | Heavy metals, dioxins | Earth ≤ 2 %, temp ≤ 110 °C | Every 30 min | ICP-MS monthly |
| **Hydrogenation** | Trans fat, nickel | IV drop 30–50 units, Ni ≤ 0.1 ppm | Every batch | GC of FAMEs |
| **Deodorization** | 3-MCPD esters, GE | Temp 220–260 °C, time 60–90 min | Continuous | LC-MS/MS quarterly |
| **Packaging** | Microbial, metal | Seal integrity, O₂ in headspace ≤ 2 % | Every 30 min | Periodic sterility test |
| **Storage** | Aflatoxin (groundnut), oxidation | Totox ≤ 10, PV ≤ 5 meq/kg | Weekly | Peroxide + anisidine |

---

## 3.6 Numerical: Calculating Totox Value

**Totox = 2×PV + AV**, where PV = Peroxide Value, AV = Anisidine Value.

**Given:** Refined sunflower oil: PV = 2 meq O₂/kg, AV = 4.

$$Totox = 2 \times 2 + 4 = 8$$

This is **well within** the industry-accepted limit (≤ 10 for refined oil; ≤ 20 for crude). The oil is acceptable for human consumption.

---

## 3.7 Numerical: Flash Point Reduction with Hexane Spill

**Problem:** At 25 °C, 5 L of hexane spill (density 0.659 g/cm³). Calculate mass evaporated and lower-flammability concentration in a 6 m × 6 m × 3 m room (assuming instant vaporization — worst case).

### Step 1: Mass of spilled hexane

$$m = V \times \rho = 5000 \times 0.659 = 3295\;\text{g} = 3.295\;\text{kg}$$

### Step 2: Total moles

$$n = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{3295}{86.18} = 38.23\;\text{mol}$$

### Step 3: Room volume in litres

$$V_{room} = 6 \times 6 \times 3 = 108\;\text{m}^3 = 1.08 \times 10^5\;\text{L}$$

### Step 4: Volume of hexane vapour (at 25 °C = 298 K, 1 atm)

$$V_{vap} = \frac{nRT}{P} = \frac{38.23 \times 0.0821 \times 298}{1} = 935\;\text{L}$$

### Step 5: Concentration in % by volume

$$\% \text{hexane} = \frac{935}{108000} \times 100 = 0.866\%$$

This is **below LEL of 1.1 %** — non-explosive but **highly flammable** (well within flammable range). Ignition source must be eliminated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Take-away:** Even a 5 L hexane spill can create a flammable atmosphere if released in a closed room. Real plants use **explosion-proof ventilation** rated at **6 air changes per hour minimum**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 HACCP Logic Flow Diagram (Codex-Aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Assemble HACCP Team] --> A1[Describe Product and Intended Use]
    A1 --> A2[Construct Process Flow Diagram]
    A2 --> A3[Verify Flow Diagram On-Site]
    A3 --> P1["Principle 1: List All Hazards and Do Risk Analysis R = S x P"]
    P1 --> P2["Principle 2: Apply CCP Decision Tree"]
    P2 --> P3["Principle 3: Set Critical Limits per CCP"]
    P3 --> P4["Principle 4: Define Monitoring Procedure and Frequency"]
    P4 --> P5["Principle 5: Establish Corrective Actions"]
    P5 --> P6["Principle 6: Verification Activities Calibration Audits Sampling"]
    P6 --> P7["Principle 7: Record-Keeping and Documentation"]
    P7 --> A4[Continuous Improvement PDCA Cycle]
    A4 --> P1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** The flow is cyclic — HACCP is a living system (Plan-Do-Check-Act), not a one-time certificate.

---

## 4.2 CCP Decision Tree (Codex Four-Question)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Q1 Do preventive control measures exist for the identified hazard?}
    Q1 -- No --> N1["Modify process or product Not a CCP"]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Q2 Is this step specifically designed to eliminate or reduce the hazard to an acceptable level?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> CCP1["CCP - Establish limits monitoring CA verification records"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Q3 Could contamination occur in excess of acceptable levels or could it increase to unacceptable levels?}
    Q3 -- No --> NCP1["Not a CCP - Controlled by PRP"]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Q4 Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the identified hazard to an acceptable level?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> NCP2["Not a CCP - Downstream control"]
    Q4 -- No --> CCP2["CCP - Last line of defence"]
```

---

## 4.3 Solvent Extraction Plant — Safety Zone Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Z1[Zone A: Hexane Storage - Explosion Proof]
        Z1A[Tank A1] --> Z1B[Tank A2]
        Z1B --> Z1C[Vent with Flame Arrestor]
    end
    subgraph Z2[Zone B: Extraction Building - NFPA 36 Compliant]
        Z2A[Extractor] --> Z2B[DTDC Desolventiser]
        Z2B --> Z2C[Meal Cooler]
    end
    subgraph Z3[Zone C: Solvent Recovery - Closed Loop]
        Z3A[Evaporator] --> Z3B[Stripping Column]
        Z3B --> Z3C[Condenser]
        Z3C --> Z3D[Water Separator]
        Z3D --> Z1
    end
    Z2 --> Z3
    Z1 -- Hexane via Pipeline --> Z2
    Z4[Vent Stack Min 12m Height] --> Z3C
    Z5[Earth Pit with Water Seal] --> Z1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering rationale:** Hexane vapour is **2.97× heavier than air** — it pools at low levels. The earth pit with water seal traps vapour safely; the vent stack **12 m above grade** releases lighter-than-air combustion products after controlled burn.

## 4.4 Hydrogenation Reactor Safety Interlock Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H2[Hydrogen Cylinder Manifold] --> PRV[Pressure Regulator 0 to 6 bar]
    PRV --> FT[Flow Transmitter]
    FT --> V1[Block Valve 1 - Fail Safe NC]
    V1 --> CAT[Nickel Catalyst Bed]
    CAT --> TT[Temperature Transmitter 160 to 200 C]
    TT --> V2[Block Valve 2 - Quench]
    V2 --> PROD[Hardened Oil Outlet]
    FT --> ESD1[ESD Logic Solver]
    TT --> ESD1
    ESD1 --> V1
    ESD1 --> V2
    ESD1 --> VENT[Emergency Vent to Safe Location]
```

## 4.5 HACCP Plan Integration with ISO 22000:2018

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PRP[Prerequisite Programmes PRP] --> OPRP[Operational PRPs]
    OPRP --> CCP[Critical Control Points]
    PRP --> ISO[ISO 22000 Management System]
    ISO --> PDCA[PDCA Cycle Plan Do Check Act]
    PDCA --> ISO
    CCP --> HACCP[HACCP Plan per Codex]
    HACCP --> ISO
    ISO --> AUDIT[Internal and External Audits]
    AUDIT --> IMP[Continual Improvement]
    IMP --> PRP
```

## 4.6 Risk Matrix (Severity vs Probability)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph R1[Risk Level Low R = 1 to 3]
        R1A[Trivial - GMP control]
    end
    subgraph R2[Risk Level Moderate R = 4 to 8]
        R2A[Action Required within 30 days]
    end
    subgraph R3[Risk Level High R = 9 to 12]
        R3A[Immediate Corrective Action]
    end
    subgraph R4[Risk Level Very High R = 13 to 16]
        R4A[Stop Operation Re-engineer]
    end
```

| | **Severity 1** | **Severity 2** | **Severity 3** | **Severity 4** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Probability 4** | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 |
| **Probability 3** | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 |
| **Probability 2** | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 |
| **Probability 1** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**List and briefly explain any six principles of HACCP.**

**Model Answer (6 key points, ~150 words):**

1. **Principle 1 — Hazard Analysis:** Identify biological, chemical, and physical hazards at every step of the process and determine their severity and probability of occurrence.
2. **Principle 2 — Determine CCPs:** Use the Codex decision tree to identify Critical Control Points where hazards can be prevented, eliminated, or reduced to acceptable levels.
3. **Principle 3 — Establish Critical Limits:** Define the maximum/minimum values (temperature, time, pH, dosage) that must be met to keep the CCP under control.
4. **Principle 4 — Monitoring Procedures:** Define what is observed, how, when, and by whom at each CCP to confirm it remains within critical limits.
5. **Principle 5 — Corrective Actions:** Pre-planned actions to be taken when a deviation from critical limits is observed.
6. **Principle 6 — Verification:** Confirm the HACCP system works through audits, calibrations, and microbiological tests.
7. **Principle 7 — Record-Keeping:** Maintain documentation of CCPs, monitoring, corrective actions, and verification.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Why is n-Hexane considered a major hazard in solvent extraction plants? List four safety measures.**

**Model Answer:**

n-Hexane is hazardous due to its **low flash point (−22 °C)**, **wide explosive range (LEL 1.1 % – UEL 7.5 %)**, **TLV-TWA of only 50 ppm**, and **vapour density 2.97 (heavier than air, pools at floor)**. It is also neurotoxic (causes peripheral neuropathy on chronic exposure).

**Four safety measures:**
1. **Earth-pit with water seal** for vapour collection; floor sloped (≥1:100) to drain spills safely.
2. **Flame arrestors** on all storage tank vents; vent stack ≥12 m height.
3. **Nitrogen blanketing** of storage tanks and **explosion-proof electrical fittings** (Class I, Division 1).
4. **Continuous LEL detectors** with alarm at 10 % LEL and automatic solvent pump shutdown at 25 % LEL.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** Discuss the major safety hazards encountered in a **solvent extraction plant** for oilseeds. Explain in detail the engineering and operational controls used to mitigate these hazards. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hexane storage tank operates at 30 °C. Vapour pressure of hexane at 30 °C = 22.6 kPa. Calculate the **vapour concentration in ppm** and **compare it with the LEL**. Comment on the safety implications. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to B-A (a):**

**1. Major Hazards in Solvent Extraction Plant:**

(i) **Fire and Explosion:** Hexane vapour forms explosive mixtures with air between 1.1 % and 7.5 % (v/v). The flash point is −22 °C, so even at ambient temperature it releases ignitable vapour.

(ii) **Health Hazards:** Chronic exposure above 50 ppm causes **peripheral neuropathy**, dizziness, and CNS depression. Acute high exposure leads to narcosis.

(iii) **Asphyxiation:** Vapour displaces oxygen in confined spaces (vapour density 2.97 → heavier than air → pools).

(iv) **Mechanical & Electrical:** Sparks from non-flameproof motors, static electricity from flow, rotating expeller shafts.

(v) **Environmental:** Spillage contaminates soil and groundwater.

**2. Engineering Controls:**

(i) **Plant Layout per IS 1986 / NFPA 36:**
   - Hexane storage in open, isolated area; explosion-proof boundary walls.
   - Minimum 30 m distance from refinery and deodorizer.
   - Earth-pit with water seal; floor sloped ≥1:100 toward pit.

(ii) **Electrical Classification:**
   - All fittings **Class I, Division 1, Group D** (hexane group).
   - Flameproof enclosures, intrinsically safe instruments.
   - Static electricity control: bonding and grounding of all pipelines (resistance ≤ 1 Ω).

(iii) **Ventilation & Vapour Recovery:**
   - Cross-ventilation ≥ 6 air changes/hour.
   - Vapour recovery system (carbon adsorbers or refrigerated condensers).
   - Vent stacks ≥ 12 m high with **flame arrestors**.

(iv) **Process Controls:**
   - Continuous LEL monitoring (10 % LEL alarm, 25 % LEL auto-shutdown).
   - Nitrogen blanketing of storage and miscella tanks.
   - Pressure relief valves (PRV) and rupture discs on all vessels.
   - Emergency Shutdown (ESD) system with manual push-button stations.

(v) **Operational Controls:**
   - Permit-to-work system for hot jobs, confined-space entry.
   - Hot work ban within 15 m of solvent area unless gas-tested safe.
   - Training of operators in fire-fighting (Class B foam, CO₂, dry chemical).
   - Use of **PPE** — chemical-resistant gloves, goggles, respirators.

(vi) **Fire-Fighting System:**
   - Foam pourers on storage tanks (AFFF foam).
   - Water spray cooling of adjacent vessels (deluge system).
   - DCP (Dry Chemical Powder) extinguishers at every 10 m.

(vii) **Emergency Planning:**
   - Emergency Action Plan (EAP), evacuation drills quarterly.
   - Dyke capacity 110 % of largest tank volume.

---

#### **Model Solution to B-A (b):**

**Given:** $P_{hexane} = 22.6\;\text{kPa}$, $T = 30\;\text{°C} = 303\;\text{K}$, $M = 86.18\;\text{g/mol}$, $R = 8.314\;\text{J/(mol·K)}$, $P_{atm} = 101.325\;\text{kPa}$.

**Step 1 — Molar concentration (Ideal Gas Law):**

$$\frac{n}{V} = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{22600}{8.314 \times 303} = \frac{22600}{2519.1} = 8.972\;\text{mol/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Concentration in ppm (by volume):**

$$C_{vap} = \frac{P_{hexane}}{P_{atm}} \times 10^6 = \frac{22.6}{101.325} \times 10^6$$

$$C_{vap} = 0.22304 \times 10^6 = 2.23 \times 10^5\;\text{ppm} = 223040\;\text{ppm}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to volume %:**

$$\% \text{hexane} = 22.3\%$$

**Step 4 — Compare with LEL:**

$$LEL = 1.1\%$$

$$\frac{C_{vap}}{LEL} = \frac{22.3}{1.1} = 20.27$$

**Conclusion:** The vapour concentration is **20.3 times the LEL** of hexane — extremely hazardous. The storage tank **must never be opened to atmosphere** without nitrogen blanketing, and any leak would immediately form an explosive cloud. Safety measures must include flame arrestors, nitrogen padding, leak detection sensors, and continuous LEL monitoring.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often (a) forget to convert temperature to Kelvin, (b) use $P = 1\;\text{atm}$ instead of partial pressure, or (c) compare ppm directly with LEL % without converting to the same unit. Always bring both to % or both to ppm before comparing.

### **Question B-B (Alternative Choice for Internal Option)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What is HACCP? Explain the **seven principles of HACCP** as adopted by the Codex Alimentarius Commission. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Develop a **HACCP plan for the refining and deodorization section of an edible oil plant**, identifying at least three CCPs with their critical limits, monitoring procedures, and corrective actions. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to B-B (a):**

**Definition:**
HACCP (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points) is a **systematic, science-based, preventive** food safety management system that identifies, evaluates, and controls hazards which are significant for food safety. It was developed by Pillsbury, NASA, and the U.S. Army Natick Laboratories in the 1960s and adopted by the **Codex Alimentarius Commission in 1993 (CAC/RCP 1-1969 Rev. 3)**.

**The Seven Principles:**

**Principle 1 — Conduct a Hazard Analysis:**
List all potential biological (Salmonella, moulds), chemical (pesticides, aflatoxin, heavy metals), and physical (metal, glass) hazards at each process step. Estimate risk using $R = S \times P$ (Severity × Probability) on a 1–4 scale each.

**Principle 2 — Determine the Critical Control Points (CCPs):**
A CCP is a step at which control can be applied to prevent, eliminate, or reduce a food safety hazard to an acceptable level. The Codex decision tree is used.

**Principle 3 — Establish Critical Limits:**
Maximum and/or minimum values to which biological, chemical, or physical parameters must be controlled at a CCP to prevent, eliminate, or reduce the occurrence of a hazard. Examples: temperature ≤ 4 °C for storage; pH ≤ 4.5 for acidified foods; oil temperature 220–260 °C in deodorizer.

**Principle 4 — Establish Monitoring Procedures:**
A planned sequence of observations or measurements to assess whether a CCP is under control. Monitoring frequency is defined for each CCP.

**Principle 5 — Establish Corrective Actions:**
Pre-determined actions to take when monitoring shows that a CCP has deviated from its critical limit. The action must ensure that the affected product is not released and that the cause of deviation is corrected.

**Principle 6 — Establish Verification Procedures:**
Activities (other than monitoring) that determine the validity of the HACCP plan and confirm the system is operating according to plan — calibration, sampling, microbiological testing, internal audits.

**Principle 7 — Establish Record-Keeping and Documentation:**
Maintain records of CCP monitoring, deviations, corrective actions, verification activities, calibration of instruments, and training records. Documents must be retained for at least **2 years** or shelf-life + 1 year, whichever is longer (FSSAI).

---

#### **Model Solution to B-B (b):**

**HACCP Plan for Refining and Deodorization of Edible Oil**

**Step 1 — Product Description:**
Refined, bleached, deodorized (RBD) sunflower oil packed in 1 L PET bottles, 15 kg tin, or 200 L drum. Intended for retail consumers and food service.

**Step 2 — Process Flow:**
Crude oil → Degumming → Neutralization (Caustic) → Bleaching (Earth) → Deodorization → Polishing filtration → Packaging.

**Step 3 — CCP Identification and Critical Limits:**

| CCP | Process Step | Hazard | Critical Limit | Monitoring | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CCP-1** | Neutralization (Caustic Refining) | Excess NaOH → soap formation → carry-over into bleached oil | FFA of neutralized oil ≤ 0.1 %; Soap content ≤ 50 ppm | pH titration every 2 hours; conductivity probe online | Re-refine lot; adjust NaOH dose |
| **CCP-2** | Bleaching | Heavy metals (Pb, Cd), dioxins from spent earth; polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAH) | Bleaching earth dose 0.5–2 %; temperature 90–110 °C; contact time 20–30 min; Pb ≤ 0.1 ppm in finished oil | Temperature every 30 min; ICP-MS monthly | Re-bleach with fresh earth; quarantine lot if Pb exceeds limit |
| **CCP-3** | Deodorization | 3-MCPD esters, glycidyl esters, trans-fat formation | Temperature 220–260 °C; vacuum 3–6 mbar; time 60–90 min; stripping steam 2–5 % of oil flow; trans fat ≤ 2 % (FSSAI) | Continuous thermocouple + vacuum gauge; GC of FAMEs every batch | Reduce temperature; extend residence time; re-process |
| **CCP-4** | Polishing Filtration | Trace earth, nickel from hydrogenation | Filter ≤ 5 µm; Ni ≤ 0.1 ppm; Earth residue ≤ 0.02 % | Differential pressure across filter; ICP-MS weekly | Replace filter element |

**Verification:**
- Quarterly internal audit by QA team.
- Annual third-party audit (FSSC 22000 / ISO 22000).
- Calibration of all temperature and pressure sensors every 6 months.
- Microbiological testing of finished oil (TPC < 10 cfu/mL).

**Record-Keeping:**
- CCP logbook (manual + digital SCADA backup).
- Lab reports of every batch.
- Calibration certificates retained for 5 years.
- FSSAI traceability records.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students commonly lose marks by (a) confusing CCPs with Quality Control Points (QCPs), (b) omitting the **decision-tree logic** for CCP determination, and (c) missing **verification and record-keeping** (Principles 6 & 7). Always show **all seven principles**, not just 4–5.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Safety Aspects and HACCP Standards in Oil Industries**

- **HACCP** = Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points; **preventive**, not reactive.
- **Seven Principles:** Hazard Analysis → CCP Determination → Critical Limits → Monitoring → Corrective Actions → Verification → Record-Keeping.
- **Codex Decision Tree** is the standard for identifying CCPs (Q1–Q4).
- **Risk Score $R = S \times P$** (Severity × Probability, both 1–4 scale).
- **Prerequisites (PRPs)** must be in place before HACCP: GMP, SSOPs, pest control, calibration.
- **Hexane properties to memorize:** FP = −22 °C, AIT = 225 °C, LEL = 1.1 %, UEL = 7.5 %, vapour density = 2.97, TLV-TWA = 50 ppm.
- **Hexane vapour is heavier than air** → pools in pits and trenches; need earth pit with water seal and floor slope ≥ 1:100.
- **Hydrogenation safety:** PRV, rupture disc, flame arrestor, ESD valve, N₂ purge, catalyst quench.
- **Common CCPs in oil refining:** Aflatoxin testing at receipt, bleaching temperature/earth dosage, deodorization temperature/vacuum, polishing filtration, packaging seal integrity.
- **Critical limits:** Aflatoxin ≤ 20 µg/kg, hexane residue ≤ 500 ppm, PV ≤ 5 meq/kg, trans fat ≤ 2 %, Ni ≤ 0.1 ppm.
- **Vapour concentration formula:** $C_{vap} = \dfrac{P \cdot M}{R \cdot T} \times 10^6$ (in ppm).
- **Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for hexane:** ~15.2 kg air per kg hexane (~45 volumes).
- **NFPA 68 vent area formula:** $A_v = \dfrac{C \cdot A_s}{\sqrt{P_{red}}}$ — used for explosion relief sizing.
- **PPE matrix:** Nitrile gloves + goggles for hexane/NaOH; aluminized suit + face shield for hot oil; FFP2 for dust.
- **Key standards:** IS 1986, NFPA 36, OSHA 29 CFR 1910.106, FSSAI 2006, Codex CAC/RCP 1-1969 Rev. 4 (2003), ISO 22000:2018.
- **Totox value** = $2 \times \text{PV} + \text{AV}$; for refined oil ≤ 10 is acceptable.
- **FSSAI mandates HACCP** for all food business operators (FBOs) handling edible oils.
- **HACCP records** must be retained for **minimum 2 years** or product shelf-life + 1 year, whichever is longer.
- **HACCP is a living system** (PDCA cycle), not a one-time certificate.
- **KTU exam tip:** Always show risk scoring $R = S \times P$, decision-tree logic, and all seven principles for full marks.
- **Industrial safety ≠ HACCP:** Safety protects the **worker**; HACCP protects the **consumer**. Both are mandatory in oil industries.
